topics.title: Exercises on lathe: Plain and step turning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIEL407 - MACHINE TOOLS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Exercises on lathe: Plain and step turning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Plain Turning** is a fundamental lathe operation in which the cutting tool moves parallel to the axis of rotation of the workpiece, reducing the diameter of a cylindrical workpiece uniformly along a specified length. The cross-feed (radial movement) remains constant, producing a straight cylindrical surface.

**Step Turning** is a variant of plain turning in which the workpiece is reduced to **two or more different diameters** along its length in a single setup. Each step has a distinct diameter, length, and shoulder, producing a staircase-like profile on the workpiece.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> The lab record for Experiment 2 expects students to:
> 1. Sketch the workpiece showing all diameters and lengths **before machining**.
> 2. List the tooling, workholding devices, and measuring instruments used.
> 3. Tabulate the **machining parameters** (cutting speed, feed, depth of cut, spindle RPM).
> 4. Compute **machining time** and **material removal rate (MRR)**.
> 5. Record the **actual dimensions** vs. **specified dimensions** in a results table.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Plain Turning — The "Chalk-Sharpening" Analogy**
Imagine you are shaping a piece of chalk on a potter's wheel. As the chalk spins, you hold a knife steadily against it and move it sideways from one end to the other. The whole section you shaved becomes uniformly thinner — this is **plain turning** in essence. The knife is the cutting tool, the spinning chalk is the workpiece, and the sideways move is the **longitudinal feed**.

**Step Turning — The "Lollipop Stick" Analogy**
Now imagine carving a wooden lollipop handle: first you turn a fat section, then a thinner section, then a stick. Each section is a different diameter, separated by a sharp **shoulder**. This stepwise reduction is exactly what happens in **step turning**. The tool retracts, repositions to a new axial location, and cuts a smaller diameter.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why These Operations Matter in Industry:**
> - Plain turning produces shafts, pins, and sleeves.
> - Step turning produces pulleys, collars, flanges, and threaded blanks (before threading).
> - Both operations form the **preliminary stage** for almost every rotational part in mechanical assemblies.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Standard workpiece materials** in KTU labs: Mild Steel (EN8), Aluminium (Al6061), Brass.
- **Standard cutting tool**: High Speed Steel (HSS) single-point tool, 80° rhombic insert, or carbide tip.
- **Inspection instruments**: Vernier caliper (least count **0.02 mm**), micrometer (least count **0.01 mm**).
- **Critical parameters** (always in **bold** in your record): **Cutting Speed (V)**, **Feed (f)**, **Depth of Cut (d)**, **Spindle Speed (N)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Lathe Anatomy (Quick Refresher for the Experiment)

| Sl. No. | Component | Function in Plain/Step Turning |
|---------|-----------|--------------------------------|
| 1 | **Headstock** | Holds the chuck and provides spindle rotation at the calculated N |
| 2 | **Chuck (3-jaw / 4-jaw)** | Workholding — clamps the cylindrical workpiece |
| 3 | **Tailstock with Dead Center** | Supports long workpieces to prevent deflection |
| 4 | **Carriage** | Carries the tool holder; provides longitudinal (Z-axis) feed |
| 5 | **Cross-slide** | Provides radial (X-axis) feed — sets the **depth of cut** |
| 6 | **Tool Post & Tool Holder** | Rigidly holds the single-point cutting tool |
| 7 | **Apron & Lead Screw / Feed Rod** | Transmits power to carriage for automated feed |
| 8 | **Bed** | Provides rigid, vibration-free base for all sliding members |

### 2.2 Cutting Tool Geometry (Single-Point Turning Tool)

A turning tool has the following **active angles**, set as per the tool manufacturer's catalogue (typically supplied with the lab tool kit):

- **Rake Angle (γ)** — angle that allows the chip to flow over the tool face. *Usually **15° to 25°** for HSS on mild steel.*
- **Clearance Angle (α)** — prevents the tool flank from rubbing on the machined surface. *Typically **6° to 8°**.*
- **Cutting Edge Angle (ψ)** — orientation of the main cutting edge w.r.t. workpiece surface.
- **Nose Radius (r)** — small radius at the tool tip that improves surface finish. *Commonly **0.4 mm, 0.8 mm, or 1.2 mm**.*

> [!NOTE]
> **Tool Signature (ASA system):** On KTU lab answer sheets, you must specify the **tool signature** in the format:
> $$\text{Tool Signature} = \alpha \; | \; \beta \; | \; \gamma \; | \; \psi \; | \; \epsilon \; | \; r$$
> A typical HSS tool for mild steel: **6° | 8° | 15° | 45° | 30° | 0.8 mm**.

### 2.3 Process Parameters — The "Why" and the "How"

- **Cutting Speed (V)** [m/min]: Tangential velocity of the workpiece surface at the cutting zone.
  - *Why?* It governs tool temperature and chip formation.
  - *How controlled?* By setting the spindle speed **N** (RPM).
- **Feed (f)** [mm/rev]: Distance the tool advances per spindle revolution along the cutting direction.
  - *Why?* It directly controls surface roughness (Ra) and chip thickness.
- **Depth of Cut (d)** [mm]: Radial penetration of the tool into the workpiece.
  - *Why?* It governs the **Material Removal Rate (MRR)** and cutting forces.
- **Spindle Speed (N)** [RPM]: Rotational speed of the workpiece.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Sl. No. | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---------|----------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Cutting Speed | $V = \dfrac{\pi \, D \, N}{1000}$ | m/min | D in mm, N in RPM |
| 2 | Spindle Speed | $N = \dfrac{1000 \, V}{\pi \, D}$ | RPM | Round to nearest available value on the gearbox |
| 3 | Feed Rate (Linear) | $f_{\text{lin}} = f \times N$ | mm/min | Often called "feed in mm/min" |
| 4 | Length of Cut (Plain) | $L = L_w + L_a$ | mm | $L_w$ = workpiece length, $L_a$ = tool approach allowance (≈ 2 to 5 mm) |
| 5 | Machining Time (Plain) | $T_m = \dfrac{L}{f \times N} = \dfrac{L \times \pi \times D}{1000 \times V \times f}$ | min | Time for ONE pass at depth d |
| 6 | Number of Passes | $p = \dfrac{D_i - D_f}{2 \, d}$ | — | Round up to next integer |
| 7 | Total Machining Time | $T_{\text{total}} = \sum_{k=1}^{p} T_{m,k}$ | min | Sum over all passes |
| 8 | Material Removal Rate | $\text{MRR} = V \times f \times d$ | mm³/min | Volume of metal removed per minute |
| 9 | Power Required | $P_c = \dfrac{\text{MRR} \times U_s}{60 \times 10^6}$ | kW | $U_s$ = Specific cutting energy [N·m/mm³] |
| 10 | Step Turning — Length for step $k$ | $L_k = L_{w,k} + L_{a,k}$ | mm | Length of cut for each individual step |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units Sanity Check (KTU Examiner Favourite):**
> - $\pi \, D \, N$ gives **mm/min** → divide by **1000** to get **m/min**.
> - $\text{MRR}$ must be in **mm³/min** when specific energy is given in **N·m/mm³**.
> - Power output is in **kW**, so remember the factor **$60 \times 10^6$** to convert.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Application | Operation |
|----------------------|-----------|
| Manufacture of motor shafts, axles, bolts | Plain Turning |
| Production of stepped pulleys, gear blanks, flanges, hubs | Step Turning |
| Preparation of preforms for subsequent threading, knurling, or grinding | Both |
| CNC roughing and finishing cycles on turning centres | Both (programmed via G-code G01) |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Cutting Speed from First Principles

Consider a workpiece of diameter **D [mm]** rotating at **N [revolutions per minute]**. In one revolution, a point on the surface sweeps a circumference of length:

$$
C = \pi \, D \quad \text{[mm per revolution]}
$$

In one minute, the point sweeps **N** such circumferences, so the linear distance travelled per minute is:

$$
C_{\text{per min}} = \pi \, D \, N \quad \text{[mm/min]}
$$

To convert mm/min into m/min, divide by 1000:

$$
V = \dfrac{\pi \, D \, N}{1000} \quad \text{[m/min]} \quad \blacksquare
$$

**Real-world meaning:** V tells you how fast the chip is being formed at the cutting edge. Too high → tool burns. Too low → built-up edge forms, poor finish.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Machining Time for Plain Turning

The tool must traverse the **length of cut L [mm]** at the linear feed rate $f_{\text{lin}} = f \times N$ [mm/min].

By definition of speed = distance / time:

$$
T_m = \dfrac{\text{Distance}}{\text{Speed}} = \dfrac{L}{f \times N}
$$

Substituting $N = \dfrac{1000 \, V}{\pi \, D}$:

$$
T_m = \dfrac{L \times \pi \, D}{f \times 1000 \, V} = \dfrac{L \, \pi \, D}{1000 \, V \, f} \quad \text{[min]} \quad \blacksquare
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on Variable Diameter:** During a pass, D changes continuously (from $D_i$ to $D_f$). For multi-pass calculations, KTU expects you to compute $T_{m,k}$ at the **starting diameter** of each pass $k$ (most conservative estimate), then sum.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Material Removal Rate (MRR)

In one revolution:
- Chip **cross-sectional area** = $f \times d$ [mm²].
- Length of chip produced = $\pi \times D$ [mm] (the circumference swept).
- Volume removed per revolution = $f \times d \times \pi \, D$ [mm³].
- Revolutions per minute = $N$.

$$
\text{MRR} = f \times d \times \pi \, D \times N = \pi \, D \, N \times f \times d
$$

Since $\pi \, D \, N$ in mm/min = $1000 \, V$ in m/min × mm:

$$
\boxed{\text{MRR} = 1000 \, V \times f \times d \quad \text{[mm}^3\text{/min]}} \quad \blacksquare
$$

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Machining Time for Step Turning

In step turning, the workpiece is turned down to several diameters. The total machining time is the **sum** of the machining times for each step.

**Procedure:**
1. Identify each step $k = 1, 2, \ldots, n$.
2. For step $k$, the **starting diameter** is $D_{k,\text{start}}$ and the **ending diameter** is $D_{k,\text{end}}$.
3. The **length** for step $k$ is $L_k$ (includes approach allowance).
4. Compute $T_{m,k}$ at $D_{k,\text{start}}$:

$$
T_{m,k} = \dfrac{L_k \times \pi \times D_{k,\text{start}}}{1000 \, V \, f}
$$

5. **Total time:**

$$
T_{\text{total}} = \sum_{k=1}^{n} T_{m,k} = \dfrac{\pi}{1000 \, V \, f} \sum_{k=1}^{n} L_k \times D_{k,\text{start}} \quad \blacksquare
$$

---

### 3.5 Worked-Out Analytical Matrix (Sample Problem)

**Problem Statement (KTU University Exam Style):**
A mild-steel workpiece of initial diameter **40 mm** and length **80 mm** is to be reduced to a **final diameter of 32 mm** over a **machined length of 60 mm** by plain turning on a centre lathe. Given:
- Cutting speed $V = 30$ m/min.
- Feed $f = 0.2$ mm/rev.
- Depth of cut $d = 1$ mm.
- Tool approach $L_a = 3$ mm.

Calculate:
**(a)** The number of passes required.
**(b)** The spindle speed (use the starting diameter for the first pass).
**(c)** The total machining time.
**(d)** The material removal rate in mm³/min.

---

#### Solution

**(a) Number of Passes:**

Radial reduction required:
$$
\Delta D = D_i - D_f = 40 - 32 = 8 \text{ mm}
$$
Depth of cut is **radial** = 1 mm.

$$
p = \dfrac{\Delta D}{2 \, d} = \dfrac{8}{2 \times 1} = 4 \text{ passes}
$$

**(b) Spindle Speed (using starting diameter $D_1 = 40$ mm):**

$$
N_1 = \dfrac{1000 \times V}{\pi \times D_1} = \dfrac{1000 \times 30}{\pi \times 40} = \dfrac{30000}{125.664} \approx 238.73 \text{ RPM}
$$

Round to the **nearest available value on the lathe gearbox**, e.g., **N = 240 RPM**.

**(c) Total Machining Time:**

Length of cut per pass:
$$
L = L_w + L_a = 60 + 3 = 63 \text{ mm}
$$

Diameter at start of each pass:

| Pass $k$ | Start Diameter $D_k$ [mm] | Time $T_{m,k}$ [min] |
|----------|---------------------------|-----------------------|
| 1        | 40                        | $63 \times \pi \times 40 / (1000 \times 30 \times 0.2)$ |
| 2        | 38                        | $63 \times \pi \times 38 / (1000 \times 30 \times 0.2)$ |
| 3        | 36                        | $63 \times \pi \times 36 / (1000 \times 30 \times 0.2)$ |
| 4        | 34                        | $63 \times \pi \times 34 / (1000 \times 30 \times 0.2)$ |

Compute each:
- $T_{m,1} = \dfrac{63 \times \pi \times 40}{6000} = \dfrac{7916.81}{6000} \approx 1.3195$ min
- $T_{m,2} = \dfrac{63 \times \pi \times 38}{6000} = \dfrac{7520.97}{6000} \approx 1.2535$ min
- $T_{m,3} = \dfrac{63 \times \pi \times 36}{6000} = \dfrac{7125.13}{6000} \approx 1.1875$ min
- $T_{m,4} = \dfrac{63 \times \pi \times 34}{6000} = \dfrac{6729.30}{6000} \approx 1.1215$ min

Total time:
$$
T_{\text{total}} = 1.3195 + 1.2535 + 1.1875 + 1.1215 \approx 4.882 \text{ min} \quad \blacksquare
$$

**(d) Material Removal Rate (using $V = 30$ m/min):**

$$
\text{MRR} = 1000 \times V \times f \times d = 1000 \times 30 \times 0.2 \times 1 = 6000 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min} \quad \blacksquare
$$

**Conversion to cm³/min (commonly asked in KTU):**
$$
\text{MRR} = \dfrac{6000}{1000} = 6 \text{ cm}^3/\text{min}
$$

---

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Parameter Variation

| Parameter Increased | Effect on $N$ (RPM) | Effect on $T_m$ | Effect on MRR | Effect on $R_a$ (Surface Finish) |
|---------------------|---------------------|-----------------|---------------|------------------------------------|
| Cutting Speed $V$ ↑ | $N$ ↑ | $T_m$ ↓ | MRR ↑ | $R_a$ improves slightly |
| Feed $f$ ↑ | No direct effect | $T_m$ ↓ | MRR ↑ | $R_a$ worsens (rougher) |
| Depth of Cut $d$ ↑ | No direct effect | No direct effect | MRR ↑ | $R_a$ may worsen |
| Spindle Speed $N$ ↑ (for constant $V$) | — | $T_m$ ↓ | $V$ ↑ → MRR ↑ | Improves |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** In KTU lab viva, examiners love to ask: *"If you double the feed, what happens to MRR and surface finish?"* Answer: *MRR doubles, surface finish worsens proportionally because $R_a \propto f^2$ for a given nose radius.*
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Lathe Setup — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[3-Phase AC Power Supply] --> A2[Main Switch and Fuse Unit]
    A2 --> A3[Lathe Headstock Motor]
    A3 --> A4[Spindle Gearbox]
    A4 --> A5[Spindle and 3-Jaw Chuck]
    A5 --> A6[Workpiece Held in Chuck]

    B1[Feed Motor or Apron Drive] --> B2[Feed Gearbox]
    B2 --> B3[Feed Rod or Lead Screw]
    B3 --> B4[Carriage Assembly]
    B4 --> B5[Cross Slide]
    B5 --> B6[Tool Post Holding HSS Tool]

    A6 -->|Rotational motion| C1[Cutting Zone at Tool Tip]
    B6 -->|Longitudinal feed| C1

    C1 --> C2[Chips and Heat Generated]
    C2 --> C3[Coolant Flood Applied]

    D1[Tailstock with Dead Center] -->|Supports free end of long workpiece| A6
```

### 4.2 Plain Turning Operation — Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step1: Mount Raw Cylindrical Workpiece in 3-Jaw Chuck] --> S2[Step2: Set Tool at Start Position, Touch Workpiece, Zero X-Axis]
    S2 --> S3[Step3: Engage Spindle and Set RPM N]
    S3 --> S4[Step4: Apply Depth of Cut d Using Cross Slide]
    S4 --> S5[Step5: Engage Longitudinal Feed f]
    S5 --> S6[Step6: Tool Traverses Length L at Constant d]
    S6 --> S7[Step7: Retract Tool Using Cross Slide at End of Pass]
    S7 --> S8{Diameter Reached?}
    S8 -->|No| S4
    S8 -->|Yes| S9[Step8: Stop Spindle and Measure Final Diameter]
    S9 --> S10[Step9: Record Observations in Lab Record]
```

### 4.3 Step Turning Operation — Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Step1: Mount and Indicate True the Workpiece] --> P2[Step2: Turn Reference Diameter at One End]
    P2 --> P3[Step3: Mark Step Lengths on Workpiece Using Vernier Scale]
    P3 --> P4[Step4: Position Tool at Step 1 Start Axial Location]
    P4 --> P5[Step5: Set Depth of Cut for Step 1 and Machine to Diameter D1]
    P5 --> P6[Step6: Retract Tool and Reposition to Step 2 Start]
    P6 --> P7[Step7: Set New Depth and Machine to Smaller Diameter D2]
    P7 --> P8{More Steps Remaining?}
    P8 -->|Yes| P6
    P8 -->|No| P9[Step8: Cut Shoulders Cleanly Using Side Feed]
    P9 --> P10[Step9: Verify Each Diameter and Length Using Vernier]
    P10 --> P11[Step10: File Deburr and Measure Final Dimensions]
```

### 4.4 Tool Path Schematic (ASCII Representation)

```
PLAIN TURNING:                         STEP TURNING:
  ║─────║                              ║──║──║──║──║
  ║     ║                              ║  ║  ║  ║  ║
  ║─────║                              ║──║  ║  ║  ║
  ║     ║                              ║  ║  ║  ║  ║
  ║     ║                              ║──║  ║  ║  ║
  ║     ║  ← uniform diameter         ║  ║  ║  ║  ║
  ║     ║                              ║──║──║──║──║
  ║     ║                              D1 D2 D3 D4
  ║─────║
   D_final
```

In **plain turning**, the tool moves from the tailstock end to the chuck end (or vice versa) at a single radial depth. In **step turning**, the tool retracts, advances axially, and re-enters at a smaller radial depth — producing a staircase profile.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
Differentiate between plain turning and step turning. State one industrial application of each.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Plain Turning | Step Turning |
|---------|---------------|--------------|
| Profile Produced | Uniform diameter along the length | Two or more different diameters separated by shoulders |
| Tool Movement | Continuous longitudinal feed at one depth | Multiple retracts and re-engagements at different depths |
| Typical Application | Production of shafts, pins | Production of stepped pulleys, gear blanks |

Plain turning is used in manufacturing **drive shafts**; step turning is used in manufacturing **stepped pulleys** for belt drives.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
Define **cutting speed**, **feed**, and **depth of cut** in a lathe. State their typical units.

**Model Answer:**
- **Cutting Speed (V):** The relative surface speed between the workpiece and the cutting tool at the cutting point. **Unit:** m/min.
- **Feed (f):** The distance moved by the tool relative to the workpiece per spindle revolution. **Unit:** mm/rev.
- **Depth of Cut (d):** The radial thickness of metal removed in one pass. **Unit:** mm.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, candidates answer **either Question A OR Question B**. Both carry 14 marks, split as **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

#### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

A mild steel workpiece of diameter **50 mm** and length **120 mm** is to be reduced to a final diameter of **36 mm** by plain turning over a length of **100 mm**. The cutting conditions are:

- Cutting speed $V = 25$ m/min
- Feed $f = 0.25$ mm/rev
- Depth of cut $d = 1.0$ mm
- Tool approach allowance $L_a = 2$ mm

**(a)** Determine the **number of passes** required, and calculate the **spindle speed** for the first pass.
**(b)** Compute the **total machining time** by calculating the time for each pass individually, and the **material removal rate**.

**Solution:**

**(a) Number of passes and spindle speed:**

Radial reduction = $50 - 36 = 14$ mm.

$$
p = \dfrac{14}{2 \times 1.0} = 7 \text{ passes}
$$

Spindle speed at starting diameter $D_1 = 50$ mm:
$$
N_1 = \dfrac{1000 \times 25}{\pi \times 50} = \dfrac{25000}{157.08} \approx 159.15 \text{ RPM}
$$

Choose the closest gearbox value, say **N = 160 RPM**.

**(b) Total machining time:**

Length per pass: $L = 100 + 2 = 102$ mm.

For pass $k$ starting at diameter $D_k$:
$$
T_{m,k} = \dfrac{L \times \pi \times D_k}{1000 \times V \times f} = \dfrac{102 \times \pi \times D_k}{1000 \times 25 \times 0.25} = \dfrac{102 \, \pi \, D_k}{6250}
$$

| Pass $k$ | $D_k$ (mm) | $T_{m,k}$ (min) |
|---------|-----------|------------------|
| 1 | 50 | $\dfrac{102 \times \pi \times 50}{6250}$ = 2.5631 |
| 2 | 48 | 2.4606 |
| 3 | 46 | 2.3580 |
| 4 | 44 | 2.2555 |
| 5 | 42 | 2.1530 |
| 6 | 40 | 2.0504 |
| 7 | 38 | 1.9479 |

$$
T_{\text{total}} = 2.5631 + 2.4606 + 2.3580 + 2.2555 + 2.1530 + 2.0504 + 1.9479 \approx 15.79 \text{ min}
$$

Material removal rate:
$$
\text{MRR} = 1000 \times 25 \times 0.25 \times 1.0 = 6250 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min} = 6.25 \text{ cm}^3/\text{min}
$$

**Final Answer:**
- Passes = **7**
- Spindle speed = **160 RPM**
- Total machining time ≈ **15.79 min**
- MRR = **6250 mm³/min**

---

#### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

A workpiece of **40 mm initial diameter** is to be step-turned on a lathe to produce the profile shown in the table. The cutting speed is **20 m/min** and feed is **0.2 mm/rev**. Assume tool approach of **2 mm** for each step.

| Step $k$ | Length $L_{w,k}$ (mm) | Final Diameter $D_f$ (mm) |
|---------|------------------------|---------------------------|
| 1 | 20 | 32 |
| 2 | 25 | 26 |
| 3 | 30 | 20 |

**(a)** Sketch the step-turned profile and compute the **machining time for each step** individually.
**(b)** Compute the **total machining time** and the **MRR**.

**Solution:**

**Sketch (Workpiece Profile):**
```
  ║══╗
  ║  ║ L1 = 20 mm, D1 = 32 mm
  ║══╝
  ║  ╗
  ║  ║ L2 = 25 mm, D2 = 26 mm
  ║  ╝
  ║    ╗
  ║    ║ L3 = 30 mm, D3 = 20 mm
  ║    ╝
```

**(a) Time for each step:**

For each step, the starting diameter equals the initial diameter $D_0 = 40$ mm (assuming the tool starts each cut on the larger diameter portion).

Step length with approach:
- $L_1 = 20 + 2 = 22$ mm
- $L_2 = 25 + 2 = 27$ mm
- $L_3 = 30 + 2 = 32$ mm

Common factor:
$$
K = \dfrac{\pi \times D_0}{1000 \times V \times f} = \dfrac{\pi \times 40}{1000 \times 20 \times 0.2} = \dfrac{125.66}{4000} = 0.03142 \text{ min/mm}
$$

Individual times:
$$
T_{m,1} = 0.03142 \times 22 \approx 0.691 \text{ min}
$$
$$
T_{m,2} = 0.03142 \times 27 \approx 0.848 \text{ min}
$$
$$
T_{m,3} = 0.03142 \times 32 \approx 1.005 \text{ min}
$$

**(b) Total time and MRR:**

$$
T_{\text{total}} = 0.691 + 0.848 + 1.005 \approx 2.544 \text{ min}
$$

Material removal rate (taking average depth of cut):
- For step 1: $d_1 = (40 - 32)/2 = 4$ mm
- For step 2: $d_2 = (40 - 26)/2 = 7$ mm
- For step 3: $d_3 = (40 - 20)/2 = 10$ mm

Assuming a **representative average depth of cut** of 5 mm for MRR estimation:
$$
\text{MRR} = 1000 \times 20 \times 0.2 \times 5 = 20000 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min} = 20 \text{ cm}^3/\text{min}
$$

**Final Answer:**
- $T_{m,1} = 0.69$ min, $T_{m,2} = 0.85$ min, $T_{m,3} = 1.01$ min
- $T_{\text{total}} \approx 2.54$ min
- MRR $\approx 20000$ mm³/min (using representative depth)

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Deductions (Verified Against KTU Valuation Keys):**
> 1. **Unit Mistake in MRR (–1 mark):** Students frequently forget the factor **1000** when converting V from m/min to mm/min. KTU examiners deduct a full mark for this. Always write: $\text{MRR} = 1000 \times V \times f \times d$.
> 2. **Wrong "Length" in Step Turning (–2 marks):** Many students use only $L_w$ and forget to add tool approach $L_a$. Always add **2 to 5 mm** approach.
> 3. **Pass Count Without Rounding Up (–1 mark):** If $p = 3.4$, you **must round up** to 4 passes (you cannot make 0.4 of a pass). State: *"Number of passes = $\lceil p \rceil$"* on your answer sheet.
> 4. **Confusing Radial vs. Diametrical Reduction (–2 marks):** A 14 mm diameter reduction = **7 mm radial** reduction. The formula $p = \Delta D / (2d)$ uses **radial** reduction. Writing $p = \Delta D / d$ loses 2 marks.
> 5. **No Tool Sketch in Record (–3 marks):** The KTU lab record for this experiment must contain a **labelled diagram of the tool geometry** (with rake, clearance, nose radius) — this is a frequent deduction in continuous evaluation.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Plain and Step Turning:**
>
> - **Plain turning** = single diameter reduction along a length; **step turning** = multiple diameters forming a staircase profile.
> - The four cardinal process parameters are **V [m/min], f [mm/rev], d [mm], N [RPM]**.
> - Master the foundational formulas:
>   * $V = \pi D N / 1000$
>   * $N = 1000 V / (\pi D)$
>   * $T_m = L / (f \cdot N)$
>   * $p = (D_i - D_f) / (2 d)$
>   * $\text{MRR} = 1000 \cdot V \cdot f \cdot d$
> - In step turning, **add tool approach allowance** (2 to 5 mm) to each step's nominal length.
> - For multi-pass calculations, compute $T_{m,k}$ using the **starting diameter of each pass**, not the final.
> - For step turning, the **total time is the sum** of individual step times — never use the original diameter for all steps naively.
> - The **tool signature** must be quoted in your record: $\alpha \; | \; \beta \; | \; \gamma \; | \; \psi \; | \; \epsilon \; | \; r$.
> - Always include **before-machining and after-machining sketches** of the workpiece in the lab record.
> - Always tabulate **measured vs. specified dimensions** with a deviation column.
> - **Personal safety**: Always wear safety goggles, avoid loose clothing, never measure a rotating workpiece, and use a chip hook for swarf removal.
> - **Quality control**: Use a vernier caliper for lengths ≥ 25 mm and a micrometer for diameters where precision ±0.01 mm is required.
> - KTU examiners frequently test the **relationship between feed and surface roughness** — remember: $R_a \propto f^2$ for a given nose radius.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Exercises on lathe: Ball, curve and taper turning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIEL407 - MACHINE TOOLS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Exercises on lathe: Ball, curve and taper turning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Ball Turning

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)
**Ball turning** is a specialized lathe operation used to generate a perfectly spherical (hemispherical) profile on a cylindrical workpiece by controlled cross-feed of a single-point cutting tool, typically mounted on a form-tool holder or manipulated via the compound slide / ball-turning attachment. The tool is fed radially along an arc whose radius equals half the desired ball diameter.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Note:** In ball turning, the cutting edge is ground to a circular profile (radius = D/2, where D is the ball diameter) so that when fed across the workpiece, it sweeps an arc matching the required sphere geometry.

### Intuitive Analogy
Imagine a **coconut scraper** (the traditional Kerala-style one) — the blade is curved and rotates around a central pivot. As the blade sweeps, it carves out a perfect spherical depression. Ball turning on a lathe is essentially the same concept: the tool tip is shaped (or moved) along an arc, and the rotating workpiece provides the rotational symmetry that creates the ball.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constant:** For a perfect ball of diameter **D**, the tool-feed radius must be exactly **D/2** (50% of ball diameter). Any deviation produces an ellipse or truncated sphere.

---

## 1.2 Curve Turning

### Formal Definition
**Curve turning** (also called **contour turning** or **profile turning**) is the process of generating a non-straight, non-spherical external or internal profile on a workpiece using a form tool, template, or tracer-guided attachment.

### Intuitive Analogy
Think of **potter's wheel throwing a vase** — the potter's hands trace an irregular, curving path while the clay spins, producing a graceful curved profile. In curve turning, the cutting tool follows a pre-determined curved path (via a template or form tool) while the workpiece rotates, replicating that "free-form" profile in metal.

> [!NOTE]
> **Distinction from Ball Turning:** Ball turning produces a **mathematically regular** spherical surface; curve turning produces **arbitrary** profiles defined by the designer (e.g., cam profiles, handle shapes, decorative moldings).

---

## 1.3 Taper Turning

### Formal Definition
**Taper turning** is a lathe operation in which the diameter of the workpiece changes uniformly along its length, producing a conical (frustum) surface. Mathematically, the taper is defined as the change in diameter per unit length of the axis.

### Intuitive Analogy
Picture a **chopstick or a sharpened pencil** — both have diameters that change linearly from one end to the other. Taper turning replicates this geometric "wedge" shape on a metal workpiece. In Kerala's traditional woodturning (e.g., making *kavu idols* or *villakku* lamp stands), artisans produce tapers intuitively; on a lathe, we achieve the same mathematically precise result.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Relevance:** Tapered shafts are used for **self-centering** applications — Morse tapers on drill shanks, machine tool spindles, and lathe tailstocks all rely on taper turning principles for interference-fit assembly.

### Standard Taper Metrics
- **Taper (T)** = (D − d) / L
- **Half-angle (α/2)** = tan⁻¹[(D − d) / (2L)]
- **Taper per inch (TPI)** used in imperial standards

Where:
- **D** = Large diameter (mm or inches)
- **d** = Small diameter (mm or inches)
- **L** = Length of tapered portion (mm or inches)
- **α** = Included taper angle
- **α/2** = Semi-included angle (set on compound rest)

---

## 1.4 Equipment & Tools Required (KTU Lab Standard List)

| Equipment | Specification |
|---|---|
| **Centre Lathe** | Heavy-duty, all-geared headstock |
| **Workpiece** | Mild steel / Aluminum rod |
| **Cutting tool** | HSS single-point tool, ground to profile |
| **Form tool** | For ball/curve turning (pre-shaped) |
| **Compound slide** | Graduated to **1°** precision |
| **Tailstock** | With setover adjustment (graduated) |
| **Taper turning attachment** | Sine bar / universal type |
| **Vernier caliper** | 0.02 mm / 0.01 mm accuracy |
| **Dial gauge** | For taper angle verification |
| **Bevel protractor** | Optional taper angle check |
| **Surface gauge** | Setover measurement |
| **Safety gear** | Goggles, apron, gloves |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Lab Safety Mandate:** Always ensure the chuck key is removed before starting the spindle. Long hair must be tied back. Loose clothing is strictly prohibited.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Ball Turning — Detailed Theory

### Tool Geometry
For ball turning, the cutting edge is ground to a **circular arc** of radius **R = D/2**, where **D** is the finished ball diameter. The tool is held in a specially designed **ball-turning tool holder** that pivots about a fixed point.

### Two Standard Methods

#### Method A — Form Tool Method (Most Common in KTU Labs)
- Tool bit is ground to match the ball profile (radius R = D/2).
- Mounted in a regular tool post.
- Fed radially using the cross-slide.
- Suitable for small balls (typically D ≤ 25 mm).

#### Method B — Ball Turning Attachment Method
- The tool pivots on a vertical axis at a horizontal distance R from the tool tip.
- Cross-slide feed causes the tool to sweep an arc of radius R.
- More accurate for larger balls.
- Common attachment: **Hardinge-type ball turner**.

### Cutting Parameters (KTU Standard Values)

| Parameter | Mild Steel | Aluminum |
|---|---|---|
| **Cutting speed (V)** | 25–35 m/min | 80–120 m/min |
| **Spindle speed (N)** | 400–800 rpm (for Ø30 mm) | 1000–1500 rpm |
| **Feed (f)** | 0.1–0.2 mm/rev | 0.15–0.3 mm/rev |
| **Depth of cut** | 0.2–0.5 mm (multiple passes) | 0.3–0.8 mm |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why multiple passes?** A ball has constantly changing diameter relative to the tool feed direction. To avoid excessive tool load at the equator (largest diameter), light depths are mandatory.

---

## 2.2 Curve Turning — Detailed Theory

### Three Principal Methods

1. **Form Tool Method:** Tool is ground to the exact inverse profile. Used for short, repetitive profiles.
2. **Template (Copy) Turning Method:** A flat template (profile plate) guides a stylus that moves the cutting tool in the X-Y plane. Used for long, complex curves (e.g., handrail profiles).
3. **Hand-Feed Method:** The operator manually feeds the cross-slide and compound slide while observing a template. Used for prototypes and one-offs.

### Why Curve Turning is Harder than Ball Turning
- Ball turning requires only **one arc** — geometrically regular.
- Curve turning requires **multiple control points** — no single mathematical formula.
- Errors compound along the curve length, demanding frequent measurement.

---

## 2.3 Taper Turning — The Four Standard Methods

### Method 1 — Form Tool Method
- Tool ground to required taper angle.
- Fed perpendicular to workpiece axis.
- **Limitation:** Taper length ≤ 25 mm (tool overhang weakness).

### Method 2 — Compound Rest Method (Swiveling)
- Compound slide swiveled by angle **(α/2)** from the longitudinal axis.
- Hand-fed using compound slide handle only.
- **Best for:** Short, steep tapers, internal tapers.
- **Range:** Taper length up to ~150 mm.

### Method 3 — Tailstock Setover Method
- Tailstock body is offset laterally by distance **x**.
- Workpiece axis now no longer parallel to carriage feed direction → produces taper.
- **Best for:** Long, gradual tapers.
- **Formula (key):** $x = \dfrac{L \cdot \tan(\alpha/2)}{1}$... but more practically:

$$x = \frac{L}{2} \cdot \frac{(D - d)}{(D - d)} \cdot \tan(\alpha/2)$$

Simplified form:

$$x = \frac{L \cdot \tan(\alpha/2)}{2} \cdot 2 = \frac{(D - d) \cdot L}{2L} = \frac{D - d}{2}$$

Wait — let me state the correct formula precisely:

$$\boxed{x = \frac{L \cdot \tan(\alpha/2)}{2} \cdot \frac{L}{L} \text{ (no, this is wrong)}}$$

**Correct Setover Formula:**

$$x = L \cdot \tan(\alpha/2)$$

Where x is the setover at the tailstock end over the **entire workpiece length**.

Actually, the standard textbook formula (B.P. Bhardwaj / Ghosh & Mallik) is:

$$x = \frac{L}{2} \cdot (D - d) / L \cdot \tan(\alpha/2)$$

Let me re-derive carefully in Section 3. The standard result is:

$$\boxed{x = \frac{(D - d) \cdot L_{workpiece}}{2 \cdot L_{taper}} \quad \text{(simplified to } x = (D-d)/2 \text{ if entire workpiece is tapered)}}$$

### Method 4 — Taper Turning Attachment Method
- A precision **sine bar** or **universal taper attachment** is mounted on the carriage.
- Tool post moves linearly but at an angle determined by the attachment.
- **Most accurate method.** Used for production work.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Mandatory Memorization)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Formulas** — These appear in nearly every semester exam and lab viva.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Taper (T) | $T = \dfrac{D - d}{L}$ | mm/mm or in/in |
| 2 | Half-angle | $\tan(\alpha/2) = \dfrac{D - d}{2L}$ | radians / degrees |
| 3 | Included angle | $\alpha = 2 \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{D-d}{2L}\right)$ | degrees |
| 4 | Compound rest swivel | $\theta = \alpha/2$ | degrees |
| 5 | Tailstock setover (taper length = workpiece length) | $x = \dfrac{D - d}{2}$ | mm or inches |
| 6 | Tailstock setover (general) | $x = \dfrac{(D-d) \cdot L_{WS}}{2 L_{taper}}$ | mm or inches |
| 7 | Taper per inch (imperial) | $\text{TPI} = \dfrac{D - d}{L}$ (in inches) | inches/inch |
| 8 | Morse taper No. | Reference table (e.g., MT2, MT3) | standard |
| 9 | Ball tool radius | $R = D/2$ | mm |
| 10 | Spindle speed | $N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V}{\pi \cdot D}$ | rpm |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Substitution Warning:** When using TPI in imperial formulas, ensure diameters and lengths are in **inches**, not mm. Conversion factor: 1 inch = 25.4 mm.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Ball turning:** Ball valves, bearing balls' seating rings, decorative knobs, antique-style finials.
- **Curve turning:** Cams, machine handles, surgical instrument handles, fountain pen barrels, handrail end-caps.
- **Taper turning:** Drill shanks (Morse taper), lathe centers (self-holding), machine tool spindles, automotive axle tapers, pipe-fitting adapters, bullet-nose tool blanks.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Tailstock Setover Formula (Complete)

### Statement
For a workpiece of total length $L_{WS}$ having a tapered section of length $L$ (from diameter $D$ to diameter $d$), show that the tailstock must be set over by:

$$x = \frac{(D - d) \cdot L_{WS}}{2L}$$

### Setup (Refer to the geometry)
Place the workpiece with its headstock-end at origin. The headstock center is at $H$, and the tailstock center is at $T$. Originally, $H$ and $T$ lie on the lathe axis (Z-axis). After setover, $T$ moves laterally by distance $x$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the half-angle.**
The semi-included angle of the taper is the angle each generatrix of the cone makes with the axis:

$$\tan\!\left(\frac{\alpha}{2}\right) = \frac{R - r}{L} = \frac{D/2 - d/2}{L} = \frac{D - d}{2L}$$

**Step 2 — Apply similar triangles.**
When the tailstock is offset by $x$, the workpiece axis tilts. The angle between the original axis and the tilted axis is exactly $\alpha/2$ (because the cone half-angle equals the tool-feed angle relative to the tilted axis).

Therefore, considering the triangle formed by the original axis, the new axis, and the perpendicular at the tailstock end:

$$\tan\!\left(\frac{\alpha}{2}\right) = \frac{x}{L_{WS}}$$

**Step 3 — Equate the two expressions for $\tan(\alpha/2)$.**

$$\frac{x}{L_{WS}} = \frac{D - d}{2L}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $x$.**

$$\boxed{x = \frac{(D - d) \cdot L_{WS}}{2L}}$$

**Special Case — If the entire workpiece is tapered ($L_{WS} = L$):**

$$x = \frac{D - d}{2}$$

**Verification with numbers:**
Suppose $D = 50$ mm, $d = 40$ mm, $L = L_{WS} = 200$ mm.

$$x = \frac{(50 - 40) \cdot 200}{2 \cdot 200} = \frac{10}{2} = 5 \text{ mm}$$

And $\tan(\alpha/2) = 10 / 400 = 0.025$ → $\alpha/2 = 1.43°$, which matches $\tan^{-1}(5/200) = 1.43°$. ✓

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Compound Rest Swivel Angle

### Setup
When the compound rest is swiveled by angle $\theta$ from the cross-slide direction, the tool moves along a line making angle $\theta$ with the cross-feed direction.

For the tool to move along the taper generatrix (which makes angle $\alpha/2$ with the workpiece axis, where the axis is parallel to the carriage feed):

$$\theta = \frac{\alpha}{2}$$

So:

$$\boxed{\theta = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{D - d}{2L}\right)}$$

### Worked Numerical Example

**Problem (KTU-style):**
A workpiece 300 mm long is to be tapered from Ø60 mm to Ø50 mm over a length of 150 mm starting from the headstock end. Calculate: (a) Taper, (b) Half-angle, (c) Compound rest setting, (d) Tailstock setover.

**Solution:**

Given: $D = 60$ mm, $d = 50$ mm, $L = 150$ mm, $L_{WS} = 300$ mm.

**(a) Taper:**

$$T = \frac{D - d}{L} = \frac{60 - 50}{150} = \frac{10}{150} = 0.0667 \text{ mm/mm}$$

**(b) Half-angle:**

$$\tan\!\left(\frac{\alpha}{2}\right) = \frac{D - d}{2L} = \frac{10}{2 \cdot 150} = 0.0333$$

$$\frac{\alpha}{2} = \tan^{-1}(0.0333) = 1.91°$$

$$\alpha = 3.82°$$

**(c) Compound rest setting:**

$$\theta = \alpha/2 = 1.91°$$

**(d) Tailstock setover:**

$$x = \frac{(D - d) \cdot L_{WS}}{2L} = \frac{10 \cdot 300}{2 \cdot 150} = \frac{3000}{300} = 10 \text{ mm}$$

---

## 3.3 Ball Turning — Tool Geometry Derivation

### Goal
Derive the relationship between tool-tip radius $R$ and finished ball diameter $D$.

### Derivation

Consider the ball as a sphere of diameter $D$. When a single-point tool sweeps an arc of radius $R$ across the rotating workpiece, the resulting profile is a sphere of diameter $2R$.

For a perfect sphere:

$$D = 2R \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{R = \frac{D}{2}}$$

### Off-Axis Tool Height Verification
If the tool is mounted on a ball-turning attachment, the pivot axis is at horizontal distance $R = D/2$ from the tool tip. When the cross-slide feeds by $\Delta x$, the tool tip moves on a circle of radius $R$ — exactly the geometry required.

### Worked Example — Ball Turning Speed Calculation

**Problem:**
Turn a ball of Ø40 mm on a mild steel workpiece of starting diameter 50 mm. The lathe has a maximum spindle speed of 1500 rpm. Calculate the optimum cutting speed and feed.

**Solution:**
At the equator (largest diameter = 50 mm during roughing):

$$N = \frac{1000 \cdot V}{\pi \cdot D}$$

For mild steel, $V = 30$ m/min:

$$N = \frac{1000 \cdot 30}{\pi \cdot 50} = \frac{30000}{157.08} = 190.99 \text{ rpm}$$

Practical selection: **200 rpm** (next available on the lathe's gear box). ✓

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Method Selection

| Method | Taper Length | Steepness | Accuracy | Skill Required | Production Suitability |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Form Tool** | ≤ 25 mm | Any | Moderate (±0.05 mm) | Low | High (repetitive) |
| **Compound Rest** | ≤ 150 mm | Any (steep OK) | High (±0.02 mm) | Moderate | Low–Medium |
| **Tailstock Setover** | Full length | Shallow only | Moderate (±0.05 mm) | Moderate | Medium |
| **Taper Attachment** | Any length | Any | Very High (±0.01 mm) | Low | High |

### Decision Tree (KTU Lab Procedure)

```
START
  │
  ├── Taper length > 150 mm?
  │     YES → Use Taper Attachment
  │     NO ↓
  │
  ├── Need internal taper or steep angle?
  │     YES → Use Compound Rest
  │     NO ↓
  │
  ├── Mass production (same taper 100+ pieces)?
  │     YES → Use Form Tool
  │     NO ↓
  │
  └── DEFAULT → Use Compound Rest (best for learning)
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked "which method would you choose?", always state the **taper length first**, then justify based on accuracy and production volume. Examiners award marks for logical justification.

---

## 3.5 Curve Turning — Mathematical Form (For Analytical Curves)

For curves defined by an equation $y = f(x)$ where $x$ is along the axis and $y$ is the radius:

| Curve Type | Equation | Tool-Path Strategy |
|---|---|---|
| **Parabola** | $y = ax^2$ | Multiple short straight-line approximations |
| **Circular arc** | $(x-h)^2 + (y-k)^2 = r^2$ | Form tool or attachment |
| **Exponential** | $y = a \cdot e^{bx}$ | Hand-feed with template |
| **Ellipse** | $\frac{x^2}{a^2} + \frac{y^2}{b^2} = 1$ | Form tool (special grinding) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For arbitrary curves, the most practical KTU lab method is **template (copy) turning** using a profile plate and follower (stylus) mechanism.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Lathe Setup Block Diagram — Taper Turning Operation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Power Supply & Motor] --> B[Gearbox]
    B --> C[Headstock Spindle]
    C --> D[3-Jaw Chuck]
    D --> E[Workpiece MS Rod]
    E --> F[Tailstock Center]
    G[Carriage Handwheel] --> H[Carriage Slide]
    H --> I[Compound Rest Swiveled alpha/2]
    I --> J[Cross Slide]
    J --> K[Tool Post]
    K --> L[HSS Tool Bit]
    L --> M[Tapered Profile Generated]
    
    N[Swivel Locking Bolt] -.-> I
    O[Setover Screw] -.-> F
    P[Coolant Supply] -.-> L
    
    stepA[Operator] -->|Sets compound angle| I
    stepB[Operator] -->|Feeds compound handle| G
    stepC[Operator] -->|Verifies with Vernier| stepD{Quality Check}
    stepD -->|Pass| stepE[Complete]
    stepD -->|Fail| stepF[Re-adjust feed]
    stepF --> G
```

---

## 4.2 Ball Turning Attachment — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Cross Slide Lead Screw] --> B[Slider Block]
    B --> C[Pivot Pin Radius R]
    C --> D[Tool Holder Arm]
    D --> E[Tool Tip Position]
    E -->|Sweeps Arc| F[Circular Profile]
    
    G[Workpiece Rotating] --> F
    F -->|Ball Geometry| H[Finished Ball Surface]
    
    subgraph Geometry
        I[D = 2R]
        J[Pivot center fixed]
        K[Tool tip moves on circle]
    end
    
    C -.-> I
    C -.-> J
    D -.-> K
```

---

## 4.3 Tailstock Setover Mechanism — Schematic Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Original Lathe Axis Z] --> B[Workpiece Before Setover]
    B --> C{Tailstock Body}
    C -->|Lateral Shift x| D[Workpiece Axis Tilted by alpha/2]
    D --> E[Tapered Cone Generated]
    
    F[Setover Graduated Screw] -->|mm scale| C
    G[Calculation Formula] -->|x = D-d times Lws over 2L| F
    
    subgraph Verification
        H[Dial Gauge on Cross Slide]
        I[Measures TIR at Tailstock End]
        J[Confirms Setover Distance x]
    end
    
    F -.-> H
    H -.-> I
    I -.-> J
```

---

## 4.4 Comparative Method Selection — Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Lab Requirement: Produce Taper] --> q1{Taper Length}
    q1 -->|>150 mm| m1[Taper Turning Attachment]
    q1 -->|<150 mm| q2{Angle Steepness}
    q2 -->|alpha over 10 deg| m2[Compound Rest Method]
    q2 -->|alpha under 10 deg| q3{Production Volume}
    q3 -->|>100 pieces| m3[Form Tool Method]
    q3 -->|<100 pieces| m4{Internal Taper?}
    m4 -->|Yes| m2
    m4 -->|No| m5[Tailstock Setover Method]
    
    m1 --> done[Select Method & Justify]
    m2 --> done
    m3 --> done
    m5 --> done
```

---

## 4.5 Curve Turning — Template (Copy) Turning Setup Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Template Profile Plate] -->|Stylus traces| B[Floating Lever Arm]
    B -->|Mechanical Linkage| C[Tool Holder on Carriage]
    C -->|Moves in X-Y| D[Workpiece]
    D -->|Rotates via Chuck| E[Spindle]
    E -->|Replicates Profile| F[Finished Curved Surface]
    
    subgraph Components
        G[Template Holder on Bed]
        H[Stylus with Roller]
        I[Linkage Ratio 1 to 1 typically]
    end
    
    A -.-> G
    B -.-> H
    C -.-> I
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** The above Mermaid block diagrams are *functional architecture* representations of the physical lathe setups. In a real KTU lab, students would photograph the actual lathe with attachments installed.

---

## 4.6 Part-Profile Reference Matrix

| Operation | Starting Profile | Final Profile | Tool Type | Cross-Slide Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Ball turning** | Cylinder Ø D₀ | Sphere Ø D | Form tool / Attachment | Arc sweep (radius D/2) |
| **Curve turning** | Cylinder Ø D₀ | Arbitrary f(x) | Form tool / Template | Variable, multi-axis |
| **Taper turning (compound)** | Cylinder Ø D | Cone (D → d) | HSS straight tool | Linear feed at angle α/2 |
| **Taper turning (setover)** | Cylinder Ø D | Cone (D → d) | HSS straight tool | Linear feed along axis (workpiece tilted) |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
**Q:** Define taper turning. List any **four** methods of producing a taper on a lathe.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Taper turning is a lathe operation in which the diameter of a workpiece changes uniformly along its axis, producing a conical surface.

Four methods:
1. **Form tool method**
2. **Compound rest (swiveling) method**
3. **Tailstock setover method**
4. **Taper turning attachment method**

(Mentioning all four methods: **2 marks**; correct definition: **1 mark**.)

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**
**Q:** What is the basic principle of ball turning? Why must the tool-tip radius equal exactly D/2?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Ball turning is based on the principle of **sweeping a single-point cutting tool along a circular arc** of radius R equal to half the desired ball diameter, while the workpiece rotates about its axis.

The tool-tip radius must equal **D/2** because the swept arc, when superimposed on the rotational symmetry of the spinning workpiece, generates a sphere. Mathematically, $D = 2R$, so $R = D/2$. Any deviation produces an **ellipsoid** or **truncated sphere** instead of a true ball.

(Principle statement: **1.5 marks**; justification with formula: **1.5 marks**.)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question B-A (Choice A — Taper Turning Heavy)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Model]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the **tailstock setover method** of taper turning. Derive the formula for the setover distance.

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Principle:** In the tailstock setover method, the tailstock body is shifted laterally by a calculated distance $x$ so that the workpiece axis is no longer parallel to the carriage feed direction. This produces a tapered cut.

**Sketch description (refer to Section 4.3):**
- Lathe bed, headstock center $H$, tailstock center $T$ shifted by $x$.
- Workpiece tilted, with the large diameter end near the tailstock (or headstock, depending on offset direction).
- Tool fed longitudinally along the carriage axis.

**Derivation (refer to Section 3.1):**
From the geometry of the tilted cone:

$$\tan(\alpha/2) = \frac{x}{L_{WS}} = \frac{D - d}{2L}$$

Solving:

$$x = \frac{(D - d) \cdot L_{WS}}{2L}$$

**Procedure:**
1. Calculate the required setover $x$ using the formula above.
2. Loosen the tailstock clamping bolts.
3. Use the setover screw to shift the tailstock by $x$ mm (read on the graduated scale).
4. Re-tighten clamps.
5. Mount and center the workpiece (use a dial gauge to verify alignment).
6. Turn the taper with light cuts, checking frequently.

(Marks: Sketch — **2**, Formula derivation — **3**, Procedure — **2**.)

---

**(b)** A workpiece of length **250 mm** is to be tapered from **Ø80 mm** at the headstock end to **Ø60 mm** at a length of **200 mm** from the headstock end. Calculate:
- (i) The taper
- (ii) The included angle
- (iii) The compound rest swivel angle
- (iv) The tailstock setover

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Given: $D = 80$ mm, $d = 60$ mm, $L = 200$ mm, $L_{WS} = 250$ mm.

**(i) Taper:**

$$T = \frac{D - d}{L} = \frac{80 - 60}{200} = \frac{20}{200} = 0.1 \text{ mm/mm}$$

**(ii) Half-angle:**

$$\tan(\alpha/2) = \frac{D - d}{2L} = \frac{20}{2 \cdot 200} = 0.05$$

$$\alpha/2 = \tan^{-1}(0.05) = 2.86°$$

$$\boxed{\alpha = 5.73°}$$

**(iii) Compound rest swivel angle:**

$$\theta = \alpha/2 = 2.86°$$

**(iv) Tailstock setover:**

$$x = \frac{(D - d) \cdot L_{WS}}{2L} = \frac{20 \cdot 250}{2 \cdot 200} = \frac{5000}{400}$$

$$\boxed{x = 12.5 \text{ mm}}$$

(Each sub-part: **1.5 marks**, plus **0.5 mark** for correct units.)

---

### Question B-B (Choice B — Ball & Curve Turning)

**[KTU University Exam — June 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the **ball turning attachment method** with a neat sketch. List its **advantages** over the form-tool method.

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Principle:** A ball turning attachment is a device mounted on the cross-slide that constrains the cutting tool to move along a circular arc as the cross-slide is fed. The tool pivots about a fixed point at distance $R = D/2$ from the cutting edge.

**Sketch description (refer to Section 4.2):**
- Pivot pin at horizontal distance $R$ from tool tip.
- Tool holder arm.
- Cross-slide feeding mechanism.
- Workpiece shown rotating.

**Advantages over form-tool method:**
1. **Larger balls possible** (up to 100+ mm) — form tools are limited to ~25 mm diameter.
2. **Better surface finish** — tool geometry is optimal throughout the cut, not just at one radius.
3. **Lower tool cost** — uses standard HSS tool bits; no special form grinding.
4. **Easier tool change** — only the HSS bit needs grinding, not a complex form.
5. **Reduced tool chatter** — form tools have long overhung cutting edges that vibrate.

(Marks: Sketch — **2**, Principle — **2**, Advantages (any 3 of 5) — **3**.)

---

**(b)** With a neat sketch, describe the **template (copy) turning method** for producing an irregular curved profile. State any **two precautions** during operation.

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Principle:** A flat metal template (profile plate) carrying the inverse of the desired curve is mounted on the lathe bed. A stylus attached to the tool holder traces the template, and the cutting tool — mechanically linked to the stylus — replicates the profile on the rotating workpiece.

**Sketch description (refer to Section 4.5):**
- Template holder fixed on lathe bed.
- Stylus with small follower roller touching template.
- Linkage from stylus to tool post.
- Workpiece between centers.

**Procedure (summary):**
1. Prepare the template (1:1 scale profile) using a profile projector or hand filing.
2. Mount template parallel to workpiece axis.
3. Install stylus with light spring tension against template.
4. Engage carriage power feed along Z-axis; stylus automatically guides the tool in the X-direction.
5. Take light finishing cut (~0.1 mm depth).

**Two Precautions:**
1. **Stylus roller must be clean and free-running** — any debris causes profile errors.
2. **Light finishing cuts only** — heavy cuts will deflect the linkage and distort the profile.
3. (Optional) **Lubricate template roller** to reduce wear and stick-slip motion.
4. (Optional) **Verify with profile gauge** every 50 mm along the curve.

(Marks: Sketch — **2**, Procedure — **3**, Two precautions — **2**.)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Points in KTU Exams:**

1. **Unit Mismatch in Setover Formula:** Students often forget to distinguish $L$ (taper length) from $L_{WS}$ (workpiece length). When taper occupies the **whole** workpiece, $L = L_{WS}$, and the formula simplifies to $x = (D-d)/2$. Always clarify which is which.

2. **Angle Confusion:** Examiners specifically test whether you know whether $\alpha$ or $\alpha/2$ is set on the compound rest. The compound rest is set to **$\alpha/2$**, not $\alpha$.

3. **Tool-Tip Radius Error in Ball Turning:** Writing $R = D$ instead of $R = D/2$ is a classic error. Always draw the geometry — the diameter is **twice** the radius.

4. **Spindle Speed Mis-calculation:** For ball turning, students often use the *finished* ball diameter for speed calculation, but the *roughing diameter* (initial cylinder) governs the speed. Calculate speed based on **largest diameter encountered**.

5. **No Justification of Method Choice:** KTU examiners award marks for "which method would you choose and why?" — choosing without justification loses 1–2 marks.

6. **Neglecting Coolant:** Especially for taper and curve turning on mild steel, dry cutting causes poor finish. Examiners deduct marks for omitting coolant in the procedure.

7. **Workpiece Centering:** Before ANY taper operation, dial-gauge centering is mandatory. Mentioning this in viva fetches full marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module Experiment 2**

- [ ] **Ball Turning** = sweeping tool along arc of radius **R = D/2**; requires form tool OR attachment.
- [ ] **Curve Turning** = generating non-spherical profiles via form tool, template, or hand-feed.
- [ ] **Taper Turning** = conical surface; four methods exist — pick by **taper length** and **angle steepness**.
- [ ] **Master formula:** $T = (D - d)/L$ — appears in **every** taper question.
- [ ] **Half-angle:** $\tan(\alpha/2) = (D-d)/(2L)$ — convert to degrees for compound rest.
- [ ] **Compound rest setting** = **α/2** degrees (NOT α).
- [ ] **Tailstock setover** = $(D-d) \cdot L_{WS} / (2L)$; special case = $(D-d)/2$.
- [ ] **Taper turning attachment** is the **most accurate** method and the most versatile.
- [ ] **Always** center the workpiece with a dial gauge before taper turning.
- [ ] **Coolant** must be used for mild steel taper and curve turning.
- [ ] **Ball-turning tool** must be ground to radius **D/2**, not D.
- [ ] **Spindle speed** calculated on **largest diameter** encountered (roughing, not finishing).
- [ ] **Hand-feed** only with compound rest method — never engage power feed.
- [ ] **Tailstock setover** shifts tailstock *toward the operator* to reduce diameter at tailstock end; *away from operator* to increase diameter.
- [ ] **Morse tapers** (MT0 to MT7) are standard tapers with predefined dimensions — useful for viva.
- [ ] **Safety:** chuck key removed, goggles worn, spindle stopped before any measurement.
- [ ] **Curve turning** for long profiles → use **template method**, not hand-feed.
- [ ] **Practical TPI:** for imperial tapers, 1 inch = 25.4 mm conversion is essential.

---

> **End of Notes — Module Experiment 2 | Lathe: Ball, Curve & Taper Turning**
> *Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme — Premium Engineering Study Material*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Exercises on lathe: Thread cutting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIEL407 - MACHINE TOOLS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Exercises on lathe: Thread cutting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Thread Cutting on a Lathe** is a fundamental machining operation in which a single-point cutting tool, ground to the profile of the desired thread, is fed linearly along the rotating workpiece at a precisely controlled feed rate equal to the **pitch** of the thread, thereby generating a continuous helical groove on the cylindrical (or conical) surface.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned) Machine Tools Lab syllabus, **Experiment 2** specifically mandates hands-on *Exercises on Lathe* with emphasis on *Thread Cutting*. The operation is classified under the broader domain of **forming-type material removal processes** governed by the kinematic relationship between the **spindle rotation** and the **carriage feed**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Screw and the Ramp"

Imagine wrapping a **ramp (inclined plane)** around a cylindrical pillar — that spiraling ramp is essentially a **thread**. Now imagine scraping the ramp's shape into a rotating rod with a chisel while slowly advancing the chisel sideways by exactly the *width of one step* every time the rod completes one full turn. That chisel is your **thread-cutting tool**, the rod is the **workpiece**, and the sideways advance per revolution is the **pitch**. This is, in essence, what a lathe does when it cuts threads.

### 1.3 Essential Physical Quantities

> [!NOTE]
> The following key parameters govern every thread cutting exercise and must be internalised by the student:
> - **Pitch (P)** — Distance between two corresponding points on adjacent thread profiles (in mm for metric, in threads-per-inch for inch systems).
> - **Lead (L)** — Axial advance per revolution (L = P for single-start threads; L = n·P for n-start threads).
> - **Depth of cut (h)** — Radial penetration of the tool, calculated as **h = 0.6134 × P** for ISO metric threads.
> - **Major diameter (d)** — The largest diameter of the thread (the nominal size, e.g., M20 means d = 20 mm).
> - **Minor diameter (d₁)** — The smallest diameter at the root of the thread.
> - **Pitch diameter (d₂)** — The theoretical cylinder on which the thread profile width equals the space width.
> - **Thread angle (α)** — **60°** for ISO metric and Unified National; **55°** for BSW/BSF.

### 1.4 Classification of Threads Commonly Cut on a Lathe

| Thread Family | Standard | Angle | Pitch Unit |
|---------------|----------|-------|-----------|
| ISO Metric | M-series | 60° | mm |
| British Standard Whitworth (BSW) | BSW | 55° | TPI (threads per inch) |
| British Standard Fine (BSF) | BSF | 55° | TPI |
| ACME | ACME | 29° (Trapezoidal) | inch |
| Square | — | 0° (square) | inch or mm |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Geometric Foundation of an ISO Metric V-Thread

The fundamental (sharp) profile of a metric thread is an **equilateral V** of apex angle **60°**. Truncation is applied at both crest and root to:
- (a) prevent sharp stress concentration,
- (b) ease machining,
- (c) accommodate manufacturing tolerances.

The **fundamental triangle height** $H$ for a pitch $P$ is:

$$
H = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \cdot P \approx 0.866 \, P
$$

### 2.2 Logic Sequence of a Thread Cutting Operation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following **five-step logic chain** is the cognitive scaffold examiners expect a student to reproduce:

1. **Specification decoding** — Interpret the thread designation (e.g., M20 × 2.5 → major diameter 20 mm, pitch 2.5 mm).
2. **Tool selection and grinding** — Select HSS single-point tool; grind rake and clearance angles; verify 60° point angle using a tool-grinding gauge.
3. **Kinematic synchronisation** — Engage the **lead screw** through the change-gear train such that the carriage advances exactly one pitch per spindle revolution.
4. **Incremental radial infeed** — Take multiple shallow passes, gradually increasing infeed until the full theoretical depth (or the calibrated minor diameter) is achieved.
5. **Verification and finishing** — Use a **screw pitch gauge** and **thread ring/plug gauge** to confirm pitch accuracy and crest fit.

### 2.3 The Three Infeed Strategies

| Infeed Strategy | Tool Path | Advantages | Disadvantages |
|-----------------|-----------|-----------|---------------|
| **Straight (radial) infeed** | Perpendicular to workpiece axis | Simple, accurate pitch | One flank only bears load → rapid flank wear |
| **Compound (angular) infeed** | At half thread angle (e.g., 30° for metric) using compound slide | Load distributed, better finish | Slight thread-tooth error on long threads |
| **Alternate (zig-zag) infeed** | Alternates left and right flanks | Symmetric wear | Complex, requires attention |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Thread Cutting (Exam Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **must-know** for KTU ESE. Practice numerical substitution until it is second nature.

| S.No | Parameter | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|------|-----------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Pitch (metric) | $P = \text{distance between 2 crests}$ | mm | Read directly from thread callout |
| 2 | TPI (inch) | $\text{TPI} = \dfrac{25.4}{P_{mm}}$ | threads/inch | Conversion for unified threads |
| 3 | Fundamental height | $H = 0.866 \, P$ | mm | For 60° V-thread |
| 4 | Depth of cut (metric) | $h = 0.6134 \, P$ | mm | ISO metric empirical formula |
| 5 | Depth of cut (BSW/BSF) | $h = 0.6403 \, P$ | inch | 55° thread family |
| 6 | Minor diameter | $d_1 = d - 2h$ | mm | For external thread |
| 7 | Pitch diameter | $d_2 = d - 0.6495 \, P$ | mm | Approximation for 60° |
| 8 | Lead relationship | $L = n \cdot P$ | mm | n = number of thread starts |
| 9 | Spindle speed | $N = \dfrac{V \cdot 1000}{\pi \cdot D}$ | rpm | V typically **15–25 m/min** |
| 10 | Carriage feed | $f_{\text{carriage}} = P \cdot N$ | mm/min | Equal to pitch × rpm |
| 11 | Number of passes | $\text{Passes} \approx 6 \text{ to } 12$ | — | Depends on pitch & material |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical substitution note for tables**: The vertical bar symbol `$\vert$` is used above instead of `\|` to avoid corrupting the markdown table structure.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Thread cutting on a lathe is the **backbone of mechanical assembly**:
- Manufacture of **lead screws** for CNC and instrumentation machines,
- **Studs, bolts, and shafts** for power transmission assemblies,
- **Adjusting screws** in jigs, fixtures, and measuring instruments,
- **Couplings and fittings** in hydraulic and pneumatic systems.

Mastery of this experiment underpins advanced operations like **tapered threading**, **multi-start threading**, and **worm cutting** in higher semesters.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Depth-of-Cut Formula (h = 0.6134 P)

We begin with the **fundamental (sharp) triangle** of a 60° V-thread having pitch $P$. The two flanks are inclined at **30° each** to the horizontal (since the full apex angle is 60°). Consider the right-angled triangle formed by:
- The vertical leg = **theoretical sharp depth** $h_s$,
- The horizontal leg = **half pitch** $P/2$,
- The angle at the apex = **30°**.

Applying basic trigonometry:

$$
\tan(30^\circ) = \frac{h_s}{P/2}
$$

Solving for $h_s$:

$$
h_s = \frac{P}{2} \cdot \tan(30^\circ)
$$

Using the identity $\tan(30^\circ) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 0.5774$:

$$
h_s = \frac{P}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} = \frac{P}{2\sqrt{3}}
$$

Rationalising the denominator by multiplying numerator and denominator by $\sqrt{3}$:

$$
h_s = \frac{P \sqrt{3}}{2 \cdot 3} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{6} \, P
$$

Numerically:

$$
h_s = 0.2887 \, P
$$

This is the **theoretical sharp depth**. In practice, both crest and root are **truncated** to remove the brittle sharp corners and to give clearance. Standard ISO 68-1 mandates:
- Crest truncation = $H/8$,
- Root truncation = $H/4$.

The **effective thread depth** from crest surface to root surface of an external thread becomes:

$$
h_{\text{eff}} = \frac{5}{8} \cdot H = \frac{5}{8} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \, P = \frac{5\sqrt{3}}{16} \, P
$$

Numerically:

$$
h_{\text{eff}} = 0.5413 \, P
$$

However, in **machining practice**, the **depth of radial infeed** of the tool (measured from the original cylindrical surface of the workpiece down to the tool tip) is conventionally taken as the **empirical value**:

$$
\boxed{h = 0.6134 \, P}
$$

This empirical constant absorbs the small clearance and the rounded tool-nose radius and is the formula universally used in workshop manuals and KTU laboratory records.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Cutting an M20 × 2.5 Metric Thread

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (KTU-Style Numerical):**
> On a lathe, cut an external **M20 × 2.5** ISO metric thread on a mild-steel workpiece of 60 mm length. Determine: (i) the radial depth of cut per side, (ii) the minor diameter, (iii) the pitch diameter, (iv) the recommended spindle speed, and (v) the number of infeed passes.

#### Given Data

| Symbol | Quantity | Value |
|--------|----------|-------|
| d | Nominal (major) diameter | **20 mm** |
| P | Pitch | **2.5 mm** |
| L | Length of threaded portion | **60 mm** |
| V | Recommended cutting speed (mild steel, thread cutting) | **20 m/min** |
| n | Number of thread starts | **1** |

#### Step (i) — Radial Depth of Cut

$$
h = 0.6134 \times P = 0.6134 \times 2.5
$$

Performing the multiplication step-by-step:

$$
0.6134 \times 2.5 = 0.6134 \times 2 + 0.6134 \times 0.5
$$

$$
= 1.2268 + 0.3067 = 1.5335 \, \text{mm}
$$

$$
\boxed{h = 1.5335 \, \text{mm}}
$$

#### Step (ii) — Minor Diameter

$$
d_1 = d - 2h = 20 - 2(1.5335) = 20 - 3.067
$$

$$
\boxed{d_1 = 16.933 \, \text{mm}}
$$

#### Step (iii) — Pitch Diameter

$$
d_2 = d - 0.6495 \, P = 20 - 0.6495 \times 2.5
$$

Computing the term:

$$
0.6495 \times 2.5 = 0.6495 \times 2 + 0.6495 \times 0.5 = 1.2990 + 0.3248 = 1.6238 \, \text{mm}
$$

Therefore:

$$
d_2 = 20 - 1.6238 = 18.3762 \, \text{mm}
$$

$$
\boxed{d_2 \approx 18.376 \, \text{mm}}
$$

#### Step (iv) — Spindle Speed

$$
N = \frac{V \times 1000}{\pi \times D}
$$

Substituting $V = 20 \, \text{m/min}$ and $D = 20 \, \text{mm}$:

$$
N = \frac{20 \times 1000}{\pi \times 20} = \frac{20000}{62.832}
$$

$$
\boxed{N \approx 318 \, \text{rpm}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Thread cutting always uses **lower cutting speeds than turning** — typically **40–60%** of the turning speed for the same material — because the tool engages one flank only.

#### Step (v) — Number of Infeed Passes

A common workshop heuristic (and KTU-recommended practice) is to distribute the total infeed over **8 to 10 passes** with progressively reducing increments. The classic distribution for a depth of **1.5335 mm** is:

| Pass | Infeed (mm) | Cumulative Depth (mm) |
|------|-------------|------------------------|
| 1 | 0.30 | 0.300 |
| 2 | 0.25 | 0.550 |
| 3 | 0.20 | 0.750 |
| 4 | 0.18 | 0.930 |
| 5 | 0.16 | 1.090 |
| 6 | 0.14 | 1.230 |
| 7 | 0.12 | 1.350 |
| 8 | 0.10 | 1.450 |
| 9 | 0.05 | 1.500 |
| 10 (spring) | 0.00 | 1.5335 |

The final pass is a **spring cut** (zero infeed) to ensure a clean, accurate profile.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Why Tool Setup Geometry Matters

| Tool Setup | Compound Rest Angle | Infeed Direction | Resulting Thread Quality |
|------------|--------------------|--------------------|--------------------------|
| Perpendicular to axis | $0^\circ$ (Locked parallel) | Straight radial | Sharp but uneven flank wear |
| Inclined at half-angle | $30^\circ$ (metric) | Along trailing flank | Cleaner cut, longer tool life |
| Inclined at full angle | $60^\circ$ | Along leading flank | Not recommended — chatter |
| Compound with cross-slide mix | $30^\circ$ + cross-slide | Hybrid | Best finish — used in industry |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process-Flow Architecture of Thread Cutting on a Lathe

The block-level functional flow below captures the **decision logic and physical interaction sequence** of the experiment, as required for KTU laboratory record submissions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[STEP 01 SPECIFY THREAD M20 BY 2.5] --> nodeB[STEP 02 CALCULATE DEPTH PITCH AND DIAMETERS]
    nodeB --> nodeC[STEP 03 GRIND HSS TOOL AT 60 DEGREES PROFILE]
    nodeC --> nodeD[STEP 04 MOUNT WORKPIECE IN THREE JAW CHUCK]
    nodeD --> nodeE[STEP 05 SET COMPOUND REST AT 30 DEGREES HALF ANGLE]
    nodeE --> nodeF[STEP 06 INSTALL CHANGE GEARS FOR PITCH 2.5 MM]
    nodeF --> nodeG[STEP 07 ALIGN TOOL TIP USING CENTRE GAUGE]
    nodeG --> nodeH[STEP 08 SET SPINDLE SPEED 318 RPM]
    nodeH --> nodeI[STEP 09 ENGAGE HALF NUT AT THREAD DIAL MARK]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[STEP 10 MAKE FIRST ROUGH PASS INFEED 0.30 MM]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[STEP 11 RETRACT TOOL RETURN CARRIAGE TO START]
    nodeK --> nodeL{DEPTH REACHED FULL 1.5335 MM}
    nodeL -- NO --> nodeJ
    nodeL -- YES --> nodeM[STEP 12 FINAL SPRING PASS ZERO INFEED]
    nodeM --> nodeN[STEP 13 VERIFY PITCH WITH SCREW PITCH GAUGE]
    nodeN --> nodeO{THREAD GAUGE PASSES}
    nodeO -- YES --> nodeP[STEP 14 DISMOUNT WORKPIECE END OF EXPERIMENT]
    nodeO -- NO --> nodeJ
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture of Lathe Subsystems Engaged in Thread Cutting

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subA[POWER SUBSYSTEM SPINDLE MOTOR] --> subB[SPINDLE WITH CHUCK]
    subB --> subC[ROTATING WORKPIECE]
    subD[LEAD SCREW WITH CHANGE GEARS] --> subE[CARRIAGE TRANSLATION]
    subE --> subF[COMPOUND SLIDE WITH THREADING TOOL]
    subF --> subC
    subG[COOLANT PUMP] --> subC
    subH[THREAD DIAL INDICATOR] --> subE
    subI[HALF NUT LEVER] --> subE
    subJ[CONTROL PANEL OPERATOR INTERFACE] --> subA
    subJ --> subD
    subJ --> subG
```

### 4.3 Tool-Engagement Geometry (Functional Block Mapping)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    geomA[WORKPIECE CYLINDER NOMINAL DIAMETER D] --> geomB[MAJOR DIAMETER SURFACE]
    geomB --> geomC[RADIAL INFEED DISTANCE H EQUALS 0.6134 P]
    geomC --> geomD[MINOR DIAMETER CYLINDER AT ROOT]
    geomD --> geomE[TOOL POINT 60 DEGREES V PROFILE]
    geomE --> geomF[FLANK ANGLE 30 DEGREES EACH SIDE]
    geomF --> geomG[CREST AND ROOT TRUNCATION ZONES]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Diagram Requirement**: Lab records must include (a) a **line diagram of the tool-workpiece engagement**, (b) the **thread profile with dimensions**, and (c) the **block diagram above** as the process-flow supporting sheet.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 KTU Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

Define **thread cutting on a lathe**. List any **four essential precautions** to be observed during the operation.

**Model Answer:**

> **Thread cutting on a lathe** is a machining process in which a single-point cutting tool, shaped to the profile of the desired thread, is advanced linearly along the axis of a rotating workpiece at a feed rate equal to the thread pitch, thereby producing a helical groove.
>
> **Four essential precautions:**
> 1. The **tool must be set exactly on centre** (perpendicular to the workpiece axis), verified using a centre gauge.
> 2. The **half-nut lever must be engaged precisely at the thread-dial index line** corresponding to the spindle position.
> 3. **Cutting speed must be reduced** to 40–60% of the turning speed for the same material.
> 4. The tool should **never be retracted while the half-nut is engaged**, to avoid crashing the carriage and workpiece.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

State the formula for the **depth of cut** in thread cutting for an ISO metric thread and briefly justify why multiple shallow passes are preferred over a single deep pass.

**Model Answer:**

> The depth of cut for an ISO metric thread is given by
>
> $$h = 0.6134 \, P$$
>
> where $P$ is the pitch. Multiple shallow passes are preferred because (a) it prevents **sudden tool breakage** due to excessive cutting force, (b) it reduces **chatter and vibration**, (c) it allows progressive **heat dissipation**, and (d) it yields a **smoother, more accurate thread profile** at the final pass.

---

### 5.2 KTU Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]

**(a)** Describe with a neat sketch the **step-by-step procedure** for cutting an **M20 × 2.5 ISO metric external thread** on a centre lathe. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Workpiece preparation**: Cut a mild-steel rod of diameter 22–24 mm and length ≈ 80 mm. Face one end and reduce the diameter over a 60 mm length to **19.95 mm** (slight undersize allowance for threading).

2. **Tool preparation**: Grind an HSS single-point threading tool with **60° point angle**, **15° side clearance**, **0° top rake**, and a **nose radius of 0.4 mm**. Verify with a **thread-grinding gauge**.

3. **Lathe setup**:
   - Mount workpiece in a 3-jaw self-centring chuck.
   - Install the **change gears** to obtain a lead of **2.5 mm/rev** on the carriage.
   - Set the **compound rest at 30°** (half of 60° metric thread angle) from the workpiece axis.
   - Set the **tool** on centre using a centre gauge and a try-square against the chuck face.

4. **Speed selection**: For mild steel with $V = 20 \, \text{m/min}$,
   $$N = \frac{V \cdot 1000}{\pi \cdot D} = \frac{20000}{\pi \times 20} \approx 318 \, \text{rpm}$$
   Use the closest available gear — typically **300 rpm**.

5. **Engagement**: With spindle running, wait until the **thread dial indicator** shows the appropriate mark (e.g., any mark on 2 mm pitch). Engage the **half-nut lever**.

6. **Infeed sequence**: Take the radial infeed in steps of 0.30, 0.25, 0.20, 0.18, 0.16, 0.14, 0.12, 0.10, 0.05 mm — 10 passes total. The **cross-slide** retracts at the end of each pass to clear the thread; the carriage returns to the start manually, and the half-nut is re-engaged at the same thread-dial position.

7. **Spring cut**: Take one final pass with **zero infeed** to refine the profile.

8. **Verification**: Check the pitch with a **screw pitch gauge (2.5 mm)** and the fit with a **thread ring gauge (M20 × 2.5)**.

---

**(b)** Calculate for the same thread: **(i)** depth of cut, **(ii)** minor diameter, **(iii)** pitch diameter, and **(iv)** the total radial infeed per pass if 8 equal passes are used. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given**: $d = 20 \, \text{mm}$, $P = 2.5 \, \text{mm}$.

**(i) Depth of cut**

$$
h = 0.6134 \, P = 0.6134 \times 2.5 = 1.5335 \, \text{mm}
$$

**(ii) Minor diameter**

$$
d_1 = d - 2h = 20 - 2(1.5335) = 20 - 3.067 = 16.933 \, \text{mm}
$$

**(iii) Pitch diameter**

$$
d_2 = d - 0.6495 \, P = 20 - 0.6495 \times 2.5 = 20 - 1.6238 = 18.376 \, \text{mm}
$$

**(iv) Equal infeed per pass (8 passes)**

$$
\text{Infeed per pass} = \frac{h}{8} = \frac{1.5335}{8} = 0.1917 \, \text{mm/pass}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Question A**: Students frequently (i) use the wrong formula ($0.866 P$ instead of $0.6134 P$), losing 2 marks; (ii) forget to **subtract the pre-machining allowance** before the first pass; (iii) confuse **pitch** and **lead** when the thread is multi-start; and (iv) draw the tool profile sketch without the **nose radius**, losing the sketching marks. Always label every line dimension on the diagram.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]

**(a)** Explain the **geometry of an ISO metric thread** with a labelled sketch and state the relationship between major, pitch, and minor diameters. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

An ISO metric thread has a **60° symmetric V-profile**. The relevant geometric features (labelled on the workshop sketch) are:

- **Major diameter ($d$)** — the diameter measured at the **crest** of the thread. This is the **nominal** size, e.g., 20 mm for M20.
- **Minor diameter ($d_1$)** — the diameter measured at the **root** of the thread.
- **Pitch diameter ($d_2$)** — the diameter of the imaginary cylinder on which the width of the thread groove equals the width of the thread ridge. It lies between $d$ and $d_1$.
- **Pitch ($P$)** — the axial distance between adjacent crests (or roots).
- **Thread angle ($\alpha = 60^\circ$)** — the included angle between the two flanks.
- **Flank angle = $\alpha/2 = 30^\circ$** — each flank is inclined at 30° to the perpendicular to the thread axis.
- **Truncation** — both crest and root are flattened: crest truncation = $H/8$, root truncation = $H/4$, where $H$ is the fundamental triangle height.

**Mathematical relationships**:

$$
h = 0.6134 \, P, \quad d_1 = d - 2h = d - 1.2268 \, P
$$

$$
d_2 = d - 0.6495 \, P
$$

$$
H = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \, P \approx 0.866 \, P
$$

---

**(b)** Compare the **three infeed methods** used in lathe thread cutting. Which method is most commonly used in industry and why? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Feature | Straight Infeed | Compound Infeed | Alternate Infeed |
|---------|----------------|-----------------|------------------|
| **Tool path** | Perpendicular to axis | At half thread angle | Alternates sides |
| **Setup complexity** | Simplest | Requires compound-slide setting | Most complex |
| **Flank load** | One flank only | Trailing flank | Both flanks alternately |
| **Tool wear** | Rapid, uneven | Symmetric, slower | Balanced |
| **Surface finish** | Average | Best | Good |
| **Chatter tendency** | High at deep cuts | Low | Moderate |
| **Preferred in** | Coarse pitch, low-precision work | Precision threads, fine pitches | Long threads, hardened materials |

**Most commonly used in industry: Compound infeed.**

**Reasons:**
1. The angular contact progressively shears the chip rather than impacting it, reducing tool-tip stress.
2. The **majority of the cutting load is borne by the rear flank**, which is supported by the compound-slide body — minimising tool deflection.
3. It yields a **superior surface finish** with built-up edge control, especially on long-thread jobs.
4. It permits higher feed rates and thus **shorter cycle time** while maintaining dimensional accuracy.

> [!WARNING]
