> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Question B**: Many students lose marks by (i) drawing the thread profile with a 90° angle (confusing with a square thread) instead of 60°; (ii) failing to label **pitch diameter** distinctly; (iii) omitting the **truncation zones** in the sketch; (iv) in the comparison table, missing the column on "tool wear" which is the deciding factor; and (v) writing only "compound is best" without quantitative or qualitative justification. Always provide a reason.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep)**

- **Definition**: Thread cutting = single-point tool + rotating workpiece + synchronised carriage feed equal to **pitch**.
- **Core formula (must memorise)**:
  $$h = 0.6134 \, P \quad (\text{ISO metric})$$
  $$h = 0.6403 \, P \quad (\text{BSW/BSF})$$
- **Diameter relationships**:
  $$d_1 = d - 1.2268 \, P, \qquad d_2 = d - 0.6495 \, P$$
- **Thread angle**: 60° (metric & unified), 55° (BSW), 29° (ACME trapezoidal).
- **Compound rest angle** = half thread angle → 30° (metric), 27.5° (BSW).
- **Cutting speed for threading** = **40–60%** of turning speed for the same material.
- **Spindle speed formula**:
  $$N = \frac{V \times 1000}{\pi \times D}$$
- **Half-nut engagement**: must occur **at the marked spindle position** on the thread dial — never arbitrarily.
- **Infeed pattern**: multiple shallow passes (8–12), with the **last pass as a spring cut** (zero infeed).
- **Tool material**: HSS preferred for low-to-medium speeds; carbide for production lathes with coated inserts.
- **Coolant**: soluble oil or cutting fluid; essential to prevent work-hardening, especially on stainless steel and aluminium.
- **Verification instruments**: screw pitch gauge, thread ring gauge (external thread), thread plug gauge (internal thread).
- **Safety**: wear safety glasses, **never retract the tool while half-nut is engaged**, keep hands clear of the rotating chuck, and never leave the spindle running unattended.
- **Common KTU viva question**: *"What is the difference between pitch and lead?"* → **Pitch** is the distance between adjacent crests; **Lead** is the axial advance per revolution. For single-start threads, lead = pitch; for multi-start, lead = n × pitch.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Exercises on shaping machine: Machining of flat surfaces
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIEL407 - MACHINE TOOLS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Exercises on shaping machine: Machining of flat surfaces

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Shaping Machine** is a reciprocating-type machine tool in which a single-point cutting tool is mounted on a ram that moves linearly in a straight-line path across a stationary workpiece to produce a flat surface by means of a linear cutting motion and an intermittent feed motion. It is classified under the category of **planing machines** (light-duty class) and is primarily employed for the production of flat surfaces, slots, keyways, dovetails, and contours on small to medium-sized workpieces.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology (KTU Board Standard):**
> - **Ram**: The reciprocating member that carries the tool holder and imparts the linear cutting stroke.
> - **Stroke Length (L)**: The total distance travelled by the ram during one complete forward (cutting) + backward (return) cycle.
> - **Quick Return Mechanism**: A kinematic arrangement that makes the return stroke faster than the cutting stroke, reducing non-productive time.

## 1.2 Types of Flat Surfaces Produced on a Shaper

A shaping machine can produce several categories of flat surfaces:

| S.No. | Type of Surface | Tool Used | Application |
|-------|----------------|-----------|-------------|
| 1 | Horizontal Flat Surface | Plain turning tool | Slideways, base plates |
| 2 | Vertical Flat Surface | Side-facing tool | Slots, side walls |
| 3 | Inclined Surface (using swivel vice) | Standard tool | Angular components |
| 4 | Internal Flat Surfaces (slots/keyways) | Internal shaping tool | Keyways, T-slots |
| 5 | Curved/Contoured Surfaces (using forming tool) | Form tool | Cams, shaped grooves |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Wood Saw Pulled Back" Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine a carpenter using a hand saw on a piece of wood. The carpenter pushes the saw **forward** to cut (this is the *cutting stroke*), then pulls it back **above** the wood surface without cutting (this is the *return stroke*). The carpenter naturally pulls back faster than they push forward because no work is being done. A shaping machine is the **industrial, mechanized version** of this exact concept — the *ram* is the saw, the *workpiece* is the wood, and the *quick return mechanism* is the carpenter's natural tendency to retract faster. The shaper's genius is that it automates this "cut-slowly, return-fast" rhythm mechanically.

## 1.4 Classification of Shapers

Based on the type of driving mechanism, shaping machines are classified as:

1. **Crank and Slotted Lever Shaper** (Most common, used in KTU labs)
2. **Whitworth Quick Return Shaper** (Faster return, larger stroke lengths)
3. **Hydraulic Shaper** (Smooth, constant cutting speed, used in modern CNC shapers)
4. **Geared Shaper** (Bull gear mechanism)

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus PCIEL407 lab primarily uses the **Crank and Slotted Lever Shaper** for the flat surface machining experiment.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Principal Parts of a Shaping Machine

The standard shaper consists of the following major components:

1. **Base** – Heavy cast iron bed that supports the column and table; absorbs vibrations.
2. **Column** – Vertical support on the base that houses the drive mechanism and guides the ram.
3. **Cross-rail** – Horizontal member mounted on the column, provides cross-feed mechanism.
4. **Ram** – Reciprocating slide that carries the tool slide and tool head.
5. **Tool Head** – Holds the cutting tool; can be swiveled to any angle.
6. **Table** – Supports the workpiece; provides cross-feed (perpendicular to ram motion) and vertical feed.
7. **Driving Mechanism** – Bull gear + pinion + crank and slotted lever for quick return.
8. **Strock Adjustment Mechanism** – Alters the length of stroke.

## 2.2 Working Principle (Step-by-Step Logic)

The operational sequence of the shaper follows a precise 4-step cycle:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 4-Step Shaping Cycle:**
> 1. **Cutting Stroke** — The ram moves forward (leftward) carrying the tool across the workpiece. The workpiece is fed perpendicular to the stroke direction only during this stroke. Material is removed as a chip.
> 2. **Tool Lifting** — At the end of the cutting stroke, the tool is automatically lifted (via clapper box mechanism) to avoid dragging across the freshly cut surface.
> 3. **Return Stroke** — The ram retracts (rightward) at higher speed. The tool is held above the work by the clapper box.
> 4. **Feed Motion** — The table is given a small lateral (cross-feed) movement to expose a new strip of uncut material for the next cutting stroke.

## 2.3 The Crank and Slotted Lever Quick Return Mechanism

This is the **heart of the shaper's kinematics** and is the most frequently asked concept in KTU theory exams.

**Components:**
- A large **bull gear** rotating at uniform angular velocity ω.
- A **crank pin** (offset from the gear center by radius R) — the crank pin slides in a horizontal slot.
- A **slotted lever** (length L) oscillating about a fixed pivot.
- The **ram** is connected to the slotted end of the lever.

**Kinematic Logic:**
- When the crank rotates, the crank pin moves along a circular path.
- The slotted lever's slotted end (where the pin engages) is forced to move along a straight horizontal line.
- The lever rocks about its fixed pivot, and the slotted end moves horizontally — this horizontal motion IS the ram's reciprocating motion.
- **Why is the return stroke faster?** Because the crank pin travels a larger arc during the return portion of the gear rotation (when the crank is on the side closer to the lever's pivot) than during the cutting portion.

## 2.4 Quick Return Ratio (QRR)

The ratio of return stroke time to cutting stroke time:

$$QRR = \frac{\text{Time of cutting stroke}}{\text{Time of return stroke}} = \frac{2\pi - 2\alpha}{2\alpha} = \frac{\pi - \alpha}{\alpha}$$

Where **α** is the crank angle (in radians) at the start of the cutting stroke measured from the inner dead center (IDC).

**Typical value for KTU lab shapers:** QRR ≈ 2:1 to 3:2 (i.e., 2:1 means return is twice as fast as cutting).

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Cutting Speed | $V_c = \frac{L(1+r) N_s}{1000}$ | m/min | L = stroke length (mm), r = 1/QRR, Nₛ = strokes/min |
| 2 | Strokes per Minute | $N_s = \frac{1000 \, V_c}{L(1+r)}$ | strokes/min | Direct from above |
| 3 | Feed | $f$ (given or selected) | mm/stroke | Lateral table movement per stroke |
| 4 | Depth of Cut | $d$ (given) | mm | Vertical depth of penetration |
| 5 | Material Removal Rate (MRR) | $MRR = L \cdot f \cdot d \cdot N_s$ | mm³/min | Volume of chip removed per minute |
| 6 | Machining Time | $T_m = \frac{L + l_1 + l_2}{f \cdot N_s}$ | min | l₁, l₂ = approach & overtravel (mm) |
| 7 | Power Required | $P = \frac{F_c \cdot V_c}{60 \times 1000}$ | kW | F_c = cutting force (N) |
| 8 | Quick Return Ratio | $QRR = \frac{\pi - \alpha}{\alpha}$ | dimensionless | α in radians |
| 9 | Cutting Force (approx.) | $F_c = K_s \cdot f \cdot d$ | N | Kₛ = specific cutting force (N/mm²) |
| 10 | Theoretical Surface Roughness | $R_a \approx \frac{f^2}{8R}$ | µm | For form tool, R = tool nose radius |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Note:** In shaper calculations, the **stroke length L is taken as the cutting stroke length** (forward ram travel). The return stroke is faster but contributes zero to material removal. Always use $V_c = L \cdot N_s / 1000$ when QRR is not given (i.e., assume r = 0 for simplicity in KTU numerical problems).

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Shapers, despite being one of the oldest machine tools, remain relevant because:
- **Low initial cost** — ideal for small workshops, tool rooms, and educational labs (like KTU).
- **High rigidity** — the workpiece is held stationary, so heavy cuts on difficult-to-clamp parts (e.g., long castings) are possible.
- **Versatility** — can machine internal surfaces, dovetails, and contours unreachable by planers.
- **Modern descendant** — the **slotting machine** (vertical shaper) and **CNC shapers** still use the same kinematic principles.
- **Maintenance & Repair** — used for fabricating replacement parts, jigs, fixtures, and prototypes.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Industrial Context:** Industries such as railway workshops, die-making units, and heavy-machinery repair shops still rely on shaping machines because they can handle workpieces of complex shape without complex CNC programming.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Cutting Speed for Shaper

### Given:
- Stroke length = $L$ mm
- Number of double strokes per minute = $N_s$
- Quick return ratio = $r = 1 / QRR$

### To Derive:
The relationship between cutting speed $V_c$, stroke length $L$, and $N_s$.

### Derivation:

**Step 1:** In one complete cycle (1 double stroke), the ram travels:
- During cutting stroke: distance = $L$ mm (forward, slow)
- During return stroke: distance = $L$ mm (backward, fast)
- Total distance per cycle = $2L$ mm

**Step 2:** The cutting stroke occupies a larger portion of the cycle time because it is slower. If the total time per cycle is $T_{cycle}$:

$$T_{cycle} = \frac{1}{N_s} \text{ min}$$

**Step 3:** Time taken by cutting stroke alone (call it $t_c$):

$$t_c = \frac{T_{cycle}}{1 + r} = \frac{1}{N_s (1+r)} \text{ min}$$

**Step 4:** Cutting speed is the distance covered during cutting stroke divided by the time of cutting stroke:

$$V_c = \frac{L}{t_c} = \frac{L \cdot N_s (1+r)}{1} \text{ mm/min}$$

**Step 5:** Converting to m/min (divide by 1000):

$$\boxed{V_c = \frac{L \cdot N_s \cdot (1+r)}{1000} \text{ m/min}}$$

> **Engineering Insight:** If $r = 0$ (no quick return, theoretical), then $V_c = L \cdot N_s / 1000$. If $r = 0.5$ (typical, meaning return is twice as fast), then $V_c = 1.5 \cdot L \cdot N_s / 1000$. So the **quick return mechanism effectively increases the cutting speed** for the same stroke length and stroke rate.

## 3.2 Derivation of Machining Time

### Scenario:
A workpiece of length $W$ mm is to be machined with a single-point tool. The cutting stroke length $L$ must exceed $W$ by an approach distance $l_1$ and an overtravel distance $l_2$ (to ensure the tool fully enters and exits the workpiece region).

### Derivation:

**Step 1:** Each cutting stroke removes a strip of width equal to the feed $f$ (mm). The total width of material to be removed is $B$ (mm) — the breadth of the workpiece perpendicular to the stroke.

**Step 2:** Number of effective cutting strokes required:

$$n = \frac{B}{f} \text{ strokes}$$

**Step 3:** Time per stroke = $\frac{1}{N_s}$ minutes.

**Step 4:** Total machining time:

$$T_m = n \cdot \frac{1}{N_s} = \frac{B}{f \cdot N_s} \text{ min}$$

**Step 5:** If we must also account for the approach and overtravel (the tool travels a slightly longer distance than the workpiece length), the effective stroke length becomes $L_{eff} = W + l_1 + l_2$. The velocity during cutting is $V_c$, so the time for one cutting stroke is:

$$t_{c,eff} = \frac{W + l_1 + l_2}{V_c \times 1000} \text{ min} = \frac{(W + l_1 + l_2) \times 60}{V_c \times 1000 \times 60} \text{ (in seconds)}$$

**Step 6:** Combining with the feed term for multiple strokes:

$$\boxed{T_m = \frac{B \times (W + l_1 + l_2)}{1000 \times V_c \times f} \text{ min}}$$

## 3.3 Solved KTU-Style Numerical Problem (Step-by-Step)

### Problem Statement:
A flat surface of **200 mm length** and **80 mm width** on a mild steel workpiece is to be machined on a shaping machine. The following data is given:
- Cutting speed $V_c = 15$ m/min
- Feed $f = 0.8$ mm/stroke
- Depth of cut $d = 3$ mm
- Quick return ratio $= 2:1$ (so $r = 0.5$)
- Approach distance $l_1 = 10$ mm
- Overtravel distance $l_2 = 15$ mm
- Specific cutting force $K_s = 2000$ N/mm²

**Find:** (a) Strokes per minute, (b) Cutting stroke length, (c) Machining time, (d) Material removal rate, (e) Cutting force, (f) Power required.

### Solution:

**Part (a) — Strokes per minute:**

Using the formula:
$$N_s = \frac{1000 \cdot V_c}{L \cdot (1+r)}$$

The cutting stroke length must be:
$$L = W + l_1 + l_2 = 200 + 10 + 15 = 225 \text{ mm}$$

Substituting:
$$N_s = \frac{1000 \times 15}{225 \times (1 + 0.5)} = \frac{15000}{225 \times 1.5} = \frac{15000}{337.5}$$

$$\boxed{N_s = 44.44 \text{ strokes/min} \approx 44 \text{ strokes/min}}$$

**Part (b) — Cutting stroke length:** (already calculated above)

$$\boxed{L = 225 \text{ mm}}$$

**Part (c) — Machining time:**

$$T_m = \frac{B \times L}{1000 \times V_c \times f} = \frac{80 \times 225}{1000 \times 15 \times 0.8}$$

$$T_m = \frac{18000}{12000} = 1.5 \text{ min}$$

$$\boxed{T_m = 1.5 \text{ min} = 1 \text{ min } 30 \text{ sec}}$$

**Part (d) — Material Removal Rate (MRR):**

$$MRR = L \cdot f \cdot d \cdot N_s = 225 \times 0.8 \times 3 \times 44.44$$

$$MRR = 225 \times 0.8 \times 3 \times 44.44 = 24000 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}$$

$$\boxed{MRR = 24 \times 10^3 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min} = 24 \text{ cm}^3/\text{min}}$$

**Part (e) — Cutting Force:**

$$F_c = K_s \cdot f \cdot d = 2000 \times 0.8 \times 3 = 4800 \text{ N}$$

$$\boxed{F_c = 4800 \text{ N} = 4.8 \text{ kN}}$$

**Part (f) — Power Required:**

$$P = \frac{F_c \cdot V_c}{60 \times 1000} = \frac{4800 \times 15}{60 \times 1000} = \frac{72000}{60000}$$

$$\boxed{P = 1.2 \text{ kW}}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Parameter Effects on Output

| If we increase... | Effect on $V_c$ | Effect on $T_m$ | Effect on $MRR$ | Effect on $F_c$ | Practical Trade-off |
|------------------|-----------------|-----------------|-----------------|-----------------|---------------------|
| Stroke length $L$ | Increases | Increases | Increases | No direct effect | Limited by machine capacity |
| Strokes/min $N_s$ | Increases | Decreases | Increases | No direct effect | Limited by vibration & heating |
| Feed $f$ | No effect | Decreases | Increases | Increases linearly | Surface finish worsens |
| Depth of cut $d$ | No effect | No effect | Increases | Increases linearly | Tool wear increases, power rises |
| QRR (more aggressive) | Increases | Decreases | No effect | No effect | Better productivity |
| Specific force $K_s$ (harder material) | No effect | No effect | No effect | Increases | Power & rigidity must rise |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of Shaper Operation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Electric Motor - Power Source] --> B[Bull Gear - Speed Reduction]
    B --> C[Crank and Slotted Lever - Quick Return]
    C --> D[Sliding Ram - Reciprocating Motion]
    D --> E[Tool Head with Single Point Cutting Tool]
    E --> F[Cutting Stroke - Leftward - Material Removal]
    F --> G[Tool Lifts via Clapper Box]
    G --> H[Return Stroke - Rightward - No Cutting]
    H --> I[Table Cross Feed via Screw]
    I --> J[New Strip of Workpiece Exposed]
    J --> K{All Width Covered?}
    K -->|No| F
    K -->|Yes| L[Finished Flat Surface - Output]
    M[Workpiece on Table] --> N[Stationary During Cutting]
    N --> F
```

## 4.2 Block Architecture of Crank and Slotted Lever Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DRIVE[Drive Subsystem]
        A1[Bull Gear - Constant omega] --> A2[Crank Pin - Radius R - Eccentric]
    end
    subgraph CONVERT[Motion Conversion]
        A2 --> A3[Slotted Lever - Engages Crank Pin]
        A3 --> A4[Oscillates about Fixed Pivot O]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT[Output - Ram Motion]
        A4 --> A5[Slotted End - Translates Horizontally]
        A5 --> A6[Ram - Cutting Stroke then Return Stroke]
    end
    style DRIVE fill:#e1f5ff
    style CONVERT fill:#fff4e1
    style OUTPUT fill:#e8f5e9
```

## 4.3 Process Flow for Machining a Flat Surface

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Step 1 - Mount Workpiece on Table] --> P2[Step 2 - Clamp Tightly with Vise or Bolts]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3 - Check Tool Geometry - Rake and Clearance Angles]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4 - Set Stroke Length - L greater than Workpiece]
    P4 --> P5[Step 5 - Set Stroke Position - Tool just clears Workpiece]
    P5 --> P6[Step 6 - Set Depth of Cut d using Vertical Feed]
    P6 --> P7[Step 7 - Set Cutting Speed and Feed]
    P7 --> P8[Step 8 - Start Machine - Engage Ram]
    P8 --> P9[Step 9 - Engage Cross Feed - Automatic]
    P9 --> P10[Step 10 - Machine in Progress - Monitor Chips]
    P10 --> P11{Required Dimensions Achieved?}
    P11 -->|No| P9
    P11 -->|Yes| P12[Step 11 - Stop Machine and Remove Workpiece]
    P12 --> P13[Step 12 - Inspect with Vernier and Surface Plate]
```

## 4.4 Surface Classification Map for Shaper

```mermaid
mindmap
  root((Flat Surfaces on Shaper))
    Horizontal
      Plain Tool
      Slideways
    Vertical
      Side Facing Tool
      Slot Walls
    Inclined
      Swivel Vice
      Angular Faces
    Internal
      Slotting Tool
      Keyways
      T-Slots
    Contoured
      Form Tool
      Cam Profiles
      Dovetails
```

## 4.5 Kinematic Diagram of Quick Return Ratio (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    K1[Bull Gear - 1 Full Rotation] --> K2[Crank Pin traces Circle]
    K2 --> K3{Slot Position}
    K3 -->|Outer Arc - Larger Angle - Slower Ram| K4[Cutting Stroke - alpha angle]
    K3 -->|Inner Arc - Smaller Angle - Faster Ram| K5[Return Stroke - beta angle]
    K4 --> K6[alpha is Greater - Slow Cutting]
    K5 --> K7[beta is Smaller - Fast Return]
    K6 --> K8[QRR = alpha by beta Greater than 1]
    K7 --> K8
    style K4 fill:#ffe0e0
    style K5 fill:#e0ffe0
    style K8 fill:#fff4cc
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** The above block diagrams represent the functional and kinematic architecture of the shaping machine. For a physical part-drawing of the shaper with labeled components, refer to standard workshop technology textbooks (such as B.S. Raghuwanshi or Hajra Choudhury) — these are not fabricated here to avoid any inaccuracy.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]
**Define a shaping machine. List any four operations that can be performed on it.**

**Model Answer:**
A shaping machine is a reciprocating-type machine tool in which a single-point cutting tool mounted on a ram moves in a linear reciprocating path across a stationary workpiece to produce flat surfaces, grooves, and keyways.

**Four operations performed on a shaper:**
1. Machining of horizontal flat surfaces.
2. Machining of vertical flat surfaces (slots).
3. Machining of inclined surfaces using a swivel vice.
4. Cutting of internal keyways and T-slots using internal shaping tools.

**(3 marks: 1 mark for definition + ½ mark × 4 = 2 marks for operations)**

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]
**What is a quick return mechanism? State its purpose in a shaping machine.**

**Model Answer:**
A quick return mechanism is a kinematic arrangement in the shaper that makes the return stroke (idle stroke) of the ram faster than the cutting stroke (working stroke).

**Purpose:**
- Reduces the total cycle time per stroke.
- Increases the effective cutting speed for a given stroke length and strokes-per-minute.
- Improves productivity by minimizing non-productive (return) time.
- Ensures the tool spends more time cutting and less time idling.

**The most common type is the crank and slotted lever mechanism, which typically gives a QRR between 1.5:1 and 3:1.**

**(3 marks: 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for purpose with example)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Pattern

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern Note:** Part B Module-2 questions carry 14 marks and follow an internal choice (OR). Each question typically has two sub-parts: (a) carrying 7 marks and (b) carrying 7 marks.

---

### ❖ QUESTION A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - June 2024 Model]

**Q. (a)** With a neat sketch, explain the working principle of a crank and slotted lever quick return mechanism used in a shaping machine. Derive the expression for the quick return ratio. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A flat surface 250 mm long and 100 mm wide on a cast iron workpiece is to be machined on a shaping machine. Given: cutting speed = 18 m/min, feed = 1.0 mm/stroke, depth of cut = 4 mm, approach = 12 mm, overtravel = 18 mm, quick return ratio = 2:1, and specific cutting force for cast iron = 1500 N/mm². Calculate:
  (i) Cutting stroke length
  (ii) Number of strokes per minute
  (iii) Machining time
  (iv) Material removal rate
  (v) Cutting force and power required. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) — Crank and Slotted Lever Quick Return Mechanism:**

**Working Principle:**

The crank and slotted lever mechanism converts the rotary motion of the bull gear into the reciprocating linear motion of the ram. The main components are:

1. **Bull gear (B)** rotating at uniform angular velocity $\omega$.
2. **Crank pin (C)** fixed eccentrically at a distance $R$ from the gear center.
3. **Slotted lever (SL)** of length $L$ pivoted at a fixed point $O$.
4. **Ram (R)** connected to the slotted end of the lever.

The crank pin slides inside the slot of the lever. As the bull gear rotates, the pin moves along a circular path. The lever rocks about the fixed pivot $O$, causing the slotted end to move in a straight horizontal line. This horizontal motion is transmitted to the ram.

**Cutting vs. Return Stroke:**

- When the crank pin moves along the **outer arc** of its circle (the arc farther from the lever pivot $O$), the lever rotates through a **larger angle** $2\alpha$ — this corresponds to the **cutting stroke** (slow, productive).
- When the crank pin moves along the **inner arc** (closer to the lever pivot), the lever rotates through a **smaller angle** $2\beta$ — this is the **return stroke** (fast, non-productive).

Since the bull gear rotates at constant $\omega$, the time for each stroke is proportional to the angle traversed:

$$\frac{T_{cutting}}{T_{return}} = \frac{2\alpha}{2\beta} = \frac{\alpha}{\beta}$$

**Derivation of Quick Return Ratio (QRR):**

For one full rotation of the bull gear:
$$2\alpha + 2\beta = 2\pi \implies \beta = \pi - \alpha$$

Therefore, the quick return ratio is:

$$QRR = \frac{T_{cutting}}{T_{return}} = \frac{\alpha}{\pi - \alpha}$$

$$\boxed{QRR = \frac{\alpha}{\pi - \alpha}}$$

The corresponding return-to-cutting time ratio (the conventional engineering form) is:

$$r = \frac{T_{return}}{T_{cutting}} = \frac{\pi - \alpha}{\alpha}$$

**Typical values:** $\alpha$ ranges from $120°$ to $150°$, giving QRR values from 2:1 to 3:1.

**(7 marks: 2 for sketch description + 3 for working + 2 for derivation)**

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- $W = 250$ mm (workpiece length), $B = 100$ mm (workpiece width)
- $V_c = 18$ m/min, $f = 1.0$ mm/stroke, $d = 4$ mm
- $l_1 = 12$ mm (approach), $l_2 = 18$ mm (overtravel)
- QRR = 2:1 $\implies r = 0.5$
- $K_s = 1500$ N/mm²

**Step (i) — Cutting stroke length:**

$$L = W + l_1 + l_2 = 250 + 12 + 18 = \boxed{280 \text{ mm}}$$

**Step (ii) — Number of strokes per minute:**

$$N_s = \frac{1000 \cdot V_c}{L \cdot (1+r)} = \frac{1000 \times 18}{280 \times 1.5} = \frac{18000}{420} = \boxed{42.86 \approx 43 \text{ strokes/min}}$$

**Step (iii) — Machining time:**

$$T_m = \frac{B \cdot L}{1000 \cdot V_c \cdot f} = \frac{100 \times 280}{1000 \times 18 \times 1.0} = \frac{28000}{18000} = \boxed{1.556 \text{ min} \approx 1 \text{ min } 33 \text{ sec}}$$

**Step (iv) — Material Removal Rate:**

$$MRR = L \cdot f \cdot d \cdot N_s = 280 \times 1.0 \times 4 \times 42.86 = 48,000 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}$$

$$\boxed{MRR = 48 \times 10^3 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min} = 48 \text{ cm}^3/\text{min}}$$

**Step (v) — Cutting force and power:**

Cutting force:
$$F_c = K_s \cdot f \cdot d = 1500 \times 1.0 \times 4 = \boxed{6000 \text{ N} = 6 \text{ kN}}$$

Power required:
$$P = \frac{F_c \cdot V_c}{60 \times 1000} = \frac{6000 \times 18}{60 \times 1000} = \frac{108000}{60000} = \boxed{1.8 \text{ kW}}$$

**(7 marks: 1 mark each for (i), (ii), (iii), (iv) + 2 marks for (v) — split as 1 for force + 1 for power)**

---

### ❖ QUESTION B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model] — INTERNAL CHOICE

**Q. (a)** Describe the construction and working of a shaping machine with a neat block diagram. Explain the functions of: (i) Clapper box, (ii) Cross-slide, (iii) Table swivel mechanism. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the step-by-step procedure to machine a flat surface on a shaping machine. List the safety precautions to be observed. A shaping machine operates at 30 strokes/min with a stroke length of 200 mm and feed of 0.5 mm/stroke. Calculate the cutting speed (m/min) and material removal rate if the depth of cut is 3 mm. Take quick return ratio as 1.5:1. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) — Construction and Working of Shaping Machine:**

A shaping machine consists of a heavy cast-iron **base** on which a vertical **column** is mounted. The column houses the **drive mechanism** (bull gear, crank and slotted lever) and supports the **ram** through guides. The ram is a heavy casting that reciprocates horizontally and carries the **tool head** (which can be swiveled to set the cutting angle). The **table** is mounted on the **cross-rail** and moves perpendicular to the ram direction (cross-feed) and vertically (vertical feed) to set the depth of cut. The workpiece is clamped on the table.

**Working:** The electric motor drives the bull gear, which rotates the crank. The crank pin engages the slotted lever, which converts rotary motion into the linear reciprocating motion of the ram. During the forward stroke, the tool cuts the workpiece. The clapper box lifts the tool during the return stroke to avoid rubbing. After each return stroke, the table feeds laterally by a small amount (the feed $f$).

**Functions of the named components:**

**(i) Clapper Box:** It is a hinged tool holder that allows the tool to lift automatically during the return stroke, preventing the cutting edge from dragging on the freshly machined surface (which would cause rubbing, tool wear, and a poor finish).

**(ii) Cross-Slide:** It is the mechanism on the table that provides the **cross-feed motion** — a small lateral movement of the table after each cutting stroke. This exposes a new strip of uncut material, enabling the full width of the workpiece to be machined progressively.

**(iii) Table Swivel Mechanism:** It allows the table to be rotated (swiveled) about a vertical axis through a desired angle, enabling the machining of **inclined flat surfaces** (e.g., dovetails, angular faces) without resetting the workpiece.

**(7 marks: 3 for construction/working + 2 for clapper box + 1 for cross-slide + 1 for swivel mechanism)**

---

**Part (b) — Procedure and Numerical:**

**Step-by-Step Procedure for Machining a Flat Surface:**

1. **Selection and preparation:** Select the workpiece material, mark the area to be machined, and clean the surfaces.
2. **Workpiece mounting:** Clamp the workpiece firmly on the shaper table using a vise or step clamps. Ensure the surface to be machined is parallel to the table movement.
3. **Tool selection:** Choose a single-point high-speed steel (HSS) tool with appropriate rake and clearance angles. Grind the tool to the required geometry.
4. **Tool mounting:** Mount the tool in the tool holder on the tool head with minimum overhang.
5. **Stroke length adjustment:** Adjust the stroke length $L$ so that the tool just clears the workpiece at both ends (accounting for approach and overtravel).
6. **Stroke position adjustment:** Set the starting and ending positions of the ram so the tool enters and exits the workpiece correctly.
7. **Depth of cut:** Adjust the vertical position of the table to set the required depth of cut $d$.
8. **Speed and feed:** Set the stroke rate (strokes/min) and feed rate as per the recommended cutting parameters.
9. **Trial cut:** Take a trial cut on a small portion to verify dimensions.
10. **Machining:** Engage the ram and cross-feed; allow the machine to operate and monitor chip formation.
11. **Inspection:** Stop the machine, remove the workpiece, and inspect dimensions using vernier calipers and surface finish using a surface comparator.

**Safety Precautions:**

- Always wear safety goggles and avoid loose clothing.
- Never adjust the stroke or depth while the machine is running.
- Keep hands away from the moving ram and table.
- Ensure the workpiece is clamped firmly to prevent flying projectiles.
- Do not leave the chuck key in the chuck.
- Stop the machine before taking measurements.
- Use a brush to clear chips — never use bare hands.
- Ensure the tool is sharp and properly ground.
- Verify lubrication of moving parts before starting.

**Numerical:**

**Given:** $N_s = 30$ strokes/min, $L = 200$ mm, $f = 0.5$ mm/stroke, $d = 3$ mm, QRR = 1.5:1, so $r = 1/1.5 = 0.667$.

**Cutting speed:**

$$V_c = \frac{L \cdot N_s \cdot (1+r)}{1000} = \frac{200 \times 30 \times (1 + 0.667)}{1000}$$

$$V_c = \frac{200 \times 30 \times 1.667}{1000} = \frac{10000}{1000} = \boxed{10 \text{ m/min}}$$

**Material Removal Rate:**

$$MRR = L \cdot f \cdot d \cdot N_s = 200 \times 0.5 \times 3 \times 30 = \boxed{9000 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min} = 9 \text{ cm}^3/\text{min}}$$

**(7 marks: 3 for procedure + 1 for safety + 1.5 for $V_c$ + 1.5 for MRR)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Mix-up in $V_c$ formula:** Many students write $V_c = \pi D N / 1000$ (lathe formula). This is **WRONG** for shaper — there is no diameter here. Always use $V_c = L \cdot N_s (1+r)/1000$ for shaper.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor $(1+r)$:** When quick return ratio is given, the $(1+r)$ factor MUST appear. If you use $V_c = L \cdot N_s / 1000$ when QRR = 2:1, you will under-predict $V_c$ by 50% and lose full marks.
> 3. **Stroke length vs. workpiece length:** The cutting stroke length $L$ is **NOT** equal to the workpiece length. Always add approach and overtravel: $L = W + l_1 + l_2$.
> 4. **Confusing feed units:** Feed $f$ in shaper is **mm/stroke** (not mm/rev as in a lathe).
> 5. **MRR unit conversion:** Final MRR should be in mm³/min or cm³/min — be consistent. The examiner often deducts ½ mark for unit inconsistency.
> 6. **Quick return ratio direction:** QRR = cutting time / return time (not the reverse). A QRR of 2:1 means cutting is 2 times slower than return.
> 7. **Clapper box purpose:** Students often say "it lifts the tool" — be precise: it lifts the tool **during return stroke to prevent rubbing/dragging on the finished surface**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Bullets — Read This Before the Exam:**

- ☐ **Shaper = Reciprocating machine tool**, workpiece is **stationary**, tool moves in a **straight line**.
- ☐ The **ram** is the reciprocating member; the **tool head** swivels for angled cuts; the **table** provides cross-feed and vertical feed.
- ☐ **Clapper box** is a hinged tool holder that lifts the tool during the return stroke to prevent rubbing on the finished surface.
- ☐ The **crank and slotted lever** is the most common quick return mechanism (QRR typically 1.5:1 to 3:1).
- ☐ **Quick Return Ratio (QRR)** = (Time of cutting stroke) / (Time of return stroke) = $\alpha / (\pi - \alpha)$, where $\alpha$ is the half-angle of cutting.
- ☐ **Cutting speed formula (UNIQUE to shaper):**
  $$V_c = \frac{L \cdot N_s \cdot (1+r)}{1000} \text{ m/min}$$
  ☐ **NOT** the lathe formula $V_c = \pi D N / 1000$ — there is no diameter!
- ☐ **Cutting stroke length:** $L = W + l_1 + l_2$ (workpiece + approach + overtravel).
- ☐ **Feed $f$ is in mm/stroke** (not mm/rev like in lathe).
- ☐ **Depth of cut $d$** is set by the vertical position of the table, before starting the cut.
- ☐ **MRR = $L \cdot f \cdot d \cdot N_s$** (mm³/min) — note: this includes $L$, unlike lathe MRR.
- ☐ **Machining time:** $T_m = B \cdot L / (1000 \cdot V_c \cdot f)$ minutes.
- ☐ **Cutting force:** $F_c = K_s \cdot f \cdot d$ Newtons ($K_s$ depends on material).
- ☐ **Power:** $P = (F_c \cdot V_c) / (60 \times 1000)$ kW.
- ☐ **Five types of surfaces:** horizontal, vertical, inclined (swivel vice), internal (slotting tool), contoured (form tool).
- ☐ **Five principal parts to remember:** Base, Column, Ram, Cross-rail, Table.
- ☐ **Safety rules:** No loose clothing, no measuring while running, always clamp workpiece firmly, no hands near moving parts, use chip brush not bare hands.
- ☐ **Key trick for numericals:** If QRR is not given, assume $r = 0$ (i.e., $V_c = L \cdot N_s / 1000$). If approach/overtravel are not given, assume $L = W$ (slight approximation).
- ☐ **Whitworth quick return** uses a different kinematic chain (elliptical trunnion); for KTU lab, focus on **crank and slotted lever**.
- ☐ **Hydraulic shapers** provide constant cutting speed throughout the stroke (no quick return) — modern variant.
- ☐ **KTU viva tip:** Be ready to draw the crank and slotted lever diagram from memory and explain why the return is faster (lever rocking geometry).
- ☐ **Industrial relevance:** Shapers are still used in tool rooms, prototype workshops, and for large castings that cannot be easily rotated on a lathe.

---

*End of Notes — KTU 2024 Scheme | PCIEL407 Machine Tools Lab | Module Experiment 2*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Exercises on shaping machine: Machining of grooves and key ways
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCIEL407 - MACHINE TOOLS LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Exercises on shaping machine: Machining of grooves and key ways # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Shaping Machine (as per KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):** > A shaping machine is a **reciprocating type machine tool** in which a single-point cutting tool traverses linearly across a stationary or intermittently-fed workpiece to generate flat surfaces, grooves, slots, keyways, and contours. The relative motion between tool and workpiece is linear reciprocation, powered by a **Crank and Slotted Lever (Whitworth Quick-Return) Mechanism**. For the specific lab exercise, the operation involves producing: - **Internal Keyways** — slots cut inside a bore or hole to accommodate a machine key for shaft-hub power transmission. - **External Keyways / Flat Surfaces** — open slots on shafts for key fitting. - **Rectangular and T-slots** — grooves of defined width and depth. | Parameter | Standard Metric (KTU) | |---|---| | Cutting Speed (V) | **15–30 m/min** for Mild Steel | | Feed per Stroke (f) | **0.1–0.8 mm/stroke** | | Depth of Cut (d) | **1–6 mm per pass** | | Stroke Length (L) | Generally **Stroke + 20 mm overtravel** | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!IMPORTANT] > **The "Wood-Saw and Crank" Analogy:** > Imagine you are sawing a log with a hand saw. Your **arm moves back and forth** (this is the shaper's ram motion). But while sawing, you also **slowly push the log forward** after every cut so the saw cuts a new line (this is the **intermittent table feed**). Now imagine a big mechanical version where an electric motor turns a crank, the crank drives a slotted lever, and the lever pushes a heavy arm (the **ram**) holding a sharp tool — that is exactly how a shaping machine works! **Why use a shaper for grooves/keyways instead of a milling machine?** - Shapers are **far cheaper** than milling machines. - For **short, internal, or odd-angled slots**, the shaper's linear stroke is **superior** because the tool can plunge into a bore directly. - Setup is **simpler** — only tool alignment is needed, no rotary cutter balancing. ## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics - **Quick Return Ratio (Q):** Typically **2:1 to 3:1** (cutting stroke takes 40–50% of cycle time). - **Ram Stroke Adjustment:** Done by rotating the **bull gear** relative to the crank pin. - **Stroke Position Adjustment:** Done by rotating the **bull gear** on the pinion shaft to shift ram position (forward/backward/centered). - **Standard Shaper Drive Motor:** **2.2 kW to 5.5 kW** for lab-scale models. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Crank and Slotted Lever (Whitworth) Mechanism — Why and How The shaper's cutting tool moves with **non-uniform velocity** during the working (cutting) stroke, then retracts quickly during the return stroke. This is achieved as follows: 1. **Crank Pin (P)** is mounted on a rotating **bull gear**. As the gear rotates, pin P moves in a circle. 2. **Slotted Lever (OS)** has a slot in which pin P slides freely. One end (O) is pivoted, the other end (S) is connected to the **ram** via a connecting rod. 3. As pin P rotates, lever OS oscillates about pivot O — this pushes the ram forward and back. 4. Because the slotted lever's angular travel is **non-symmetrical** (P moves through a larger arc on one side), the forward (cutting) stroke is **slow**, and the return stroke is **fast**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why is the return stroke faster?** > Because the slotted lever sweeps a larger angular travel during the return, the ram traverses the same linear distance in less time — saving **~50% of cycle time** per stroke. This directly **increases productivity**. ## 2.2 Why Use a Shaper for Keyways? | Feature | Shaper Advantage | |---|---| | **Internal keyway in a bore** | Tool enters bore directly — milling cutter cannot easily plunge into deep bores | | **Cost** | 5–10× cheaper than a vertical mill | | **Setup simplicity** | Only tool and stroke alignment needed | | **Tool cost** | Standard HSS single-point tools are inexpensive | | **Surface finish** | Achievable **1.6 µm Ra** with proper feed | ## 2.3 Types of Keyways and Grooves Made on a Shaper 1. **Open Keyway on Shaft** — single straight cut along the shaft length. 2. **Closed/Internal Keyway in Bore** — slot machined inside a hub/bore. 3. **Woodruff Key Slot** — semi-circular slot (made with a special circular cutter; sometimes done on shaper with form tool). 4. **T-Slot** — for machine tool tables. 5. **Dovetail Slide** — angled groove for sliding fixtures. 6. **Rectangular Groove** — open ended slot of defined width and depth. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **ALL key formulas required for KTU university exam calculations on shaping machine time, feed, and speed:** | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Cutting Speed** | $V = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000}$ | m/min | D in mm, N in rpm (here D, N refer to **equivalent crank-driven ram velocity** equivalent — for shaper, use stroke length & strokes/min) | | 2 | **Shaper Cutting Speed (Practical)** | $V = \dfrac{2 \cdot L_s \cdot N_s}{1000}$ | m/min | $L_s$ = stroke length in mm, $N_s$ = strokes/min | | 3 | **Strokes per minute** | $N_s = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V}{2 \cdot L_s}$ | strokes/min | Inverting the above | | 4 | **Feed per stroke** | $f = \dfrac{L_w}{n}$ | mm/stroke | $L_w$ = work length, $n$ = no. of strokes to cover it | | 5 | **Feed rate** | $f_r = f \cdot N_s$ | mm/min | | | 6 | **Machining Time** | $T_m = \dfrac{L_w + L_a}{f \cdot N_s}$ | min | $L_a$ = approach + overtravel | | 7 | **Material Removal Rate (MRR)** | $MRR = L_s \cdot f \cdot d \cdot N_s$ | mm³/min | $d$ = depth of cut | | 8 | **Quick Return Ratio** | $Q = \dfrac{T_c + T_r}{T_c}$ | dimensionless | $T_c$ = cutting stroke time, $T_r$ = return stroke time | | 9 | **Cutting Stroke Time** | $T_c = \dfrac{L_s}{v_c \cdot 1000}$ | min | $v_c$ = average cutting velocity (m/min) | | 10 | **Power Required** | $P = \dfrac{F_c \cdot V}{60 \cdot 1000 \cdot \eta}$ | kW | $F_c$ = cutting force (N), $\eta$ = efficiency (~0.8) | > [!NOTE] > **Important Note:** Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of the pipe symbol `|` in any further formulas to avoid breaking markdown table structure. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Automotive:** Keyways on crankshafts, transmission shafts, gear hubs. - **Machine Tool Industry:** T-slots on milling machine tables, dovetail slides on lathe beds. - **Pump and Valve Manufacturing:** Internal keyways in impeller hubs. - **Heavy Machinery:** Open keyways on coupling shafts. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Shaper Machining Time for a Keyway ### Given (Typical KTU Numerical Problem) - Stroke length $L_s = 100$ mm - Length of workpiece (keyway) $L_w = 80$ mm - Approach length + overtravel $L_a = 20$ mm - Cutting speed $V = 20$ m/min - Feed per stroke $f = 0.5$ mm/stroke - Depth of keyway $d = 4$ mm - Quick Return Ratio $Q = 2$ ### Step 1: Calculate Strokes per Minute From the formula: $$ \begin{aligned} N_s &= \dfrac{1000 \cdot V}{2 \cdot L_s} \\ N_s &= \dfrac{1000 \times 20}{2 \times 100} \\ N_s &= \dfrac{20000}{200} \\ N_s &= 100 \text{ strokes/min} \end{aligned} $$ **Logic:** The ram travels forward by $L_s$ (100 mm) and backward by $L_s$ (100 mm) every cycle, so total linear travel per minute = $2 \cdot L_s \cdot N_s$. Equating this to the cutting velocity gives strokes/min. ### Step 2: Calculate Cutting Stroke Time $$ \begin{aligned} T_c &= \dfrac{L_s}{V \times 1000} \\ T_c &= \dfrac{100}{20 \times 1000} \\ T_c &= \dfrac{100}{20000} \\ T_c &= 0.005 \text{ min} = 0.3 \text{ seconds} \end{aligned} $$ **Logic:** Cutting stroke time = distance ÷ cutting velocity. Convert minutes properly. ### Step 3: Calculate Return Stroke Time using Quick Return Ratio $$ \begin{aligned} Q &= \dfrac{T_c + T_r}{T_c} \\ 2 &= \dfrac{0.005 + T_r}{0.005} \\ 0.01 &= 0.005 + T_r \\ T_r &= 0.005 \text{ min} \end{aligned} $$ **Logic:** With Q=2, the return stroke time equals cutting stroke time in this idealized case. (In real machines, return is faster, so Q > 2 typically means return takes less than half cycle.) ### Step 4: Calculate Machining Time $$ \begin{aligned} T_m &= \dfrac{L_w + L_a}{f \cdot N_s} \\ T_m &= \dfrac{80 + 20}{0.5 \times 100} \\ T_m &= \dfrac{100}{50} \\ T_m &= 2.0 \text{ minutes} \end{aligned} $$ **Logic:** Total stroke length traversed for the workpiece is $L_w + L_a$ (must include approach so tool enters workpiece and overtravel so tool exits fully). Each stroke advances workpiece by $f$, so number of strokes = total / f. Divide by strokes/min to get time in minutes. ### Step 5: Calculate Material Removal Rate $$ \begin{aligned} MRR &= L_s \cdot f \cdot d \cdot N_s \\ MRR &= 100 \times 0.5 \times 4 \times 100 \\ MRR &= 20000 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min} \\ MRR &= 20 \text{ cm}^3/\text{min} \end{aligned} $$ **Logic:** Each stroke removes a chip of volume = stroke length × feed × depth. Multiply by strokes per minute for volumetric rate. ## 3.2 Analytical Decision Matrix: When to Choose Shaper vs. Other Machines | Selection Criterion | Shaper | Milling Machine | Broaching Machine | |---|---|---|---| | **Internal keyway in deep bore** | ✅ Excellent | ⚠️ Difficult | ✅ Excellent but costly | | **External keyway on shaft** | ✅ Good | ✅ Excellent | ❌ Not used | | **T-slot on table** | ✅ Excellent | ✅ Good | ❌ Not used | | **Volume production** | ❌ Slow | ✅ Good | ✅ Best | | **Tool cost** | Low (HSS) | High (inserts) | Very high | | **Surface finish** | Good (1.6 µm) | Better (0.8 µm) | Best (0.4 µm) | | **Setup time** | Short | Medium | Long | | **Capital cost** | Low | Medium-High | Very High | ## 3.3 Detailed Procedure: Machining an Internal Keyway on Shaper | Step | Operation | KTU Practical Note | |---|---|---| | 1 | **Mark the keyway** on the bore using vernier height gauge + scriber. | Use marking blue for visibility. | | 2 | **Mount workpiece** on the shaper table using clamps and parallels. | Ensure workpiece is rigid — overhang causes chatter. | | 3 | **Select cutting tool** — HSS parting/keysteel tool, ground to exact width. | Tool width = keyway width (e.g., 10 mm for standard key). | | 4 | **Set stroke length** = keyway length + 20 mm overtravel. | Use crank-handle adjustment on bull gear. | | 5 | **Set stroke position** so tool enters bore correctly. | Test with hand rotation — NO power yet. | | 6 | **Adjust feed dial** to 0.3–0.5 mm/stroke. | Higher feed → rougher finish but faster. | | 7 | **Take trial cut** at low depth (0.5 mm). | Check alignment, parallelism. | | 8 | **Take finishing cut** at full depth with reduced feed. | Final pass: 0.1–0.2 mm feed for fine finish. | | 9 | **Inspect** with bore gauge, depth micrometer, go/no-go plug. | Compare to drawing tolerances. | | 10 | **Deburr and clean** workpiece. | Use file or hand deburring tool. | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Shaper Kinematic Architecture ```mermaid graph TD M1[Electric Motor
2.2 to 5.5 kW] --> C1[Coupling] C1 --> BG1[Bull Gear Shaft] BG1 --> BG2[Bull Gear with Crank Pin P] BG2 --> SL[Slotted Lever OS
Pivot at O, End at S] SL --> CR[Connecting Rod] CR --> RM[Ram with Tool Holder] RM --> CUT[Single Point Cutting Tool] BG1 --> PC[Pinion] PC --> RG[Rack Gear on Table] RG --> TB[Shaper Table
Intermittent Feed] TB --> WP[Workpiece Mounted
with Clamps] WP --> KEY[Keyway being Cut] CUT -. cuts .- KEY RM -. reciprocates .- KEY TB -. feeds .- WP ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Flow Diagram: Keyway Machining Procedure ```mermaid graph TD A[Start Experiment] --> B[Study Workpiece Drawing] B --> C[Mark Keyway Location
Height Gauge + Scriber] C --> D[Mount Workpiece on Table
Using Clamps and Parallels] D --> E[Select HSS Keyway Tool
Width equals Keyway Width] E --> F[Set Stroke Length
equals Keyway Length + 20 mm] F --> G[Set Stroke Position
Tool enters Bore Correctly] G --> H[Adjust Feed
0.3 to 0.5 mm per Stroke] H --> I[Test Hand Rotation
No Power] I --> J{Alignment OK?} J -->|No| K[Realign Tool and Workpiece] K --> I J -->|Yes| L[Set Cutting Speed
15 to 30 m per min] L --> M[Take Trial Cut
0.5 mm Depth] M --> N[Inspect Trial Cut] N --> O{Trial Satisfactory?} O -->|No| P[Adjust Feed or Speed] P --> M O -->|Yes| Q[Take Roughing Passes
Full Depth] Q --> R[Take Finishing Pass
Reduced Feed] R --> S[Final Inspection
Bore Gauge + Depth Micrometer] S --> T[Deburr and Clean] T --> U[Record Observations
in Lab Manual] U --> V[End Experiment] ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Types of Keyways and Grooves Made on Shaper ```mermaid graph TD SH[Shaper Machined Features] --> SK[Straight Keyway] SH --> WK[Woodruff Key Slot] SH --> TS[T-Slot] SH --> DT[Dovetail Slide] SH --> RG[Rectangular Groove] SH --> SH1[Semi Circular Slot] SK --> SK1[Open Keyway on Shaft] SK --> SK2[Internal Keyway in Bore] SK1 --> SK1A[Single Cut Along Shaft] SK2 --> SK2A[Tool Plunges into Bore] WK --> WK1[Special Form Tool Used] WK1 --> WK2[Arc Profile Generated] TS --> TS1[First Cut Vertical] TS1 --> TS2[Second Cut Horizontal] TS2 --> TS3[T Profile Complete] DT --> DT1[Angled Tool Used] DT1 --> DT2[Dovetail Angle 45 or 60 deg] RG --> RG1[Defined Width and Depth] RG1 --> RG2[Open or Closed Type] ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Comparison Matrix: Shaper vs Slotter ```mermaid graph LR SHAPER[Shaping Machine] --> SA1[Ram Moves Horizontally] SHAPER --> SA2[Workpiece on Table] SHAPER --> SA3[Best for External Slots
and Open Keyways] SHAPER --> SA4[Tool Holder Above Workpiece] SLOTTER[Slotting Machine] --> SL1[Ram Moves Vertically Downward] SLOTTER --> SL2[Workpiece on Circular Table] SLOTTER --> SL3[Best for Internal Keyways
in Deep Bores] SLOTTER --> SL4[Tool Plunges into Bore] SA3 -. contrasts with .- SL3 SA1 -. contrasts with .- SL1 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Why this fallback?** Physical drawings of shaping machine mechanisms are difficult to render in Mermaid. Instead, **functional block architecture** is provided which clearly shows component relationships and signal/power flow. This complies with KTU's emphasis on understanding process architecture over memorizing sketches. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define Quick Return Ratio of a shaping machine. **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The Quick Return Ratio (Q) of a shaping machine is defined as the ratio of the total time per cycle (cutting stroke time + return stroke time) to the cutting stroke time alone. It is given by: $$ Q = \dfrac{T_c + T_r}{T_c} $$ where $T_c$ is the cutting stroke time and $T_r$ is the return stroke time. Typically, Q lies between **2:1 and 3:1** for most industrial shapers. This mechanism is achieved using the **Crank and Slotted Lever (Whitworth)** mechanism, which makes the return stroke faster than the cutting stroke, thereby increasing productivity. **(3 marks)** ### Question 2: List any three applications of a shaping machine. **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Machining of internal keyways** in bores and hubs — tool enters bore directly. 2. **Machining of open slots and grooves** on flat surfaces and shafts. 3. **Production of T-slots** on milling machine tables and **dovetail slides** on machine tool beds. 4. **Cutting of flat surfaces** on small workpieces. 5. **Form cutting** using specially shaped single-point tools. **(Any 3 for 3 marks — 1 mark each)** --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]** **(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the working principle of the **Crank and Slotted Lever mechanism** used in shaping machines. Also derive the expression for the **Quick Return Ratio**. **Model Answer:** **Working Principle (4 marks):** The Crank and Slotted Lever mechanism, also known as the Whitworth Quick-Return mechanism, converts **uniform rotary motion** of the bull gear into **oscillating motion** of the slotted lever, which is then converted to **linear reciprocating motion** of the ram carrying the cutting tool. Components: - **Bull Gear:** Rotates at constant angular velocity, driven by the motor through a pinion. - **Crank Pin (P):** Mounted on the bull gear at an offset radius (r) from its center. - **Slotted Lever (OS):** A lever pivoted at point O on the machine frame. It contains a long slot in which the crank pin P slides freely. - **Connecting Rod:** Links end S of the slotted lever to the ram. **Working:** As the bull gear rotates, the crank pin P traces a circular path. The slotted lever pivots about O, causing its free end S to oscillate. The connecting rod transforms this oscillation into linear reciprocating motion of the ram. The slot allows pin P to slide within the lever, accommodating the varying distance between P and O as P rotates. **Quick Return Action:** Because the slotted lever swings through a **larger angular travel during the return stroke** than during the cutting stroke, the ram completes its return motion in less time. This is the "quick return" effect. **Derivation (3 marks):** Let the bull gear rotate at constant angular velocity $\omega$. Let the crank pin P be at angles: - $\alpha$ — angle traversed during **cutting stroke** (forward ram motion). - $\beta$ — angle traversed during **return stroke** (backward ram motion). - $\alpha + \beta = 360° = 2\pi$ rad Time for cutting stroke: $$ T_c = \dfrac{\alpha}{\omega} $$ Time for return stroke: $$ T_r = \dfrac{\beta}{\omega} $$ Quick Return Ratio: $$ \begin{aligned} Q &= \dfrac{T_c + T_r}{T_c} = \dfrac{(\alpha + \beta)/\omega}{\alpha/\omega} \\ Q &= \dfrac{\alpha + \beta}{\alpha} = \dfrac{2\pi}{\alpha} \end{aligned} $$ Typically, $\alpha = 240°$ and $\beta = 120°$, giving $Q = 360°/240° = 1.5$ in this idealized case, but with slotted lever geometry amplifying this to practical values of 2–3. **(7 marks total)** --- **(b) [7 Marks]** A keyway of length **60 mm** and depth **5 mm** is to be cut on a shaping machine. The cutting speed is **18 m/min**, the feed is **0.4 mm/stroke**, the stroke length is **90 mm**, and the Quick Return Ratio is **2**. Calculate: (i) Strokes per minute (ii) Cutting stroke time (iii) Machining time (assume approach + overtravel = 20 mm) **Model Answer:** **Given:** - $L_w = 60$ mm, $d = 5$ mm - $V = 18$ m/min - $f = 0.4$ mm/stroke - $L_s = 90$ mm - $Q = 2$ - $L_a = 20$ mm **(i) Strokes per minute:** $$ \begin{aligned} N_s &= \dfrac{1000 \cdot V}{2 \cdot L_s} \\ N_s &= \dfrac{1000 \times 18}{2 \times 90} \\ N_s &= \dfrac{18000}{180} \\ N_s &= 100 \text{ strokes/min} \end{aligned} $$ **(ii) Cutting stroke time:** $$ \begin{aligned} T_c &= \dfrac{L_s}{V \times 1000} \\ T_c &= \dfrac{90}{18 \times 1000} \\ T_c &= \dfrac{90}{18000} \\ T_c &= 0.005 \text{ min} = 0.3 \text{ seconds} \end{aligned} $$ **(iii) Machining time:** $$ \begin{aligned} T_m &= \dfrac{L_w + L_a}{f \cdot N_s} \\ T_m &= \dfrac{60 + 20}{0.4 \times 100} \\ T_m &= \dfrac{80}{40} \\ T_m &= 2.0 \text{ minutes} \end{aligned} $$ **Results:** (i) 100 strokes/min, (ii) 0.3 s, (iii) 2.0 min. **(7 marks total)** --- ### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Describe with a neat sketch the **step-by-step procedure** for machining an **internal keyway** in a bored hub on a shaping machine. Mention the tools, fixtures, and inspection instruments used. **Model Answer:** **Procedure (7 marks — 1 mark per key step + 1 mark for sketch description):** | Step | Operation | Tools/Fixtures Used | |---|---|---| | 1 | Study the engineering drawing; note keyway width, length, depth, and tolerance. | Drawing, vernier caliper | | 2 | Mark the keyway location on the bore using a height gauge and scriber. | Vernier height gauge, scriber, marking blue | | 3 | Mount the bored hub on the shaper table using **V-blocks and clamps**; align bore axis parallel to ram motion using a dial indicator. | V-blocks, clamps, parallels, dial indicator | | 4 | Select an **HSS keyway tool** (width = keyway width, e.g., 8 mm or 10 mm standard). Grind relief angles properly. | HSS tool, tool grinder | | 5 | Mount the tool in the tool holder on the ram. Set tool projection. | Tool holder, spanner | | 6 | Adjust **stroke length** = keyway length + 20 mm. | Crank handle on bull gear | | 7 | Adjust **stroke position** so the tool enters and exits the bore correctly. | Stroke position knob | | 8 | Set **feed** to 0.3–0.5 mm/stroke using the feed dial. | Feed dial | | 9 | Rotate the bull gear **manually** to verify alignment — tool should enter bore without hitting walls. | Hand rotation | | 10 | Take a **trial cut** at 0.5 mm depth; check parallelism and position. | Depth micrometer, vernier | | 11 | Take **roughing cuts** at full depth (multiple passes if depth > 4 mm). | — | | 12 | Take a **finishing cut** with reduced feed (0.1–0.15 mm/stroke). | — | | 13 | **Inspect** the keyway using a **bore gauge** (for width and parallelism) and **depth micrometer** (for depth). | Bore gauge, depth micrometer, Go/No-Go plug | | 14 | **Deburr** the keyway edges using a hand file. | File, deburring tool | | 15 | Clean the workpiece and record observations in the lab record. | Cleaning cloth, lab manual | --- **(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the difference between a **shaping machine** and a **slotting machine**. When would you prefer one over the other for machining keyways? **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Shaping Machine | Slotting Machine | |---|---|---| | **Ram motion** | Horizontal (linear reciprocation) | Vertical (downward reciprocation) | | **Tool approach** | Tool moves over workpiece horizontally | Tool plunges down into bore vertically | | **Workpiece mounting** | On horizontal table; fed horizontally | On circular table; can be rotated | | **Best for** | External slots, open keyways, flat surfaces | Internal keyways in deep bores, blind slots | | **Cost** | Lower | Higher | | **Power** | Generally lower (2.2–5.5 kW) | Generally higher (3.7–11 kW) | | **Tool** | Standard single-point HSS | Slotting tool with vertical clearance | **Selection Logic (3 marks):** - **Choose Shaper when:** Keyway is **external** (on shaft surface), **open-ended**, or when workpiece is a flat plate with a groove. - **Choose Slotter when:** Keyway is **internal** in a **deep bore**, **blind** (closed at one end), or when a **circular contour** must be generated (slotter's circular table helps). - For shallow internal keyways, both can be used, but slotter gives better chip evacuation and rigidity. **(7 marks total)** --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting the overtravel term** $L_a$ in machining time formula — Examiner deducts 1–2 marks for this. Always write $T_m = (L_w + L_a) / (f \cdot N_s)$. > 2. **Mixing up Quick Return Ratio formula** — Some students write $Q = T_c / T_r$. The correct formula is $Q = (T_c + T_r) / T_c$. This is a **2-mark trap**. > 3. **Unit confusion in cutting speed** — Always convert $L_s$ from mm to m (or multiply by 1000) when using $V = 1000V / (2L_s)$. > 4. **Forgetting to convert seconds to minutes** — Examiner expects time in **minutes** in final answer. > 5. **Not drawing/sketching** in 7-mark questions — Even a rough hand-sketched diagram showing crank, slotted lever, ram earns **at least 2 marks**. > 6. **Writing "shaper" instead of "shaping machine"** — Use correct terminology as per KTU syllabus. > 7. **Confusing shaper with planer** — Planer has moving workpiece and stationary tool; shaper has moving tool and stationary/feeding workpiece. Examiner often tests this. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Shaping Machine: Grooves and Keyways:** - ✅ Shaping machine is a **reciprocating type machine tool** with **single-point cutting tool**. - ✅ Drive mechanism: **Crank and Slotted Lever (Whitworth Quick-Return)**. - ✅ **Quick Return Ratio** $Q = (T_c + T_r) / T_c$ typically **2:1 to 3:1**. - ✅ **Strokes per minute** $N_s = (1000V) / (2L_s)$. - ✅ **Machining time** $T_m = (L_w + L_a) / (f \cdot N_s)$ — **never forget $L_a$!** - ✅ **MRR** $= L_s \cdot f \cdot d \cdot N_s$. - ✅ **Cutting speed** for Mild Steel: **15–30 m/min**. - ✅ **Feed per stroke** typical: **0.1–0.8 mm/stroke**. - ✅ **Standard HSS tool** used; tool width = keyway width. - ✅ **Workpiece mounting**: V-blocks + clamps + parallels on shaper table. - ✅ **Inspection**: Bore gauge (width + parallelism), depth micrometer (depth). - ✅ **Shaper vs Slotter**: Shaper — horizontal ram, external slots. Slotter — vertical ram, internal slots in deep bores. - ✅ **Shaper vs Planer**: Shaper — tool moves, workpiece small. Planer — workpiece moves, tool stationary, workpiece large. - ✅ **Safety**: Always test hand-rotation before powering on; never adjust stroke while machine is running. - ✅ **Typical lab keyway dimensions**: width 4–12 mm, length 20–100 mm, depth 2–6 mm. - ✅ **Chatter prevention**: Use sharp tool, rigid clamping, lower feed, shorter overhang. - ✅ **Common fault**: Tool rubbing on return stroke → adjust stroke length and position properly.

topics.title: Exercises on drilling machine: Drilling, boring, reaming, tapping and countersinking
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIEL407 - MACHINE TOOLS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Exercises on drilling machine: Drilling, boring, reaming, tapping and countersinking

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Drilling Machine (Workshop Context)

A **drilling machine** is a powered, multi-purpose machine tool primarily designed to originate cylindrical holes in a solid workpiece by using a rotating multi-point cutting tool called a **drill bit**. The workpiece is held stationary (usually clamped on a drill table) while the rotating drill advances axially into the material along its own axis, generating chips through a shearing action. In addition to plain hole-making, the same machine—with suitable tooling—can perform **boring, reaming, tapping, countersinking, counterboring, and spot-facing**, which is why it is called a *hole-making centre* in any KTU machine shop.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PCIEL407):** "Exercises on drilling machine shall enable the student to perform drilling, boring, reaming, tapping and countersinking operations on given workpieces, calculate machining parameters (cutting speed, feed, spindle speed, drilling time, MRR), and verify dimensional accuracy using appropriate measuring instruments."

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are making a hole in a wooden plank using a hand-held **auger**—the same principle is industrialised in a drilling machine.

- The **drill bit** is like the rotating auger; it spins and "bites" the material.
- The **spindle** is the arm that holds and rotates the bit (your rotating wrist).
- The **feed handle/quill** is the force that pushes the bit into the material (your pushing arm).
- The **drill table** is the workbench that holds the job (the wooden plank clamped down).
- A **boring bar** is like a single-tooth scraper that enlarges an existing hole—think of using a small spoon to widen a pudding hole.
- A **reamer** is a finishing tool—like a precision sander that smooths a pre-existing hole to an exact diameter.
- A **tap** is a hardened "screw-cutter" that cuts internal threads, just like threading a nut onto a bolt in reverse.
- A **countersink** is a conical cutter that creates a chamfered entry for a flat-head screw—mimicking the seat of a Phillips-head screw in a wooden door hinge.

### 1.3 Operation-Wise Core Definitions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Definitions for KTU Lab Viva & Theory (5-Mark Frequently Asked):**

**1. Drilling** — A hole-producing operation in which a **multi-point rotating tool (twist drill)** is fed linearly along its axis into a stationary workpiece to originate a blind or through hole. The standard tool is the **twist drill** made of HSS (High-Speed Steel) or carbide, with a standard point angle of **118°** (for general steels) and **135°** (for hardened/cast materials).

**2. Boring** — An **enlarging** operation performed on an already-drilled (or cast/fired) hole using a **single-point cutting tool** (boring bar) mounted on the spindle. It improves dimensional accuracy, circularity, and surface finish but **does not** originate a hole. Tolerances achievable: **±0.025 mm**; surface roughness **Ra 0.8–3.2 μm**.

**3. Reaming** — A **sizing and finishing** operation that uses a **multi-tooth cutting tool called a reamer** to produce a hole of precise diameter, high accuracy (tolerance **IT7–IT8**), and excellent surface finish (**Ra 0.4–1.6 μm**). The hole must be pre-drilled with a diameter slightly smaller than the reamer size (typically **0.1–0.5 mm** undersize).

**4. Tapping** — A **thread-cutting** operation that produces **internal threads** in a pre-drilled hole (the drilled hole is called the *tap drill hole*) using a **threaded, multi-tooth tool called a tap**. Three types of taps are used sequentially: **taper tap → plug tap → bottoming tap** to cut complete threads in a blind hole.

**5. Countersinking** — A **chamfering** operation that produces a **conical enlargement** at the entry of a previously drilled hole to seat a **flat-head (countersunk) screw or bolt**. The standard included angle of the countersink matches the screw head: **82°** (for inch-standard flat-head screws) or **90°** (for metric standard).

### 1.4 Types of Drilling Machines (KTU Board-Exam Knowledge)

| Type | Description | Application |
|------|-------------|-------------|
| **Bench Drilling Machine** | Small, mounted on workbench; capacity ≤ 12 mm | Lab / light work |
| **Floor/Pillar Drilling Machine** | Heavy, floor-mounted; capacity 12–50 mm | Workshop |
| **Radial Drilling Machine** | Spindle on radial arm; large workpiece | Heavy industry |
| **Gang Drilling Machine** | Multiple spindles in a row | Mass production |
| **CNC Drilling/Tapping Centre** | Automated, multi-axis | Industry 4.0 |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 syllabus emphasises the **bench / pillar type** and the **radial drilling machine** for lab exercises. The bench drill is what students will physically operate.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Drilling — Operational Logic

The **twist drill** is the heart of the operation. The cutting action happens at the **chisel edge** (centre) and the **cutting lips** (two flanks). Chips are evacuated through the **flutes** (helical grooves), and cutting fluid is delivered through the flutes or external nozzle.

**Step-by-step logic:**
1. **Workpiece is clamped** on the drill table (vise or direct clamping).
2. **Drill is mounted** in the drill chuck and tightened with a chuck key.
3. **Spindle is started** — the drill rotates at a calculated RPM.
4. **Feed is applied** — the quill is lowered via the feed handle, advancing the drill axially.
5. **Cutting fluid is applied** — to cool and lubricate (especially for HSS drills on steel).
6. **Hole is produced** — chips exit upward through the flutes.
7. **Drill is withdrawn** after breakthrough to avoid breakage.

**Key parameters (KTU mandatory to memorise):**

- **Cutting speed, V (m/min):** The peripheral speed of the drill at its outer diameter. For HSS on mild steel, **V = 20–30 m/min**; for carbide, **V = 80–150 m/min**.
- **Feed, f (mm/rev):** The axial advance per spindle revolution. Typical value: **0.05–0.3 mm/rev** for drilling; **0.1–0.5 mm/rev** for reaming; **1.5–3 mm/rev** for tapping.
- **Spindle speed, N (rpm):** Derived from V and drill diameter D.
- **Drilling time, T (min):** Time to drill one hole of depth L.
- **Material Removal Rate (MRR):** Volume of metal removed per unit time.

### 2.2 Boring — Operational Logic

Boring uses a **single-point tool** held in a boring bar. It is performed **after** drilling to:
- Enlarge the hole.
- Correct misalignment of a drilled hole.
- Produce internal profiles, steps, or counter-bores.
- Achieve high dimensional and geometric accuracy.

**Why boring instead of re-drilling?** Because the boring tool is **adjustable** (diameter can be finely tuned by moving the tool tip), giving better dimensional control.

**Step-by-step logic:**
1. Drill a *pilot hole* of diameter ≈ 2–5 mm smaller than the final bore.
2. Mount the boring bar in the spindle.
3. Set the tool tip to the desired radius (use a micrometer/dial indicator).
4. Rotate the spindle; advance the boring bar into the hole.
5. Use a light cut (depth of cut = 0.25–1.5 mm) at a low feed.
6. The hole is enlarged with high concentricity and finish.

### 2.3 Reaming — Operational Logic

A **reamer** has many cutting edges (6 to 16) and a small radial relief (typically 0.005–0.015 mm on the cutting edge). It cannot originate a hole—only **finish** one.

**Step-by-step logic:**
1. Drill a hole of diameter D' = (Final diameter – allowance). Allowance ≈ 0.1–0.5 mm.
2. Mount the reamer in the spindle (rigidly, no float).
3. Run the spindle at **half the drilling speed** (re-cutting is light).
4. Feed at **2–3× the drilling feed**.
5. Use plenty of cutting fluid.
6. **Do not stop** the reamer inside the hole—back out while rotating.

> [!WARNING]
> Stopping a reamer inside the hole causes a "bell-mouth" or scrapes the wall, ruining the finish. KTU examiners frequently test this rule.

### 2.4 Tapping — Operational Logic

Tapping is unique because the **pitch of the thread controls the feed**. The tap must be fed in synchronisation with the spindle rotation; otherwise, the threads will be torn.

**Step-by-step logic:**
1. Calculate the **tap drill diameter** using:  
   **D_tap = D_major – P** (for ISO metric coarse threads; P = pitch)
2. Drill the tap drill hole.
3. Chamfer the hole entry (helps the tap enter square).
4. Mount the **taper tap** in a **tapping head** (or use a "T" wrench for hand tapping).
5. Apply cutting fluid.
6. Rotate the tap *and* feed it axially at **f = P mm/rev**.
7. After cutting, back the tap out (still rotating in reverse).
8. Repeat with **plug tap**, then **bottoming tap** for blind holes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU rule:** The feed during tapping is **always equal to the thread pitch**. If you change feed, the thread pitch will be wrong. Most modern drilling machines use a **reversible tapping attachment** or **lead-screw tapping** to enforce this synchronisation.

### 2.5 Countersinking — Operational Logic

A **countersink** is a conical tool (usually with 4–6 flutes) used to create a conical seat for a flat-head screw. The tool has a **pilot** that centres it in the drilled hole.

**Step-by-step logic:**
1. Drill the through hole first.
2. Insert the countersink in the chuck — the pilot fits inside the drilled hole and self-centres.
3. Run at **low RPM** (V ≈ 10–15 m/min).
4. Hand-feed the quill; the conical cutting edges remove material and form the chamfer.
5. Stop when the chamfer depth matches the screw head seating.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!NOTE]
> Memorise the following table. Every KTU numerical question in the lab exam (and theory viva) is built from these.

| S. No. | Quantity | Symbol & Formula | Standard Units | Typical Range / Value |
|:------:|----------|------------------|:--------------:|-----------------------|
| 1 | Cutting speed | $V = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000}$ | m/min | HSS steel: 20–30; Carbide: 80–150 |
| 2 | Spindle speed | $N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V}{\pi \cdot D}$ | rpm | 100–3000 (machine limited) |
| 3 | Feed rate | $f_r = f \cdot N$ | mm/min | 25–250 |
| 4 | Drilling time | $T = \dfrac{L}{f \cdot N}$ | min | Depends on L and f·N |
| 5 | Material Removal Rate | $\text{MRR} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D^2}{4} \cdot f \cdot N$ | mm³/min | — |
| 6 | Thrust force (approximate) | $F_t = K_s \cdot \dfrac{\pi \cdot D^2}{4}$ | N | Ks ≈ 1500–2500 N/mm² for steel |
| 7 | Tap drill diameter | $D_{tap} = D_{major} - P$ | mm | For ISO metric threads |
| 8 | Power required | $P = \dfrac{F_c \cdot V}{60 \cdot 1000}$ | kW | 0.5–5 kW (lab machines) |
| 9 | Reaming allowance | $\delta = D_{final} - D_{drill}$ | mm | 0.1–0.5 |
| 10 | Countersink depth | $h = \dfrac{D_{cs} - D_{hole}}{2 \tan(\alpha/2)}$ | mm | α = 82° or 90° |

**Where:**
- $D$ = drill/reamer diameter (mm)
- $N$ = spindle speed (rpm)
- $V$ = cutting speed (m/min)
- $f$ = feed per revolution (mm/rev)
- $L$ = total depth of hole including approach = $L_{actual} + 0.3 D$ (for drill point) (mm)
- $F_c$ = cutting force (N)
- $K_s$ = specific cutting force (N/mm²)
- $P$ = thread pitch (mm)
- $D_{major}$ = nominal (major) thread diameter (mm)
- $D_{cs}$ = countersink outer diameter (mm)
- $\alpha$ = countersink included angle (°)

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

These five operations together constitute **~70% of all hole-making activities** in any manufacturing industry:

- **Drilling** — for fasteners, assembly clearances, oil holes in gears/shafts.
- **Boring** — for engine cylinder blocks, hydraulic manifolds, gearbox housings.
- **Reaming** — for bearing seats, dowel pin holes, pin-joint clearances.
- **Tapping** — for assembly threads in machine frames, jigs, fixtures.
- **Countersinking** — for flush-mounting of screws in aerospace panels, cabinet making, sheet-metal assemblies.

In the **automotive industry**, a single engine block undergoes drilling, boring, reaming, and tapping in a sequence on a dedicated transfer line. KTU expects students to appreciate this **process planning logic** in the viva.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Spindle Speed (N) from Cutting Speed (V)

**Statement:** Show that the spindle speed in rpm is given by $N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V}{\pi \cdot D}$ for a drill of diameter D cutting at peripheral speed V.

**Given:** Cutting speed V (m/min), drill diameter D (mm).

**Step 1 — Define peripheral speed.**  
The peripheral speed of the drill is the linear velocity at the outer cutting lip. If the drill rotates at N revolutions per minute, then in one minute the lip traces a circular path of circumference $\pi \cdot D$ (with D in mm) N times.

**Step 2 — Express the linear distance travelled per minute.**  
The distance covered by the lip per minute is:
$$L_{lip} = \pi \cdot D \cdot N \quad \text{(in mm/min, since D is in mm)}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to m/min to match V's unit.**  
$$V = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000} \quad \text{(m/min)}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for N.**  
Rearranging:
$$N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V}{\pi \cdot D} \quad \text{(rpm)} \qquad \blacksquare$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Drilling Time (T)

**Statement:** Derive the time required to drill a hole of depth L using a feed f (mm/rev) and spindle speed N (rpm).

**Step 1 — Feed per revolution is f mm/rev.**  
In one minute, the drill rotates N times, so the axial advance per minute (feed rate) is:
$$f_r = f \cdot N \quad \text{(mm/min)}$$

**Step 2 — Account for the drill point.**  
The drill point is not flat — it has a point angle of 118°. The drill must travel an extra distance equal to **0.3 × D** beyond the actual workpiece depth to clear the hole fully. Hence the total axial travel L_T is:
$$L_T = L_{actual} + 0.3 \, D \quad \text{(mm)}$$

**Step 3 — Time is distance divided by rate.**
$$T = \dfrac{L_T}{f_r} = \dfrac{L_{actual} + 0.3 \, D}{f \cdot N} \quad \text{(min)} \qquad \blacksquare$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Material Removal Rate (MRR) in Drilling

**Statement:** Derive MRR for a cylindrical drill of diameter D with feed f and spindle N.

**Step 1 — Cross-sectional area of the hole.**
$$A = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D^2}{4} \quad \text{(mm}^2\text{)}$$

**Step 2 — Volume removed per revolution.**  
In one revolution, the drill advances f mm, so the volume removed per revolution is:
$$V_{rev} = A \cdot f = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D^2}{4} \cdot f \quad \text{(mm}^3\text{)}$$

**Step 3 — Volume removed per minute.**
$$\text{MRR} = V_{rev} \cdot N = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D^2}{4} \cdot f \cdot N \quad \text{(mm}^3\text{/min)} \qquad \blacksquare$$

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Tap Drill Diameter for ISO Metric Thread

**Statement:** Derive $D_{tap} = D_{major} - P$ for cutting an internal metric thread.

**Step 1 — Definition.**  
A standard ISO metric thread has a triangular profile with angle 60°. The thread depth h is related to the pitch P by:
$$h = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \cdot P \approx 0.866 \, P$$

**Step 2 — Tap drill diameter logic.**  
The tap drill must leave material for the thread crests. Approximately 75% of the theoretical thread depth is left (this is the standard "75% thread engagement" rule, which gives optimum strength without excessive tap torque).

**Step 3 — Applying the simplified approximation.**  
For the standard simplified formula used in KTU lab:
$$D_{tap} = D_{major} - P \quad \text{(mm)} \qquad \blacksquare$$

**Example:** For an M10 × 1.5 thread, $D_{tap} = 10 - 1.5 = 8.5$ mm.

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style Full Solution)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Question]** A 20 mm diameter hole is to be drilled in a mild steel plate of 30 mm thickness. Cutting speed = 25 m/min and feed = 0.2 mm/rev. Calculate (a) spindle speed, (b) feed rate, (c) drilling time, and (d) MRR.

**Given:**
- $D = 20$ mm
- $L_{actual} = 30$ mm
- $V = 25$ m/min
- $f = 0.2$ mm/rev

**Part (a) — Spindle speed N:**
$$N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V}{\pi \cdot D} = \dfrac{1000 \times 25}{\pi \times 20} = \dfrac{25000}{62.832} = 397.89 \text{ rpm}$$

Adopt nearest machine-available speed: **N ≈ 400 rpm**.

**Part (b) — Feed rate:**
$$f_r = f \cdot N = 0.2 \times 400 = 80 \text{ mm/min}$$

**Part (c) — Drilling time:**
$$L_T = L_{actual} + 0.3 \, D = 30 + 0.3 \times 20 = 30 + 6 = 36 \text{ mm}$$

$$T = \dfrac{L_T}{f_r} = \dfrac{36}{80} = 0.45 \text{ min} = 27 \text{ seconds}$$

**Part (d) — MRR:**
$$\text{MRR} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D^2}{4} \cdot f \cdot N = \dfrac{\pi \times 20^2}{4} \times 0.2 \times 400 = 314.16 \times 80 = 25133 \text{ mm}^3\text{/min}$$

Convert to cm³/min for industrial readability: **25.13 cm³/min**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Always write the final answer with the correct unit.** KTU examiners deduct ½ mark for missing units. Adopt the practice of writing units in the calculation step itself.

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Operation Selection Guide

| Desired Outcome | Recommended Operation | Tolerance Achievable | Ra (μm) | Tool | Pre-Operation Required? |
|-----------------|----------------------|----------------------|---------|------|------------------------|
| Rough hole, no precision | Drilling | ±0.25 mm | 3.2–6.3 | Twist drill | No |
| Larger precise hole | Boring | ±0.025 mm | 0.8–3.2 | Boring bar | Yes (drill) |
| Finish hole of exact size | Reaming | IT7–IT8 (0.015–0.03 mm) | 0.4–1.6 | Reamer | Yes (drill) |
| Internal threads | Tapping | Thread fit (6H/6g) | 1.6 | Tap | Yes (drill + chamfer) |
