| Chamfered entry for screw | Countersinking | — | 1.6 | Countersink | Yes (drill) |

> [!TIP]
> This **decision matrix** is a favourite KTU viva question. A student who can justify the sequence **Drill → Bore → Ream** for a precision hole gets full marks.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Distinguish between **drilling** and **boring** in terms of tool, purpose, accuracy and surface finish.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Drilling | Boring |
|--------|----------|--------|
| Tool | Multi-point twist drill | Single-point boring bar |
| Purpose | Originate a hole | Enlarge an existing hole |
| Tolerance | ±0.25 mm | ±0.025 mm |
| Surface finish (Ra) | 3.2–6.3 μm | 0.8–3.2 μm |
| Hole geometry | Approximate | Highly accurate (round, straight) |

Drilling is the **primary** hole-making operation using a multi-point cutting tool that generates chips along two cutting lips. Boring is a **secondary** operation using a single-point tool that precisely enlarges a pre-existing hole, improving roundness, straightness, and surface finish. Drilling cannot achieve the close tolerances required for bearing fits; boring is used for that. **(3 marks)** — Award full marks only if all four aspects are tabulated or compared explicitly.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Why is a pilot hole drilled before boring? What is the recommended pilot hole diameter?

**Model Answer:**  
A pilot hole (smaller than the final bore) is essential before boring for the following reasons:

1. The boring bar has a **single cutting edge** and cannot start a hole from a solid surface (no central guidance).
2. The pilot hole provides **centring and guidance** for the boring bar, ensuring concentricity and straightness.
3. It reduces the **depth of cut per pass**, which lowers cutting force, tool deflection, and chatter, improving accuracy and surface finish.
4. It minimises the risk of **tool breakage**, especially with long boring bars.

**Recommended pilot hole diameter:** Generally **2 to 5 mm smaller** than the final bored diameter, depending on workpiece length, material, and boring bar rigidity. For a 50 mm bore in mild steel, a pilot of 45–48 mm is typical. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, PCIEL407 Module 2]**  
A through hole of 30 mm diameter and 40 mm depth is to be drilled in a mild steel workpiece (Grade EN8). The HSS twist drill has a point angle of 118°. The recommended cutting speed is 25 m/min and the feed is 0.25 mm/rev.

**(a)** Calculate the **spindle speed**, **feed rate**, **drilling time**, and **material removal rate (MRR)** for the operation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain with a neat sketch the **drilling machine setup**, the **drill geometry**, and the **chips formed**. List **four safety precautions** to be observed while operating a drilling machine. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q.A (a):**

**Given:**
- $D = 30$ mm
- $L_{actual} = 40$ mm
- $V = 25$ m/min
- $f = 0.25$ mm/rev
- Point angle = 118°

**Step 1 — Spindle speed N:**
$$N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V}{\pi \cdot D} = \dfrac{1000 \times 25}{\pi \times 30} = \dfrac{25000}{94.248} = 265.26 \text{ rpm}$$

Nearest machine speed: **N ≈ 265 rpm**. **(2 marks)**

**Step 2 — Feed rate:**
$$f_r = f \cdot N = 0.25 \times 265 = 66.25 \text{ mm/min} \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 3 — Drilling time:**
$$L_T = L_{actual} + 0.3 \, D = 40 + 0.3 \times 30 = 40 + 9 = 49 \text{ mm}$$
$$T = \dfrac{L_T}{f_r} = \dfrac{49}{66.25} = 0.7396 \text{ min} \approx 44.4 \text{ seconds} \quad \text{(2 marks)}$$

**Step 4 — Material Removal Rate:**
$$\text{MRR} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D^2}{4} \cdot f \cdot N = \dfrac{\pi \times 30^2}{4} \times 0.25 \times 265$$
$$= 706.86 \times 66.25 = 46824.5 \text{ mm}^3\text{/min} \approx 46.82 \text{ cm}^3\text{/min} \quad \text{(2 marks)}$$

**Summary of answers:**
- Spindle speed ≈ 265 rpm
- Feed rate ≈ 66.25 mm/min
- Drilling time ≈ 0.74 min (44 s)
- MRR ≈ 46824 mm³/min

---

**Model Solution to Q.A (b):**

**Drilling Machine Setup:** A pillar-type drilling machine consists of:
- **Base** mounted on the floor.
- **Column** rising vertically from the base.
- **Work table** (with T-slots) for clamping the workpiece.
- **Spindle head** housing the spindle, motor, and gear box.
- **Quill** that moves axially via the feed handle.
- **Drill chuck** holding the twist drill.
- **Coolant pump** with nozzle directed at the cutting zone.

**Drill Geometry** (refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.4):  
The twist drill has a **shank**, **flutes**, **land/margin**, **web**, **cutting lips**, **point angle (118°)**, and **chisel edge**. **(2 marks)**

**Chips Formed:**  
During drilling, the two cutting lips shear the metal, producing **continuous curly chips** (for ductile materials like mild steel) that travel up the flutes. The chip shape is typically **helical ribbon**; for brittle materials, chips are **discontinuous segments**. **(1 mark)**

**Four Safety Precautions:** **(2 marks)**
1. **Tie back loose hair and clothing**; remove rings and wristwatches.
2. **Clamp the workpiece securely** on the table before starting the machine.
3. **Never hold the workpiece by hand**; always use a vise or clamp.
4. **Stop the spindle** before attempting to clear chips or adjust the drill.
5. **Use safety goggles** to protect eyes from flying chips.
6. **Do not leave the chuck key** in the chuck after tightening.
7. **Apply cutting fluid** properly; do not let hands contact the rotating spindle.

(Any four well-explained = 2 marks; one-liners without explanation = ½ mark each.)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, PCIEL407 Module 2 (Internal Choice)]**  
Describe the operations of **reaming** and **tapping** as performed on a drilling machine.

**(a)** Explain the **reaming operation** with a neat sketch of the reamer. State the **reaming allowance** and list the parameters (speed, feed, fluid) used. Why must the reamer be backed out only **while rotating**? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **tapping operation** with a neat sketch of a tap. Derive the **tap drill diameter formula** and calculate the tap drill size for an **M16 × 2.0** thread. State the three types of taps used in sequence. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q.B (a):**

**Reaming Definition:** Reaming is a sizing and finishing operation that uses a multi-tooth tool called a **reamer** to produce a hole of precise diameter, high accuracy (IT7–IT8), and excellent surface finish (Ra 0.4–1.6 μm). The hole must be pre-drilled with a diameter slightly smaller (0.1–0.5 mm) than the reamer size. **(1 mark)**

**Sketch of Reamer (text representation):** A reamer has a **tapered lead** (entrance), a **chamfer** (primary cutting edge), **straight lands** (bearing), and a **back taper** (to reduce friction and prevent jamming). It has 6–16 flutes. The sketch should show: shank, neck, cutting end with flutes, and chamfer. **(1 mark)**

**Reaming Allowance:**  
$\delta = D_{final} - D_{drill}$ where $\delta$ is typically **0.1–0.5 mm** for general-purpose reaming, depending on hole diameter and material. For a 20 mm hole, allowance = 0.15–0.30 mm. **(1 mark)**

**Reaming Parameters:** **(1.5 marks)**
- **Speed:** N_re = ½ × N_drill (e.g., if drilling at 400 rpm, ream at 200 rpm). For HSS reamer on mild steel, V = 8–12 m/min.
- **Feed:** f_re = 2–3 × f_drill (typically 0.15–0.6 mm/rev).
- **Cutting fluid:** Soluble oil or sulphurised oil — generous flow to flush chips and prevent seizing.

**Why rotate while backing out?**  
If the reamer is stopped inside the hole, the chip load on the cutting edges becomes uneven, and any slight misalignment causes the reamer to **scrape** (not cut) the wall. This leads to:
- A bell-mouthed or oversized hole.
- Scored, rough surface finish.
- Possible tool seizure and breakage.

By **rotating while reversing**, the cutting edges continue to shear material uniformly, and the reamer releases cleanly from the hole. **(2.5 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q.B (b):**

**Tapping Definition:** Tapping is a thread-cutting operation that produces internal threads in a pre-drilled hole (tap drill hole) using a threaded multi-tooth tool called a **tap**. **(0.5 marks)**

**Sketch of Tap:** A tap has a **shank**, **square drive** (for the tap wrench), **threads** (the cutting part), **chamfered lead** (first 4–7 threads are tapered for gradual cutting), and **flutes** (straight or helical, for chip evacuation). **(1 mark)**

**Derivation of Tap Drill Diameter:**  
For ISO metric threads, the thread has a 60° profile and depth $h = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} P$. The tap drill must remove the bulk of the material but leave ~75% of the thread depth intact for the tap to cut the crests. A widely used **simplified formula** for KTU-level problems is:
$$D_{tap} = D_{major} - P \quad \text{(mm)} \qquad \text{(Derivation given in Section 3.4)}$$

This approximation works because the theoretical thread depth is $0.866 P$, and removing exactly $P$ leaves the right amount of material. **(2 marks)**

**Calculation for M16 × 2.0:**  
$D_{major} = 16$ mm, $P = 2.0$ mm.
$$D_{tap} = 16 - 2.0 = 14.0 \text{ mm}$$

So a **14 mm drill** must be used before tapping. **(0.5 mark)**

**Three Types of Taps Used in Sequence:** **(2 marks)**

1. **Taper Tap (No. 1 tap):** Has 7–10 chamfered threads; used first to start the thread gradually. Cuts about 50% of the full thread depth.
2. **Plug Tap (No. 2 tap):** Has 3–5 chamfered threads; used second to cut most of the remaining thread depth. Cuts about 75% of full thread.
3. **Bottoming Tap (No. 3 tap):** Has only 1–1.5 chamfered threads; used last to cut threads to the bottom of a blind hole. Cuts the full thread depth.

For **through holes**, the taper tap alone is usually sufficient. For **blind holes**, all three are used in sequence. The taps must be **rotated in reverse** (while still spinning) to back out without damaging the freshly cut threads.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the drill-point correction** (0.3 D) when calculating drilling time. This is the single most common error; it costs 1–2 marks per question.
> 2. **Unit mismatch**: Mixing mm and cm without conversion in MRR. Always express MRR in **mm³/min** for consistency, then convert if asked.
> 3. **In tapping, using $D_{tap} = D_{major} - P$ for non-metric threads** (e.g., BSW, BSF). The formula is for ISO metric only. For BSW: $D_{tap} = D_{major} - 1.28 P$ (different profile). Always verify the thread standard.
> 4. **Not mentioning cutting fluid** in reaming — examiners consider it a **mandatory** point; omitting it loses ½ mark.
> 5. **Stopping the reamer inside the hole** — a viva answer that fails to explain *why* this is dangerous scores low.
> 6. **Confusing countersink (82°/90°, conical) with counterbore (cylindrical, flat-bottomed)**. A counterbore is for socket-head cap screws; a countersink is for flat-head screws. KTU students frequently mix these up.
> 7. **Drawing a "neat sketch" without labels** — labelled sketches get full marks; unlabelled ones get 50% marks for that part.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — 5-Minute Summary Before Exam:**

- ☐ **Drilling** uses a **multi-point twist drill** to **originate** a hole; tolerance ±0.25 mm; Ra 3.2–6.3 μm.
- ☐ **Boring** uses a **single-point tool** to **enlarge** a hole; tolerance ±0.025 mm; Ra 0.8–3.2 μm. Requires a pilot hole.
- ☐ **Reaming** uses a **multi-tooth reamer** to **finish** a hole; tolerance IT7–IT8; Ra 0.4–1.6 μm. Always rotate while backing out.
- ☐ **Tapping** cuts **internal threads** using a **tap**; feed = pitch. Use **taper → plug → bottoming** sequence for blind holes.
- ☐ **Countersinking** creates a **conical seat** (82° or 90°) for flat-head screws; uses a piloted tool.
- ☐ **Cutting speed** V = πDN/1000 m/min; **Spindle speed** N = 1000V/(πD) rpm.
- ☐ **Feed rate** f_r = f × N mm/min; **Drilling time** T = (L + 0.3D) / (f·N) min.
- ☐ **MRR** = (πD²/4) × f × N mm³/min.
- ☐ **Tap drill diameter** (ISO metric) = D_major − P.
- ☐ **Drill point angle** = **118°** (general steel), **135°** (hardened/cast iron).
- ☐ **Reaming speed** = ½ drilling speed; **Reaming feed** = 2–3× drilling feed.
- ☐ **Tapping feed** = thread pitch (mandatory, automatic on CNC/manual via lead screw).
- ☐ **Pilot hole for boring** = 2–5 mm smaller than final diameter.
- ☐ **Reaming allowance** = 0.1–0.5 mm.
- ☐ Always **clamp the workpiece** — never hold it by hand.
- ☐ Always **apply cutting fluid** for HSS tools on steel.
- ☐ **Inspect** with vernier (0.02 mm), micrometer (0.01 mm), and bore gauge.
- ☐ Common errors to avoid: missing 0.3 D correction, unit mismatch, stopping reamer in hole, mixing countersink vs counterbore.
- ☐ KTU 2024 expects **calculations + process knowledge + safety** — all three are mandatory for full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Exercises on cylindrical grinding machine: Grinding of a plain cylindrical surface
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCIEL407 - MACHINE TOOLS LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Exercises on cylindrical grinding machine: Grinding of a plain cylindrical surface ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Cylindrical Grinding** is defined as a precision abrasive machining process in which the **external cylindrical surface** of a rotating workpiece is finished by the cutting action of a bonded abrasive grinding wheel, while the workpiece is held and rotated about its own axis between a work-head live center and a tailstock dead center (or in a chuck/collet). **Plain (or Straight) Cylindrical Grinding** refers to the simplest variant of external cylindrical grinding where: - The workpiece is ground along its **length (axial direction)** using a **traverse (oscillating) feed** of the grinding wheel or workpiece. - The grinding wheel is dressed to a **straight (non-profiled) face**, generating a constant-diameter cylindrical geometry. - The depth of cut is taken radially, with no plunge grinding used for primary material removal. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Students must be able to *demonstrate* the safe setup, operation, and measurement of a finished cylindrical component to a specified tolerance (typically ±0.01 mm) and surface finish (typically 0.4–1.6 µm Ra). ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Lathe-with-Sandpaper" Idea Imagine you took a **woodworker's lathe** (which spins wood and cuts it with a chisel) and replaced the sharp chisel with a **fast-spinning disc coated in industrial sandpaper**. That is essentially a cylindrical grinder. - The **workpiece** spins slowly (like a kebab on a rotisserie). - The **grinding wheel** spins very fast (like a circular saw at 30 m/s). - The wheel *creeps along* the length of the workpiece from left to right and back, nibbling off thousandths of a millimetre each pass. - A **constant stream of coolant** (water + oil emulsion) pours over the contact zone, washing away chips and preventing the spark-filled fire that would otherwise ignite the metal dust. | Feature | Lathe Turning | Cylindrical Grinding | |---|---|---| | Tool | Single-point cutting tool | Multi-point abrasive wheel | | Chip thickness | ~0.1 – 1.0 mm | ~0.001 – 0.05 mm (uneven) | | Surface finish | 1.6 – 6.3 µm Ra | 0.1 – 1.6 µm Ra | | Wheel speed | (workpiece) ~200 m/min | (wheel) 1500 – 3000 m/min | | Hardness limit | ~HRC 40 | Up to HRC 65+ | > [!TIP] > **Key Metric to Memorise:** Cylindrical grinding is chosen when the workpiece is **too hard** to be machined by turning (e.g., hardened bearing steel, tool steel after heat treatment) and when a **mirror-grade surface finish** is required. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Operating Principle — Step by Step 1. **Work Holding (Setup):** The cylindrical workpiece is mounted between a **live center** (in the work head, rotating with the headstock spindle) and a **dead center** (in the tailstock, stationary but supporting the load). Centers must be perfectly aligned; a *lathe dog* drives the rotation. 2. **Wheel Selection:** A vitrified-bonded **Aluminium Oxide (Al₂O₃)** wheel is used for high-tensile steels, and **Silicon Carbide (SiC)** for cast iron and non-ferrous alloys. Wheel hardness (grade A–Z) and grit size (e.g., 60, 80) are chosen based on material and finish required. 3. **Wheel Truing & Dressing:** - *Truing* removes wobble and restores concentricity using a diamond dresser. - *Dressing* sharpens the wheel by fracturing dull abrasive grains using a dressing stick. 4. **Engagement & Spark-Out:** The wheel is advanced radially by a fine pitch (0.005–0.025 mm per pass) until sparks appear. The wheel then makes 2–4 *spark-out* passes with no further in-feed, allowing elastic recovery of the system and improving roundness. 5. **Coolant Delivery:** A flood of coolant (~10–15 L/min) is aimed at the arc of contact, typically with a nozzle angled 10–15° against the wheel rotation. 6. **Measurement & Verification:** The diameter is checked with a **micrometer** (resolution 0.01 mm) or a **vernier caliper** (0.02 mm), and roundness is verified with a **dial test indicator (DTI)** set to 0.01 mm. ### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step - **Why live + dead centers?** A single point would bend under the radial cutting force; two supports provide rigidity. - **Why spark-out?** The machine–workpiece–wheel system has elastic deflection. Spark-out lets the system "settle" into final geometry. - **Why coolant?** Heat generated in grinding is ~60% of the total energy input (compared to ~10% in turning). Without coolant, the workpiece would burn (thermal damage) and the wheel would glaze (lose sharpness). - **Why a straight wheel face?** Because plain cylindrical grinding is intended for *constant-diameter* shafts (shafts, pins, journals), not for profiles. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > All formulas below are tested directly in KTU 2024 Scheme lab viva voce and end-semester theory. | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Wheel surface speed | $V_c = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000}$ | m/s | $D$ = wheel diameter (mm), $N$ = rps | | 2 | Workpiece surface speed | $V_w = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d \cdot n}{1000}$ | m/s | $d$ = work diameter (mm), $n$ = rps | | 3 | Equivalent contact diameter | $D_e = \dfrac{D \cdot d}{D - d}$ | mm | Used for contact-length calculations | | 4 | Geometric contact length | $l_g = \sqrt{D_e \cdot d_{infeed}}$ | mm | $d_{infeed}$ = depth per pass | | 5 | Material Removal Rate (traverse) | $Q = \pi \cdot d \cdot L \cdot f \cdot r$ | mm³/s | $L$ = traverse length, $f$ = feed (mm/rev), $r$ = radial infeed | | 6 | Material Removal Rate (plunge) | $Q = \pi \cdot d \cdot L \cdot V_f$ | mm³/s | $V_f$ = infeed rate (mm/s) | | 7 | Specific Energy | $U = \dfrac{P}{Q}$ | N·m/mm³ (J/mm³) | $P$ = net power (W) | | 8 | Tangential grinding force | $F_t = U \cdot Q$ | N | Equals $F_c$ in grinding | | 9 | Spindle power | $P = F_t \cdot V_c$ | W | $V_c$ in m/s | | 10 | Spark-out distance | $s = 2 \cdot k$ | mm | $k$ = system stiffness (mm/N) × $F_t$ | | 11 | Wheel wear ratio (G-ratio) | $G = \dfrac{V_{work}}{V_{wheel wear}}$ | – | Higher = better | | 12 | Infeed per pass (roughing) | $r_{rough} = 0.01$ – $0.025$ | mm | | | 13 | Infeed per pass (finishing) | $r_{finish} = 0.002$ – $0.005$ | mm | | | 14 | Number of spark-out passes | $n_{so} = 2$ – $4$ | – | Empirical | > [!NOTE] > **CRITICAL NOTATION GUARD:** In all formula tables, **never** write $\vert x \vert$ with vertical pipes — use $\lvert x \rvert$ to avoid breaking Markdown table syntax. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Cylindrical grinding is the **backbone of bearing manufacturing** (inner/outer raceways), **automotive crankshaft journals**, **hydraulic spools**, **tool shanks**, and **surgical implant shafts** (Ti-6Al-4V). Without cylindrical grinding, every rotating shaft in every machine on Earth would either seize or vibrate. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of Wheel Surface Speed ($V_c$) The grinding wheel rotates about its axis at a spindle speed $N$ (in revolutions per second, rps). A point on the periphery of the wheel traces out a circle of circumference $\pi D$ per revolution, where $D$ is the wheel diameter. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Linear distance per revolution} &= \pi \cdot D \quad \text{[mm/rev]} \\ \text{Linear distance per second} &= \pi \cdot D \cdot N \quad \text{[mm/s]} \\ \text{Convert mm to m (divide by 1000)}: \quad V_c &= \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000} \quad \text{[m/s]} \\ \end{aligned} $$ > **Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard):** A grinding wheel of diameter 200 mm rotates at 1500 rpm. Compute the wheel surface speed. > > - $N = 1500 \text{ rpm} = 1500/60 = 25 \text{ rps}$ > - $V_c = \dfrac{\pi \times 200 \times 25}{1000} = \dfrac{15708}{1000} = 15.71 \text{ m/s}$ This is the **standard range** for safe cylindrical grinding (15–35 m/s). --- ### 3.2 Derivation of Equivalent Contact Diameter ($D_e$) When a grinding wheel of diameter $D$ engages a cylindrical workpiece of diameter $d$, the **two cylinders deform** against each other elastically. The contact geometry is identical to a *gear-mesh-like* rolling pair. The classical Hertzian solution (and the **equivalent diameter** used in grinding theory by G. Werner) is: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Curvature of wheel} &= \kappa_1 = \dfrac{2}{D} \\ \text{Curvature of workpiece} &= \kappa_2 = \dfrac{2}{d} \\ \text{Combined curvature (sum)} &= \kappa_1 + \kappa_2 = \dfrac{2}{D} + \dfrac{2}{d} = \dfrac{2(D+d)}{D \cdot d} \\ \text{Equivalent diameter } D_e \text{ satisfies: } \dfrac{2}{D_e} &= \kappa_1 + \kappa_2 \\ \therefore D_e &= \dfrac{D \cdot d}{D + d} \quad \text{[mm]} \end{aligned} $$ > **Worked Numerical Example:** A 300 mm diameter wheel grinds a 50 mm diameter shaft. Find $D_e$. > > - $D_e = \dfrac{300 \times 50}{300 + 50} = \dfrac{15000}{350} = 42.86 \text{ mm}$ This equivalent diameter is used next to compute the **geometric contact length**. --- ### 3.3 Derivation of Geometric Contact Length ($l_g$) When the wheel is fed radially into the workpiece by a depth $d_{infeed}$ (per pass), the wheel–work contact zone forms a **chord-like arc** of length $l_g$. From the geometry of a chord of a circle of radius $R_e = D_e/2$, with sagitta $d_{infeed}$: $$ \begin{aligned} R_e - h &= \text{distance from chord to centre} \\ \text{Chord half-length}^2 &= R_e^2 - (R_e - d_{infeed})^2 \\ l_g^2 / 4 &= R_e^2 - (R_e^2 - 2R_e \cdot d_{infeed} + d_{infeed}^2) \\ l_g^2 / 4 &= 2R_e \cdot d_{infeed} - d_{infeed}^2 \\ \text{For small } d_{infeed}, d_{infeed}^2 \text{ is negligible, so:} \\ l_g^2 &\approx 4 R_e \cdot d_{infeed} \\ l_g &\approx 2\sqrt{R_e \cdot d_{infeed}} = \sqrt{2R_e \cdot 2 d_{infeed}} \\ \text{Substituting } R_e = D_e/2: \quad l_g &= \sqrt{D_e \cdot d_{infeed}} \quad \text{[mm]} \end{aligned} $$ > **Worked Numerical Example:** Using $D_e = 42.86$ mm and a roughing infeed of 0.02 mm: > - $l_g = \sqrt{42.86 \times 0.02} = \sqrt{0.8572} = 0.926 \text{ mm}$ This is the **arc of contact** — the length of the wheel face actively engaged in cutting at any instant. --- ### 3.4 Derivation of Material Removal Rate (MRR) in Traverse Grinding In traverse cylindrical grinding, the workpiece rotates and the wheel moves axially back-and-forth along the workpiece length $L$. Let: - $d$ = workpiece diameter (mm) - $n$ = workpiece spindle speed (rps) - $f$ = axial feed per workpiece revolution (mm/rev) - $r$ = radial infeed per pass (mm) The **volume removed per revolution of the workpiece** equals the cylindrical layer stripped off: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Circumference removed per rev} &= \pi \cdot d \quad \text{[mm]} \\ \text{Area of cross-section removed} &= f \times r \quad \text{[mm}^2\text{]} \\ \text{Volume removed per rev} &= \pi \cdot d \cdot f \cdot r \quad \text{[mm}^3\text{/rev]} \\ \text{Volume per second (MRR)} &= \pi \cdot d \cdot f \cdot r \cdot n \quad \text{[mm}^3\text{/s]} \\ \therefore Q &= \pi \cdot d \cdot n \cdot f \cdot r \end{aligned} $$ > **Worked Numerical Example:** A 60 mm diameter shaft is rotated at 200 rpm, with axial feed 5 mm/rev and radial infeed 0.01 mm. > - $n = 200/60 = 3.33$ rps > - $Q = \pi \times 60 \times 3.33 \times 5 \times 0.01 = 31.4$ mm³/s = **1884 mm³/min** --- ### 3.5 Derivation of Spindle Power Requirement The **specific energy** $U$ (J/mm³) is a tabulated property of the workpiece material–wheel combination. For hardened steel with Al₂O₃, typical $U \approx 20$ J/mm³. $$ \begin{aligned} F_t &= U \times Q \quad \text{[N]} \\ P &= F_t \times V_c \quad \text{[W]} \\ P &= U \times Q \times V_c \end{aligned} $$ > **Worked Numerical Example:** Using $Q = 31.4$ mm³/s, $U = 20$ J/mm³, and $V_c = 15.71$ m/s: > - $F_t = 20 \times 31.4 = 628$ N > - $P = 628 \times 15.71 = 9864$ W $\approx 9.86$ kW A 11 kW spindle would be the correct machine selection. --- ### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Process Parameter Cross-Reference | Operation | Infeed $r$ (mm) | Feed $f$ (mm/rev) | Work speed $V_w$ (m/min) | Wheel $V_c$ (m/s) | Final $R_a$ (µm) | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Roughing** | 0.015 – 0.025 | 5 – 10 | 15 – 30 | 25 – 30 | 1.6 | | **Semi-finish** | 0.005 – 0.010 | 2 – 5 | 10 – 20 | 28 – 32 | 0.8 | | **Finishing** | 0.002 – 0.005 | 0.5 – 2 | 5 – 15 | 30 – 35 | 0.4 | | **Spark-out only** | 0.000 | 0 (idle) | 5 | 30 | 0.1 – 0.2 | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Plain Cylindrical Grinder The diagram below shows the **major subsystems** of a cylindrical grinding machine and the energy/material flow between them. This is a *functional block* view rather than a physical layout — chosen because physical 2D drawings of machine tool beds are too complex for ASCII/Mermaid. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph PowerTrain["POWER TRAIN SUBSYSTEM"] MainMotor["MAIN MOTOR
7.5 to 15 kW"] BeltDrive["V-BELT DRIVE

Cast Iron, Hand Scraped"] WheelHead["WHEEL HEAD

Cylindrical Shaft"] Workpiece --> Tailstock WorkHead --> Bed Tailstock --> Bed WheelHead --> Bed Table --> Bed Table --> WheelHead DiamondDresser --> Wheel StickDresser --> Wheel Tank --> Pump --> Filter --> Nozzle Nozzle --> Workpiece Handwheel --> WheelHead AutoInfeed --> WheelHead DialGauge --> Workpiece ``` ### 4.2 Process-Flow Diagram — Plain Cylindrical Grinding Operation ```mermaid flowchart LR A["STEP 1

KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Set A]** *Define cylindrical grinding. Differentiate between traverse and plunge cylindrical grinding with neat sketches.* **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > **Definition (1 mark):** Cylindrical grinding is an abrasive machining process in which the external cylindrical surface of a workpiece is finished using a rotating grinding wheel while the workpiece rotates between centres. > > **Traverse Grinding (1 mark):** The grinding wheel is fed *axially* along the length of the workpiece in a reciprocating motion. Used for long shafts. Infeed is taken radially. Final surface is a series of fine helical grooves. > > **Plunge Grinding (1 mark):** The grinding wheel is fed *radially* into the workpiece without axial movement. The wheel face is profiled (e.g., concave for a journal). Used for short, rigid components and shoulder grinding. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Repeated Question]** *List the function of (i) work head live centre, (ii) tailstock dead centre, and (iii) coolant in cylindrical grinding.* **Model Answer:** > (i) **Live centre (1 mark):** Mounted in the work-head spindle, it *rotates with the workpiece*, providing a low-friction support that drives the part via a lathe dog. > > (ii) **Dead centre (1 mark):** Mounted in the tailstock, it is *stationary* (often tipped with carbide or HSS) and only supports the workpiece against radial cutting forces. Prevents workpiece deflection. > > (iii) **Coolant (1 mark):** (a) Carries away the **60% of input energy** converted to heat, preventing thermal damage to the workpiece. (b) Flushes away grinding chips (swarf). (c) Lubricates the contact zone, reducing wheel wear. (d) Prevents wheel loading/glazing. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) --- **QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Set B]** **(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a block diagram, explain the major subsystems of a plain cylindrical grinding machine. State the function of each. **(b) [7 Marks]** A cylindrical grinding operation is performed on a 75 mm diameter EN8 steel shaft. The grinding wheel has a diameter of 250 mm and rotates at 1800 rpm. The work spindle runs at 150 rpm with an axial feed of 4 mm/rev and a radial infeed of 0.012 mm per pass. Calculate: (i) Wheel surface speed, (ii) Workpiece surface speed, (iii) Material removal rate, and (iv) Spindle power assuming specific energy = 25 J/mm³. --- #### MODEL SOLUTION TO QUESTION A **Part (a) — Major Subsystems (7 marks)** | Subsystem | Function | Marks | |---|---|---| | **Power Train** | A main motor (7.5–15 kW) drives the wheel spindle (1500–3000 rpm) via V-belt and step pulleys, and the work spindle (50–300 rpm) via a separate gear train. | 1.5 | | **Mechanical Bed & Slides** | Hand-scraped cast-iron bed provides vibration damping. The wheel head carries the grinding wheel and has radial and traverse slides. The work head holds the live centre; the tailstock holds the dead centre. | 1.5 | | **Abrasive (Wheel + Dressers)** | Straight-face vitrified Al₂O₃ wheel (e.g., AA-60-J-V). Diamond dresser for truing; stick dresser for sharpening. | 1.5 | | **Coolant System** | Tank → Pump → Filter → Nozzle → directed at arc of contact. Flow ~15 L/min. Prevents thermal damage and chip welding. | 1.5 | | **Control & Measurement** | Micrometric handwheel (0.01 mm), optional hydraulic autofeed, dial test indicator for roundness check. | 1.0 | **Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)** **Given:** - $d = 75$ mm, $D = 250$ mm - $N_{wheel} = 1800$ rpm, $n_{work} = 150$ rpm - $f = 4$ mm/rev, $r = 0.012$ mm - $U = 25$ J/mm³ **Step 1: Wheel surface speed (2 marks)** $$ \begin{aligned} N_{wheel} &= \dfrac{1800}{60} = 30 \text{ rps} \\ V_c &= \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000} = \dfrac{\pi \times 250 \times 30}{1000} \\ V_c &= 23.56 \text{ m/s} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Workpiece surface speed (1 mark)** $$ \begin{aligned} n_{work} &= \dfrac{150}{60} = 2.5 \text{ rps} \\ V_w &= \dfrac{\pi \cdot d \cdot n}{1000} = \dfrac{\pi \times 75 \times 2.5}{1000} = 0.589 \text{ m/s} \\ V_w &= 35.34 \text{ m/min} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Material Removal Rate (2 marks)** $$ \begin{aligned} Q &= \pi \cdot d \cdot n \cdot f \cdot r \\ Q &= \pi \times 75 \times 2.5 \times 4 \times 0.012 \\ Q &= 28.27 \text{ mm}^3/\text{s} = 1696 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4: Spindle Power (2 marks)** $$ \begin{aligned} F_t &= U \times Q = 25 \times 28.27 = 706.86 \text{ N} \\ P &= F_t \times V_c = 706.86 \times 23.56 = 16{,}653 \text{ W} \\ P &\approx 16.65 \text{ kW} \end{aligned} $$ **Answer:** $V_c = 23.56$ m/s, $V_w = 0.589$ m/s, $Q = 28.27$ mm³/s, $P = 16.65$ kW. An 18.5 kW or 22 kW machine is the correct selection. --- #### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the procedure for *wheel truing* and *wheel dressing* in cylindrical grinding. Why are both operations necessary before grinding a hardened shaft? **(b) [7 Marks]** A grinding wheel of 200 mm diameter and 20 mm width grinds a 50 mm diameter mild-steel shaft at a work speed of 20 m/min and traverse feed of 2 mm/rev. The radial infeed per pass is 0.015 mm. Calculate: (i) Equivalent contact diameter, (ii) Geometric contact length, and (iii) Length of spark-out required if the system stiffness is 0.005 mm/N and tangential force is 400 N. --- #### MODEL SOLUTION TO QUESTION B **Part (a) — Truing and Dressing Procedure (7 marks)** 1. **Wheel Truing (3 marks):** A sharp single-point diamond dresser is mounted on a holder and traversed slowly across the rotating wheel face. The diamond fractures away the worn abrasive grains, exposing fresh sharp points and restoring the **geometric profile** (concentricity, flatness). Done with 0.01–0.025 mm infeed per pass and copious coolant. 2. **Wheel Dressing (2 marks):** A stick of soft abrasive (e.g., aluminium-oxide stick) is fed lightly against the rotating wheel to *break open* the dulled cutting edges and create **porosity** (chip clearance space). Dressed wheels cut freely; undressed wheels "glaze" and burn the workpiece. 3. **Why both are needed for hardened shafts (2 marks):** Hardened steel (≥ HRC 50) requires a **fresh, sharp, open-structured wheel** to avoid (a) excessive **grinding heat** (causes thermal cracks and metallurgical damage), and (b) **workpiece burn** (oxidation at the surface). Both operations restore the wheel to a state where it can remove material efficiently without overheating the part. **Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)** **Given:** - $D = 200$ mm, $d = 50$ mm - $V_w = 20$ m/min, $f = 2$ mm/rev, $r = 0.015$ mm - $k = 0.005$ mm/N, $F_t = 400$ N **Step 1: Equivalent contact diameter (2 marks)** $$ \begin{aligned} D_e &= \dfrac{D \cdot d}{D + d} = \dfrac{200 \times 50}{200 + 50} = \dfrac{10000}{250} \\ D_e &= 40 \text{ mm} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Geometric contact length (2 marks)** $$ \begin{aligned} l_g &= \sqrt{D_e \cdot d_{infeed}} = \sqrt{40 \times 0.015} \\ l_g &= \sqrt{0.6} = 0.7746 \text{ mm} \approx 0.775 \text{ mm} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Spark-out distance (3 marks)** $$ \begin{aligned} s &= 2 \cdot k \cdot F_t = 2 \times 0.005 \times 400 \\ s &= 4.0 \text{ mm} \end{aligned} $$ This means after the final infeed, the wheel should traverse at least **4 mm of spark-out** (typically 2–4 passes) for the workpiece to reach final roundness. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls** > 1. **Unit conversion trap (–1 to –2 marks):** Students often confuse $N$ in **rps** vs **rpm**. The formula $V_c = \pi D N$ requires $N$ in *rps* (i.e., revolutions per second). If you leave it in rpm, your speed will be **60× too high** — an instant 1–2 mark deduction. > 2. **Infeed vs depth of cut confusion (–1 mark):** The radial infeed $r$ is the *depth* of cut per pass, not the *axial feed*. These are two different parameters. > 3. **Forgetting to convert mm³/s to mm³/min (–0.5 mark):** Always state units in the final answer. > 4. **Skipping the spark-out step (–1 mark):** Many students omit spark-out in their procedural answer. Examiners specifically look for it in lab-viva questions. > 5. **Confusing truing with dressing (–2 marks):** *Truing* = restoring **geometry** (diamond tool). *Dressing* = restoring **sharpness** (stick). Mixing these up is a KTU-favourite trap. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Print This Box Before Your Lab Exam** - [ ] **Definition:** Cylindrical grinding = external cylindrical surface finished by abrasive wheel; workpiece rotates between centres. - [ ] **Plain cylindrical grinding** = straight wheel face + traverse feed; for uniform-diameter shafts. - [ ] **Live centre** rotates (in work head); **Dead centre** is stationary (in tailstock). - [ ] **Truing** (diamond) restores **geometry**; **Dressing** (stick) restores **sharpness**. - [ ] **Coolant flow** ~ 15 L/min, nozzle angled 10–15° against wheel rotation. - [ ] **Wheel speed** $V_c = \pi D N / 1000$ m/s (use rps). - [ ] **Work speed** $V_w = \pi d n / 1000$ m/s. - [ ] **Equivalent diameter** $D_e = D \cdot d / (D + d)$. - [ ] **Contact length** $l_g = \sqrt{D_e \cdot d_{infeed}}$. - [ ] **MRR (traverse)** $Q = \pi \cdot d \cdot n \cdot f \cdot r$ mm³/s. - [ ] **Spindle power** $P = U \cdot Q \cdot V_c$ Watts. - [ ] **Infeed:** Roughing 0.015–0.025 mm, Finishing 0.002–0.005 mm. - [ ] **Spark-out:** 2–4 passes, no infeed — improves roundness. - [ ] **Force ratio:** $F_t : F_n : F_a \approx 1 : 2.5 : 1.5$. - [ ] **Wheel selection:** Al₂O₃ for steel, SiC for cast iron/non-ferrous. - [ ] **Measurement tool:** Micrometer (±0.01 mm), DTI for roundness (0.01 mm). - [ ] **Safety:** Wear goggles, ensure coolant flow before starting, never grind without guarding. - [ ] **Typical Ra:** Cylindrical grinding achieves **0.1 – 1.6 µm** — far better than turning. - [ ] **KTU tolerance target:** ±0.01 mm on diameter; roundness < 0.005 mm. - [ ] **Always** convert $N$ from rpm → rps before substituting into $V_c$ formula.

topics.title: Exercises on surface grinding machine: Grinding of a flat surface
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIEL407 - MACHINE TOOLS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Exercises on surface grinding machine: Grinding of a flat surface

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Surface Grinding** is a precision abrasive machining process in which a rotating abrasive grinding wheel is used to remove material from the surface of a workpiece to produce a flat, smooth, and dimensionally accurate surface. The operation is performed on a **Surface Grinding Machine**, which is classified under the category of **Abrasive Machining Processes** (one of the eight fundamental material removal processes in manufacturing technology).

According to KTU 2024 Scheme (PCIEL407 – Machine Tools Lab) terminology, the experiment on *"Grinding of a flat surface"* involves performing a horizontal-spindle surface grinding operation on a mild steel / cast iron workpiece using a **reciprocating table** (or rotary table) setup to obtain a flat surface with specified surface finish (Ra value), dimensional tolerance, and parallelism.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Definition to Memorize:**
> "Surface grinding is an abrasive material removal process in which the cutting tool is an abrasive wheel consisting of randomly oriented, sharp, hard crystals bonded together, where material is removed in the form of microscopic chips by the abrasive grits on the wheel periphery."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are trying to level a rough wooden plank using sandpaper. You hold the sandpaper in one hand and rub it back-and-forth across the plank. The sand grains embedded in the paper scratch off tiny pieces of wood, slowly making the surface smooth and flat.

A **surface grinding machine works exactly like this**, but in a much more precise, mechanized, and powerful way:

- The **sandpaper** = the **grinding wheel** (thousands of abrasive grits bonded together)
- The **wooden plank** = the **workpiece** (mild steel, HSS, cast iron, etc.)
- **Your hand rubbing back-and-forth** = the **reciprocating table motion**
- The **downward push** = the **down-feed (infeed)** setting on the machine

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Surface Grinding is Special:** Unlike milling or turning where a single cutting edge removes chips, surface grinding uses **thousands of microscopic cutting edges (grits)** simultaneously, each removing a chip of size **typically 1–50 µm**. This is why surface grinding can achieve extremely fine surface finishes (Ra as low as **0.2 µm**) and very tight tolerances (±**0.005 mm**).

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Parameters (KTU Board Examination Highlights)

The following parameters are critical for the experiment and must be memorized:

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value (Flat Surface Grinding) | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Wheel diameter | D | 200 – 400 | mm |
| Wheel speed (peripheral) | V_c | 25 – 35 | m/s |
| Table speed | V_w | 5 – 25 | m/min |
| Infeed (depth of cut) | d | 0.005 – 0.05 | mm/pass |
| Spark-out passes | – | 2 – 4 | passes |
| Cross-feed per pass | f | 0.5 – 0.8 × wheel width | mm |
| Coolant | – | Soluble oil + water | – |
| Workpiece material | – | Mild steel / HSS | – |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The most commonly tested numerical in board exams is the calculation of **Material Removal Rate (MRR)** and **Grinding Ratio (G)** for a flat surface grinding operation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Working Principle – Step by Step

The surface grinding process can be broken down into a structured sequence:

- **Step 1 — Wheel Rotation (Primary Motion):** The abrasive grinding wheel rotates at high speed about its own axis on a spindle. The peripheral speed of the wheel is the **cutting speed (V_c)**. The grits on the wheel periphery act as tiny single-point cutting tools.
- **Step 2 — Table Motion (Feed Motion):** The workpiece is mounted on a magnetic chuck fixed to the machine table. The table reciprocates linearly under the wheel at a controlled **table speed (V_w)**. This is analogous to the feed in a shaping machine.
- **Step 3 — Infeed (Depth of Cut):** After each table pass, the wheel is lowered by a small amount called the **infeed (d)** in the vertical (Z) direction. This is the actual material removed per pass.
- **Step 4 — Cross-feed:** After completing one longitudinal pass and return stroke, the wheel is moved sideways by a small amount called the **cross-feed (f)**. This ensures fresh grits engage the workpiece on the next pass.
- **Step 5 — Spark-out:** In the final passes, the infeed is set to zero and the wheel continues to traverse over the workpiece. This burnishing action improves surface finish and dimensional accuracy.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Spark-out Matters:** During spark-out, the system reaches a steady-state where wheel wear, machine deflections, and workpiece elastic recovery are all balanced. This is what gives surface grinding its reputation for **mirror-like finishes** on flat surfaces.

## 2.2 Classification of Surface Grinding (KTU Board-Exam Favorite)

There are **four types** of surface grinding operations, classified based on the orientation of the wheel spindle:

1. **Horizontal Spindle with Reciprocating Table (most common in KTU lab)** — Wheel axis is horizontal, table moves linearly.
2. **Horizontal Spindle with Rotary Table** — Workpiece rotates under a horizontal wheel; used for large flat circular parts.
3. **Vertical Spindle with Reciprocating Table** — Wheel face grinds; cup wheel used.
4. **Vertical Spindle with Rotary Table** — Used for high-volume production of flat surfaces.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table summarizes **all** key equations required for solving KTU numerical problems on flat surface grinding:

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Wheel peripheral speed | $V_c = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{60}$ | m/s | D = wheel diameter (m), N = rpm |
| 2 | Table (workpiece) speed | $V_w = \dfrac{L \cdot (1 + n_s)}{T}$ | mm/min | L = table stroke length, n_s = number of spark-out strokes, T = time per stroke (min) |
| 3 | Material Removal Rate (MRR) | $\text{MRR} = V_w \cdot d \cdot w$ | mm³/min | w = width of grinding (mm) |
| 4 | Specific Material Removal Rate (per unit wheel width) | $\text{MRR}' = V_w \cdot d$ | mm²/min | Used for comparing operations |
| 5 | Metal Removal Parameter | $Z_w = \dfrac{V_w}{V_c} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{d}{D}}$ | dimensionless | Dimensionless chip thickness parameter |
| 6 | Equivalent Chip Thickness | $h_{eq} = \dfrac{V_w}{V_c} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{d}{D}}$ | µm | Same as Z_w; critical for surface finish |
| 7 | Surface Roughness (approx.) | $R_a \approx 0.5 \cdot h_{eq}^2 / r$ | µm | r = nose radius equivalent of grit |
| 8 | Grinding Ratio (G-ratio) | $G = \dfrac{\text{Volume of material removed}}{\text{Volume of wheel wear}}$ | dimensionless | Higher G = better wheel life |
| 9 | Average grit chip thickness (Malkin’s equation) | $h_m = \left[ \dfrac{4 \cdot V_w}{V_c \cdot C \cdot r} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{d}{D}} \right]^{1/2}$ | mm | C = active grit density (grits/mm²) |
| 10 | Power consumed (approx.) | $P = F_c \cdot V_c$ | W | F_c = tangential cutting force (N) |
| 11 | Specific Energy | $U = \dfrac{P}{\text{MRR}}$ | W·s/mm³ (J/mm³) | For steel grinding ≈ 20–60 J/mm³ |
| 12 | Spindle power (approx.) | $P_{sp} = U \cdot V_w \cdot d \cdot w$ | W | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU Pitfall:** In the G-ratio formula, *both* volumes **must be in the same units** (typically mm³). Many students lose 2–3 marks in board exams by mixing mm³ with cm³.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Tool and Die Making:** Surface grinding is the final finishing operation for dies, molds, jigs, and fixtures where flatness within **0.005 mm over 300 mm** is required.
- **Automotive Industry:** Flat surfaces on brake rotors, engine blocks, and cylinder heads.
- **Aerospace:** Flat reference surfaces on turbine blade fixtures and structural components.
- **Precision Slides and Guideways:** Surface-ground ways on machine tools (e.g., lathe beds, milling tables).
- **Bearing Manufacturing:** Raceway surfaces on flat bearing components.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Derivation of Wheel Peripheral Speed

The grinding wheel rotates about its own axis. If the wheel diameter is **D** and rotational speed is **N** (in revolutions per minute, rpm), then the linear distance traveled by a point on the wheel periphery per revolution is the circumference **π·D**.

**Derivation:**

Let:
- D = wheel diameter (in metres)
- N = rotational speed (rpm) = N revolutions per minute
- 1 revolution = **π·D** linear distance
- N revolutions = **N · π · D** linear distance per minute

To convert from per minute to per second, divide by 60:

$$
V_c = \frac{N \cdot \pi \cdot D}{60} \quad \text{(m/s)}
$$

**Equivalently in m/min:**

$$
V_c = N \cdot \pi \cdot D \quad \text{(m/min)}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Multiplication by π·D accounts for the circular path, and division by 60 converts minutes to seconds because the standard unit of cutting speed in grinding is **m/s** (not m/min as in turning/milling).

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Wheel Peripheral Speed (KTU Board Pattern, 3 Marks)

**Problem:** A surface grinding wheel has a diameter of **250 mm** and runs at **1440 rpm**. Calculate the peripheral (cutting) speed of the wheel in **m/s** and verify whether it is suitable for grinding mild steel.

**Given:**
- D = 250 mm = 0.25 m
- N = 1440 rpm

**Solution:**

Convert diameter to metres: D = 0.25 m.

Apply the formula:

$$
V_c = \frac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{60}
$$

Substitute values:

$$
V_c = \frac{\pi \cdot 0.25 \cdot 1440}{60}
$$

$$
V_c = \frac{3.14159 \cdot 0.25 \cdot 1440}{60}
$$

$$
V_c = \frac{3.14159 \cdot 360}{60}
$$

$$
V_c = \frac{1130.97}{60}
$$

$$
V_c = 18.85 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Verification:** The recommended peripheral speed for mild steel surface grinding with Al₂O₃ wheel is **25–35 m/s**. Hence **18.85 m/s is too low** — the operator should either increase spindle speed or use a larger wheel.

**Required spindle speed for V_c = 30 m/s:**

$$
N = \frac{V_c \cdot 60}{\pi \cdot D} = \frac{30 \cdot 60}{\pi \cdot 0.25} = 2292.83 \text{ rpm}
$$

**Answer:** V_c = 18.85 m/s; recommended N ≈ 2293 rpm for V_c = 30 m/s.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Material Removal Rate (KTU Board Pattern, 7 Marks)

**Problem:** In a surface grinding operation on a flat mild steel workpiece, the following data is given:
- Table speed: V_w = 12 m/min
- Infeed (depth of cut): d = 0.02 mm/pass
- Width of grinding (cross-feed per stroke): w = 25 mm
- Workpiece length = 200 mm
- Number of spark-out passes: n_s = 2

Calculate: (a) MRR per pass, (b) MRR per minute, (c) specific MRR, (d) time per pass.

**Given:**
- V_w = 12 m/min = 12000 mm/min
- d = 0.02 mm
- w = 25 mm
- L = 200 mm
- n_s = 2

**Solution (a) — MRR per pass (Volume removed in one pass):**

$$
\text{MRR}_{\text{pass}} = L \cdot d \cdot w
$$

$$
\text{MRR}_{\text{pass}} = 200 \cdot 0.02 \cdot 25 = 100 \text{ mm}^3
$$

**Solution (b) — MRR per minute:**

Table traverses 12000 mm of stroke per minute, with workpiece length 200 mm, so number of effective passes per minute:

$$
n_p = \frac{V_w}{L} = \frac{12000}{200} = 60 \text{ passes/min}
$$

$$
\text{MRR} = n_p \cdot \text{MRR}_{\text{pass}} = 60 \cdot 100 = 6000 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Table speed V_w is linear distance per minute. Dividing by workpiece length L gives the number of complete passes per minute. Multiplying by the volume removed per pass gives the volumetric removal rate.

**Alternative — Direct Formula:**

$$
\text{MRR} = V_w \cdot d \cdot w
$$

$$
\text{MRR} = 12000 \cdot 0.02 \cdot 25 = 6000 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}
$$

**Solution (c) — Specific MRR (per mm width):**

$$
\text{MRR}' = \frac{\text{MRR}}{w} = \frac{6000}{25} = 240 \text{ mm}^2/\text{min}
$$

Or directly:

$$
\text{MRR}' = V_w \cdot d = 12000 \cdot 0.02 = 240 \text{ mm}^2/\text{min}
$$

**Solution (d) — Time per pass:**

$$
t_{\text{pass}} = \frac{L}{V_w} = \frac{200}{12000} = 0.01667 \text{ min} = 1 \text{ second}
$$

> **Note on Spark-out:** Spark-out passes do **not** contribute to MRR (d = 0 during spark-out). They only burnish the surface. Therefore, the time per pass remains the same, but no material is removed during the last **n_s = 2** passes.

**Answer:** MRR = 6000 mm³/min; Specific MRR = 240 mm²/min; t_pass = 1 s.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Grinding Ratio (G-ratio) and Wheel Wear (KTU Board Pattern, 7 Marks)

**Problem:** A flat surface grinding operation is performed on a hardened steel workpiece using a wheel of diameter **200 mm** rotating at **3000 rpm**. After the operation, the following data is recorded:
- Volume of material removed = **30,000 mm³**
- Radial wheel wear = **0.05 mm**
- Wheel width = **25 mm**

Calculate the **Grinding Ratio (G)**.

**Given:**
- V_material = 30,000 mm³
- Δr = 0.05 mm (radial wear)
- b = 25 mm (wheel width)

**Solution:**

Volume of wheel wear = (cylindrical shell volume)

$$
V_{\text{wheel}} = \pi \cdot D \cdot \Delta r \cdot b
$$

Wait — for **radial** wear, the wheel is reduced uniformly. The worn-out cylindrical shell has:
- Mean circumference × radial thickness × width
- Mean diameter after wear ≈ D (since Δr << D), so:

$$
V_{\text{wheel}} = \pi \cdot D \cdot \Delta r \cdot b
$$

Substitute:

$$
V_{\text{wheel}} = \pi \cdot 200 \cdot 0.05 \cdot 25
$$

$$
V_{\text{wheel}} = 3.14159 \cdot 200 \cdot 0.05 \cdot 25
$$

$$
V_{\text{wheel}} = 3.14159 \cdot 250 = 785.40 \text{ mm}^3
$$

Grinding Ratio:

$$
G = \frac{V_{\text{material}}}{V_{\text{wheel}}} = \frac{30000}{785.40} = 38.20
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The wheel wear volume is the thin cylindrical shell that has been "consumed." Mean diameter stays approximately D because radial wear Δr is small compared to D.

**Answer:** Grinding Ratio G = 38.2.

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A G-ratio of 38 is typical for grinding hardened steel with Al₂O₃ wheel. A higher G-ratio (e.g., 100+) is preferred and indicates a good wheel-workpiece combination with adequate coolant supply.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example 4 — Average Chip Thickness (Malkin's Equation, 7 Marks)

**Problem:** In a horizontal spindle surface grinding operation:
- Wheel diameter D = 300 mm
- Wheel speed V_c = 30 m/s
- Table speed V_w = 0.005 m/s
- Depth of cut d = 0.02 mm
- Active grit density C = 4 grits/mm²
- Average grit nose radius r = 0.05 mm

Calculate the **average grit chip thickness (h_m)** using Malkin's equation.

**Given:**
- D = 0.3 m
- V_c = 30 m/s, V_w = 0.005 m/s
- d = 0.02 mm, C = 4 grits/mm², r = 0.05 mm

**Solution:**

$$
h_m = \left[ \frac{4 \cdot V_w}{V_c \cdot C \cdot r} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{d}{D}} \right]^{1/2}
$$

All lengths in mm for consistency:
- V_c = 30 m/s = 30,000 mm/s
- V_w = 0.005 m/s = 5 mm/s
- d = 0.02 mm
- D = 300 mm
- r = 0.05 mm

Compute each term:

$$
\frac{4 \cdot V_w}{V_c \cdot C \cdot r} = \frac{4 \cdot 5}{30000 \cdot 4 \cdot 0.05} = \frac{20}{6000} = 0.00333
$$

$$
\sqrt{\frac{d}{D}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.02}{300}} = \sqrt{6.667 \times 10^{-5}} = 0.008165
$$

$$
\text{Product} = 0.00333 \cdot 0.008165 = 2.719 \times 10^{-5}
$$

$$
h_m = \sqrt{2.719 \times 10^{-5}} = 5.215 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mm} = 5.215 \text{ µm}
$$

**Answer:** Average chip thickness h_m ≈ 5.22 µm.

> **Engineering Insight:** Chips in surface grinding are **microscopic** (typically 1–50 µm) — a thousand times smaller than chips in turning or milling. This is why grinding produces superior surface finish and tighter tolerances, but also generates much higher specific energy and heat.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Functional Architecture of a Surface Grinding Machine

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the functional subsystems of a horizontal-spindle reciprocating-table surface grinding machine, showing how the operator inputs command the kinematic axes.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Operator Control Panel] --> B[Wheel Head Spindle Drive]
    A --> C[Hydraulic / Servo Table Drive]
    A --> D[Vertical Infeed Handwheel]
    A --> E[Cross Feed Handwheel]
    A --> F[Coolant Pump System]
    A --> G[Magnetic Chuck Controller]

    B --> B1[AC Motor 1.5 to 5 kW]
    B1 --> B2[Spindle Bearings]
    B2 --> B3[Grinding Wheel 200 to 400 mm dia]

    C --> C1[Hydraulic Cylinder / Servo Motor]
    C1 --> C2[Reciprocating Table]
    C2 --> C3[Workpiece on Magnetic Chuck]

    D --> D1[Ball Screw Vertical]
    D1 --> D2[Wheel Head Z Axis Infeed]

    E --> E1[Ball Screw Cross]
    E1 --> E2[Wheel Head X Axis Cross Feed]

    F --> F1[Sump with Soluble Oil Emulsion]
    F1 --> F2[Nozzle Directed at Grinding Zone]

    G --> G1[DC Supply to Chuck Coils]
    G1 --> G2[Magnetic Holding Force on Workpiece]

    B3 --> H[Grinding Zone Contact]
    C3 --> H
    F2 --> H
    H --> I[Flat Smooth Workpiece Surface]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **Operator Control Panel** is the central command interface.
- The **Wheel Head Spindle Drive** provides the primary cutting motion (rotation).
- The **Table Drive** provides the feed motion (reciprocation).
- The **Vertical Infeed** sets depth of cut per pass.
- The **Cross Feed** advances the wheel sideways after each pass.
- The **Coolant System** delivers cutting fluid to the grinding zone.
- The **Magnetic Chuck** holds ferromagnetic workpieces securely.

## 4.2 Process Flow Chart — Experimental Procedure for Flat Surface Grinding

```mermaid
graph TD
    P1[Step 1 Study Drawing and Identify Workpiece Material] --> P2[Step 2 Select Grinding Wheel Al2O3 for Steel]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3 Check Wheel for Cracks Tap Test]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4 Mount Wheel and Dress with Diamond Tool]
    P4 --> P5[Step 5 Clean Magnetic Chuck Surface]
    P5 --> P6[Step 6 Place Workpiece and Energize Magnetic Chuck]
    P6 --> P7[Step 7 Set Table Speed Vw and Stroke Length]
    P7 --> P8[Step 8 Set Infeed d and Cross Feed f]
    P8 --> P9[Step 9 Start Coolant Flow]
    P9 --> P10[Step 10 Start Spindle Motor]
    P10 --> P11[Step 11 Run Table and Engage Infeed]
    P11 --> P12[Step 12 Rough Grind to within 0.05 mm of Final Size]
    P12 --> P13[Step 13 Reduce Infeed to 0.005 mm for Finishing]
    P13 --> P14[Step 14 Perform 2 to 4 Spark Out Passes]
    P14 --> P15[Step 15 Retract Wheel and Stop Table]
    P15 --> P16[Step 16 De energize Chuck and Remove Workpiece]
    P16 --> P17[Step 17 Measure Dimensions and Surface Roughness]
    P17 --> P18[Step 18 Compare with Specified Tolerance and Ra Value]
```

## 4.3 Kinematic Axes Nomenclature (KTU Convention)

```mermaid
graph LR
    X[Cross Feed Direction X Axis] -->|Lateral| WH[Wheel Head]
    Y[Longitudinal Table Feed Y Axis] -->|Reciprocation| T[Table]
    Z[Vertical Infeed Z Axis] -->|Down Feed| WH
    WH --> GZ[Grinding Zone]
    T --> GZ
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Convention:** In the KTU 2024 scheme lab manual, the axes are denoted as **X (cross-feed)**, **Y (longitudinal table feed)**, and **Z (vertical infeed)**, following the ISO 841 standard for machine tool axis identification.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]
**Define surface grinding. List any four operations performed on a surface grinding machine.**

**Model Answer:**

Surface grinding is an abrasive machining process in which a rotating abrasive wheel is used to produce a flat, smooth, and accurate surface on a workpiece. The cutting tool is a grinding wheel with numerous hard abrasive grits bonded together; material is removed as microscopic chips by these grits.

**Four operations performed on a surface grinding machine:**
1. **Grinding flat surfaces** (horizontal spindle, reciprocating table).
2. **Grinding flat surfaces using the side of the wheel** (peripheral surface grinding).
3. **Slot grinding** (using a thin wheel).
4. **Form grinding** (using a contoured wheel).

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Retest]
**What is "spark-out" in surface grinding? Why is it performed?**

**Model Answer:**

**Spark-out** refers to the final few passes of the grinding wheel over the workpiece in which the vertical infeed is set to **zero** and the wheel is allowed to traverse freely over the surface. No further material removal takes place; the action is purely **burnishing**.

**Purpose of spark-out:**
1. Improves **surface finish** (reduces Ra value) by eliminating micro-irregularities.
2. Improves **dimensional accuracy** by allowing the system to reach steady-state after elastic recovery.
3. Compensates for **machine deflection** and **workpiece spring-back**.
4. Reduces **residual stresses** in the surface layer.

Typically **2 to 4 spark-out passes** are recommended in KTU lab practice.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Main Paper, Module 2, Q4]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of a horizontal spindle reciprocating table surface grinding machine. List its main components.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A surface grinding wheel of diameter **300 mm** rotates at **1800 rpm** while grinding a flat surface on a mild steel workpiece. The table speed is **10 m/min**, depth of cut is **0.025 mm/pass**, and width of cut is **30 mm**. Calculate:
1. Wheel peripheral speed in m/s
2. Material Removal Rate (MRR) in mm³/min
3. Specific MRR in mm²/min

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A (a):

A horizontal-spindle reciprocating-table surface grinding machine consists of the following main components:

| # | Component | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Base** | Rigid cast iron bed supporting all subassemblies |
| 2 | **Column** | Vertical support carrying the wheel head |
| 3 | **Wheel head** | Houses spindle, bearings, and grinding wheel |
| 4 | **Spindle** | Rotates the grinding wheel at high speed |
| 5 | **Grinding wheel** | Abrasive cutting tool |
| 6 | **Table** | Reciprocates carrying the workpiece |
| 7 | **Magnetic chuck** | Holds the workpiece |
| 8 | **Coolant system** | Supplies cutting fluid |
| 9 | **Hydraulic / servo unit** | Drives the table |
| 10 | **Cross-feed handwheel** | Moves wheel sideways (X-axis) |
| 11 | **Vertical infeed handwheel** | Moves wheel down (Z-axis) |

**Working:** The grinding wheel rotates about a horizontal axis at high speed. The workpiece mounted on the magnetic chuck reciprocates under the wheel at a controlled table speed. After each pass, the wheel is lowered by a small infeed. The cross-feed moves the wheel sideways to ensure fresh grits engage the workpiece. Coolant is sprayed at the grinding zone to reduce heat and flush away chips.

#### Model Solution to Q-A (b):

**Given:**
- D = 300 mm = 0.3 m
- N = 1800 rpm
- V_w = 10 m/min = 10,000 mm/min
- d = 0.025 mm
- w = 30 mm

**(1) Wheel peripheral speed:**

$$
V_c = \frac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{60} = \frac{\pi \cdot 0.3 \cdot 1800}{60} = 28.27 \text{ m/s}
$$

**(2) Material Removal Rate:**

$$
\text{MRR} = V_w \cdot d \cdot w = 10000 \cdot 0.025 \cdot 30 = 7500 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}
$$

**(3) Specific MRR:**

$$
\text{MRR}' = V_w \cdot d = 10000 \cdot 0.025 = 250 \text{ mm}^2/\text{min}
$$

**Answer:** V_c = 28.27 m/s; MRR = 7500 mm³/min; Specific MRR = 250 mm²/min.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Retest Paper, Module 2, Q4]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define the following terms related to surface grinding:
1. Grinding ratio (G-ratio)
2. Specific material removal rate
3. Infeed
4. Cross-feed
5. Dressing
6. Truing
7. Active grits

**(b) [7 Marks]** In a surface grinding operation, **20,000 mm³** of material is removed while the wheel undergoes **0.04 mm** of radial wear. The wheel diameter is **250 mm** and width is **20 mm**. Calculate the **Grinding Ratio**. Comment on the wheel performance.

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B (a):

1. **Grinding ratio (G-ratio):** The ratio of the volume of material removed from the workpiece to the volume of wheel material worn away. Higher G-ratio = better wheel performance.
2. **Specific material removal rate:** The MRR per unit width of grinding, expressed in mm²/min. Useful for comparing different grinding operations.
3. **Infeed:** The downward vertical movement of the grinding wheel per pass, equivalent to the depth of cut in surface grinding.
4. **Cross-feed:** The lateral movement of the wheel after each longitudinal table pass; usually set between 0.5 and 0.8 times the wheel width.
5. **Dressing:** The process of exposing fresh sharp abrasive grits on the wheel surface using a dressing tool (e.g., diamond dresser).
6. **Truing:** The process of restoring the geometric profile (roundness) of the grinding wheel using a diamond tool.
7. **Active grits:** Those grits on the wheel surface which are sharp, have proper clearance angle, and are actively engaged in cutting chips (as opposed to rubbing or ploughing grits).

#### Model Solution to Q-B (b):

**Given:**
- V_material = 20,000 mm³
- Δr = 0.04 mm (radial wear)
- D = 250 mm
- b = 20 mm (wheel width)

**Volume of wheel wear (cylindrical shell):**

$$
V_{\text{wheel}} = \pi \cdot D \cdot \Delta r \cdot b
$$

$$
V_{\text{wheel}} = \pi \cdot 250 \cdot 0.04 \cdot 20 = \pi \cdot 200 = 628.32 \text{ mm}^3
$$

**Grinding Ratio:**

$$
G = \frac{V_{\text{material}}}{V_{\text{wheel}}} = \frac{20000}{628.32} = 31.83
$$

**Comment on performance:** A G-ratio of ~32 indicates **moderate wheel performance** for steel grinding. Typical values:
- 10–30 → poor wheel condition or wrong grade
- 30–60 → acceptable
- 60–120 → good
- >120 → excellent

For improved performance, the operator should consider: better coolant supply, dressing at regular intervals, lighter infeed, and verifying the wheel specification (grit size, grade, bond) for the given workpiece material.

**Answer:** G = 31.83 (moderate performance).

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 Board Exams:**
>
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency in V_c formula:** Converting D from mm to m but **forgetting to divide N by 60** (or vice-versa). The result is off by a factor of 60. **Always check units at the end.**
>
> 2. **Mixing mm³ with cm³ in G-ratio:** Both volumes must be in the same unit. If volume removed is 30 cm³ and wheel wear is 0.5 cm³, do **not** convert one and not the other.
>
> 3. **Forgetting spark-out passes in MRR calculation:** During spark-out, d = 0; hence **no material is removed**. Students who add spark-out passes to MRR over-estimate by 20–40%.
>
> 4. **Confusing V_w (table feed) with V_c (wheel speed):** The wheel speed V_c is the **cutting speed** and is typically 25–35 m/s. The table speed V_w is the **feed** and is typically 5–25 m/min. Students sometimes swap them.
>
> 5. **Ignoring coolant in answers:** KTU examiners deduct 0.5 to 1 mark if the role of coolant (heat removal, chip flushing, lubrication) is not mentioned in descriptive answers.
>
> 6. **Writing "grinding wheel is made of sand"** in definitions — use correct terms: **abrasive grits (Al₂O₃, SiC, CBN, diamond)** + **bond (vitrified, resin, metal)**.
>
> 7. **Not dressing the wheel** before the experiment — examiners verify this in the lab record. Always mention "wheel was dressed using a single-point diamond dresser" in the procedure.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ☐ **Definition:** Surface grinding = abrasive machining using a rotating wheel to produce flat surfaces.
- ☐ **Wheel peripheral speed formula:** $V_c = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60}$ (m/s, with D in metres).
- ☐ **MRR formula:** $\text{MRR} = V_w \cdot d \cdot w$ (mm³/min if all dimensions in mm and V_w in mm/min).
- ☐ **G-ratio:** $G = \dfrac{V_{\text{material removed}}}{V_{\text{wheel worn}}}$ — dimensionless, must use same units.
- ☐ **Wheel wear volume:** $V_{\text{wheel}} = \pi \cdot D \cdot \Delta r \cdot b$ (cylindrical shell model).
- ☐ **Average chip thickness (Malkin):** $h_m = \left[\dfrac{4 V_w}{V_c C r} \sqrt{\dfrac{d}{D}}\right]^{1/2}$.
- ☐ **Four types:** Horizontal/Vertical spindle × Reciprocating/Rotary table.
- ☐ **Most common in KTU lab:** Horizontal spindle + reciprocating table.
- ☐ **Spark-out:** Final 2–4 passes with d = 0; improves finish and accuracy.
- ☐ **Cross-feed:** 0.5 to 0.8 × wheel width.
- ☐ **Coolant:** Soluble oil emulsion; critical for thermal damage prevention.
- ☐ **Dressing vs. Truing:** Dressing exposes fresh grits; Truing restores geometry. Both done with a diamond tool.
- ☐ **Magnetic chuck** used for ferromagnetic workpieces (mild steel); non-magnetic parts need mechanical clamping.
- ☐ **Typical tolerances achievable:** ±0.005 mm flatness; Ra 0.2–0.8 µm surface finish.
- ☐ **Typical V_c:** 25–35 m/s for mild steel with Al₂O₃ wheel; 15–25 m/s for hardened steel with CBN.
- ☐ **G-ratio benchmark:** 30–60 acceptable; >100 excellent.
- ☐ **Specific energy for grinding:** 20–60 J/mm³ (much higher than turning or milling).
- ☐ **Always mention** wheel balancing, dressing, coolant, and magnetic chuck in KTU lab viva answers.

---

> **End of Module Experiment 2 — Grinding of a Flat Surface**
> *PCIEL407 – Machine Tools Lab | KTU 2024 Scheme*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Exercises on tool and cutter grinding machine: Grinding of a single-point cutting tool
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIEL407 - MACHINE TOOLS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Exercises on tool and cutter grinding machine: Grinding of a single-point cutting tool

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Tool and Cutter Grinder (TCG)** is a specialized precision grinding machine used for sharpening, re-sharpening, and forming the geometry of various cutting tools, including single-point cutting tools (lathe tools), milling cutters, drill bits, reamers, hobs, and form tools. In the context of this experiment, the focus is on **grinding a single-point cutting tool** — typically a High-Speed Steel (HSS) or Carbide-tipped lathe tool bit — to precise cutting edge geometry comprising the **rake angle (γ), clearance angle (α), cutting edge angle, and nose radius**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Reference:** *Single-point cutting tool grinding on a TCG involves the controlled abrasive removal of material from a tool blank using a formed or dressed grinding wheel, while the tool blank is indexed and clamped on a universal workhead, to generate the principal clearance, side clearance, and rake surfaces needed for efficient chip formation during turning.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of the tool and cutter grinder as a **"sculptor's chisel for cutting tools"**. Just as a barber uses a fixed-position scissor-sharpening jig to put a precise edge on a blade (rather than grinding freehand), the TCG uses a **universal workhead that pivots in two planes (swivels + tilts)** to hold the tool bit at exact, calculated angles. The grinding wheel acts like a stationary sandpaper disc, and by tilting/rotating the tool relative to the wheel, the student "carves" the geometry into the steel.

Imagine trying to sharpen a pencil by hand — the result is uneven and inconsistent. Now imagine clamping the pencil in a small vise that you can tilt at 15°, 30°, etc. against a spinning abrasive disc. That vise is the **universal workhead**, and the angle settings on its graduated rings are what give the tool its precise geometry.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constant / Standard Metric Reference:** Grinding wheels are specified by **bond type (V - vitrified, R - resin, B - metal)**, **abrasive type (A - aluminium oxide for HSS, C - silicon carbide for carbide, diamond for carbide finishing)**, **grit size (typically 46–60 for roughing, 80–120 for finishing)**, and **hardness grade (A–Z)**. Standard spindle speed for tool grinders is **~2800–3500 RPM**.

### 1.3 Salient Features of a Tool and Cutter Grinder

| Feature | Purpose |
|---|---|
| **Universal Workhead** | Holds the tool blank; swivels 360° horizontally and tilts vertically |
| **Grinding Wheel Spindle** | Carries the abrasive wheel; may swivel for taper grinding |
| **Wheel Slide (Cross-slide)** | Allows X-Y movement of the wheel relative to the tool |
| **Tapered Workhead Spindle** | Accepts collets (1 mm – 25 mm) for cylindrical tool shanks |
| **Coolant Tray & Pump** | Flood coolant to prevent thermal damage to HSS tools |
| **Tool Rest / Indexing Fixture** | For setting multi-point tools (drills, mills) |
| **Eye Shield & Spark Guard** | Mandatory safety features |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU PCIEL407 expects the student to **demonstrate the procedure in the lab viva**, identify machine parts by name, **list the standard tool angles** for turning mild steel, and **grind a tool freehand following the index marks on the workhead**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Geometry of a Single-Point Cutting Tool (Turning Tool)

A single-point turning tool is essentially a wedge with specific angles ground on its front face, side face, and end face. The standard nomenclature (as per **ISO 3685 / ASA B94.5**) is:

- **Rake Angle (γ – gamma):** Angle between the rake face (top) and a plane perpendicular to the workpiece surface. Can be **positive (sharp), negative (strong), or zero**.
- **Clearance Angle (α – alpha):** Angle between the clearance (flank) face and the workpiece surface — prevents rubbing of the tool flank.
- **Side Clearance Angle (α₁):** Side flank clearance.
- **End Clearance Angle (α₂):** End flank clearance.
- **Cutting Edge Angle (ψ – psi):** Angle between the major cutting edge and the workpiece surface.
- **Nose Radius (r):** Small radius at the junction of major and minor cutting edges (typically **0.4 mm – 1.6 mm**).
- **Side Rake Angle (γₛ)** and **Back Rake Angle (γᵦ)** when measured on the tool shank.
- **Approach Angle / Side Cutting Edge Angle:** Determines tool strength.

### 2.2 Standard Tool Angles for Turning Mild Steel (HSS Tool)

| Angle | Typical Value | Range | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Back Rake (γᵦ)** | **+8°** | +5° to +15° | Reduces cutting force, improves chip flow |
| **Side Rake (γₛ)** | **+14°** | +10° to +20° | Reduces chip thickness force |
| **End Clearance (α)** | **6°** | 5° to 8° | Prevents flank rubbing |
| **Side Clearance (α₁)** | **6°** | 5° to 8° | Prevents side flank rubbing |
| **Cutting Edge Angle (ψ)** | **75°** | 60° to 90° | Strengthens cutting edge |
| **Nose Radius (r)** | **0.8 mm** | 0.4 to 1.6 mm | Improves surface finish |
| **Plan Approach Angle** | **15°** | 10° to 30° | |

### 2.3 Classification of Grinding Wheels (Marking System)

A grinding wheel is specified as: **A 60 L 5 V 23** where:

- **A** = Abrasive type (Aluminium Oxide for HSS, Silicon Carbide for carbide)
- **60** = Grit size (coarse 16–36, medium 46–60, fine 80–120, very fine 150+)
- **L** = Hardness grade (Soft A–H, Medium I–P, Hard R–Z)
- **5** = Structure (1–8, dense to open)
- **V** = Bond type (V = Vitrified, R = Resinoid, B = Resinoid + metal)
- **23** = Manufacturer's code

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Recommended Wheel for HSS Single-Point Tool Grinding:** **Al₂O₃ (A) wheel, Grit 60, Grade K (medium-hard), Vitrified bond (V)** — designated typically as **A 60 K 5 V**.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Relation | Symbol Meaning | Application |
|---|---|---|
| $\tan\gamma = \frac{h}{b}$ | h = height of tool point, b = base length (2D geometry) | Quick sketch of rake angle |
| $V_w = \pi D N$ | Surface wheel speed (m/s), D = wheel dia, N = spindle RPM | Selecting wheel RPM |
| $V_f = f \times N$ | Feed speed (mm/min), f = feed/rev, N = spindle RPM of workpiece (for turning) | Theoretical link |
| $\phi = 90° - \psi$ | $\phi$ = approach angle, $\psi$ = cutting edge angle | Quick angle conversion |
| $R_a \approx \frac{f^{2}}{8r}$ | Surface finish estimate, f = feed, r = nose radius | Choice of nose radius |
| $T_c = \frac{f \times d}{N}$ | Cutting time, d = depth, N = rpm | Cycle time |
| $\alpha_{\text{total}} = \alpha_1 + \alpha_2 - 90°$ | Combined clearance check | Tool geometry check |

> **Note on table notation:** Where absolute value notation would be needed, use $\vert x \vert$ rendered as LaTeX. The angle marks (°) use the unicode degree symbol, not the vertical bar.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The principles mastered in this experiment are foundational for:
1. **CNC turning tooling:** Modern CNC inserts use negative rake angles (e.g., CNMG 120408) requiring the operator to know geometry to choose holders.
2. **Tool and Cutter Maintenance in Industry:** Every workshop has a tool-crib grinder to resharpen tools; the geometry must be restored to OEM specs.
3. **CAD/CAM Tool Path Generation:** CAM software requires the precise entry of side rake, back rake, and clearance to generate correct tool paths.
4. **Non-Traditional Tool Materials:** When grinding carbide tools, **diamond wheels with green silicon carbide** are used, never plain Al₂O₃, because Al₂O₃ chemically reacts with carbide at high temperatures.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation: Relationship Between Rake Angle and Tool Geometry

Consider the 2D side view of a single-point tool held in the workhead. The **rake face** is inclined at angle γ to the horizontal (reference plane perpendicular to the cutting velocity).

Let:
- $L$ = length of the rake face (slant length)
- $h$ = vertical rise of the rake face over its length
- $b$ = horizontal projection of the rake face

From basic trigonometry:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sin\gamma &= \frac{h}{L} \\[4pt]
\cos\gamma &= \frac{b}{L} \\[4pt]
\tan\gamma &= \frac{h}{b}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step-by-step logical conversion:**
1. The rake face is a straight inclined line of length L.
2. Drop a perpendicular from the upper end to the base — we get a right triangle.
3. Opposite side = h, Adjacent side = b, Hypotenuse = L.
4. By SOH-CAH-TOA, sin γ = opposite/hypotenuse = h/L.
5. Therefore h = L sin γ.
6. For a given L, doubling γ doubles h non-linearly.

### 3.2 Derivation: Optimal Surface Wheel Speed

The peripheral (surface) speed of the grinding wheel must lie in a recommended range to avoid either burning the tool (too fast) or rapid wheel wear (too slow).

Given:
- Standard wheel diameter on TCG: D = **200 mm = 0.2 m**
- Recommended surface speed for Al₂O₃ on HSS: Vₛ = **25–30 m/s**

Formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_s &= \pi D N \\[4pt]
N &= \frac{V_s}{\pi D} \\[4pt]
N &= \frac{30}{\pi \times 0.2} \\[4pt]
N &= \frac{30}{0.6283} \\[4pt]
N &\approx 47.75 \text{ rev/s} \\[4pt]
N &\approx 2865 \text{ RPM}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step-by-step logical conversion:**
1. Start with the surface speed formula $V_s = \pi D N$.
2. Solve for N: $N = V_s / (\pi D)$.
3. Substitute Vₛ = 30 m/s and D = 0.2 m.
4. Compute denominator: $\pi \times 0.2 \approx 0.6283$ m.
5. Divide: 30 / 0.6283 = 47.75 rev/s.
6. Convert to RPM: multiply by 60 → 2865 RPM.

This matches the typical nameplate speed of tool grinders (**2800–3000 RPM**).

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Effect of Each Angle on Cutting Performance

| Parameter Increased | Effect on Cutting Force | Effect on Tool Life | Effect on Surface Finish | Typical Trade-off |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Rake angle (γ) ↑ | ↓ Decreases | ↓ Decreases (chipping risk) | ↑ Improves (lower forces) | Higher γ = weaker edge |
| Clearance angle (α) ↑ | ↑ Increases slightly | ↓ Decreases (weakens) | ↑ Improves | Too low α → rubbing → overheating |
| Cutting edge angle (ψ) ↑ | ↑ Increases | ↑ Increases | ↑ Improves (smaller approach) | Strength vs. chatter |
| Nose radius (r) ↑ | ↑ Slight increase | ↑ Increases | ↑↑ Strongly improves | Too large → chatter at low feed |
| End clearance (α₂) ↑ | Neutral | ↓ Decreases | Neutral | Avoid edge chipping |

### 3.4 Procedure Step Matrix (KTU Lab Manual Sequence)

| Step | Action | Critical Setting |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Mount tool blank in workhead collet | Protrusion ~25–30 mm |
| 2 | Set workhead swivel for side rake | γₛ = 14° on graduated scale |
| 3 | Set workhead tilt for back rake | γᵦ = 8° |
| 4 | Dress the grinding wheel | Use diamond dresser to expose fresh abrasive |
| 5 | Start coolant flow | Adjust nozzle to flood contact zone |
| 6 | Rough-grind flank (clearance) face | Use end of wheel; light passes |
| 7 | Grind rake face | Tilt tool to required angle |
| 8 | Form nose radius | Small swivel / hand-feed pivot motion |
| 9 | Dress off burrs with fine hand stone | Deburr carefully |
| 10 | Inspect with protractor / optical projector | Verify α, γ within ±1° |

### 3.5 Numerical Example: Calculating Side Rake from Shank Geometry

Suppose a square HSS tool bit (10 mm × 10 mm × 100 mm) is to be ground such that the side rake face starts **1.5 mm** below the top edge over a **6 mm** horizontal run. Compute the side rake angle.

**Given:** Opposite h = 1.5 mm, Adjacent b = 6 mm.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tan\gamma_s &= \frac{h}{b} = \frac{1.5}{6} = 0.25 \\[4pt]
\gamma_s &= \arctan(0.25) \\[4pt]
\gamma_s &= 14.04° \\[4pt]
\gamma_s &\approx 14° \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification:** This matches the standard side rake of 14° for mild-steel turning.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Tool and Cutter Grinder — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PowerPack["Power Pack 3-Phase Induction Motor 1.5 to 2 HP 2800 RPM"] --> WheelSpindle["Wheel Spindle Assembly V-Belt Drive"]
    WheelSpindle --> GrindingWheel["Grinding Wheel Al2O3 A60KV5"]
    PowerPack --> WorkheadSpindle["Workhead Spindle Collet Holder"]
    WorkheadSpindle --> UniversalChuck["Universal Swivel Chuck 360 deg Horizontal"]
    UniversalChuck --> VerticalTilt["Vertical Tilt Plate Graduated 0 to 45 deg"]
```
