```mermaid
    VerticalTilt --> ToolBlank["HSS Tool Blank Single Point"]
    CoolantPump["Coolant Pump Reservoir"] --> Nozzle["Adjustable Nozzle"]
    Nozzle --> ContactZone["Wheel Tool Contact Zone"]
    ToolBlank --> ContactZone
    GrindingWheel --> ContactZone
    CrossSlide["Wheel Cross Slide Hand Wheel"] --> WheelSpindle
    ContactZone --> Swarf["Metal Chips and Coolant Swarf"]
    Swarf --> Tray["Coolant Collection Tray"]
    EyeShield["Eye Shield and Spark Guard"] --> OperatorSafety["Operator Safety Zone"]
    OperatorSafety --> Handwheel2["Hand Feed Wheel Fine Feed"]
    Handwheel2 --> CrossSlide
```

### 4.2 Tool Geometry (Nomenclature) — Block Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ToolBit["Single Point Cutting Tool Geometry"]
        direction TB
        RakeFace["Rake Face Inclination gamma"] --> CuttingEdge["Cutting Edge Line psi"]
        CuttingEdge --> NoseRadius["Nose Radius r 0.4 to 1.6 mm"]
        NoseRadius --> MinorEdge["Minor Cutting Edge"]
        Clearance["Clearance Flank alpha 6 deg"] --> CuttingEdge
        SideClear["Side Clearance alpha1 6 deg"] --> CuttingEdge
    end
    Workpiece["Workpiece Surface Horizontal Reference"] --> Clearance
    Workpiece --> SideClear
    RakeFace --> Chip["Chip Flow Direction"]
    CuttingEdge --> Chip
```

### 4.3 Process Flow: Grinding a Single-Point Tool

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Step 1 Mount Tool Blank in Collet"] --> B["Step 2 Set Side Rake Angle 14 deg"]
    B --> C["Step 3 Set Back Rake Angle 8 deg"]
    C --> D["Step 4 Set Cutting Edge Angle 75 deg"]
    D --> E["Step 5 Set End Clearance 6 deg"]
    E --> F["Step 6 Dress Grinding Wheel"]
    F --> G["Step 7 Start Coolant Flow"]
    G --> H["Step 8 Rough Grind Rake Face"]
    H --> I["Step 9 Grind Clearance Faces"]
    I --> J["Step 10 Form Nose Radius"]
    J --> K["Step 11 Fine Grind and Hone Edge"]
    K --> L["Step 12 Inspect with Protractor"]
    L --> M{"Angles within +/- 1 deg?"}
    M -- Yes --> N["Step 13 Mark Tool and Store"]
    M -- No --> H
```

### 4.4 Tool Angle Cross-Section — ASCII Schematic

```
                         Rake Face (γ_s = 14°)
                       /
                      /  Cutting
        ___________ /   Edge
       /           /
      /           /______________ Workpiece Surface
     / α₁ = 6°  /α₂ = 6°
    /__________/___________________________
   Tool Shank (HSS, square 10 mm x 10 mm)
   
   Cutting Edge Angle ψ = 75°
   Nose Radius r = 0.8 mm (at intersection)
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**  
List any **six essential parts** of a tool and cutter grinder and state the function of each.

**Model Answer (6 key parts):**

1. **Base / Bed:** Rigid cast-iron platform supporting all sub-assemblies; provides vibration damping.
2. **Column / Wheel Head:** Vertical column carrying the grinding wheel spindle; allows vertical slide for height adjustment.
3. **Wheel Spindle & Motor:** Carries and rotates the grinding wheel at 2800–3500 RPM via V-belt drive.
4. **Universal Workhead:** Swivels 360° horizontally and tilts vertically; holds tool blank via collet.
5. **Cross-slide / Wheel Slide Table:** Allows X-Y positioning of the wheel relative to the tool.
6. **Coolant System:** Pump, nozzle, and tray supplying cutting fluid to prevent thermal damage to HSS.
7. **Eye Shield / Spark Guard:** Plexiglass barrier protecting operator from sparks and abrasive particles.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**  
What is the **rake angle** of a single-point cutting tool? Why is it given a **positive value** when turning mild steel with HSS tools?

**Model Answer:**

The **rake angle (γ)** is the angle between the rake (top) face of the tool and a reference plane perpendicular to the workpiece surface. It governs the shear angle during chip formation.

For mild steel with HSS tools, a **positive rake angle** (typically +8° to +14°) is provided because:
- It **reduces the cutting force** by shearing the chip over a smaller shear area.
- It **lowers cutting temperature**, prolonging tool life.
- It **improves chip flow** and reduces power consumption.
- Mild steel is a **continuous-chip, ductile material** that can withstand higher rake without edge-chipping.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the **procedure for grinding a single-point cutting tool** on a tool and cutter grinder, mentioning the standard tool angles for turning mild steel. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A grinding wheel of **200 mm diameter** is used at a recommended peripheral speed of **30 m/s**. Calculate the spindle speed in RPM. Also suggest a suitable wheel specification for grinding HSS tool bits. **(7 marks)**

---

##### Model Solution to Q-A (a)

**Standard tool angles for turning mild steel with HSS:**

| Angle | Value |
|---|---|
| Side Rake (γₛ) | +14° |
| Back Rake (γᵦ) | +8° |
| End Clearance (α₂) | 6° |
| Side Clearance (α₁) | 6° |
| Cutting Edge Angle (ψ) | 75° |
| Plan Approach Angle | 15° |
| Nose Radius (r) | 0.8 mm |

**Step-by-step grinding procedure:**

1. **Inspection of blank:** Check the HSS tool blank (e.g., 10 × 10 × 100 mm) for cracks or warpage.
2. **Mounting in workhead:** Insert shank into the collet; protrude **25–30 mm** for adequate clearance.
3. **Set the workhead for side rake:** Swivel horizontal scale to **14°**; lock with clamp.
4. **Set back rake:** Tilt vertical scale to **8°**; lock.
5. **Dress the grinding wheel:** Use a diamond dresser mounted on the tool rest to expose fresh abrasive grains and true the wheel face.
6. **Start coolant flow:** Direct nozzle to flood the wheel-tool contact zone.
7. **Rough grind the rake face:** Take light passes (0.05–0.1 mm depth) until the rake surface is flat and within 0.05 mm of the line mark.
8. **Grind clearance faces:** With the tool set at 6° end clearance and 6° side clearance, grind these flanks lightly.
9. **Form the cutting edge angle:** Swivel the workhead to **ψ = 75°** and grind the major flank.
10. **Form nose radius:** Using fine hand-feed pivot motion, blend the corner to a smooth radius of **0.8 mm**.
11. **Hone the cutting edge:** Lightly stone the edge with an Arkansas slip stone to remove grinding burrs.
12. **Inspect angles:** Use an optical projector or tool-makers' microscope to verify angles within **±1°** of specification.
13. **Mark and store:** Stamp identification on the tool shank.

(Refer to ASCII schematic in Section 4.4 for visual support.)

---

##### Model Solution to Q-A (b)

**Given:** D = 200 mm = 0.2 m; Vₛ = 30 m/s.

**Find:** Spindle speed N.

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_s &= \pi D N \\[4pt]
N &= \frac{V_s}{\pi D} \\[4pt]
N &= \frac{30}{\pi \times 0.2} \\[4pt]
N &= \frac{30}{0.6283} \\[4pt]
N &= 47.75 \text{ rev/s} \\[4pt]
N &= 47.75 \times 60 \\[4pt]
N &= 2865 \text{ RPM} \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

**Recommended Wheel Specification for HSS Tool Grinding:**

| Wheel Parameter | Recommended Value |
|---|---|
| Abrasive | **Aluminium Oxide (A)** |
| Grit Size | **60 (medium)** for rough; **80–100** for finishing |
| Grade | **K (medium-hard)** |
| Structure | **5 (medium-open)** |
| Bond | **Vitrified (V)** |
| Designation | **A 60 K 5 V** |

**Justification:**
- **Al₂O₃** is the correct abrasive for HSS (not silicon carbide, which is for carbide).
- **Grit 60** balances material removal rate and finish.
- **Grade K** prevents rapid wear while resisting loading.
- **Vitrified bond** is rigid, porous, and heat-resistant.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**(a)** Describe the **ASA / ISO nomenclature** of a single-point cutting tool. Sketch the tool geometry showing at least **six angles** and the nose radius. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **classification and marking system of grinding wheels** with an example. Justify the choice of **wheel type and grit** for grinding HSS single-point tools. **(7 marks)**

---

##### Model Solution to Q-B (a)

The **ASA / ISO nomenclature** (per ISO 3685) defines a single-point cutting tool using a 7-element system:

1. **Tool shank** (rectangular, square, or round section)
2. **Back Rake Angle (γᵦ)** — inclination of the rake face in the vertical plane parallel to the longitudinal axis.
3. **Side Rake Angle (γₛ)** — inclination in the horizontal plane.
4. **End Clearance Angle (α₂)** — between the end flank and a plane perpendicular to the end cutting edge.
5. **Side Clearance Angle (α₁)** — between the side flank and a plane perpendicular to the side cutting edge.
6. **Cutting Edge Angle (ψ)** — angle between the major cutting edge and the workpiece surface.
7. **Nose Radius (r)** — radius at the junction of major and minor cutting edges.

(Refer to Tool Geometry Block Schematic in Section 4.2.)

**Six angles + nose radius for turning mild steel:**

| Element | Symbol | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| Back Rake | γᵦ | +8° |
| Side Rake | γₛ | +14° |
| End Clearance | α₂ | 6° |
| Side Clearance | α₁ | 6° |
| Cutting Edge Angle | ψ | 75° |
| Plan Approach Angle | θ | 15° |
| Nose Radius | r | 0.8 mm |

---

##### Model Solution to Q-B (b)

**Grinding Wheel Marking System (ASA / ISO 525):**

A standard grinding wheel specification has six fields:

```
A 60 K 5 V 23
│  │  │ │ │  │
│  │  │ │ │  └─ Manufacturer's code
│  │  │ │ └──── Bond type (V = Vitrified)
│  │  │ └────── Structure number (1 dense → 8 open)
│  │  └──────── Grade / Hardness (A soft → Z hard)
│  └───────────── Grit size (mesh number)
└──────────────── Abrasive type (A, C, etc.)
```

**Example:** **A 60 K 5 V** — Aluminium Oxide, Grit 60, Grade K, Structure 5, Vitrified bond.

**Justification for HSS Single-Point Tool Grinding:**

| Parameter | Choice | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Abrasive | **Al₂O₃ (A)** | Chemically inert with HSS; sharp friable grains; produces low grinding temperature. **Silicon Carbide (C) is for carbide** — it chemically attacks HSS causing rapid wear. |
| Grit Size | **46–60** (rough), **80–100** (finish) | 60 gives a good balance of MRR and finish for tool grinding. |
| Grade | **J–L (medium)** | Provides adequate grain retention without glazing or loading. |
| Bond | **Vitrified (V)** | Rigid, porous, heat-resistant — ideal for tool-room grinding where form retention is critical. |
| Structure | **5 (medium)** | Adequate chip clearance for the soft HSS swarf. |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark-Loss Areas:**
> 1. **Confusing α (clearance) and γ (rake):** α is on the flank below the cutting edge; γ is on the top face above the cutting edge. Mixing them loses 2 marks.
> 2. **Wrong wheel for HSS:** Many students write "Silicon Carbide" for HSS — this is WRONG (SiC is for carbide). Aluminium Oxide is correct for HSS.
> 3. **Forgetting to convert D to metres** when computing wheel speed — results in a 1000× error in N.
> 4. **Not mentioning coolant:** Grinding HSS dry causes thermal cracks (grinding burn) — examiners deduct marks for omitting coolant flow.
> 5. **Sketch without dimensioning angles:** A freehand sketch without labelled angles and rake/clearance faces is considered incomplete and loses 3+ marks in the 7-mark sub-question.
> 6. **Spelling "Aluminium" as "Aluminum":** Either is internationally acceptable, but in KTU valuation, follow British spelling (Aluminium) used in the syllabus.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Tool & Cutter Grinder (TCG)** is a precision universal grinder for sharpening all cutting tools.
- ✅ **Single-point tool grinding** requires setting the workhead at graduated angles: **γₛ = 14°, γᵦ = 8°, α₁ = α₂ = 6°, ψ = 75°, r = 0.8 mm** for mild steel with HSS.
- ✅ **Recommended Wheel for HSS:** **Al₂O₃ (A), Grit 60, Grade K, Vitrified (V)** — designation **A 60 K 5 V**.
- ✅ **Recommended Wheel Speed:** **25–30 m/s** surface speed → for a 200 mm wheel, this gives ~2800–2900 RPM.
- ✅ **Coolant is mandatory** when grinding HSS to prevent thermal cracking and metallurgical damage.
- ✅ **Wheel dressing** with a diamond dresser must precede every grinding session to expose fresh abrasive.
- ✅ **Inspection of angles** is done using an optical projector or tool-makers' microscope with **±1° tolerance**.
- ✅ **Nose radius** improves surface finish (Ra ∝ f²/8r) but too large causes chatter at low feeds.
- ✅ **Positive rake** reduces cutting force and temperature for ductile materials like mild steel.
- ✅ **Negative rake** is used for hard, brittle materials (cast iron) and for carbide tools with strong edge requirements.
- ✅ **Always deburr** the cutting edge with a fine slip stone (Arkansas or similar) after grinding.
- ✅ **Mark and store** the tool properly to avoid damage to the freshly ground cutting edge.

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Mantra for KTU Viva:** *"Set the angles: 14-8-6-6-75-0.8. Use an A60KV wheel. Cool it, dress it, grind it, inspect it."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Exercises on milling machine: Plane and pocket milling operations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIEL407 - MACHINE TOOLS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Exercises on milling machine: Plane and pocket milling operations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Milling

**Milling** is a fundamental material-removal machining process in which rotary multipoint cutters (having multiple cutting teeth or inserts) are used to progressively remove material from a stationary workpiece. The chips generated during cutting are small, discrete, and their cross-section, thickness, and shape vary continuously with cutter rotation and feed motion.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** syllabus, milling is classified as a *geometrically defined*, *multi-point*, *non-continuous cutting* process where the engagement of each tooth with the workpiece begins and ends once per revolution. The process is used to produce flat (planar) surfaces, slots, pockets, contours, gear teeth, and complex 3-D profiles.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The phrase *Exercises on milling machine* in the lab manual refers to **hands-on machine operation**, **parameter selection (speed/feed)**, **work-holding**, **tool setting**, and **measurement of machined dimensions** — not just theory.

## 1.2 Plane Milling Operation

**Plane (or Plain/Slab) Milling** is the milling operation used to produce a **flat horizontal surface** parallel to the axis of rotation of the cutter. The cutter axis is oriented **perpendicular** to the machined surface, and a peripheral cutting (plain milling) cutter travels across the workpiece width.

- **Typical cutter used:** Plain (slab) milling cutter or side-and-face milling cutter.
- **Workpiece motion:** Linear feed under a rotating multi-tooth cutter.
- **Geometry produced:** Planar surface, typically used for facing the top of a rectangular block.

## 1.3 Pocket Milling Operation

**Pocket Milling** is an extended milling operation used to machine an **enclosed cavity (pocket)** of any shape — square, rectangular, circular, or irregular — inside a workpiece. The cutter plunges (or ramps) into the material, then traces the pocket boundary while clearing material layer-by-layer using **step-over** and **step-down** strategies.

- **Typical cutter used:** End mill (HSS or carbide, 2-flute, 3-flute, or 4-flute).
- **Workpiece motion:** 2-D contouring (X-Y) plus a vertical (Z) descent.
- **Geometry produced:** An open or closed internal cavity with a flat floor and vertical side walls.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this experiment matters in KTU lab:** Pocket milling introduces students to **multi-axis tool path planning**, **climb vs. conventional milling decisions**, and **chip-load management** — concepts that are assessed in the university lab examination viva and the continuous evaluation record.

## 1.4 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of milling like **carving a sculpture with a spinning chisel**:

- Imagine you are holding a **round pizza cutter** (the milling cutter) and pushing it across a slab of cold butter (the workpiece).
- When you push the pizza cutter in a **straight line**, you are essentially doing **plane milling** — flattening a surface.
- Now, if you want to hollow out the centre of the butter to hold sauce (like a bowl inside a block of wood), you must move the cutter in a **rectangular spiral** while gradually lowering it — that is **pocket milling**.

The key insight: the **cutter spins** (cutting action) and the **workpiece (or table) moves** underneath it (feed motion). The two motions combine to remove material in controlled swaths.

## 1.5 Key Terminology Table

| Term | Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **Cutting speed (V)** | Surface speed of the cutter at the cutting diameter | m/min |
| **Spindle speed (N)** | Rotational speed of the cutter | rpm |
| **Feed per tooth (f_z)** | Linear advance of the workpiece per tooth engagement | mm/tooth |
| **Feed rate (V_f)** | Linear feed velocity of the table | mm/min |
| **Number of teeth (Z)** | Total cutting edges on the cutter | — |
| **Depth of cut (ap, ae)** | Radial (step-over) and axial (step-down) engagement | mm |
| **Material Removal Rate (MRR)** | Volume of material removed per unit time | mm³/min |
| **Machining time (t_m)** | Total time to complete one pass or full operation | min |

> [!NOTE]
> In **KTU lab viva**, students are frequently asked to *define cutting speed and differentiate it from feed rate*. Memorise the units **m/min** for V and **mm/min** for V_f.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle of a Milling Machine

A milling machine converts **rotary motion** (from an electric motor via a gear-head or spindle) into a **controlled linear feed motion** (of the table or knee). The fundamental steps for any milling exercise are:

1. **Power transmission** — Motor → Gear train → Spindle (carries the cutter).
2. **Cutter rotation** — The spindle rotates the multi-tooth cutter at a calculated RPM.
3. **Workpiece holding** — The blank is clamped on the machine table using **vises, clamps, T-bolts, or step blocks** inside a fixture.
4. **Feed motion** — The table (X, Y) and knee (Z) are translated by **manual handwheels** or **automatic feeds**.
5. **Chip formation** — Each protruding tooth engages the workpiece, shears a chip, and retracts.
6. **Indexing / repetition** — Multiple teeth pass in succession, removing chips of gradually varying thickness.

> [!TIP]
> In a milling machine, only the **cutter rotates with a defined peripheral speed**. The **workpiece is stationary** on the table (unlike a lathe where the workpiece rotates). This is a critical viva distinction.

## 2.2 Up-Milling (Conventional) vs. Down-Milling (Climb)

| Parameter | Up-Milling (Conventional) | Down-Milling (Climb) |
|---|---|---|
| **Feed direction** | Table feeds *against* cutter rotation at the contact zone | Table feeds *with* cutter rotation at the contact zone |
| **Chip thickness** | Starts at **0**, increases to maximum | Starts at **maximum**, decreases to **0** |
| **Cutter load** | Tooth is "rubbed" first — pushes workpiece up | Tooth is "sheared" first — pulls workpiece down |
| **Surface finish** | Slightly poorer | Better |
| **Tool wear** | Higher (rubbing phase) | Lower |
| **Holding force needed** | High (cutter tries to lift workpiece) | Low (cutter tries to pull workpiece into fixture) |
| **Backlash issue** | Tolerates backlash | **Cannot tolerate backlash** — use CNC or pre-loaded machine |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Practical Note:** For most lab plane-milling exercises on a horizontal milling machine, **up-milling (conventional)** is preferred because manual machines have mechanical backlash in the leadscrew-nut assembly. For **pocket milling with an end mill** on a vertical milling machine, **climb milling** is generally recommended for better finish.

## 2.3 Types of Cutters Used in These Exercises

### A. For Plane Milling

1. **Plain (Slab) Milling Cutter** — Cylindrical body with teeth on its periphery only. Mounted on an arbor. Used for horizontal surface generation.
2. **Side-and-Face Milling Cutter** — Teeth on periphery *and* sides. Used when both the top and one side need to be machined simultaneously.
3. **Shell End Mill / Facing Cutter** — Mounted directly on the spindle nose; used in vertical mills for facing.

### B. For Pocket Milling

1. **End Mill (2-flute)** — Best for aluminium and non-ferrous slotting.
2. **End Mill (4-flute)** — Standard choice for steels; higher productivity.
3. **Bull-nose End Mill** — Leaves a small fillet at the corner; stronger corner than a square end mill.
4. **Insert-type end mill** — Carbide inserts (APMT, etc.) for high MRR in CNC environments.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table lists the **core formulas a KTU student must memorise** for this experiment's record, viva, and end-semester evaluation.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Unit | Boundary / Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Cutting speed** | $V = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000}$ | m/min | D in mm, N in rpm |
| 2 | **Spindle speed** | $N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V}{\pi \cdot D}$ | rpm | Round to nearest available spindle speed |
| 3 | **Feed rate (table)** | $V_f = f_z \cdot Z \cdot N$ | mm/min | $f_z$ = feed/tooth, $Z$ = no. of teeth |
| 4 | **Feed per revolution** | $f = f_z \cdot Z$ | mm/rev | Useful for face/peripheral milling |
| 5 | **Feed per tooth** | $f_z = \dfrac{V_f}{Z \cdot N}$ | mm/tooth | Compare with cutter catalogue value |
| 6 | **Material removal rate (MRR)** | $\text{MRR} = V_f \cdot a_p \cdot a_e$ | mm³/min | $a_p$ = depth, $a_e$ = width |
| 7 | **Average chip thickness** | $h_{av} = f_z \cdot \sin(\kappa)$ | mm | $\kappa$ = approach angle (typical 30°–90°) |
| 8 | **Power at cutter** | $P_c = \dfrac{\text{MRR} \cdot k_c}{60 \cdot 10^6 \cdot \eta}$ | kW | $k_c$ = specific cutting force (N/mm²), $\eta$ = efficiency (0.7–0.85) |
| 9 | **Plane-milling length of cut** | $L = L_w + D + A$ | mm | $L_w$ = workpiece length, $A$ = approach allowance (≈3–5 mm) |
| 10 | **Plane-milling time (per pass)** | $t_m = \dfrac{L}{V_f}$ | min | Use total engaged length |
| 11 | **Pocket-milling total time** | $t_{tot} = \dfrac{A_p \cdot t_m}{a_p} + t_{plunge}$ | min | $A_p$ = pocket depth, $a_p$ = axial step-down |
| 12 | **Step-over for pocket** | $a_e \le 0.5 \cdot D$ (finish) ; $a_e \le 0.8 \cdot D$ (rough) | mm | Prevents cutter stall; reduces tool load |
| 13 | **Plunge feed factor** | $V_{f,plunge} \approx 0.3 \cdot V_f$ | mm/min | Reduce feed during Z-axis entry |

> [!NOTE]
> **LaTeX best practice inside tables:** I have written all absolute-value-style quantities using $\vert$ and $\le$ symbols wrapped in `$...$` so the markdown table pipes `vert` are never confused with column separators. For example, $a_e \le 0.5 \cdot D$ is safe.

## 2.5 Recommended Cutting Parameters (Lab Reference)

The values below are typical for **Mild Steel (EN8 / AISI 1040)** workpieces, HSS cutters, on a standard KTU lab milling machine. Always refer to the **cutter manufacturer's catalogue** when available.

| Operation | Cutter | Material | D (mm) | V (m/min) | $f_z$ (mm/tooth) | $a_p$ (mm) | $a_e$ (mm) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Plane (slab) | Plain milling | Mild steel | 80 | 20–25 | 0.10–0.15 | 2–4 | 60–70 |
| Plane (facing) | Shell end mill | Mild steel | 50 | 25–30 | 0.08–0.12 | 2–3 | 40–45 |
| Pocket (rough) | 4-flute HSS end mill | Mild steel | 12 | 20–25 | 0.04–0.06 | 1.0–1.5 | 5–8 |
| Pocket (finish) | 4-flute HSS end mill | Mild steel | 12 | 25–30 | 0.03–0.05 | 0.3–0.5 | 1.0–1.5 |
| Pocket (aluminium) | 2-flute HSS end mill | Al 6061 | 10 | 80–120 | 0.05–0.08 | 1.0–2.0 | 5–8 |

> [!TIP]
> **Why these numbers matter:** Choosing V too high → cutter burn; choosing V too low → built-up edge, poor finish. Choosing $f_z$ too high → tooth breakage; too low → rubbing and rapid tool wear. The KTU examiner will look for *justification* of these choices in the lab record.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Spindle Speed from Cutting Speed

### Statement
Derive the expression for **spindle speed (N)** in rpm given the **cutting speed (V)** in m/min and the **cutter diameter (D)** in mm.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Definition of cutting speed.**
Cutting speed is the **peripheral linear speed** at which the cutting edge sweeps past the workpiece. If the cutter rotates at N revolutions per minute, the distance travelled by one point on the cutter periphery in 1 minute is the **circumference times N**:

$$
\text{Distance per minute} = \pi \cdot D \cdot N \quad \text{(with } D \text{ in mm, the distance is in mm/min)}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert mm/min to m/min.**
Since 1 m = 1000 mm, divide by 1000:

$$
V \;(\text{m/min}) = \frac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for N.**
Rearrange the equation by multiplying both sides by 1000 and dividing by $\pi \cdot D$:

$$
N \;(\text{rpm}) = \frac{1000 \cdot V}{\pi \cdot D}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitution and unit check.**
- $V$ is in m/min, $D$ is in mm.
- $\pi \cdot D$ is in mm; multiplying $V$ by 1000 converts it to mm/min so that the ratio is dimensionally rpm.

### Worked Numerical Example
A Ø 50 mm HSS plain milling cutter is to be run at a cutting speed of $V = 25$ m/min on mild steel. Find the spindle speed.

$$
N = \frac{1000 \times 25}{\pi \times 50} = \frac{25000}{157.08} \approx 159.15 \text{ rpm}
$$

Rounded to the **nearest available spindle speed** on the lab machine (typically multiples of 20, 25, 31.5, 40, 50, 63, 80, 100, 125, 160, 200, ...), we select $N = 160$ rpm.

**Recompute the actual cutting speed for the record:**

$$
V_{actual} = \frac{\pi \times 50 \times 160}{1000} = 25.13 \text{ m/min}
$$

The deviation from the desired value is well within 1 % — acceptable in lab practice.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Feed Rate from Feed-Per-Tooth

### Statement
Derive the **table feed rate $V_f$** in mm/min given the feed per tooth $f_z$, the number of teeth $Z$, and the spindle speed $N$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Teeth engagements per minute.**
The cutter has $Z$ teeth and rotates at $N$ rpm. Therefore, the number of tooth engagements per minute is:

$$
\text{Engagements/min} = Z \cdot N
$$

**Step 2 — Linear advance per engagement.**
Each tooth engagement advances the table by $f_z$ mm (along the feed direction). Hence, the total linear advance per minute is the product of engagements/min and mm/engagement:

$$
V_f = f_z \cdot Z \cdot N
$$

**Step 3 — Unit consistency.**
$f_z$ (mm/tooth) $\times$ $Z$ (teeth/rev) $\times$ $N$ (rev/min) = mm/min. ✔

### Worked Numerical Example
A 4-flute end mill ($Z = 4$) rotating at 400 rpm with a feed per tooth of $f_z = 0.05$ mm/tooth. Find the table feed rate.

$$
V_f = 0.05 \times 4 \times 400 = 80 \text{ mm/min}
$$

This is a comfortable feed for a 12 mm carbide end mill in mild steel during roughing.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Material Removal Rate (MRR) in Plain Milling

### Statement
Derive MRR for plain (slab) milling where the cutter engages a workpiece of width $w$ with an axial depth of cut $a_p$, fed at $V_f$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Geometry of the engaged region.**
The cutter engages a **rectangular swath** in the workpiece. The swath has:
- Cross-section area = $a_p \times w$ (axial depth × radial width/engagement).
- Length swept per minute = $V_f$ (mm/min).

**Step 2 — Volume removed per minute.**

$$
\text{MRR} = V_f \times a_p \times w
$$

This is the **volumetric material removal rate**, expressed in mm³/min. ✔

### Worked Numerical Example
A slab mill removes a 4 mm depth across a 70 mm workpiece width at a feed of 80 mm/min.

$$
\text{MRR} = 80 \times 4 \times 70 = 22400 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min} = 22.4 \text{ cm}^3/\text{min}
$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Machining Time for a Plane-Milling Pass

### Statement
Determine the time required for one pass of a plain milling cutter across a workpiece of length $L_w$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Account for cutter entry and exit.**
The cutter must travel a small **approach allowance** $A$ before the cutter is fully engaged, plus a small **overtravel** so the workpiece is fully cleared. Total table travel:

$$
L = L_w + D + A
$$

where $D$ is the cutter diameter (the cutter must travel its own diameter past the leading edge) and $A \approx 3$–5 mm.

**Step 2 — Machining time.**

$$
t_m = \frac{L}{V_f}
$$

**Step 3 — Unit consistency.**
$L$ (mm) / $V_f$ (mm/min) = min. ✔

### Worked Numerical Example
Workpiece length $L_w = 120$ mm, cutter diameter $D = 50$ mm, approach $A = 5$ mm, feed rate $V_f = 80$ mm/min.

$$
L = 120 + 50 + 5 = 175 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
t_m = \frac{175}{80} = 2.19 \text{ min}
$$

For multiple passes at incremental depths (e.g., 4 mm depth at 2 passes of 2 mm each), the total plane-milling time is:

$$
t_{plane} = n_{passes} \times t_m
$$

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Total Time for a Rectangular Pocket

### Statement
Estimate the total machining time to mill a rectangular pocket of length $L_p$, width $W_p$, and depth $A_p$ using a step-over $a_e$ and step-down $a_p$ strategy.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Number of Z-levels (step-downs).**
Each Z-level removes a layer of thickness $a_p$. The number of axial levels is:

$$
n_z = \left\lceil \frac{A_p}{a_p} \right\rceil
$$

where $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ denotes the *ceiling* (round up to the next integer).

**Step 2 — Number of X-passes per level (step-over).**
A standard rule is that step-over is a fraction of the cutter diameter, often $0.5D$ to $0.8D$ for roughing. For a pocket of width $W_p$ (assuming the cutter diameter $D$ is small compared to $W_p$):

$$
n_x = \left\lceil \frac{W_p}{a_e} \right\rceil
$$

**Step 3 — Effective feed length per X-pass.**
For a simple raster (zig) scan, the effective length per pass is approximately the pocket length $L_p$ plus a small approach/overtravel allowance. The number of direction-reversing passes per level is $n_x$.

**Step 4 — Time per pass.**

$$
t_{pass} = \frac{L_p + A}{V_f}
$$

**Step 5 — Total machining time.**

$$
t_{pocket} = n_z \cdot n_x \cdot t_{pass} + t_{plunge} + t_{rapid}
$$

For a back-of-envelope estimate, ignore the small rapid moves and plunge times:

$$
t_{pocket} \approx n_z \cdot n_x \cdot \frac{L_p}{V_f}
$$

### Worked Numerical Example
Pocket: $L_p = 80$ mm, $W_p = 40$ mm, depth $A_p = 6$ mm. Cutter: Ø 10 mm end mill, $V_f = 60$ mm/min. Step-over $a_e = 5$ mm, step-down $a_p = 2$ mm.

- $n_z = \lceil 6/2 \rceil = 3$ levels.
- $n_x = \lceil 40/5 \rceil = 8$ passes per level.

$$
t_{pocket} \approx 3 \times 8 \times \frac{80}{60} = 32 \text{ min}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's heuristic:** Many students forget that pocket time = (number of levels) × (number of step-overs) × (time per pass). A common KTU mistake is to write $t = L/V_f$ only, missing the multiplicity factor. Always show the matrix in the calculation sheet.

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Plane vs. Pocket Milling

| Aspect | Plane Milling | Pocket Milling |
|---|---|---|
| **Cutter type** | Plain/slab mill, side-and-face mill, shell end mill | End mill (2/3/4-flute), bull-nose mill |
| **Cutter axis orientation** | Horizontal (plain) or vertical (facing) | Vertical (always) |
| **DOF of cutter motion** | 1-D feed (linear) | 2-D contouring (X, Y) + Z plunge |
| **Engagement** | Continuous over a long length | Intermittent; tool may enter/exit multiple times |
| **Tool path** | Straight line | Spiral, raster, zig-zag, or trochoidal |
| **Primary challenge** | Cutter deflection, surface waviness | Chip evacuation, tool breakage at corners |
| **Coolant use** | Flood or abundant flow | Flood or MQL (Minimum Quantity Lubrication) |
| **Typical $a_e$** | Large (50–80 % of D) | Small (10–50 % of D, finish: ≤ 5 % of D) |
| **Time estimation** | $t = (L_w + D + A)/V_f \times n_{passes}$ | $t = n_z \cdot n_x \cdot L_p/V_f$ |
| **Tolerance achievable** | ± 0.05 mm (rough), ± 0.02 mm (finish) | ± 0.05 mm (rough), ± 0.02 mm (finish on floor) |
| **Surface roughness $R_a$** | 1.6–3.2 µm (finish) | 0.8–3.2 µm (finish) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Workflow Diagram — Plane Milling Exercise

The block diagram below shows the step-by-step workflow a KTU student follows while performing the plane-milling exercise. It is designed to be read top-to-bottom as a procedural checklist.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Step 0: Read Experiment Sheet and Machine Manual] --> A1[Step 1: Identify Workpiece Material and Blank Dimensions]
    A1 --> A2[Step 2: Select Plain Milling Cutter and Arbor]
    A2 --> A3[Step 3: Compute Cutting Speed V from Machining Data Table]
    A3 --> A4[Step 4: Compute Spindle Speed N and Table Feed Vf]
    A4 --> A5[Step 5: Clean Machine Table and Mount Vise or Fixture]
    A5 --> A6[Step 6: Clamp Workpiece Using Parallels and Step Blocks]
    A6 --> A7[Step 7: Set Quill to Required Depth Using Dial Indicator]
    A7 --> A8[Step 8: Bring Cutter to Start Position and Verify Clearance]
    A8 --> A9[Step 9: Run Spindle and Engage Auto Feed at Calculated Vf]
    A9 --> A10[Step 10: Perform First Pass and Measure Flatness with DTI]
    A10 --> A11[Step 11: Repeat for Required Number of Passes]
    A11 --> A12[Step 12: Retract Cutter, Stop Spindle, Unclamp and Measure Final Dimensions]
    A12 --> A13[Step 13: Record Observations, Calculate MRR, Submit Record]
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Pocket Milling Operation

The diagram below maps the functional interactions between the **machine axes, cutter, workpiece, and process parameters** during a pocket-milling cycle. This is the kind of architectural block flow used in CNC programming references; we adapt it for manual / teach-pendant operation as performed in the KTU lab.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Process Plan: Pocket Geometry] --> P2[Tool Selection: End Mill Diameter D]
    P2 --> P3[Parameter Setup: V, N, Vf, ap, ae]
    P3 --> P4[Work Holding: Vise with Soft Jaws]
    P4 --> P5[Origin Setting: X0 Y0 Z0 on Top Face]
    P5 --> P6[Step A: Plunge to First Z Level at Reduced Feed]
    P6 --> P7[Step B: Engaged Pass in X Direction at Full Vf]
    P7 --> P8[Step C: Step Over in Y by ae]
    P8 --> P9[Step D: Reverse Pass in X]
    P9 --> P10{All Step-Overs Done for This Z Level?}
    P10 -- No --> P8
    P10 -- Yes --> P11[Step E: Retract Z by ap to Next Level]
    P11 --> P12{Pocket Depth Reached?}
    P12 -- No --> P6
    P12 -- Yes --> P13[Step F: Finish Pass at Reduced ap and ae]
    P13 --> P14[Step G: Retract Cutter, Stop Spindle, Deburr]
    P14 --> P15[Step H: Measure with Vernier and Surface Plate]
```

## 4.3 Cutter-Workpiece Engagement Schematic (Text Block)

The following block diagram illustrates the **engagement geometry** of a plain milling cutter against a workpiece, showing the key variables used in the formulas of Section 3.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Setup[Plain Milling Cutter Setup]
        S1[Workpiece Lw x W on Machine Table]
        S2[Plain Milling Cutter of Diameter D on Arbor]
        S3[Table Feed Direction vf]
        S4[Axial Depth of Cut ap]
        S5[Approach Allowance A]
    end
    S1 --> ENG[Engagement Zone: Length Lw, Width w, Depth ap]
    S2 --> ENG
    S3 --> ENG
    S4 --> ENG
    S5 --> ENG
    ENG --> OUT[Outputs: MRR, Power, Surface Finish, Time]
```

## 4.4 Decision Tree — Choosing Up-Milling vs. Down-Milling

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Is the Milling Machine CNC with Pre-loaded Ballscrews?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Climb Milling Preferred for Finish?}
    Q1 -- No --> Q3[Use Up-Milling Conventional by Default]
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q4[Select Down-Milling Climb]
    Q2 -- No --> Q5[Select Up-Milling Conventional]
    Q3 --> END[Final Setup]
    Q4 --> END
    Q5 --> END
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR

        W1[Workpiece Length Lw]
        W2[Workpiece Width w]
        W3[Stock Removal Depth ap]
    end

    end
    C1 --> P1
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Define the term "feed per tooth" in milling and explain its role in chip load management.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Feed per tooth ($f_z$) is the **linear distance** the workpiece (or table) advances during the engagement of **one cutting tooth** of the milling cutter, expressed in **mm/tooth**. It governs the **chip load** — the cross-sectional area of the chip removed by each tooth. The feed rate $V_f$ is related to $f_z$ by $V_f = f_z \cdot Z \cdot N$. Proper selection of $f_z$ is critical: if too high, the tooth breaks; if too low, the cutter rubs and wears rapidly. It is the *primary lever* for balancing productivity and tool life in milling. **(3 marks: 1 for definition + 1 for formula + 1 for role in chip load)**

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** *Differentiate between up-milling and down-milling with respect to chip thickness variation and cutter-workpiece engagement direction.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Up-Milling (Conventional) | Down-Milling (Climb) |
|---|---|---|
| Feed direction | Opposite to cutter rotation at contact | Same as cutter rotation at contact |
| Chip thickness | Starts at **zero**, increases to maximum | Starts at **maximum**, decreases to zero |
| Force on workpiece | Cutter tends to lift the workpiece | Cutter tends to pull the workpiece into fixture |

Up-milling tolerates backlash and is preferred on manual machines; down-milling gives better surface finish and longer tool life but requires zero backlash. **(3 marks: 1 mark per row above)**

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks — Internal Choice Option 1)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2, Q. 4(a)]**

> *A horizontal milling machine is to be used to machine the top surface of a mild steel workpiece of dimensions 150 mm × 80 mm × 25 mm. A Ø 60 mm HSS plain milling cutter with 8 teeth is to be used. Recommended cutting parameters for HSS on mild steel are: cutting speed V = 22 m/min, feed per tooth $f_z$ = 0.12 mm/tooth. The depth of cut per pass is 2 mm and the final thickness is to be reduced from 25 mm to 23 mm. The cutter is set such that the approach allowance A = 5 mm.*

### Part (a) — 7 Marks
*Calculate (i) the spindle speed N, (ii) the table feed rate V_f, and (iii) the machining time for one pass. Show all steps and units.*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Spindle speed N.**

$$
N = \frac{1000 \cdot V}{\pi \cdot D} = \frac{1000 \times 22}{\pi \times 60} = \frac{22000}{188.50} \approx 116.7 \text{ rpm}
$$

Round to nearest available spindle speed → **$N = 125$ rpm** (typical available step on a lab machine). **(2 marks)**

**Step 2 — Recompute actual cutting speed (good lab practice).**

$$
V_{actual} = \frac{\pi \times 60 \times 125}{1000} = 23.56 \text{ m/min}
$$

This is within ~7 % of the recommended value, acceptable for a lab exercise. **(1 mark)**

**Step 3 — Table feed rate.**

$$
V_f = f_z \cdot Z \cdot N = 0.12 \times 8 \times 125 = 120 \text{ mm/min}
$$

**(2 marks)**

**Step 4 — Machining time per pass.**

Total table travel:

$$
L = L_w + D + A = 150 + 60 + 5 = 215 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
t_m = \frac{L}{V_f} = \frac{215}{120} \approx 1.79 \text{ min}
$$

**(2 marks)**

### Part (b) — 7 Marks
*Calculate (i) the total plane-milling time to remove 2 mm of stock, (ii) the Material Removal Rate (MRR), and (iii) the cutting power assuming specific cutting force $k_c$ = 2000 N/mm² and machine efficiency $\eta$ = 0.8.*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Total milling time.**
The required depth of cut is $A_p = 25 - 23 = 2$ mm. With depth per pass = 2 mm, only **one pass** is needed.

$$
t_{total} = 1 \times t_m = 1.79 \text{ min}
$$

**(1 mark)**

**Step 2 — Material Removal Rate.**

$$
\text{MRR} = V_f \times a_p \times w = 120 \times 2 \times 80 = 19200 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min} = 19.2 \text{ cm}^3/\text{min}
$$

**(2 marks)**

**Step 3 — Cutting Power.**

$$
P_c = \frac{\text{MRR} \times k_c}{60 \times 10^6 \times \eta} = \frac{19200 \times 2000}{60 \times 10^6 \times 0.8}
$$

$$
P_c = \frac{3.84 \times 10^7}{4.8 \times 10^7} = 0.80 \text{ kW}
$$

**(2 marks)**

**Step 4 — Comment.**
A 0.80 kW power demand is comfortably within the capacity of a typical 1.5–2.2 kW lab milling machine. **(2 marks for discussion of the practical implication, e.g., safety factor, machine selection)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students forget to **add the cutter diameter D to the workpiece length** when computing the table travel $L$. The total length should be $L_w + D + A$, not just $L_w$. This is a one-mark deduction that is consistently applied across valuation scripts.

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks — Internal Choice Option 2)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2, Q. 4(b)]**

> *A rectangular pocket of dimensions 100 mm × 60 mm × 8 mm (length × width × depth) is to be machined in an aluminium (Al 6061) plate on a vertical milling machine. A Ø 12 mm, 4-flute HSS end mill is used. Recommended cutting parameters: V = 90 m/min, $f_z$ = 0.06 mm/tooth. Step-over $a_e$ = 5 mm, step-down $a_p$ = 2 mm. The cutter is set with approach allowance A = 4 mm. Use a raster (zig-zag) tool path.*

### Part (a) — 7 Marks
*Calculate (i) the spindle speed N, (ii) the table feed rate V_f, and (iii) the number of axial levels and step-over passes required for the pocket.*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Spindle speed N.**

$$
N = \frac{1000 \times 90}{\pi \times 12} = \frac{90000}{37.70} \approx 2387 \text{ rpm}
$$

Round to nearest available → **$N = 2400$ rpm** (typical for a lab VMC; for a manual mill, choose the closest lower step). **(2 marks)**

**Step 2 — Actual cutting speed.**

$$
V_{actual} = \frac{\pi \times 12 \times 2400}{1000} \approx 90.48 \text{ m/min}
$$

**(1 mark)**

**Step 3 — Table feed rate.**

$$
V_f = 0.06 \times 4 \times 2400 = 576 \text{ mm/min}
$$

**(2 marks)**

**Step 4 — Number of axial levels (step-downs).**

$$
n_z = \left\lceil \frac{A_p}{a_p} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{8}{2} \right\rceil = 4 \text{ levels}
$$

**(1 mark)**

**Step 5 — Number of step-over passes per level.**

$$
n_x = \left\lceil \frac{W_p}{a_e} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{60}{5} \right\rceil = 12 \text{ passes}
$$

**(1 mark)**

### Part (b) — 7 Marks
*Calculate (i) the time per pass, (ii) the total pocket-milling time, and (iii) the Material Removal Rate (MRR).*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Time per pass.**

Effective feed length per pass (length of pocket + approach allowance):

$$
L_{pass} = L_p + A = 100 + 4 = 104 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
t_{pass} = \frac{L_{pass}}{V_f} = \frac{104}{576} \approx 0.181 \text{ min/pass}
$$

**(2 marks)**

**Step 2 — Total pocket-milling time.**

$$
t_{pocket} = n_z \times n_x \times t_{pass} = 4 \times 12 \times 0.181 \approx 8.68 \text{ min}
$$

Add ~10 % allowance for plunge and rapid moves → **~9.5 min total**. **(2 marks)**

**Step 3 — Material Removal Rate (per pass at full width and step-down).**

$$
\text{MRR} = V_f \times a_p \times a_e = 576 \times 2 \times 5 = 5760 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min} = 5.76 \text{ cm}^3/\text{min}
$$

**(2 marks)**

**Step 4 — Comment.**
For Al 6061 with HSS, this MRR is conservative; switching to a carbide end mill and increasing $a_e$ to 6–8 mm could double the productivity without exceeding machine capacity. **(1 mark for practical engineering judgement)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Pocket Milling):** A common mark-loss point is the **failure to multiply the per-pass time by both $n_z$ and $n_x$**. The pocket time depends on the *cartesian product* of axial levels and step-over passes, not just one of them. Always state the matrix clearly: "Total passes = $n_z \times n_x$".

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist — print this before the lab exam.**

- **Milling = multi-point, non-continuous, geometrically defined** process — only the **cutter rotates**, the **workpiece is stationary**.
- **Plane milling** uses a **plain/slab mill** to produce a flat surface; **pocket milling** uses an **end mill** to produce an internal cavity.
- The **core formulas** to memorise are: $V = \pi D N/1000$, $N = 1000 V/(\pi D)$, $V_f = f_z \cdot Z \cdot N$, $\text{MRR} = V_f \cdot a_p \cdot a_e$.
- Always **round spindle speed** to the **nearest available machine step** and **recompute** the actual cutting speed for the record.
- For **plane milling**, total table travel = $L_w + D + A$ — **do not** omit $D$.
- For **pocket milling**, total time = $n_z \times n_x \times t_{pass}$ — **do not** forget either multiplicity factor.
- **Up-milling (conventional)** is the safe default on manual machines; **down-milling (climb)** gives better finish and tool life but **demands zero backlash**.
- **Step-over $a_e$** is typically **0.5 D to 0.8 D** for roughing and **≤ 0.05 D** for finishing.
- **Step-down $a_p$** for an end mill is limited by cutter length, rigidity, and chip evacuation — start with 1–2 mm in steel.
- **Plunge feed** is approximately **0.3 × $V_f$** to prevent end-mill breakage on entry.
- **Cutting speed V** depends on **workpiece material and cutter material** — carbide allows 2–3× higher V than HSS.
- **Feed per tooth $f_z$** depends on **cutter diameter and number of teeth** — refer to the catalogue.
- **Power at cutter** $P_c = (\text{MMR} \cdot k_c)/(60 \cdot 10^6 \cdot \eta)$ — verify it is less than the machine's rated motor power.
- **Coolant** is mandatory for steel milling; aluminium can often be dry-machined with HSS but is best flooded with carbide.
- **Work-holding** must resist cutting forces — use **soft jaws** for finished parts and **hard jaws with parallels** for rough blocks.
- **Surface finish** is best on **climb-milled**, **sharp**, **finely-fed** cuts; finish with $a_p \le 0.5$ mm and $a_e \le 0.1 D$.
- **Tool wear indicators**: chatter, sudden change in cutting sound, increase in motor current, deteriorated surface finish — replace or re-sharpen.
- **Safety**: Always use **machine guards**, **safety glasses**, **no loose clothing**, and **keep one hand on the emergency stop** during the first cut.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Exercises on milling machine: Spur gear cutting operation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIEL407 - MACHINE TOOLS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Exercises on milling machine: Spur gear cutting operation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Spur gear cutting on a milling machine** is a generative (form-copying) machining process in which a **standard involute gear-tooth form cutter**, mounted on the arbor of a horizontal or vertical milling machine, is progressively fed into a rotating gear blank to produce involute spur gear teeth through a series of indexed angular movements. The operation is governed by the **Universal Dividing Head (Indexing Head)** which rotates the blank by precise angular increments equal to one tooth-space angle after each tooth is cut.

In formal KTU 2024 syllabus terminology, the experiment is defined as:
> *"To cut a spur gear on a given mild steel / aluminium workpiece using a module/disc cutter mounted on the milling machine spindle, using the indexing head for tooth spacing."*

The standard governing the tooth geometry is the **ISO 53:1998 (equivalent to AGMA 201.02)** involute spur gear standard, with **Module (m)** as the fundamental metric design parameter.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are using a **cookie-cutter shape (the involute profile)** and pressing it into a flat disc of dough (the gear blank). Each time you press, you create **one identical tooth impression**. Now rotate the disc by exactly the angular width of one tooth-gap, press again, and repeat until all teeth are stamped out.

The milling machine performs exactly this function — the **cutter** is the cookie-cutter shape, the **gear blank** is the dough, and the **indexing head** is the precision rotational indexing mechanism that ensures each tooth sits at exactly the correct angular spacing. The only difference is that in real machining, the cutter has a thin profile (one tooth space wide) that cuts a single gap, and the involute shape is preserved across the full face width of the gear.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight**: This experiment is listed as a *core practical* under Module Experiment 2 in the PCIEL407 Machine Tools Lab. Students must record all indexing calculations, gear geometry derivations, and parameter settings in the lab record. Examiners frequently ask for the derivations of **PCD (Pitch Circle Diameter)** and **indexing crank movement** in the viva voce.

## 1.3 Standard Design Parameters & Units

The fundamental gear design parameter in the **metric (SI) system** is:

- **Module (m)** = The ratio of the **pitch circle diameter (D)** to the **number of teeth (T)**.
- Unit: **millimetres (mm)**.
- Standard preferred values (per ISO 54:1996): **m = 1, 1.25, 1.5, 2, 2.5, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 10 mm** (Series 1).
- Also standard are 1.125, 1.375, 1.75, 2.25, 2.75, 3.5, 4.5, 5.5, 6.5, 7, 9 (Series 2).

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction for Examiners**: The "module" in gear design is *not* the same as the engineering "modulus of elasticity" or "modulus of section". Always state clearly that gear module has units of length (mm), not force or stress.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundations of Spur Gear Geometry

A spur gear is a **cylindrical gear** with teeth parallel to the axis of rotation. The teeth are designed based on the **involute of a circle** curve, which provides:
- **Conjugate action** — constant angular velocity ratio during mesh.
- **Line contact** during engagement — distributes load.
- **Tolerance to centre-distance errors** — the tooth profile shifts but contact remains conjugate.

The basic geometry revolves around the following concentric circles on the gear blank:

| Circle | Symbol | Radius Formula | Functional Role |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Addendum Circle** | $D_a$ | $r_a = m \cdot \left(\dfrac{T}{2} + 1\right)$ | Outer tip of the tooth |
| **Pitch Circle (Reference)** | $D$ | $r = m \cdot \dfrac{T}{2}$ | Rolling circle for mesh — design reference |
| **Dedendum Circle (Root)** | $D_f$ | $r_f = m \cdot \left(\dfrac{T}{2} - 1.157\right)$ | Bottom of the tooth gap |
| **Base Circle** | $D_b$ | $r_b = r \cos\phi$ | Generating circle for the involute |
| **Clearance Circle** | $D_c$ | $r_c = m \cdot \left(\dfrac{T}{2} - 1\right)$ | Top land reference of mating gear |

Where $\phi$ = **pressure angle**, with standard values being **20° (ISO standard)** and **14.5° (older AGMA standard)**.

## 2.2 Logic Flow of the Cutting Operation

The cutting process follows this rigorous sequence:

1. **Calculate gear blank dimensions** from module (m) and tooth count (T):
   - Pitch diameter $D = mT$
   - Outside diameter $D_o = m(T + 2)$
   - Bore diameter $D_b$ (subtracted as clearance for arbor)
2. **Select appropriate cutter number** from the **8-cutter set** based on tooth count (T):
   - The cutter set approximates the involute profile at the **mean radius** for a range of teeth.
3. **Mount the gear blank** on the indexing head spindle using a mandrel/arbor through the bore.
4. **Set the cutter-to-blank centre distance** equal to the pitch radius $\dfrac{D}{2}$ so the cutter profile matches the involute at the pitch circle.
5. **Calculate indexing movement**: $N = \dfrac{40}{T}$ revolutions of the crank per tooth division.
6. **Engage cutter, feed down** to full tooth depth $h = 2.157m$ (full-depth teeth) for roughing, then take a finishing pass of $0.05$ to $0.10$ mm.
7. **Index, cut, repeat** for all T teeth.
8. **Deburr, inspect** using a gear-tooth vernier or by rolling against a mating gear.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — every KTU exam question in this topic is solvable from this single table.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Module | $m = \dfrac{D}{T}$ | mm | Metric standard |
| 2 | Pitch Diameter | $D = mT$ | mm | Reference circle |
| 3 | Circular Pitch | $p_c = \pi m$ | mm | Arc length between adjacent teeth on pitch circle |
| 4 | Addendum | $a = 1.000 \, m$ | mm | Tooth height above pitch circle |
| 5 | Dedendum | $b = 1.157 \, m$ | mm | Tooth depth below pitch circle (full-depth) |
| 6 | Whole Depth | $h = 2.157 \, m$ | mm | Total tooth height |
| 7 | Working Depth | $h_w = 2.000 \, m$ | mm | Sum of two addenda |
| 8 | Clearance | $c = 0.157 \, m$ | mm | Gap at root |
| 9 | Outside Diameter | $D_o = m(T+2)$ | mm | Tip circle |
| 10 | Root Diameter | $D_f = m(T-2.157)$ | mm | Bottom of tooth gap |
| 11 | Tooth Thickness | $t = 1.5708 \, m$ | mm | At pitch circle |
| 12 | Space Width | $e = 1.5708 \, m$ | mm | At pitch circle |
| 13 | Base Circle Diameter | $D_b = mT \cos\phi$ | mm | $\phi = 20°$ standard |
| 14 | Indexing (Simple) | $n = \dfrac{40}{T}$ | revs | Standard index plate |
| 15 | Spindle Speed | $N = \dfrac{1000 \, V_c}{\pi D_c}$ | rpm | $V_c$ = cutting speed (m/min), $D_c$ = cutter $\varnothing$ |
| 16 | Feed Rate | $f = f_t \cdot Z \cdot N$ | mm/min | $f_t$ = feed/tooth, $Z$ = number of teeth on cutter |
| 17 | Machining Time | $t_m = \dfrac{L}{f} \cdot T$ | min | $L$ = length of cut |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility**: Spur gears cut by this method are used in low-to-medium speed applications — conveyor drives, automotive gearboxes, machine tool headstocks, and clockwork mechanisms. The milling method is preferred for prototype/repair work because the same machine can produce any module gear by changing the cutter, unlike gear hobbing which requires module-specific hobs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Pitch Circle Diameter and Module Relationship

**Starting Concept:** The pitch circle is the imaginary circle on which two meshing gears roll without slipping. The circumference of this circle must equal the sum of all tooth thicknesses plus all space widths.

### Step-by-Step Logic:

**Step 1.** On the pitch circle, there are exactly $T$ teeth and $T$ spaces, each pair occupying an angular pitch of $\dfrac{360°}{T}$.

**Step 2.** The arc length subtended by one tooth + one space on the pitch circle is defined as the **circular pitch** $p_c$.

**Step 3.** Since the circumference of the pitch circle equals $T$ times the circular pitch:

$$
\pi D = T \cdot p_c
$$

**Step 4.** By definition, the **module** $m$ is the ratio of pitch diameter to number of teeth:

$$
m = \frac{D}{T}
$$

**Step 5.** Substituting $D = mT$ into the circular pitch formula:

$$
\pi \cdot (mT) = T \cdot p_c
$$

**Step 6.** Cancelling $T$ from both sides:

$$
p_c = \pi m
$$

This proves that **circular pitch depends only on module**, not on tooth count — a powerful design simplification.

**Step 7.** Solving for pitch diameter:

$$
D = mT
$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Whole Tooth Depth (Addendum + Dedendum)

The **whole depth $h$** of a standard full-depth gear tooth is the sum of addendum (above pitch circle) and dedendum (below pitch circle).

### Step-by-Step Logic:

**Step 1.** Standard addendum is set equal to one module:

$$
a = m
$$

This places the tip circle at one module radius outside the pitch circle.

**Step 2.** Standard dedendum is set greater than the addendum by an amount equal to the **clearance** $c$:

$$
b = a + c = m + c
$$

**Step 3.** The clearance is standardised (per ISO 53) at $0.157 \, m$ to allow for the rounded tooth tip of the mating gear to enter without interference.

**Step 4.** Substituting:

$$
b = m + 0.157m = 1.157m
$$

**Step 5.** Whole depth:

$$
h = a + b = m + 1.157m = 2.157m
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Outside and Root Diameters

### Outside Diameter $D_o$:

The outside (tip) circle is the addendum circle, located at $r + a = \dfrac{mT}{2} + m$ from the gear axis. Therefore:

$$
D_o = 2 \cdot \left(\frac{mT}{2} + m\right)
$$

$$
D_o = mT + 2m = m(T+2)
$$

### Root Diameter $D_f$:

The root circle is the dedendum circle, located at $r - b = \dfrac{mT}{2} - 1.157m$:

$$
D_f = 2 \cdot \left(\frac{mT}{2} - 1.157m\right)
$$

$$
D_f = mT - 2 \cdot 1.157m = m(T - 2.314) \;\text{(alternative form)}
$$

The more commonly tabulated form is:

$$
D_f = m(T - 2.157)
$$

This single formula is used by examiners to verify the blank diameter pre-machining.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Simple Indexing Movement

The standard indexing head has a **40-tooth worm wheel** driven by a single-thread worm on the crank shaft. Therefore:

**One full turn of the crank = 1/40 turn of the spindle (workpiece).**

To advance the workpiece by **one tooth pitch** (i.e., angular movement of $\dfrac{360°}{T}$), the crank must turn:

$$
n = \frac{1 \text{ spindle rev}}{\dfrac{T}{1} \text{ tooth positions per spindle rev}} \times \frac{40 \text{ crank rev}}{1 \text{ spindle rev}}
$$

$$
n = \frac{40}{T}
$$

This is the famous **"rule of 40"** in indexing.

### Worked Numerical Example 1:

> **Problem:** A spur gear with module $m = 2$ mm and number of teeth $T = 30$ is to be cut on a milling machine using simple indexing.

**Step 1 — Pitch diameter:**

$$
D = mT = 2 \times 30 = 60 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Outside diameter:**

$$
D_o = m(T+2) = 2 \times 32 = 64 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Root diameter:**

$$
D_f = m(T - 2.157) = 2 \times 27.843 = 55.686 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4 — Whole depth:**

$$
h = 2.157 \times 2 = 4.314 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 5 — Indexing movement:**

$$
n = \frac{40}{T} = \frac{40}{30} = 1\tfrac{10}{30} = 1\tfrac{1}{3} \text{ revs}
$$

This means: **1 full turn + 1/3 turn = 1 turn + 10 holes on the 30-hole circle** (using the standard index plate).

### Worked Numerical Example 2 — Spindle Speed Selection:

> **Problem:** For the above gear, a HSS involute cutter of 75 mm diameter is to be used. Cutting speed for HSS cutter on mild steel = $25$ m/min. Calculate spindle speed.

**Step 1.** Apply the cutting speed formula:

$$
N = \frac{1000 \, V_c}{\pi D_c}
$$

**Step 2.** Substitute:

$$
N = \frac{1000 \times 25}{\pi \times 75} = \frac{25000}{235.619}
$$

**Step 3.** Evaluate:

$$
N \approx 106.1 \text{ rpm}
$$

**Step 4.** Round to nearest available machine spindle speed: **100 rpm** or **110 rpm** (typical geared head options).

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Cutter Selection Table

A standard gear cutter set contains **8 cutters**, each numbered, used across a range of tooth counts:

| Cutter No. | Cuts Teeth Range | Approximate Profile Curve |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 135 — Rack (∞) | Reference involute |
| 2 | 55 — 134 | |
| 3 | 35 — 54 | |
| 4 | 26 — 34 | |
| 5 | 21 — 25 | |
| 6 | 17 — 20 | |
| 7 | 14 — 16 | |
| 8 | 12 — 13 | |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Viva Favourite Question**: *"Why are 8 cutters sufficient for all tooth counts?"* Answer: Because the involute profile changes very slowly with tooth count beyond ~35 teeth, so each cutter approximates a band of profiles within acceptable accuracy.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Compound Indexing (Brief)

When $T$ does not yield a clean fraction from $\dfrac{40}{T}$, **compound indexing** is used. The crank engages a change gear pair to alter the effective ratio. The general index formula becomes:

$$
n_{\text{eff}} = \frac{40}{T} \cdot \frac{A}{B}
$$

Where $\dfrac{A}{B}$ is the change gear ratio selected from the indexing tables. KTU rarely requires students to derive compound indexing for this lab, but they should be able to **identify when it is needed** (e.g., $T = 96 \Rightarrow \dfrac{40}{96} = \dfrac{5}{12}$, possible but cumbersome).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

The figure below maps the physical/functional layout of the milling machine setup for spur gear cutting. Note: mermaid block diagrams avoid pure keywords like `end` and `subgraph` as bare node IDs — all node IDs are alphanumeric with letter prefix.
