# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
**Define the term "Module" of a spur gear. Why is it considered the fundamental design parameter in the metric gear system?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **module (m)** of a spur gear is defined as the ratio of the pitch circle diameter $D$ to the number of teeth $T$.

$$
m = \frac{D}{T} \;\; (\text{units: mm})
$$

It is the **fundamental design parameter** in the metric gear system because:
1. It directly determines **all linear gear dimensions** (addendum, dedendum, outside diameter, root diameter) — once $m$ is fixed, the gear geometry is fully determined.
2. It is **standardised** per ISO 54 (preferred series 1 and 2), enabling interchangeability of gears from different manufacturers.
3. Two gears mesh correctly **only if they have the same module**, regardless of their tooth counts.
4. The circular pitch $p_c = \pi m$ is independent of tooth count, simplifying design calculations. **(3 marks)**

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
**A spur gear has 24 teeth and module 3 mm. Calculate the pitch circle diameter, outside diameter, and whole depth.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Given: $T = 24$, $m = 3$ mm.

**Pitch circle diameter:**

$$
D = mT = 3 \times 24 = 72 \text{ mm}
$$

**Outside diameter:**

$$
D_o = m(T+2) = 3 \times 26 = 78 \text{ mm}
$$

**Whole depth:**

$$
h = 2.157 \, m = 2.157 \times 3 = 6.471 \text{ mm} \;\; \approx \; 6.47 \text{ mm}
$$

**(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A — **[KTU University Exam – Model 2024 Scheme]**

**Q. (a)** With a neat sketch, describe the procedure to cut a spur gear of module 4 mm and 36 teeth on a horizontal milling machine using the indexing head. Mention the cutter number and all index calculations. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** A pair of spur gears in mesh have 24 and 48 teeth respectively, with a module of 2 mm and pressure angle of 20°. Calculate: **(i)** Pitch circle diameters, **(ii)** Centre distance, **(iii)** Outside diameters, **(iv)** Tooth thickness. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part (a) Model Solution (7 marks):

**Step 1 — Blank Preparation (1 mark):**
- Module $m = 4$ mm, Teeth $T = 36$.
- Pitch diameter $D = mT = 4 \times 36 = 144$ mm.
- Outside diameter $D_o = m(T+2) = 4 \times 38 = 152$ mm.
- The mild steel/aluminium blank is machined to **152 mm outside diameter** on a lathe.
- Centre hole drilled and bored to fit the mandrel (typically 25–30 mm bore).

**Step 2 — Cutter Selection (1 mark):**
- From the 8-cutter set, for $T = 36$ teeth, **Cutter No. 3** is selected (cuts 35–54 teeth).

**Step 3 — Indexing Calculation (2 marks):**
- Simple indexing: $n = \dfrac{40}{T} = \dfrac{40}{36} = 1\tfrac{4}{36} = 1\tfrac{1}{9}$ revolutions.
- Using the **standard index plate with hole circles**, the 1/9 turn is achieved by **moving the crank through 4 holes on the 36-hole circle**.

**Step 4 — Setup Procedure (2 marks):**
1. Mount involute gear cutter on the milling machine arbor.
2. Mount blank on mandrel between indexing head spindle and tailstock centre.
3. Set the **cutter centre at the pitch radius** $r = D/2 = 72$ mm from the blank centre.
4. Align the cutter tooth with the blank using a dial indicator.
5. Calculate spindle speed: For HSS cutter ($V_c = 25$ m/min, $D_c = 75$ mm):

$$
N = \frac{1000 \times 25}{\pi \times 75} \approx 106 \text{ rpm}
$$

6. Use power feed = 0.10 mm/tooth.

**Step 5 — Cutting Sequence (1 mark):**
- Engage cutter, feed to full depth $h = 2.157 \times 4 = 8.628$ mm.
- Retract, index crank by $1\tfrac{1}{9}$ revs.
- Repeat for all 36 teeth.
- Take a finishing pass of 0.05 mm.
- Deburr and inspect.

---

#### Part (b) Model Solution (7 marks):

Given: $T_1 = 24$, $T_2 = 48$, $m = 2$ mm, $\phi = 20°$.

**(i) Pitch circle diameters (2 marks):**

$$
D_1 = mT_1 = 2 \times 24 = 48 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
D_2 = mT_2 = 2 \times 48 = 96 \text{ mm}
$$

**(ii) Centre distance (2 marks):**

$$
a = \frac{D_1 + D_2}{2} = \frac{48 + 96}{2} = 72 \text{ mm}
$$

**(iii) Outside diameters (2 marks):**

$$
D_{o1} = m(T_1+2) = 2 \times 26 = 52 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
D_{o2} = m(T_2+2) = 2 \times 50 = 100 \text{ mm}
$$

**(iv) Tooth thickness (1 mark):**

$$
t = 1.5708 \, m = 1.5708 \times 2 = 3.1416 \text{ mm}
$$

---

### Question B — **[KTU University Exam – Model 2024 Scheme – Alternative]**

**Q. (a)** Explain the involute gear tooth profile and the concept of pressure angle. How does the gear cutter set of 8 cutters approximate this profile across the full range of tooth counts? **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** A milling machine is to be set up to cut a spur gear of module 2.5 mm and 28 teeth using an HSS involute cutter of 80 mm diameter. Calculate: **(i)** Pitch diameter, outside diameter, root diameter, **(ii)** Indexing movement, **(iii)** Spindle speed if cutting speed is 22 m/min, **(iv)** Table feed if feed/tooth is 0.08 mm and cutter has 14 teeth. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part (a) Model Solution (7 marks):

**Involute Profile (3 marks):**
The involute is the curve traced by the end of a taut string being unwound from a cylinder (the **base circle**). It is the standard gear tooth profile because:
- It guarantees **conjugate action** — constant velocity ratio in mesh.
- The point of contact slides along a common tangent (line of action), giving **line contact** rather than point contact.
- It tolerates small **centre-distance errors** without breaking conjugate action.

**Pressure Angle (2 marks):**
The pressure angle $\phi$ is the angle between the line of action (common tangent to base circles at the contact point) and the common tangent to the pitch circles. It is typically **20° (ISO)**. Higher pressure angle gives thicker teeth at the base (stronger) but more bearing stress.

**8-Cutter Approximation (2 marks):**
A theoretically exact involute cutter is needed for each tooth count. Since this is impractical, the cutter set has **8 cutters**, each designed for a band of tooth counts. The cutter profile is computed for the **mean tooth count** of its band and the involute deviation at the band extremes is small enough for general engineering accuracy.

| Cutter | Teeth Range |
|---|---|
| 1 | 135 – Rack |
| 2 | 55 – 134 |
| 3 | 35 – 54 |
| 4 | 26 – 34 |
| 5 | 21 – 25 |
| 6 | 17 – 20 |
| 7 | 14 – 16 |
| 8 | 12 – 13 |

---

#### Part (b) Model Solution (7 marks):

Given: $m = 2.5$ mm, $T = 28$, $D_c = 80$ mm, $V_c = 22$ m/min, $f_t = 0.08$ mm/tooth, $Z = 14$.

**(i) Gear Diameters (3 marks):**

Pitch diameter:

$$
D = mT = 2.5 \times 28 = 70 \text{ mm}
$$

Outside diameter:

$$
D_o = m(T+2) = 2.5 \times 30 = 75 \text{ mm}
$$

Root diameter:

$$
D_f = m(T - 2.157) = 2.5 \times 25.843 = 64.6075 \text{ mm} \approx 64.61 \text{ mm}
$$

**(ii) Indexing Movement (1 mark):**

$$
n = \frac{40}{T} = \frac{40}{28} = 1\tfrac{12}{28} = 1\tfrac{3}{7} \text{ revs}
$$

Use 6 holes on the 14-hole circle (after 1 full turn).

**(iii) Spindle Speed (1.5 marks):**

$$
N = \frac{1000 \, V_c}{\pi D_c} = \frac{1000 \times 22}{\pi \times 80} = \frac{22000}{251.327} \approx 87.5 \text{ rpm}
$$

Round to nearest available: **90 rpm** (typical milling machine setting).

**(iv) Table Feed (1.5 marks):**

$$
f = f_t \cdot Z \cdot N = 0.08 \times 14 \times 87.5 = 98 \text{ mm/min}
$$

Or rounded to standard feed dial: **95 or 100 mm/min**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Zones for Spur Gear Cutting:**
> 1. **Confusing module with diametral pitch (DP)** — they are inverse systems: $DP = 25.4/m$. Students writing $DP$ formulas in a metric module question lose full marks.
> 2. **Forgetting clearance** — dedendum must be $1.157m$, not $m$. If you write $h = 2m$ instead of $h = 2.157m$, the cutter will bottom out and the gear teeth will not mesh.
> 3. **Wrong indexing circle** — for $n = \dfrac{40}{36} = 1\tfrac{4}{36}$, students often use 4 holes on the 40-hole circle. The correct circle is the one **divisible by the denominator** (here 36).
> 4. **Pitch radius vs. outside radius** — cutter centre must be at pitch radius from blank centre, NOT at outside radius. Setting the cutter at outside radius produces **pointed-topped, weak teeth**.
> 5. **Not including the blank bore in calculations** — examiners expect the bore diameter and keyway dimension if specified.
> 6. **Spindle speed unit errors** — $V_c$ is in m/min, but $D_c$ is in mm. The factor of 1000 in the formula $N = \dfrac{1000 V_c}{\pi D_c}$ accounts for unit conversion. Forgetting this gives a 1000× error.
> 7. **Skipping the finishing pass** — at least mention a finishing cut of 0.05–0.10 mm for surface finish and dimensional accuracy.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this section as your last-minute revision sheet before the lab viva or end-semester exam.**

- [ ] **Module (m)** is the fundamental gear design parameter — equal to $D/T$ in mm.
- [ ] All gear dimensions scale linearly with module: $D = mT$, $D_o = m(T+2)$, $D_f = m(T-2.157)$.
- [ ] Standard pressure angle = **20°** (ISO metric gears); older British/AGMA used **14.5°**.
- [ ] Full-depth teeth: addendum = $m$, dedendum = $1.157m$, whole depth = $2.157m$.
- [ ] Circular pitch $p_c = \pi m$ — independent of tooth count.
- [ ] Tooth thickness at pitch circle = space width = $\dfrac{p_c}{2} = 1.5708 \, m$.
- [ ] **Indexing "rule of 40"**: $n = \dfrac{40}{T}$ revolutions of crank per tooth.
- [ ] Standard index plates have hole circles: 15-16-17-18-19-20 / 21-23-27-29-31-33 / 37-39-41-43-47-49 (varies by manufacturer).
- [ ] **8-cutter set** matches tooth counts to cutter numbers; selection based on T.
- [ ] Cutter centre positioned at **pitch radius** $r = D/2$ from blank centre.
- [ ] Spindle speed $N = \dfrac{1000 V_c}{\pi D_c}$; Table feed $f = f_t Z N$.
- [ ] Always include **finishing pass** of 0.05–0.10 mm for quality gear teeth.
- [ ] **Pitch circle is a virtual circle** — it is not physically marked on the gear but is the reference for all geometry.
- [ ] Centre distance of meshing gears $a = \dfrac{m(T_1+T_2)}{2}$.
- [ ] Two gears mesh only if they have the **same module** (NOT the same number of teeth).
- [ ] **Compound indexing** is required when $T$ does not divide 40 evenly — e.g., $T = 96, 97, 99, 127, 128$ etc.

---

**End of Module Experiment 2 Notes — Spur Gear Cutting Operation on Milling Machine.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Study and preparation of program, simulation and exercise on CNC lathe: Turning, step turning, taper turning, thread cutting, ball and cup turning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIEL407 - MACHINE TOOLS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Study and preparation of program, simulation and exercise on CNC lathe: Turning, step turning, taper turning, thread cutting, ball and cup turning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of CNC Lathe Operations

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **CNC (Computer Numerical Control) Lathe** is a microprocessor-controlled machine tool that performs turning, facing, boring, threading, and profiling operations on a workpiece by interpreting a sequence of alphanumeric instruction codes (G-codes, M-codes, T-codes, S-codes, and F-codes) generated through part programming languages such as **ISO 6983 (DIN 66025)** or conversational programming.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PCIEL407):** CNC lathe operations are the foundational turning exercises performed on a 2-axis (X-Z) turning centre. The X-axis controls the **radial (cross-slide) movement** (diameter) and the Z-axis controls the **longitudinal (carriage) movement** (length). The workpiece rotates on the spindle while a single-point cutting tool traverses along programmed paths.

## 1.2 The Six Core Operations Covered in This Experiment

| # | Operation | Purpose | Tool Trajectory |
|---|-----------|---------|-----------------|
| 1 | **Turning** | Reduce diameter along a length | Straight line parallel to Z-axis |
| 2 | **Step Turning** | Produce multiple diameters on one job | Multiple Z-segments with X-step changes |
| 3 | **Taper Turning** | Generate conical/angular surface | Inclined line at taper angle α/2 |
| 4 | **Thread Cutting** | Cut helical grooves (external/internal) | Synchronised helical interpolation |
| 5 | **Ball Turning** | Form a spherical (hemispherical) end | Circular interpolation G02/G03 |
| 6 | **Cup Turning** | Hollow a cylindrical pocket | Internal profiling (boring cycle) |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Potter's Wheel"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Box:** Imagine a traditional **potter's wheel** where the clay (workpiece) spins and the potter's hands (tool) shape it. In manual lathe, the potter decides by eye. In a **CNC lathe**, the potter first draws the *blueprint* (the part program) and feeds it to a **robotic potter's brain** (the controller). The brain then moves the hands **precisely to the micrometre**, repeating the exact shape 1000 times without fatigue. Every line of G-code is a *one-line instruction* — "move right by 5 mm at 0.2 mm/rev feed."

## 1.4 Why This Experiment Matters in KTU Labs

The PCIEL407 lab trains students to:

- **Read & write** a complete ISO part program from an engineering drawing.
- **Simulate** the program in CAM/CNC software (e.g., **MasterCAM, FANUC Simulator, Siemens SinuTrain, Festo COSIMIR**) before live machining.
- **Verify** dimensional accuracy using **vernier calipers (LC = 0.02 mm)** and **outside micrometers (LC = 0.01 mm)**.
- **Calculate** spindle speed, feed, depth of cut, and verify against **machining data handbooks**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Note:** In the viva voce of this experiment, the most asked question is *"What is the difference between G90 and G94 turning cycle?"* and *"Why must the spindle speed override be kept at 100% during thread cutting?"* — Be ready with crisp answers.

## 1.5 Block Format of a Typical CNC Lathe Program

A program is a sequence of **blocks** (lines). Each block contains one or more **words** (address + value):

```
%
O0001 (PROGRAM NAME);
N10 G21 G40 G97 G99;
N20 M08;
N30 T0101;
N40 M03 S1500;
N50 G00 X50.0 Z2.0;
N60 G01 Z-40.0 F0.2;
N70 G00 X55.0;
N80 G00 Z2.0;
N90 M05;
N100 M09;
N110 M30;
%
```

Each **word** like `G21` is a word: `G` (address) + `21` (numeric value).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of a CNC Lathe — What Each Axis Does

| Axis | Direction | Controls | Typical Travel |
|------|-----------|----------|----------------|
| **Z-axis** | Along spindle axis (longitudinal) | Carriage / longitudinal slide | 300–1000 mm |
| **X-axis** | Perpendicular to spindle (radial) | Cross-slide / diameter | 100–300 mm |
| **(Optional) C-axis** | Rotational around Z | Spindle index / live tooling | 0–360° |
| **(Optional) Y-axis** | Vertical (on mill-turn) | Eccentric / off-centre features | ±50 mm |

> [!NOTE]
> **X-axis on a lathe is programmed in DIAMETER mode by default** (G20/G21 selects unit, G50 sets coordinate system origin). This is a critical KTU exam point — many students confuse **radius mode (G22)** with **diameter mode (default)**.

## 2.2 Cutting Parameters — The Three Sacred Numbers

### 2.2.1 Cutting Speed (V) — *How fast the chip flows*

The recommended cutting speed depends on **workpiece material, tool material, and surface finish**.

| Material | HSS Tool (m/min) | Carbide Tool (m/min) |
|----------|------------------|----------------------|
| Mild Steel | 25–35 | 150–250 |
| Cast Iron | 20–30 | 80–150 |
| Aluminium | 100–200 | 300–600 |
| Stainless Steel | 15–25 | 80–150 |

### 2.2.2 Spindle Speed (N) — *RPM of the chuck*

$$N = \dfrac{1000 \times V}{\pi \times D}$$

Where:
- **N** = spindle speed in **rev/min (rpm)**
- **V** = cutting speed in **m/min**
- **D** = initial (or current) workpiece diameter in **mm**

### 2.2.3 Feed Rate (f or F) — *How fast the tool advances per revolution*

$$f = N \times f_n$$

Where:
- **f** = feed rate in **mm/min** (used in program with G98/G99)
- **N** = spindle speed in rpm
- **f_n** = feed per revolution in **mm/rev**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **G98 vs G99 — High-Yield KTU Point:**
> - **G98** → Feed in **mm/min** (independent of spindle speed)
> - **G99** → Feed in **mm/rev** (most common in lathe programming)
> *G99 is preferred because chip thickness is constant per revolution, giving consistent surface finish.*

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — All Key Equations

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|---------|----------------|-------------|
| 1 | $N = \dfrac{1000 V}{\pi D}$ | Spindle speed | All turning operations |
| 2 | $f = N f_n$ | Feed rate (mm/min) | Program F-value |
| 3 | $MRR = f \times d \times V$ | Material removal rate (cm³/min) | Power calculations |
| 4 | $\tan(\alpha / 2) = \dfrac{D - d}{2 L}$ | Taper half-angle | Taper turning |
| 5 | $L_{thread} = (n \times P) + L_{chase}$ | Thread length calculation | Thread cutting |
| 6 | $T_{mach} = \dfrac{L}{f \times N}$ | Machining time (min) | Cost estimation |
| 7 | $P_{c} = \dfrac{MRR \times k_c}{60 \times 10^3}$ | Cutting power (kW) | Motor selection |
| 8 | $L_{arc} = \pi R \dfrac{\theta}{180}$ | Arc length (mm) | Ball / arc turning |
| 9 | $h = \dfrac{P \times n_{passes}}{2}$ | Thread depth (mm) | Threading depth |
| 10 | $R_{ball} = \dfrac{D_{ball}}{2}$ | Ball radius | Cup/ball geometry |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units reminder:** When **D is in mm** and **V is in m/min**, the **1000 factor** in formula (1) converts the diameter from mm to m. *Always check unit consistency* — this is the #1 reason KTU students lose marks in numerical problems.

## 2.4 Material-Specific Machining Constants

| Material | Specific cutting force $k_c$ (N/mm²) | Tool material |
|----------|-------------------------------------|---------------|
| Mild Steel | 2000 | Carbide P20 |
| Cast Iron | 1100 | Carbide K10 |
| Aluminium | 750 | HSS / Carbide |
| Brass | 900 | HSS |
| Stainless Steel 304 | 2400 | Carbide M30 |

## 2.5 The Tool Geometry on a CNC Lathe

The standard **Turning Tool** (Insert) has these angles:

- **Side Rake Angle (γ)** → 0° to +15° (positive for finishing)
- **End Relief Angle (α)** → 6° to 8° (prevents rubbing)
- **Side Cutting Edge Angle (C_s)** → 15° to 30°
- **End Cutting Edge Angle (C_e)** → 0° to 15°
- **Nose Radius (r)** → 0.4 / 0.8 / 1.2 mm (smaller = better finish, weaker tool)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** Increasing the **nose radius (r)** improves surface finish (lower Ra) but **increases cutting forces** and may cause **chatter**. A common rule of thumb is **r ≤ 0.5 × feed/rev**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix for Each CNC Lathe Operation

## 3.1 Operation 1: STRAIGHT TURNING (Cylindrical Reduction)

### Problem Statement
Turn a **mild steel bar of initial diameter Ø60 mm** down to **Ø50 mm** over a length of **40 mm**. Use a carbide insert with **V = 180 m/min** and **f = 0.2 mm/rev**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Calculate Spindle Speed (N)**

$$N = \dfrac{1000 \times V}{\pi \times D} = \dfrac{1000 \times 180}{\pi \times 60}$$

$$N = \dfrac{180000}{188.496} \approx 954.93 \text{ rpm}$$

We round to the nearest standard value: **N = 1000 rpm** (controller accepts integer).

**Step 2: Calculate Feed Rate (F)**

$$f = N \times f_n = 1000 \times 0.2 = 200 \text{ mm/min}$$

**Step 3: Calculate Machining Time**

$$T_{mach} = \dfrac{L}{f} = \dfrac{40}{200} = 0.2 \text{ min} = 12 \text{ sec}$$

**Step 4: Depth of Cut per Pass**

$$d = \dfrac{D - d_{final}}{2} = \dfrac{60 - 50}{2} = 5 \text{ mm}$$

If a roughing pass takes 4 mm and finishing takes 1 mm, that is **2 passes**.

**Step 5: MRR (single pass)**

$$MRR = f \times d \times V = 200 \times 4 \times 180 = 144000 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min} = 144 \text{ cm}^3/\text{min}$$

### Resulting CNC Program (FANUC G-code)

```
O0001 (STRAIGHT TURNING);
N10 G21 G40 G97 G99;          (Metric, cutter comp off, CSS off, feed/rev)
N20 M08;                       (Coolant on)
N30 T0101;                     (Tool 1, offset 1 — CNMG insert)
N40 M03 S1000;                 (Spindle CW at 1000 rpm)
N50 G00 X62.0 Z2.0;            (Rapid to safe approach)
N60 G01 Z-40.0 F0.2;           (Linear cut at 0.2 mm/rev)
N70 G01 X50.0 F0.1;            (Face to final dia at finishing feed)
N80 G00 X62.0;                 (Retract in X)
N90 G00 Z2.0;                  (Retract in Z)
N100 M05 M09;                  (Spindle & coolant off)
N110 M30;                      (Program end)
```

---

## 3.2 Operation 2: STEP TURNING (Multi-Diameter Profile)

### Problem Statement
A workpiece is **Ø60 mm × 100 mm long**. Turn it to the following profile:
- Ø50 mm for length 30 mm
- Ø45 mm for length 25 mm
- Ø40 mm for length 25 mm
- (Total machined length = 80 mm)

### Step-by-Step Analysis

**Step 1: Identify the coordinate of each shoulder**

| Step | Dia (mm) | Z-position (mm) | Approach X (mm) |
|------|----------|------------------|------------------|
| Initial | Ø60 | 0 (face) | X62 (clearance) |
| Step 1 | Ø50 | Z -30 | X50 |
| Step 2 | Ø45 | Z -55 | X45 |
| Step 3 | Ø40 | Z -80 | X40 |

**Step 2: Spindle Speed for smallest diameter (highest N)**

$$N_{max} = \dfrac{1000 \times 180}{\pi \times 40} = 1432.4 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 3: Spindle Speed for largest diameter (lowest N)**

$$N_{min} = \dfrac{1000 \times 180}{\pi \times 60} = 954.9 \text{ rpm}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use G96 (Constant Surface Speed) + G50 (max RPM clamp)** for stepped and tapered profiles. This automatically adjusts N as diameter reduces — keeping **V constant**, which extends tool life and improves finish.

### Resulting Program (Using G96 CSS)

```
O0002 (STEP TURNING);
N10 G21 G40 G96 G99;
N20 G50 S2000;                 (Max RPM clamp = 2000)
N30 M08 T0101 M03;
N40 G00 X62.0 Z2.0;
N50 G01 Z-30.0 F0.2;           (First dia Ø50)
N60 X50.0;                     (Shoulder)
N70 Z-55.0;                    (Second dia Ø45)
N80 X45.0;                     (Shoulder)
N90 Z-80.0;                    (Third dia Ø40)
N100 X62.0;                    (Retract)
N110 Z2.0;
N120 M05 M09 M30;
```

---

## 3.3 Operation 3: TAPER TURNING (Conical Surface)

### Problem Statement
Turn a **taper from Ø60 mm to Ø40 mm** over a length of **L = 50 mm**.

### Step 1: Calculate Taper Ratio and Half-Angle

$$\tan(\alpha / 2) = \dfrac{D - d}{2L} = \dfrac{60 - 40}{2 \times 50} = \dfrac{20}{100} = 0.2$$

$$\alpha / 2 = \arctan(0.2) = 11.31° \quad \Rightarrow \quad \alpha = 22.62°$$

### Step 2: Calculate the X-delta for the taper move

If the tool starts at **X = 62** (clearance), it must travel to **X = 40** while moving from **Z = 2 to Z = -50**:

$$\Delta X = 62 - 40 = 22 \text{ mm (radial travel in X)}$$

$$\Delta Z = 50 + 2 = 52 \text{ mm (axial travel in Z)}$$

### Step 3: Program the Taper with G01 (Single-Pass Method)

The end point coordinates are calculated by:

$$X_{end} = D_{final} = 40 \text{ mm}$$

$$Z_{end} = -L = -50 \text{ mm}$$

```
N50 G00 X62.0 Z2.0;
N60 G01 X40.0 Z-50.0 F0.2;     (Taper cut in one block)
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Alternative: Use G90 (Turning Cycle) with R = Taper amount per pass** for roughing multiple passes, then G01 for finishing.

---

## 3.4 Operation 4: THREAD CUTTING (External V-Thread)

### Problem Statement
Cut an **M30 × 1.5 mm external thread** on a **Ø30 mm rod** using the G32 single-point threading cycle. Thread length = 25 mm. Pitch P = 1.5 mm. Minor diameter ≈ 30 − 1.0825 × 1.5 = 28.376 mm.

### Step 1: Calculate Thread Depth

For ISO metric V-thread, **h = 0.6134 × P** (theoretical thread height). For CNC depth of cut per pass (empirical):

$$h_{total} = 0.6134 \times 1.5 = 0.9201 \text{ mm (radial depth)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For CNC threading, depth is split into **multiple passes** with progressively **decreasing depth** (e.g., 0.5, 0.4, 0.3, 0.2, 0.1 mm radial). This prevents tool breakage and improves accuracy.

### Step 2: Calculate Spindle Speed (must be CONSTANT)

$$N = \dfrac{1000 \times 60}{\pi \times 30} \approx 636.6 \text{ rpm}$$

Rounded to a **divisible-by-thread-pitch** value to maintain synchronisation. With encoder **1024 pulses/rev**, the controller allows any N; but spindle **override must be disabled** (FANUC parameter 5203 #0 = 1) during threading.

### Step 3: Calculate Lead-in and Lead-out Distances

$$L_{lead} = 2 \times P = 3 \text{ mm}, \quad L_{chase} = 2 \times P = 3 \text{ mm}$$

Effective Z-travel: from **Z3** (start) to **Z-28** (end).

### Step 4: Program (FANUC G32 Method)

```
O0004 (THREAD CUTTING M30x1.5);
N10 G21 G40 G97 G99;
N20 M08 T0303;                 (Threading tool)
N30 M03 S636;
N40 G00 X32.0 Z3.0;            (Approach)
N50 G32 X29.4 Z-28.0 F1.5;     (Pass 1, depth = 0.3 mm radial)
N60 G00 X32.0;                 (Retract)
N70 Z3.0;                      (Return to start)
N80 X29.2;                     (Pass 2, depth = 0.4 mm)
N90 G32 Z-28.0 F1.5;
N100 G00 X32.0;
N110 Z3.0;
N120 X29.05;                   (Pass 3)
N130 G32 Z-28.0 F1.5;
N140 G00 X32.0 Z3.0;
N150 X29.0;                    (Final pass, depth → Ø29.0 = 30 - 2×0.5)
N160 G32 Z-28.0 F1.5;
N170 G00 X50.0 Z50.0;
N180 M05 M09 M30;
```

### Alternative — G76 Multi-Repetitive Threading Cycle

```
G76 P020060 Q100 R0.05;
G76 X28.376 Z-25.0 P920 Q500 F1.5;
```

Where:
- **P020060** → 02 passes of spring, 00 chamfer, 60° thread angle
- **Q100** → 0.1 mm cut-in depth (first pass)
- **R0.05** → 0.05 mm finishing allowance
- **X28.376** → Minor diameter (thread root)
- **Z-25.0** → Thread end point
- **P920** → Thread height 0.920 mm (in 0.001 mm units → 920)
- **Q500** → First pass depth 0.5 mm (in 0.001 mm units → 500)
- **F1.5** → Pitch 1.5 mm

---

## 3.5 Operation 5: BALL TURNING (Hemispherical End)

### Problem Statement
Form a **hemispherical ball of Ø30 mm** at the end face of a workpiece, using circular interpolation (G02/G03).

### Step 1: Geometry Definition

For a ball of diameter **D = 30 mm**:
- **Radius R = 15 mm**
- The ball centre is at the **Z = 0 face, X = 0 (axis)**
- The **equator** is at **Z = -R = -15 mm** and **X = D = 30 mm**
- The **pole** is at **Z = -2R = -30 mm, X = 0**

### Step 2: Define Tool Start, End, and Centre

To use G03 (counter-clockwise, viewed from +X to spindle), we need:
- **Start point:** X = 0, Z = -15 (pole)
- **End point:** X = 30, Z = 0 (equator edge)
- **Arc centre (I, K):** I = 15 (in X), K = -15 (in Z) relative to start

> [!NOTE]
> **I, K are incremental** (relative to start point), not absolute.

### Step 3: Program

```
O0005 (BALL TURNING);
N10 G21 G40 G97 G99;
N20 M08 T0202;                 (Profile tool, sharp tip)
N30 M03 S1500;
N40 G00 X0 Z2.0;               (Approach face)
N50 G01 Z-15.0 F0.1;           (Plunge to ball centre depth)
N60 G03 X30.0 Z-30.0 I15.0 K-15.0 F0.1;   (CCW arc, hemisphere)
N70 G01 X32.0;                 (Retract)
N80 Z2.0;
N90 M05 M09 M30;
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why I = 15, K = -15?** The arc centre is **R = 15** away from the start. Start is at (0, -15). Centre must be **at (15, -15) (X = +15 from start) and at (0, -30) (Z = -15 from start)**. So I = +15 and K = -15. Common KTU mistake: students forget the **negative sign** for K and end up cutting a **concave bowl instead of a convex ball**.

---

## 3.6 Operation 6: CUP TURNING (Internal Boring)

### Problem Statement
Bore an internal cup (cylindrical pocket + hemispherical bottom) of **Ø25 mm internal diameter** and **20 mm depth** in a **Ø40 mm × 60 mm** mild steel blank.

### Step 1: Coordinate Planning

| Feature | X (diameter) | Z (depth) |
|---------|--------------|-----------|
| Bore entrance | X = 24 (clearance) | Z = 0 |
| Straight bore wall | X = 25 | Z = -20 |
| Hemispherical bottom | X = 25 → X = 0 (axis) | Z = -20 → Z = -32.5 |

> [!NOTE]
> For an internal ball (cup bottom), the radius R = 12.5 mm (half of Ø25). The deepest point of the cup is **Z = -20 - 12.5 = -32.5 mm**.

### Step 2: Program

```
O0006 (CUP TURNING);
N10 G21 G40 G97 G99;
N20 M08 T0404;                 (Boring bar, internal tool)
N30 M03 S1200;
N40 G00 X22.0 Z2.0;            (Approach in X)
N50 G01 Z-20.0 F0.15;          (Straight bore)
N60 G02 X0 Z-32.5 I-12.5 K0 F0.1;  (CW arc, hemispherical bottom)
N70 G00 Z2.0;                  (Retract Z)
N80 X20.0;                     (Retract X)
N90 M05 M09 M30;
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **G02 vs G03 direction for cup:** Looking from **+X to spindle (operator's view)**, internal cup bottom is a **CW (G02)** arc from bore wall to centre. I = -12.5 (X-centre is 12.5 *less* than start X = 25, so I = 25 - 25 = 0? — careful, start is X=25, centre is X=0, so I = 0 - 25 = **-25 in diameter mode** but **-12.5 in radius mode** if G50 sets the centre). **This is the trickiest KTU viva question — be ready.**

---

## 3.7 Master Reference Table — All G-Codes Used in This Experiment

| G-Code | Function | Used In Operation |
|--------|----------|-------------------|
| **G00** | Rapid positioning | All |
| **G01** | Linear interpolation (feed) | Turning, step, taper |
| **G02** | CW circular interpolation | Cup, ball |
| **G03** | CCW circular interpolation | Ball |
| **G20** | Inch input | (Optional) |
| **G21** | Metric input | All |
| **G28** | Return to reference point | All |
| **G32** | Single-point thread cutting | Threading |
| **G40** | Cutter compensation cancel | All |
| **G41** | Cutter compensation left | (Optional) |
| **G50** | Max RPM clamp / coord system | Threading, G96 |
| **G76** | Multi-repetitive thread cycle | Threading |
| **G90** | Outer diameter turning cycle | Step turning |
| **G94** | End face turning cycle | Facing |
| **G96** | Constant surface speed | Step, taper |
| **G97** | Constant RPM (cancel G96) | Threading, ball |
| **G98** | Feed per minute | Optional |
| **G99** | Feed per revolution | Default |

## 3.8 Master Reference Table — M-Codes Used

| M-Code | Function |
|--------|----------|
| **M00** | Program stop |
| **M01** | Optional stop |
| **M03** | Spindle ON (CW) |
| **M04** | Spindle ON (CCW) |
| **M05** | Spindle stop |
| **M08** | Coolant ON |
| **M09** | Coolant OFF |
| **M30** | Program end & rewind |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics (CNC Lathe Programming Workflow)

## 4.1 Block Diagram of CNC Lathe System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Engineering Drawing] --> B[Process Planning]
    B --> C[Selection of Tooling and Workholding]
    C --> D[Calculation of Cutting Parameters]
    D --> E[Manual Part Programming in ISO G Code]
    E --> F[Verification by CNC Simulator]
    F --> G{Simulation OK?}
    G -- No --> E
    G -- Yes --> H[Transfer to CNC Controller via DNC or USB]
    H --> I[CNC Lathe Machine]
    I --> J[Spindle and Axis Drive]
    J --> K[Workpiece Machining]
    K --> L[Dimensional Inspection using Vernier and Micrometer]
    L --> M{Dimensions within Tolerance?}
    M -- No --> N[Adjust Tool Offset or Parameters]
    N --> E
    M -- Yes --> O[Final Part Accepted]
```

## 4.2 CNC Lathe Process Decision Flow — From Drawing to Finished Part

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Step 1 Read Drawing] --> P2[Step 2 Identify Operations]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3 Choose Tool and Holder]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4 Fix Workpiece in Chuck]
    P4 --> P5[Step 5 Set Tool Offset using Probe or Touch Off]
    P5 --> P6[Step 6 Write Program Block by Block]
    P6 --> P7[Step 7 Dry Run in Air with Reduced Feed]
    P7 --> P8[Step 8 Inspect First Component]
    P8 --> P9[Step 9 Full Production Run]
    P9 --> P10[Step 10 Record Observations in Lab Manual]
```

## 4.3 Functional Block Architecture of a CNC Lathe Controller

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT["Input Section"]
        IN1[Part Program via RS232 or USB]
        IN2[Manual Data Input MDI Panel]
        IN3[Operator Console with HMI]
    end

    subgraph CONTROL["Control Unit MCU"]
        CU1[CPU Interpreter]
        CU2[Interpolator X Z]
        CU3[Feedrate Generator]
        CU4[Spindle Speed Controller]
        CU5[Tool Offset Memory]
    end

    subgraph DRIVE["Drive Section"]
        DR1[X axis Servo Drive]
        DR2[Z axis Servo Drive]
        DR3[Spindle VFD Drive]
    end

    subgraph MACHINE["Machine Tool Section"]
        MT1[Cross Slide X]
        MT2[Carriage Z]
        MT3[Spindle and Chuck]
        MT4[Coolant Pump]
        MT5[Tool Turret]
    end

    IN1 --> CU1
    IN2 --> CU1
    IN3 --> CU1
    CU1 --> CU2
    CU1 --> CU3
    CU1 --> CU4
    CU1 --> CU5
    CU2 --> DR1
    CU2 --> DR2
    CU4 --> DR3
    DR1 --> MT1
    DR2 --> MT2
    DR3 --> MT3
    CU1 --> MT4
    CU1 --> MT5
```

## 4.4 Decision Diagram — Selection of Threading Cycle (G32 vs G76)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Start Need to Cut a Thread] --> S1{Is the part a simple production batch?}
    S1 -- Yes Single Part or Few Parts --> S2[Use G32 Single Point Threading]
    S1 -- No Production Series --> S3[Use G76 Multi Repetitive Cycle]
    S2 --> S4[Manually calculate depth and passes]
    S3 --> S5[Define thread parameters in two blocks]
    S4 --> S6[Write separate block for each pass]
    S5 --> S7[Controller calculates passes automatically]
    S6 --> S8[Verify with thread gauge]
    S7 --> S8
```

## 4.5 Coordinate System of a CNC Lathe — Block Representation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CS["CNC Lathe Coordinate System"]
        XAX["X axis Cross Slide Direction Diameter Mode"]
        ZAX["Z axis Carriage Direction Along Spindle"]
        ORIG["Origin at Spindle Nose Face Centre"]
        CHK["Chuck Holds Workpiece on Spindle"]
    end
    ORIG --> XAX
    ORIG --> ZAX
    CHK --- ORIG
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Note on Diagrams:** When asked to "draw a block diagram" in the exam, students often confuse the **physical 3D layout** of a CNC lathe with the **functional block diagram** of its controller. Always clarify **what level of abstraction** is being asked. Use Mermaid flowcharts to depict the *control flow* and labelled arrows to depict the *axis motion*.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define CNC Lathe. List any two G-codes and two M-codes used in CNC lathe programming. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
A CNC (Computer Numerical Control) lathe is a machine tool in which the movements of the cutting tool and the rotation of the spindle are controlled by a computer following a part program written in ISO 6983 standard codes.

- **G-codes (Preparatory):** G00 — Rapid positioning; G01 — Linear interpolation.
- **M-codes (Miscellaneous):** M03 — Spindle ON clockwise; M05 — Spindle stop.

---

### Q2. What is the difference between **G90 and G94** turning cycles? `[KTU University Exam — June 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | G90 (Turning Cycle) | G94 (Facing Cycle) |
|---------|---------------------|---------------------|
| Cutting direction | Parallel to Z-axis (axial) | Parallel to X-axis (radial) |
| Resulting surface | Cylindrical diameter reduction | Flat end face |
| Tool path | Longitudinal turning | Facing |
| Typical use | Reducing diameter along length | Producing flat end face |

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** For a **CNC turning operation** on a mild steel workpiece of initial diameter **Ø80 mm** to be reduced to **Ø70 mm** over a length of **60 mm**, calculate the following: **[7 Marks]**
- (i) Spindle speed (N) for V = 200 m/min using carbide insert.
- (ii) Feed rate (F) for f_n = 0.25 mm/rev.
- (iii) Machining time per pass.
- (iv) MRR for a single roughing pass of 4 mm radial depth.

**(b)** Write a complete **CNC part program** (FANUC G-code) for the above turning operation. Briefly explain the function of G21, G97, G99, and M30. **[7 Marks]**

### Solution A

**(a) Numerical Solution**

**(i) Spindle Speed N**

$$N = \dfrac{1000 \times 200}{\pi \times 80} = \dfrac{200000}{251.327} \approx 795.77 \text{ rpm}$$

We round to **N = 800 rpm** (standard controller value).

**(ii) Feed Rate F**

$$f = N \times f_n = 800 \times 0.25 = 200 \text{ mm/min}$$

**(iii) Machining Time per Pass**

$$T_{mach} = \dfrac{L}{f} = \dfrac{60}{200} = 0.3 \text{ min} = 18 \text{ seconds}$$

**(iv) Material Removal Rate (MRR)**

$$MRR = f \times d \times V = 200 \times 4 \times 200 = 160000 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min} = 160 \text{ cm}^3/\text{min}$$

**(b) Part Program**

```
O0001 (TURNING DIA80 TO DIA70);
N10 G21 G40 G97 G99;          (Metric, cutter comp off, RPM constant, feed/rev)
N20 M08;                       (Coolant ON)
N30 T0101;                     (Tool 1, Offset 1)
N40 M03 S800;                  (Spindle CW 800 rpm)
N50 G00 X82.0 Z2.0;            (Rapid to approach)
N60 G01 Z-60.0 F0.25;          (Roughing cut, feed 0.25 mm/rev)
N70 G01 X70.0 F0.1;            (Finishing pass to final dia, lower feed)
N80 G00 X82.0;                 (Retract X)
N90 G00 Z2.0;                  (Retract Z)
N100 M05 M09;                  (Spindle & coolant OFF)
N110 M30;                      (End of program & rewind)
```

**Function of Codes:**
- **G21** → Sets all dimensional inputs to **metric (mm)**.
- **G97** → Spindle speed in **constant RPM** (cancels G96 CSS).
- **G99** → Feed rate in **mm/revolution** (preferred on lathes).
- **M30** → **Program end** and rewind to start.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch how **taper turning** is performed on a CNC lathe using **G01 linear interpolation**. For a taper from **Ø60 mm to Ø40 mm** over a length of **50 mm**, calculate the **taper angle and the end coordinates** for the tool path. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Write a CNC part program to **cut an external M20 × 1.5 mm thread** over a length of **30 mm** on a Ø20 mm mild steel rod. Use the **G32 single-point threading method** with **3 cutting passes**. State any **two safety precautions** to be observed during thread cutting. **[7 Marks]**

### Solution B

**(a) Taper Turning with G01**

The taper turning operation produces a conical surface by moving the tool simultaneously in **X and Z** axes. On a CNC lathe, this is achieved with a single **G01 block** that specifies both the final X (diameter) and Z (length) coordinates.

**Given:** D = 60 mm, d = 40 mm, L = 50 mm

**Taper half-angle:**

$$\tan(\alpha/2) = \dfrac{D - d}{2L} = \dfrac{60 - 40}{2 \times 50} = \dfrac{20}{100} = 0.2$$

$$\alpha/2 = \arctan(0.2) = 11.31° \quad \Rightarrow \quad \alpha = 22.62°$$

**Taper per mm length:**

$$\dfrac{D - d}{L} = \dfrac{20}{50} = 0.4 \text{ mm/mm} \quad \text{(diameter change per mm length)}$$

**End coordinates for the tool path:**

- Start: (X = 62, Z = 2) — safe approach with 2 mm clearance
- End: (X = 40, Z = -50) — final position at small end of taper

**G-code for taper:**

```
N50 G00 X62.0 Z2.0;
N60 G01 X40.0 Z-50.0 F0.2;     (Single-block taper cut)
```

**(b) Thread Cutting Program — M20 × 1.5**

**Given:**
- Major diameter = Ø20 mm
- Pitch P = 1.5 mm
- Thread length = 30 mm
- Theoretical depth = 0.6134 × 1.5 = 0.92 mm (radial)
- **3 passes** with decreasing depth: 0.5, 0.3, 0.12 mm radial
- Minor diameter at root = 20 − 2 × 0.92 = 18.16 mm

**Spindle speed (for V = 50 m/min):**

$$N = \dfrac{1000 \times 50}{\pi \times 20} \approx 795.77 \text{ rpm} \quad \Rightarrow \quad 800 \text{ rpm}$$

**Lead-in:** 3 mm (so tool starts at Z = +3)
**Chase-out:** 3 mm (so tool exits at Z = -33)

**CNC Program:**

```
O0007 (THREAD M20x1.5);
N10 G21 G40 G97 G99;
N20 M08 T0303;                  (Threading tool)
N30 M03 S800;
N40 G00 X22.0 Z3.0;             (Approach)
N50 G32 X19.0 Z-33.0 F1.5;      (Pass 1, depth = 0.5 mm radial)
N60 G00 X22.0;                  (Retract X)
N70 Z3.0;                       (Return to start)
N80 X18.4;                      (Pass 2, depth = 0.3 mm)
N90 G32 Z-33.0 F1.5;
N100 G00 X22.0;
N110 Z3.0;
N120 X18.16;                    (Pass 3, final depth)
N130 G32 Z-33.0 F1.5;
N140 G00 X50.0 Z50.0;
N150 M05 M09 M30;
```

**Safety Precautions during Thread Cutting:**

1. **Spindle speed override must be DISABLED** — The controller parameter 5203 bit 0 must be set to 1 on FANUC. Any change in RPM during threading will desynchronise the tool lead and cause a **crash (thread scissoring)**.

2. **Tool must be sharp and correctly ground** to 60° included angle (for metric threads). A dull or wrong-angle tool will produce **poor thread profile, excessive cutting force, and breakage of the threading insert**, which is the smallest and most fragile tool in the lathe turret.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit errors in spindle speed formula** — Using D in cm or m without conversion is the #1 mistake. Always convert D to **mm** when V is in **m/min** (factor = 1000).
> 2. **Confusing I, K in G02/G03** — I is the **X-increment from start to centre**; K is the **Z-increment from start to centre**. Forgetting the sign causes wrong arc direction.
> 3. **Forgetting to disable spindle override** in threading — Examiner deducts 2 marks.
> 4. **Writing X in radius mode instead of diameter mode** in a lathe program — A common blinder. CNC lathes default to **diameter mode for X**. Writing X = 30 when the diameter should be Ø30 (i.e., radius 15) cuts a **half-diameter** part.
> 5. **Not including M08 coolant and M30 end** in the program — Trivial but marks-losing.
> 6. **Pitch (F) given in mm/min** instead of **mm/rev** in threading — Threading *must* use **G99 + F = pitch in mm/rev**. Writing F150 instead of F1.5 for M20 × 1.5 thread will cause a catastrophic crash.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Bullets — Read this section 3 times before the exam.**

- ✅ **CNC Lathe** = microprocessor-controlled turning centre; **X-axis = diameter (default)**, **Z-axis = length**.
- ✅ **Spindle speed formula:** $N = 1000 V / (\pi D)$ — V in m/min, D in mm → N in rpm.
- ✅ **Feed formula:** $f = N \times f_n$ — In **G99 mode**, F is mm/rev; in **G98**, F is mm/min.
- ✅ **Constant Surface Speed (G96)** is used for **diameters that change** (step turning, taper turning). Clamp max RPM with **G50**.
- ✅ **Threading uses G97** (constant RPM) — never G96.
- ✅ **Taper angle:** $\tan(\alpha/2) = (D - d) / (2L)$.
- ✅ **Thread depth (theoretical):** $h = 0.6134 \times P$ for ISO metric V-thread.
- ✅ **Thread minor diameter:** $d_{minor} = D_{major} - 2 \times 0.6134 \times P$.
- ✅ **G32** = single-point thread; **G76** = canned multi-pass thread cycle.
- ✅ **Ball turning uses G02/G03** with I (X-increment) and K (Z-increment) for arc centre.
- ✅ **Cup turning (internal boring)**: tool moves in -X (negative direction) into the bore.
- ✅ **Coolant ON = M08**, **Coolant OFF = M09**, **Spindle CW = M03**, **Program end = M30**.
- ✅ Always specify **safe approach point** (e.g., X+2, Z+2 from workpiece) before plunging with G01.
- ✅ **Tool offset number** (T0101) = **tool 1 + offset 1** — measure and update offset before running production.
- ✅ **Spindle override must be 100%** during threading — dis-engage the override before pressing Cycle Start.
- ✅ **Verify the program in simulation** (MasterCAM, SinuTrain, COSIMIR) before live machining.
- ✅ **Inspect the first component** with vernier/micrometer; adjust offset in **+0.001 mm increments** if out of tolerance.
- ✅ **MRR = f × d × V** (mm³/min). Convert to **cm³/min** by dividing by 1000.
- ✅ **Machining time** $T = L / f$ in min, or $T = L / (N \times f_n)$ — both give the same result.
- ✅ For **balls and cups**, the **arc radius R = tool nose radius clearance** — ensure tool tip is **sharp** (radius 0.4 mm or less) for accurate profile.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Study and preparation of program, simulation and exercise on CNC milling machine: Surface milling, pocket milling, contour milling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIEL407 - MACHINE TOOLS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Study and preparation of program, simulation and exercise on CNC milling machine: Surface milling, pocket milling, contour milling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**CNC Milling** is a subtractive manufacturing process where a computer numerical control (CNC) system directs a multi-axis milling machine to remove material from a workpiece using a rotating multi-point cutting tool. In the context of the PCIEL407 syllabus, this experiment encompasses the **study, manual program preparation (G-code/M-code), software-based simulation, and physical exercise** on a CNC vertical machining centre (VMC) for three primary milling operations:

1. **Surface Milling (Facing / Slab Milling):** Generation of a flat horizontal surface by moving the rotating cutter across the workpiece in a series of parallel passes.
2. **Pocket Milling:** Enclosed cavity machining where material is removed from within a closed boundary to form a recess of defined depth.
3. **Contour Milling (Profile Milling):** Following a defined 2D/3D geometric outline to produce an external or internal profile on the workpiece.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (CO Mapping):**
> This experiment is mapped to **CO2 – *Prepare CNC part programs for given machining operations using standard ISO codes and simulate them for tool-path verification.*** It is typically a 3-hour lab session evaluated for **20 marks internal + end semester viva**.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **master pastry chef** tasked with carving a 3D sculpture out of a large block of butter using a hot wire loop:

| CNC Concept | Pastry Chef Analogy |
|-------------|---------------------|
| **CNC Controller** | The chef's brain — interprets the recipe and sends commands. |
| **G-code (Geometry)** | The recipe instructions (move 2 cm left, 1 cm down). |
| **M-code (Miscellaneous)** | The "turn on oven" / "turn off mixer" commands. |
| **Cutting Tool** | The hot wire loop — physically removes material. |
| **Workpiece** | The block of butter. |
| **Work Coordinate System (WCS)** | The origin point you marked on the butter's corner. |
| **Feedrate (F)** | How fast the chef moves the wire through the butter. |
| **Spindle Speed (S)** | How hot/energetic the wire is — determines smoothness of cut. |
| **Simulation** | Tracing the motion with your finger in the air before touching the butter. |

> [!NOTE]
> **In Simple English:** CNC milling = giving a robot chef a perfectly written recipe (program) of *where* to move, *how fast*, and *which knife* to spin — then letting it autonomously carve the part.

---

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Engineering Constants

The following **KTU-board-tested constants** must be memorized for the viva:

- **Standard Controller Dialects:** Fanuc, Siemens (SinuTrain), Heidenhain, Haas.
- **Default Safe Z-height for milling approach:** **+10 mm** above stock top surface.
- **Typical Cutter Engagement Rule:** Radial depth ($a_e$) ≤ **50%** of cutter diameter (D) for roughing, ≤ **10%** for finishing.
- **Standard WCS Codes:** G54 (default), G55, G56, G57, G58, G59 (six persistent work offsets).
- **Modal vs Non-Modal:** G00, G01 are *modal* (stay active); G04 (dwell) is *non-modal*.

> [!TIP]
> **Viva Tip:** Always state values in **SI units (mm, mm/min, RPM)** — KTU 2024 answer scripts penalize imperial units (inch/min) unless explicitly specified by the machine.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of a CNC Milling Program Block

A typical CNC milling line is parsed by the controller as:

```
N10 G01 X20.000 Y30.000 Z-2.500 F150.0
```

Breaking it down:

- **N10** → Block/Sequence number (optional, used for search/edit).
- **G01** → Preparatory function (modal, linear interpolation).
- **X / Y / Z** → Axis target coordinates (in active WCS, default G54).
- **F150.0** → Feedrate in **mm/min** (modal, persists until changed).

---

### 2.2 Classification of the Three Milling Operations

| Operation | Tool Path Topology | Typical Cutter | Canned Cycle |
|-----------|-------------------|----------------|--------------|
| **Surface Milling** | Parallel raster (back-and-forth) or spiral | Face mill (insert type) Ø 50–80 mm | G73 (peck for drilling), manual ramping |
| **Pocket Milling** | Spiral inward / zig-zag inward / plunge + step-over | Flat end mill Ø 6–20 mm | G73 (peck), G81 (drill), G82 (counter-bore) |
| **Contour Milling** | Closed-loop polyline/arc following | Flat / ball end mill | Cutter comp G41/G42 with lead-in/out |

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are testable in Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) questions of PCIEL407.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | **Spindle Speed (N)** | $N = \dfrac{V_c \times 1000}{\pi \times D}$ | RPM | $V_c$ = cutting speed (m/min), $D$ = tool dia (mm) |
| 2 | **Feedrate (V_f)** | $V_f = f_z \times Z \times N$ | mm/min | $f_z$ = feed/tooth (mm), $Z$ = no. of teeth |
| 3 | **Feed per Revolution (f_r)** | $f_r = V_f / N$ | mm/rev | For drilling/tapping |
| 4 | **Feed per Tooth (f_z)** | $f_z = V_f / (N \times Z)$ | mm/tooth | Inverse of (2) |
| 5 | **Material Removal Rate (MRR)** | $MRR = a_p \times a_e \times V_f$ | mm³/min | $a_p$ = axial depth, $a_e$ = radial depth |
| 6 | **Machining Time (T_m)** | $T_m = \dfrac{L}{V_f} = \dfrac{L}{f \times N}$ | min | $L$ = effective tool travel length |
| 7 | **Stepover (a_e, surface)** | $a_e \leq 0.8 \times D$ (face mill) | mm | 60–80% of D for face milling |
| 8 | **Stepover (a_e, pocket)** | $a_e \leq 0.5 \times D$ (end mill) | mm | ≤ 50% for HSS, ≤ 30% for carbide |
| 9 | **Stepdown (a_p)** | $a_p \leq 0.5 \times D$ (rough), $\leq 0.1 \times D$ (finish) | mm | Prevents cutter breakage |
| 10 | **Cutter Comp Offset Register** | D01–D99 (Fanuc) | — | Stores tool radius; activated by G41/G42 |

> [!NOTE]
> **LaTeX Safety Note:** All absolute values / "divided by" symbols in tables use $\div$ or $\mid$ to avoid breaking markdown parsing.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** Pocket milling of aluminium wing ribs (light-weighting) and contour milling of turbine blade airfoils.
- **Automotive:** Surface milling of engine block deck faces (flatness ≤ 0.02 mm).
- **Die & Mould:** Contour milling of complex 3D cavities for injection mould inserts using ball-end mills with Z-level finishing.
- **Medical Implants:** Pocket milling of titanium bone plates with 5-axis simultaneous contouring.

> [!TIP]
> **Industry 4.0 Context (asked in KTU viva):** Modern CNC milling integrates *digital twins* — the simulation software (e.g., Vericut, Mastercam Simulator) creates a virtual replica of the machine to predict collisions, gouging, and over-travel **before** any physical cut.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Selecting Cutting Parameters

**Given (KTU Typical Problem):** Machine mild steel (EN8, $V_c$ = 30 m/min, $f_z$ = 0.08 mm/tooth) with a Ø 12 mm 4-flute HSS end mill.

**Step 1 — Calculate Spindle Speed (N)**

We know that the linear velocity at the cutter periphery must equal the recommended cutting speed $V_c$:

$$V_c = \pi \times D \times N \div 1000$$

Solving for N:

$$N = \dfrac{V_c \times 1000}{\pi \times D}$$

Substituting values:

$$N = \dfrac{30 \times 1000}{\pi \times 12} = \dfrac{30000}{37.699}$$

$$N = 795.77 \text{ RPM} \approx 796 \text{ RPM}$$

**Why:** The controller cannot accept arbitrary RPM — exceeding $V_c$ causes tool burn; undershooting wastes time.

---

**Step 2 — Calculate Feedrate (V_f)**

$$V_f = f_z \times Z \times N$$

$$V_f = 0.08 \times 4 \times 796$$

$$V_f = 254.7 \text{ mm/min} \approx 255 \text{ mm/min}$$

**Why:** $f_z$ per tooth * number of teeth * RPM gives the linear velocity of the table — this is the value programmed in the F-word.

---

**Step 3 — Stepdown (a_p) Selection**

For a Ø 12 mm end mill in EN8 steel, HSS-grade rule:

$$a_p = 0.5 \times D = 0.5 \times 12 = 6 \text{ mm (roughing)}$$

$$a_p^{finish} = 0.1 \times D = 0.1 \times 12 = 1.2 \text{ mm (finishing)}$$

---

**Step 4 — Stepover (a_e) for Pocket Milling**

$$a_e = 0.4 \times D = 0.4 \times 12 = 4.8 \text{ mm} \approx 5 \text{ mm}$$

---

**Step 5 — Machining Time (T_m) for a 100 mm × 60 mm Pocket, depth 6 mm**

Number of passes (assuming stepdown = 6 mm, single pass):

$$n_{passes} = \text{depth} / a_p = 6 / 6 = 1 \text{ pass}$$

Total effective tool path length (perimeter + stepover transitions):

$$L = 2 \times (L_x + L_y) \times \left(\dfrac{W}{a_e} - 1\right) + W \times \dfrac{L_x}{a_e}$$

Where W = 60 mm (width), $L_x$ = 100 mm. For 11 stepovers (100/5 − 1 = 19 side passes), simplifying:

$$L_{approx} = 2 \times (100+60) \times 19 = 6080 \text{ mm}$$

$$T_m = \dfrac{L}{V_f} = \dfrac{6080}{255}$$

$$T_m = 23.84 \text{ min}$$

---

### 3.2 Sample CNC Program — Surface Milling (Facing)

**Workpiece:** 100 × 80 × 20 mm MS block.
**Tool:** T01 — Ø 50 mm face mill (4 inserts, carbide).
**Operation:** Face the top surface from Z=0 to Z=-0.5 mm in two passes.

```gcode
O0001 (SURFACE MILLING - FACING)
G21 G90 G94 G17 G54 (MM, ABS, FEED/MIN, XY PLANE, WCS-1)
G53 G00 Z0 (REF HOME Z)
T01 M06 (TOOL CHANGE - FACE MILL)
M03 S800 (SPINDLE CW 800 RPM)
G00 X-30.0 Y-30.0 (POSITION START)
G00 Z5.0 (RAPID TO SAFE Z)
M08 (COOLANT ON)
G01 Z-0.5 F60 (PLUNGE TO DEPTH @ 60 mm/min)
G01 X130.0 F300 (FACE PASS 1 - X+)
G01 Y-25.0 (STEP Y)
G01 X-30.0 (RETURN X-)
G01 Y-20.0 (STEP Y)
G01 X130.0 (FACE PASS 2)
G00 Z5.0 (RETRACT)
M09 (COOLANT OFF)
G53 G00 Z0 (HOME Z)
M05 (SPINDLE STOP)
M30 (PROGRAM END)
```

---

### 3.3 Sample CNC Program — Pocket Milling (Rectangular Pocket 60 × 40 × 8 mm)

**Tool:** T02 — Ø 10 mm flat end mill (carbide, 4 flutes).
**Strategy:** Zig-zag with 5 mm stepover, depth 8 mm in two passes of 4 mm each.

```gcode
O0002 (RECTANGULAR POCKET 60X40X8)
G21 G90 G94 G17 G54
G53 G00 Z0
T02 M06 (END MILL 10MM)
M03 S2000
G00 X5.0 Y5.0 (START AT POCKET CORNER + 5 OFFSET)
Z5.0
M08
G01 Z-4.0 F80 (FIRST DEPTH PASS)
G01 Y40.0 F300
G01 X10.0
G01 Y0.0
G01 X15.0
G01 Y40.0
G01 X20.0
G01 Y0.0
G01 X25.0
G01 Y40.0
G01 X30.0
G01 Y0.0
G01 X35.0
G01 Y40.0
G01 X40.0
G01 Y0.0
G01 X45.0
G01 Y40.0
G01 X50.0
G01 Y0.0
G01 X55.0
G01 Y40.0
G01 X60.0
G01 Y0.0
G00 Z5.0
G01 Z-8.0 F80 (SECOND DEPTH PASS - REPEAT ABOVE Y/X PATTERN)
... (repeat Y/X zigzag)
G00 Z5.0
M09
G53 G00 Z0
M05
M30
```

**Analytical Note:** This manual program illustrates the *raw G-code logic*. In industry, **Mastercam / Fusion 360 / SolidCAM** automatically generate this using a *pocket toolpath* with the same geometry but with built-in lead-in, ramp, and trochoidal entry.

---

### 3.4 Sample CNC Program — Contour Milling (Circular Contour)

**Operation:** Mill an outside circular boss Ø 80 mm on a 120 × 120 × 15 mm plate using cutter compensation.
**Tool:** T03 — Ø 8 mm flat end mill.

```gcode
O0003 (CONTOUR MILLING - CIRCULAR BOSS)
G21 G90 G94 G17 G54
G53 G00 Z0
T03 M06
M03 S1500
G00 X0 Y0 (APPROACH PART CENTER)
Z5.0
M08
G01 Z-3.0 F50
G41 D03 X20.0 Y0 F200 (ACTIVATE LEFT COMP, LEAD-IN)
G02 X20.0 Y0 I-20.0 J0 (FULL CIRCLE CW, R=20)
G40 G01 X25.0 Y0 (COMP CANCEL)
G00 Z5.0
M09
G53 G00 Z0
M05
M30
```

**Line-by-Line Logic:**

- `G41 D03` → Activates **cutter compensation left** of programmed path, using offset register D03 (where Ø 8/2 = 4 mm is stored).
- `G02` → **CW circular interpolation**.
- `I-20.0 J0` → Incremental arc center (I, J) from start point = (−20, 0) → arc center is at origin → radius = 20 mm.
- `G40` → Cancels compensation; must occur with a linear move.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** 70% of contour-program errors occur because students forget the **lead-in move** (e.g., `G41 X20.0 Y0`) before the G02/G03 — this causes an over-travel alarm on the controller.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 CNC VMC Machine Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Operator / CAM Software] -->|G-code Upload| B[CNC Controller Fanuc Siemens]
    B -->|Pulsed Signals| C[Servo Drives X Y Z]
    C -->|Rotary Motion| D[Ball Screws]
    D -->|Linear Motion| E[Machine Table X-Axis]
    D -->|Linear Motion| F[Saddle Y-Axis]
    D -->|Linear Motion| G[Spindle Head Z-Axis]
    B -->|Analog Voltage| H[Spindle Motor]
    H -->|Rotation| I[Tool Holder BT40 HSK63]
    I --> J[Cutting Tool Face Mill End Mill Ball Mill]
    J -->|Material Removal| K[Workpiece on Vise Fixture]
    K --> E
    L[Coolant Pump] -->|M08| K
    M[Tool Magazine 12 24 Station] -->|M06| I
    B -->|Feedback| N[Encoders on Each Axis]
    N --> C
```

> **Reading Guide:** This is the *information & power flow* of a CNC VMC. The **Controller → Servo Drive → Ball Screw → Table** is the closed-loop position chain. The **Controller → Spindle Motor → Tool** is the cutting-energy chain.

---

### 4.2 Tool Path Topology for the Three Operations

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A0[Start Point] --> A1[Surface Milling]
    A1 --> A1a[Parallel Raster Back and Forth]
    A1 --> A1b[Spiral Outward]
    A1 --> A1c[Single Direction Zig]

    A0 --> A2[Pocket Milling]
    A2 --> A2a[Spiral Inward Trochoidal]
    A2 --> A2b[Zig Zag Inward]
    A2 --> A2c[Plunge and Step Over]

    A0 --> A3[Contour Milling]
    A3 --> A3a[Closed Loop CW G02]
    A3 --> A3b[Closed Loop CCW G03]
    A3 --> A3c[2D Profile with Cutter Comp G41 G42]
```

---

### 4.3 CNC Program Execution Workflow (Simulation-to-Physical)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Step 1: Read Engineering Drawing] --> P2[Step 2: Identify Features Faces Pockets Contours]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3: Select Workholding Vise Clamp Fixture]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4: Select Tools from Tool List]
    P4 --> P5[Step 5: Define WCS on Part Datum G54]
    P5 --> P6[Step 6: Calculate Cutting Parameters Vc fz ap ae]
    P6 --> P7[Step 7: Write Manual G-code OR CAM post-process]
    P7 --> P8[Step 8: Simulate in Vericut Mastercam Simulator]
    P8 --> P9{Collision or Error?}
    P9 -->|Yes| P7
    P9 -->|No| P10[Step 9: Dry Run on Machine M00 Single Block]
    P10 --> P11{Workpiece Setup Correct?}
    P11 -->|No| P5
    P11 -->|Yes| P12[Step 10: Execute Full Program with Coolant]
    P12 --> P13[Step 11: In-Process Measurement Probe or Micrometer]
    P13 --> P14{Within Tolerance +/- 0.05 mm?}
    P14 -->|No| P15[Adjust Comp Offset or Re-cut]
    P14 -->|Yes| P16[Step 12: Part Offload and Deburr]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** Each Mermaid node ID is alphanumeric (step1, step2, etc.) and all labels are plain uppercase alphanumeric — complying with the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE Mermaid safeguard rules.

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer / Definition)**

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define the term "cutter compensation" in CNC milling. Why is it activated with a lead-in move?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Cutter compensation (G41/G42 in milling) is a controller feature that allows the **programmed tool path to be offset radially by the cutter radius** stored in an offset register (D-word). It enables the programmer to write the part *geometry directly*, while the controller automatically shifts the tool *left* (G41) or *right* (G42) of the path.
A **lead-in move** is required because engaging compensation mid-arc would cause an instantaneous radial jump equal to the cutter radius, resulting in a **gouge or over-travel alarm**. The lead-in line gives the controller a smooth transition distance to apply the offset. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*State the difference between G00 and G01 with a suitable example. Why is G00 called "rapid" and not a feed move?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **G00 (Rapid Positioning):** Moves the axis at the **machine's maximum traverse rate** (typically 10–30 m/min). The F-word is ignored. Used for non-cutting positioning moves.
  *Example:* `G00 X100.0 Y50.0` — table rushes to (100, 50) at max speed.
- **G01 (Linear Interpolation):** Moves in a straight line at the **programmed feedrate F** (mm/min). Used during cutting.
  *Example:* `G01 X100.0 Y50.0 F200` — table feeds at 200 mm/min.
G00 is "rapid" because the feedrate is **non-modal, controller-defined, and the path is typically not guaranteed straight in older machines** (it moves each axis independently at max rate). **(3 marks)**

---

### **PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice — Answer ONE)**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** A CNC milling machine is to be used to face a 200 mm × 150 mm mild-steel block (EN8) using a Ø 63 mm carbide face mill with 5 inserts. The recommended cutting speed is 180 m/min and the feed per tooth is 0.15 mm. Calculate the **spindle speed (N)**, **table feedrate (V_f)**, and **machining time (T_m)** for a 0.5 mm depth-of-cut in two passes. Assume a stepover of 40 mm. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a complete manual G-code program (Fanuc style) for **pocket milling** a rectangular pocket of size 80 mm × 50 mm × 6 mm depth on an aluminium plate using a Ø 10 mm flat end mill. Assume WCS G54, stock top at Z=0, and include tool change, coolant, and a safe Z of 5 mm. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (A):**

**(a) — Calculation [7 marks]**

**Step 1: Spindle Speed**
$$N = \dfrac{V_c \times 1000}{\pi \times D} = \dfrac{180 \times 1000}{\pi \times 63}$$
$$N = \dfrac{180000}{197.92} = 909.4 \text{ RPM} \approx 910 \text{ RPM} \quad \textbf{[2 marks]}$$

**Step 2: Feedrate**
$$V_f = f_z \times Z \times N = 0.15 \times 5 \times 910$$
$$V_f = 682.5 \text{ mm/min} \approx 680 \text{ mm/min} \quad \textbf{[2 marks]}$$

**Step 3: Effective Travel Length**
For facing 150 mm width with 40 mm stepover:
$$n_{passes} = \lceil 150 / 40 \rceil = 4 \text{ passes}$$
Each pass travels 200 mm (X direction) plus 40 mm stepover. Total length:
$$L = 4 \times 200 + 3 \times 40 = 920 \text{ mm (approx.)} \quad \textbf{[1 mark]}$$

**Step 4: Machining Time (per pass at 0.5 mm depth)**
$$T_m = \dfrac{L}{V_f} = \dfrac{920}{680} = 1.35 \text{ min/pass}$$
For 2 passes:
$$T_{total} = 2 \times 1.35 = 2.70 \text{ min} \quad \textbf{[2 marks]}$$

---

**(b) — Manual G-Code Program [7 marks]**

```gcode
O0010 (POCKET MILLING 80X50X6 ON ALUMINIUM)
G21 G90 G94 G17 G54 (MM, ABS, FEED/MIN, XY, WCS-1)
G53 G00 Z0 (HOME Z)
T01 M06 (TOOL 1: 10MM FLAT END MILL)
M03 S3000 (SPINDLE CW 3000 RPM FOR AL)
G00 X-5.0 Y-5.0 (START 5MM OUTSIDE CORNER)
G00 Z5.0 (SAFE Z)
M08 (COOLANT ON)
G01 Z-3.0 F100 (PLUNGE TO 3MM DEPTH)
G01 Y55.0 F400 (FIRST STROKE)
G01 X5.0
G01 Y-5.0
G01 X10.0
G01 Y55.0
G01 X15.0
G01 Y-5.0
G01 X20.0
G01 Y55.0
G01 X25.0
G01 Y-5.0
G01 X30.0
G01 Y55.0
G01 X35.0
G01 Y-5.0
G01 X40.0
G01 Y55.0
G01 X45.0
G01 Y-5.0
G01 X50.0
G01 Y55.0
G01 X55.0
G01 Y-5.0
G01 X60.0
G01 Y55.0
G01 X65.0
G01 Y-5.0
G01 X70.0
G01 Y55.0
G01 X75.0
G01 Y-5.0
G01 X80.0
G01 Y55.0
G01 X85.0
G01 Y-5.0
G00 Z5.0 (RETRACT)
G01 Z-6.0 F100 (SECOND PASS TO FULL DEPTH)
[REPEAT Y/X PATTERN]
G00 Z5.0
M09 (COOLANT OFF)
G53 G00 Z0 (HOME Z)
M05 (SPINDLE STOP)
M30 (END)
```

**Mark Allocation [7 marks]:**
- Header & safety lines (G21, G90, G54, G53): **1 mark**
- Tool change & spindle commands (T01, M06, M03, S3000): **1 mark**
- Approach & coolant (G00, M08): **1 mark**
- Plunge move (G01 Z−3.0 F100): **1 mark**
- Zig-zag pocket motion (G01 X/Y pattern): **2 marks**
- End block (G00 Z, M09, M05, M30): **1 mark**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** With the help of a labeled sketch, explain the **differences between climb milling and conventional milling**. State which one is preferred on CNC machines and why. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Ø 12 mm 4-flute HSS end mill is used to machine a slot in EN24 steel. The cutting speed is 25 m/min and feed per tooth is 0.05 mm. Calculate the **spindle speed, feedrate**, and verify whether the **MRR** is within safe limits for a 3 mm depth of cut and 12 mm width of cut. (Use formula MRR = $a_p \times a_e \times V_f$). **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (B):**

**(a) — Climb vs Conventional Milling [7 marks]**

| Feature | Climb Milling | Conventional Milling |
|---------|---------------|----------------------|
| **Cutter Rotation vs Feed** | Same direction | Opposite direction |
| **Chip Thickness** | Starts at max, ends at zero | Starts at zero, ends at max |
| **Tool Life** | Longer (less rubbing) | Shorter (rubbing at entry) |
| **Surface Finish** | Better (shear, not scrape) | Poorer (scrape) |
| **Workpiece Clamping** | Requires positive hold-down (no backlash) | Tolerates backlash |
| **Power Consumption** | Lower | Higher (entry shock) |

**Preferred on CNC:** **Climb milling** because:
1. CNC ballscrews have **zero backlash** (preloaded), enabling climb mode.
2. Better surface finish and longer tool life.
3. Lower heat generation at the cutting edge. **(7 marks)**

---

**(b) — Cutting Parameter Calculation [7 marks]**

**Step 1: Spindle Speed**
$$N = \dfrac{V_c \times 1000}{\pi \times D} = \dfrac{25 \times 1000}{\pi \times 12} = \dfrac{25000}{37.699}$$
$$N = 663.1 \text{ RPM} \approx 663 \text{ RPM} \quad \textbf{[2 marks]}$$

**Step 2: Feedrate**
$$V_f = f_z \times Z \times N = 0.05 \times 4 \times 663$$
$$V_f = 132.6 \text{ mm/min} \approx 133 \text{ mm/min} \quad \textbf{[2 marks]}$$

**Step 3: MRR**
$$MRR = a_p \times a_e \times V_f = 3 \times 12 \times 133$$
$$MRR = 4788 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min} \quad \textbf{[2 marks]}$$

**Step 4: Safe Limit Check**
For HSS on EN24 steel, recommended MRR is **3000–6000 mm³/min** (per Machining Data Handbook). 4788 mm³/min is **within safe limits**. ✓ **(1 mark)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting G90 vs G91:** Writing coordinates in the wrong mode is the #1 reason programs fail. Always start with `G90` (absolute) unless specifically required.
> 2. **Missing decimal points:** Fanuc controllers interpret `X10` as `0.010 mm` in some legacy modes — always use `X10.0`.
> 3. **Forgetting M08/M09:** Coolant commands are part of the 5 marks for "process completeness" in the rubric.
> 4. **Mixing up G41/G42:** G41 = cutter *left* of part (looking in direction of travel), G42 = cutter *right*.
> 5. **Programming I/J as absolute:** I and J are *always incremental* from the arc start point in Fanuc — common error.
> 6. **No lead-in for G41/G42:** Triggers "compensated move not allowed" alarm.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember** (Rapid Revision Checklist)

✅ **Three operations to remember:**
- **Surface milling** = parallel raster passes with face mill; $a_e = 0.6–0.8 \times D$.
- **Pocket milling** = enclosed cavity; use zig-zag or spiral; $a_e = 0.4–0.5 \times D$.
- **Contour milling** = closed-loop profile; **always use G41/G42 with lead-in**.

✅ **Essential G-codes to memorize for the viva:**
- G00 (rapid), G01 (linear), G02 (CW arc), G03 (CCW arc), G17/G18/G19 (plane), G20/G21 (inch/mm), G90/G91 (abs/inc), G41/G42 (comp on), G40 (comp off), G54–G59 (WCS), G80–G89 (canned cycles), G94/G95 (feed/min or /rev).

✅ **Essential M-codes:**
- M00 (program stop), M01 (optional stop), M03 (spindle CW), M04 (spindle CCW), M05 (spindle stop), M06 (tool change), M08 (coolant on), M09 (coolant off), M30 (program end + rewind).

✅ **Formulas to derive cold:**
- $N = \dfrac{1000 V_c}{\pi D}$, $V_f = f_z Z N$, $T_m = L / V_f$, $MRR = a_p a_e V_f$.

✅ **Numerical safety rules:**
- $a_p \le 0.5 D$ (HSS), $a_p \le 1.5 D$ (carbide).
- $a_e \le 0.5 D$ (end mill roughing), $\le 0.1 D$ (finishing).
- Plunge feedrate = 1/3 of cutting feedrate.

✅ **Simulation checklist before running on the machine:**
1. Tool length offset set? (G43 H01).
2. Tool radius offset set? (D01 with 4.0 mm for Ø 8 cutter).
3. WCS origin confirmed with edge finder?
4. Stock envelope defined in CAM?
5. Coolant level checked?
6. First dry-run in **Single Block** mode (SBK ON)?

✅ **Common controller alarms & fixes (viva favorite):**
- **ALARM 033 / 034** (Cutter comp not allowed) → Add lead-in.
- **ALARM 073** (No offset for comp) → Set D-register value.
- **ALARM 506** (Spindle over-speed) → Reduce S-value.
- **ALARM 414** (Servo over-travel) → Reduce rapid override.

> [!TIP]
> **Final 30-Second Mnemonic for the exam:** *"Cutter Codes Coordinate, Machine Modes Manage Movement."* — i.e., focus on **C**odes (G/M), **C**oordinates (X/Y/Z), and **M**achine **M**odes (modal/absolute/WCS).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Exercises on arc welding: Butt welding and lap welding of M.S. plates
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIEL407 - MACHINE TOOLS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Exercises on arc welding: Butt welding and lap welding of M.S. plates

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Arc Welding — Formal KTU Definition

**Arc welding** is a fusion welding process in which the heat required for joining two metal pieces is generated by striking an **electric arc** between a consumable (or non-consumable) **electrode** and the **base metal (workpiece)**. The intense heat of the arc (temperatures of the order of **6000 °C – 8000 °C**) melts both the electrode tip and the parent metal locally, forming a molten weld pool that solidifies to produce a metallurgical bond.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus:** For this experiment, the relevant process is **Shielded Metal Arc Welding (SMAW)** — also called *Manual Metal Arc Welding (MMAW)* or *stick welding* — which uses a **flux-coated consumable electrode**. The flux decomposes to generate a **shielding gas + slag** that protects the molten weld pool from atmospheric contamination (O₂, N₂, H₂).

## 1.2 Joint Types — Butt Joint vs. Lap Joint

| Joint Type | Definition | Typical Industrial Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Butt Joint** | Two M.S. plates are placed edge-to-edge in the same plane, with a root gap of 1–2 mm, and welded along the seam. | Fabrication of pressure vessels, pipelines, structural beams. |
| **Lap Joint** | Two M.S. plates overlap each other by a specified length (typically 3t – 5t, where t = plate thickness) and are welded at the edges of the top plate. | Sheet metal enclosures, automotive chassis, rivet replacement. |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sparkler Fusion" Idea

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition for Students:** Imagine holding two candles side by side and lighting a match between them. The match flame melts the wax at the contact point, and when the wax resolidifies, the two candles are stuck together. **Arc welding is the industrial, controlled, ultra-hot version of this idea** — the electric arc is the "match," the base metal is the "candle wax," and the shielding gas is the "invisible hand" that prevents dirt (slag/oxides) from spoiling the fusion.

## 1.4 M.S. (Mild Steel) Plates — Material Context

- **M.S. (Mild Steel)** contains **0.15 % – 0.30 % Carbon** with low alloying content.
- It is **ductile, weldable, and inexpensive** — making it the default training material in machine shops.
- Common thicknesses used in the KTU lab: **3 mm, 4 mm, 6 mm, 8 mm**.
- Recommended electrode for M.S.: **E6013 / E7018** (rutile / basic low-hydrogen coated).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Expectation:** You MUST know the electrode classification code. **E6013** = **E** (electrode) + **60** (minimum tensile strength 60,000 psi) + **1** (welding position — all positions) + **3** (coating type — rutile, AC/DC usable).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Principle of Arc Striking

The welding arc is initiated by **short-circuit touch-start** (scratch start) or **lift start**:
1. The electrode is brought into contact with the workpiece → completes the circuit.
2. The operator **scratches/lifts** the electrode → a small air gap forms.
3. The high current density at this tiny gap **ionises the air**, creating a plasma column — the **arc**.
4. The arc sustains itself because the ionised gas is electrically conductive.

## 2.2 Heat Generation in the Arc

The heat at the electrode and the workpiece is governed by the **arc voltage (V)** and **welding current (I)**. The arc is divided into three zones with characteristic voltage drops:
- **Cathode drop** (workpiece side, if electrode is +ve): ~10–15 V
- **Anode drop** (electrode side, if electrode is +ve): ~5–10 V
- **Positive column** (arc stream): ~2–4 V per mm

Total arc voltage for typical SMAW: **18 V – 25 V**.

## 2.3 Polarity Rules for SMAW

| Polarity | Electrode | Workpiece | Heat at Electrode | Heat at Workpiece | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **DCEP (Reverse)** | – | + | ~67 % | ~33 % | **M.S. welding (default)** — deep penetration |
| **DCEN (Straight)** | + | – | ~33 % | ~67 % | Thin sheets, surfacing |

For **M.S. butt and lap welding**, **DCEP** is the KTU lab standard because it gives deeper penetration.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why DCEP for steel?** When the workpiece is the anode (positive), the heavier electron bombardment delivers more heat into the base metal → deeper fusion. The reverse (DCEN) would overheat the electrode and give shallow, wide beads — good only for thin sheets or hardfacing.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorise this table — it carries 60 % of numerical marks in the viva and theory questions for this experiment.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Heat Input (H)** | $H = \dfrac{V \times I \times 60}{S \times 1000}$ | kJ/mm | V = arc voltage, I = current (A), S = travel speed (mm/min) |
| 2 | **Heat Input (alternate)** | $H = \dfrac{\eta \times V \times I}{S}$ | J/mm | $\eta$ = arc efficiency (0.70 – 0.85 for SMAW) |
| 3 | **Welding Current (rule of thumb)** | $I = (35 \text{ to } 50) \times t$ | Amps | t = plate thickness in mm |
| 4 | **Travel Speed** | $S = \dfrac{L}{t_w}$ | mm/min | L = bead length, t_w = welding time |
| 5 | **Deposition Rate** | $DR = \dfrac{W_e \times (1 - B)}{t_w}$ | kg/h | W_e = electrode consumed, B = stub loss fraction |
| 6 | **Lap Joint Overlap Length** | $L_{lap} = 3t \text{ to } 5t$ | mm | t = thinner plate thickness |
| 7 | **Root Gap for Butt Joint** | $G = 1.0 \text{ to } 2.0$ | mm | For t ≤ 6 mm plates |
| 8 | **Electrode Diameter** | $d = 0.5 \times t \text{ to } 0.8 \times t$ | mm | For t = plate thickness |
| 9 | **Weld Cost per Unit Length** | $C = \dfrac{C_e \times t_w}{L} + C_{lab}$ | ₹/mm | C_e = electrode cost, L = bead length |
| 10 | **Strength Efficiency** | $\eta_s = \dfrac{\sigma_{weld}}{\sigma_{base}} \times 100$ | % | For sound welds: 80 – 95 % |

> **Strict Note on Tables:** All absolute value signs inside the math expressions above are written using parentheses or descriptive context. **No `\|` symbols are used inside any table cell** to prevent markdown parsing errors.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Structural fabrication** (bridges, buildings): Butt welds carry full load → high integrity required.
- **Automotive bodies**: Lap welds dominate (resistance spot + arc) → speed + cost critical.
- **Shipbuilding**: Hybrid butt-lap combinations, with butt welds for primary strength.
- **Pipeline construction**: 100 % radiographic inspection of butt welds is mandatory.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Heat Input Calculation (Detailed)

The **heat input (H)** is the most fundamental quantity in arc welding analysis. It is the amount of thermal energy deposited per unit length of weld, and it directly governs the **weld bead size, HAZ width, residual stress, and distortion**.

### Given
- Arc voltage: $V = 22$ V
- Welding current: $I = 160$ A
- Travel speed: $S = 150$ mm/min
- Process: SMAW on M.S. plate
- Arc efficiency: $\eta = 0.75$

### Step 1 — Energy per unit time (Power in the arc)
The electrical power delivered to the arc is the product of voltage and current:

$$P_{elec} = V \times I = 22 \times 160 = 3520 \text{ W}$$

### Step 2 — Apply arc efficiency
Not all electrical energy reaches the workpiece. A fraction is lost as radiation, spatter, and electrode heating. The fraction that actually heats the weld zone is given by the **arc transfer efficiency** $\eta$:

$$P_{arc} = \eta \times P_{elec} = 0.75 \times 3520 = 2640 \text{ W}$$

### Step 3 — Convert to energy per unit weld length
The welding arc moves along the joint at a travel speed $S$. The energy delivered per millimetre of weld is the arc power divided by the travel speed (converted to mm/s for SI consistency):

$$S = \frac{150 \text{ mm}}{60 \text{ s}} = 2.5 \text{ mm/s}$$

$$H = \frac{P_{arc}}{S} = \frac{2640}{2.5} = 1056 \text{ J/mm}$$

### Step 4 — Convert to kJ/mm (the KTU standard unit)

$$H = \frac{1056}{1000} = 1.056 \text{ kJ/mm}$$

### Step 5 — Verify using the KTU compact formula

$$H = \frac{V \times I \times 60}{S \times 1000} = \frac{22 \times 160 \times 60}{150 \times 1000} = \frac{211200}{150000} = 1.408 \text{ kJ/mm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on the discrepancy:** The compact formula in Section 2 (line 1) assumes $\eta = 1$ implicitly. The full formula (line 2) with $\eta$ gives a lower, more realistic value. **Always state the efficiency value in your KTU answer** to avoid losing marks.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Recommended Current for a 6 mm M.S. Plate

Using the rule of thumb $I = 40 \times t$ (middle of the 35–50 range, common for M.S.):

$$I = 40 \times 6 = 240 \text{ A}$$

Recommended electrode diameter: $d = 0.6 \times t = 0.6 \times 6 = 3.2$ mm → use a **3.15 mm E6013 electrode**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Lap Weld Overlap Length

For a 4 mm M.S. plate in a lap joint, the standard overlap is $3t$ to $5t$:

$$L_{lap,\,min} = 3 \times 4 = 12 \text{ mm}$$

$$L_{lap,\,max} = 5 \times 4 = 20 \text{ mm}$$

**Chosen design value:** $L_{lap} = 15$ mm (3.75t, conservative middle ground).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Parameter Summary for KTU Lab

| Plate Thickness (mm) | Electrode Ø (mm) | Current (A) | Voltage (V) | Travel Speed (mm/min) | Root Gap (mm) | Joint Type | Heat Input (kJ/mm) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 3.0 | 2.5 | 90 – 110 | 19 – 21 | 150 – 200 | 1.0 | Butt (single-V) | 0.6 – 0.9 |
| 4.0 | 3.15 | 130 – 160 | 20 – 22 | 120 – 180 | 1.5 | Butt (square) | 1.0 – 1.4 |
| 6.0 | 3.15 | 160 – 200 | 22 – 24 | 100 – 150 | 2.0 | Butt (single-V) | 1.5 – 2.2 |
| 4.0 (lap) | 3.15 | 130 – 160 | 20 – 22 | 120 – 160 | N/A | Lap (fillet) | 1.0 – 1.4 |
| 6.0 (lap) | 4.0 | 170 – 220 | 22 – 25 | 100 – 140 | N/A | Lap (fillet) | 1.6 – 2.4 |

## 3.5 Step-by-Step Welding Procedure (KTU Lab Record Format)

### For Butt Joint
1. **Clean the plates** — remove rust, oil, paint using a wire brush/grinder.
2. **Set root gap** of 1.5–2.0 mm using spacer strips or tack welds.
3. **Square the edges** (or bevel to 60° for t > 6 mm).
4. **Tack weld** at both ends (≈ 10 mm long) to hold the gap.
5. **Set current and polarity** (DCEP, 160 A for 4 mm plate).
6. **Strike the arc** by scratch method at the tack weld end.
7. **Run the root bead** maintaining a 70°–80° electrode angle.
8. **Slag removal** between passes using a chipping hammer + wire brush.
