9. **Inspect** visually + dimensional check.

### For Lap Joint
1. **Clean both plates** at the overlap zone.
2. **Mark overlap length** (3t to 5t) and clamp the plates.
3. **Tack weld** at 25–30 mm intervals along both edges.
4. **Set parameters** (same as butt for the chosen thickness).
5. **Weld one edge** with a fillet bead, then **flip and weld the other edge**.
6. **Inspect** fillet leg length and throat thickness.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the SMAW Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[AC or DC Power Source] --> B[Electrode Holder Cable]
    C[Workpiece Clamp Cable] --> D[M.S. Plate Workpiece]
    B --> E[Flux Coated Electrode E6013]
    E --> F[Struck Electric Arc 6000 C]
    F --> G[Melting of Electrode Tip]
    F --> H[Melting of Base Metal]
    G --> I[Formation of Weld Pool]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Solidification as Weld Bead]
    K[Flux Decomposition] --> L[Shielding Gas CO2 and H2]
    K --> M[Slag Layer on Bead]
    L --> I
    M --> J
    D --> N[Heat Affected Zone HAZ]
    J --> N
    N --> O[Final Welded Joint]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Butt Joint Preparation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Select M.S. Plates] --> A2[Cut to Required Size]
    A2 --> A3[Clean Surfaces with Wire Brush]
    A3 --> A4{Plate Thickness}
    A4 -->|t less than 6 mm| A5[Square Edge Preparation]
    A4 -->|t greater than 6 mm| A6[Single V Groove 60 degree]
    A5 --> A7[Set Root Gap 1.5 to 2.0 mm]
    A6 --> A7
    A7 --> A8[Tack Weld Both Ends]
    A8 --> A9[Position Plates on Welding Table]
    A9 --> A10[Connect DCEP Polarity]
    A10 --> A11[Strike Arc and Run Root Bead]
    A11 --> A12[Remove Slag and Chip]
    A12 --> A13[Run Second Pass if Needed]
    A13 --> A14[Visual and Dimensional Inspection]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Lap Joint Preparation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B1[Select Two M.S. Plates] --> B2[Determine Overlap 3t to 5t]
    B2 --> B3[Mark Overlap Length on Both Plates]
    B3 --> B4[Clean Contact Surfaces]
    B4 --> B5[Clamp Plates Together]
    B5 --> B6[Tack Weld at 30 mm Spacing]
    B6 --> B7[Set DCEP and Current]
    B7 --> B8[Strike Arc at One Edge]
    B8 --> B9[Run Fillet Bead Edge 1]
    B9 --> B10[Flip Workpiece]
    B10 --> B11[Run Fillet Bead Edge 2]
    B11 --> B12[Measure Fillet Leg and Throat]
    B12 --> B13[Visual Inspection]
```

## 4.4 Cross-Section Schematic (Text-Block Format)

### Butt Joint Geometry

```
        Plate A                              Plate B
   ===================                 ===================
   |                 |                 |                 |
   |   <-- weld -->  |   <-- 1.5mm -->  |   <-- weld -->  |
   |     bead        |     root gap    |     bead        |
   ===================                 ===================
                              |
                              v
                       Weld penetration
                       (visible from back side)
```

### Lap Joint Geometry

```
   Top Plate:    ===========================
                  |         <-- 15mm -->    |
                  |        overlap zone     |
   Bottom Plate: ===========================
                  ^                          ^
                  |                          |
           Fillet weld 1              Fillet weld 2
           (at this edge)             (at this edge)
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Fillet Weld Geometry:** A fillet weld in a lap joint is characterised by two **legs** (vertical and horizontal) and the **throat** (the shortest distance from the weld root to the hypotenuse). For an equal-leg fillet, throat $= \text{leg} \times 0.707$.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define arc welding. List the two main types of joints demonstrated in this experiment and state one industrial application of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Arc welding** is a fusion welding process in which two metal pieces are joined by heating them to a molten state using the heat of an electric arc struck between an electrode and the workpiece. The molten metal solidifies to form a metallurgical bond.
The two joints demonstrated are:
1. **Butt joint** — plates placed edge-to-edge; application: pressure vessel fabrication.
2. **Lap joint** — plates overlap each other; application: automotive chassis and sheet metal enclosures.

*(1 mark — definition, 1 mark — both joints, 1 mark — applications.)*

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary Exam]**
*State the significance of using a flux-coated electrode in SMAW. Name the electrode recommended for M.S. plate welding.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **flux coating** serves three critical functions:
1. Generates a **shielding gas** (CO₂, H₂) that protects the molten weld pool from atmospheric oxygen and nitrogen.
2. Forms a **slag layer** over the solidified bead that slows cooling and prevents oxidation.
3. Adds **deoxidising elements** (Si, Mn) to the weld pool to improve bead quality.

The recommended electrode for M.S. plates is **E6013** (rutile-coated, AC/DC, all-position).

*(1 mark per function + 0.5 mark for E + 0.5 mark for 6013 breakdown — total 3 marks.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, End Semester]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the procedure for performing a **butt weld** on two 6 mm M.S. plates using the SMAW process. Mention the electrode size, polarity, current, and root gap used. (7 marks)

**(b)** Calculate the **heat input per unit length** of weld if the arc voltage is 24 V, current is 180 A, travel speed is 120 mm/min, and arc transfer efficiency is 0.75. Comment on whether this heat input is suitable for a 6 mm M.S. plate. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution — Q.A(a) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Joint Preparation (2 marks)**
- The two M.S. plates (6 mm thick, ~150 × 50 mm each) are cut and their edges are **squared** (or single-V grooved at 60° for full penetration).
- Surfaces are **cleaned** with a wire brush to remove rust/scale.
- Plates are positioned on the welding table with a **root gap of 1.5–2.0 mm** and tacked at both ends.

**Step 2 — Machine Setup (2 marks)**
- **Electrode:** E6013, **3.15 mm diameter**.
- **Polarity:** **DCEP (Electrode Negative, Workpiece Positive)** — for deep penetration.
- **Current:** 160 – 200 A (rule of thumb: $I = 35 \times 6$ to $50 \times 6$).
- **Voltage:** 22 – 24 V (open circuit voltage ~60 – 80 V).

**Step 3 — Welding (2 marks)**
- Strike the arc by the **scratch method** at the tack weld.
- Maintain an **electrode angle of 70°–80°** to the plate surface, with a slight **forward travel angle of 10°–15°**.
- Run the **root bead** in a single pass; travel speed ≈ 100 – 150 mm/min.
- Remove slag with a **chipping hammer + wire brush**.
- A second pass may be added if full penetration is not visible.

**Step 4 — Inspection (1 mark)**
- **Visual** check for undercuts, porosity, cracks.
- **Dimensional** check for bead width, reinforcement height, and penetration.

---

#### Model Solution — Q.A(b) (7 marks)

**Given:** $V = 24$ V, $I = 180$ A, $S = 120$ mm/min, $\eta = 0.75$.

**Step 1 — Travel speed in mm/s**

$$S = \frac{120}{60} = 2.0 \text{ mm/s}$$

**Step 2 — Electrical power**

$$P = V \times I = 24 \times 180 = 4320 \text{ W}$$

**Step 3 — Effective arc power**

$$P_{eff} = \eta \times P = 0.75 \times 4320 = 3240 \text{ W}$$

**Step 4 — Heat input per unit length**

$$H = \frac{P_{eff}}{S} = \frac{3240}{2.0} = 1620 \text{ J/mm} = 1.62 \text{ kJ/mm}$$

**Step 5 — Suitability comment (2 marks)**
From the Analytical Matrix in Section 3, the recommended heat input for a 6 mm M.S. butt weld is **1.5 – 2.2 kJ/mm**. The calculated value of **1.62 kJ/mm falls within this range**, so the heat input is **suitable**. It will give good penetration without excessive HAZ or distortion.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Supplementary]**

**(a)** Explain the procedure for performing a **lap weld** on two 4 mm M.S. plates with the help of a labelled diagram. State the overlap length, electrode, and current used. (7 marks)

**(b)** Differentiate between **butt joint and lap joint** welding with respect to: (i) edge preparation, (ii) stress distribution, (iii) weld bead location, (iv) typical strength, and (v) inspection difficulty. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution — Q.B(a) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Plate Preparation (1.5 marks)**
- Two M.S. plates (4 mm thick) are cleaned and positioned such that one **overlaps the other** by **12–20 mm** (chosen: 15 mm = 3.75t).
- The plates are **clamped** to a welding table to prevent movement.

**Step 2 — Tack Welding (1 mark)**
- **Tack welds** are placed at 25–30 mm intervals along both edges of the overlap zone to maintain the geometry.

**Step 3 — Machine Settings (1.5 marks)**
- **Electrode:** E6013, **3.15 mm Ø**.
- **Polarity:** DCEP.
- **Current:** 130 – 160 A.
- **Voltage:** 20 – 22 V.

**Step 4 — Welding (2 marks)**
- **Fillet bead** is run along one edge of the overlap (leg length ≈ plate thickness).
- The workpiece is **flipped**, and a second fillet bead is run along the opposite edge.
- Slag is chipped off after each pass.

**Step 5 — Inspection (1 mark)**
- **Fillet leg length** and **throat thickness** are measured with a weld gauge.
- Visual check for **undercut, porosity, and lack of fusion** at the root.

---

#### Model Solution — Q.B(b) (7 marks)

| S.No. | Parameter | Butt Joint | Lap Joint |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) | **Edge Preparation** | Edges squared or bevelled; root gap required | No edge preparation; only surface cleaning required |
| (ii) | **Stress Distribution** | Uniform stress across the weld; axial load carried directly | Eccentric loading causes **bending moment**; stress concentration at weld toes |
| (iii) | **Weld Bead Location** | Single seam along the joint line | Two parallel fillet beads at the two edges of the overlap |
| (iv) | **Typical Strength** | Higher static strength; 100 % joint efficiency possible | Lower (~70 – 85 % efficiency) due to eccentricity |
| (v) | **Inspection Difficulty** | Easy to inspect from both sides; radiography possible | Difficult to inspect from underside; root side hidden by overlap |

*(1.4 marks per parameter × 5 = 7 marks.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in the PCIEL407 Lab Exam:**
> 1. **Forgetting to state the arc efficiency (η) in heat input problems.** If the question gives a voltage and current, examiners expect you to **multiply by η** and convert units carefully. Writing just $V \times I / S$ without η is a **1.5-mark deduction**.
> 2. **Confusing DCEP and DCEN.** M.S. butt and lap welds **MUST use DCEP**. Writing "DCEN for M.S." will cost full marks for that sub-question.
> 3. **Mixing up root gap and overlap length.** Root gap (1.5–2.0 mm) is for butt joints. Overlap (3t–5t) is for lap joints. Examiners spot this instantly.
> 4. **Not mentioning slag removal** between passes — this is a **lab-specific procedural mark** worth at least 0.5 to 1 mark.
> 5. **Skipping PPE (Personal Protective Equipment) in safety questions** — always mention: welding helmet (auto-darkening), leather gloves, apron, safety boots, and proper **ventilation/fume extractor**.
> 6. **Using `|` (vertical bar) in formula tables** — although this is more relevant to math papers, in Machine Tools Lab reports, examiners deduct marks for **unclear handwritten formulas**. Always box your final answer.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pin this list to your lab record's last page before the viva.**

- [ ] **Arc welding** = fusion joining using an electric arc; **SMAW** uses a flux-coated consumable electrode.
- [ ] **Electrode for M.S. = E6013** (60 ksi tensile, all-position, rutile coating, AC/DC).
- [ ] **Polarity for M.S. = DCEP** (Electrode Negative, Workpiece Positive) → deep penetration.
- [ ] **Current rule:** $I = (35 \text{ to } 50) \times t$ (A), where $t$ = plate thickness in mm.
- [ ] **Electrode diameter:** $d = 0.5t \text{ to } 0.8t$ (mm).
- [ ] **Root gap (Butt):** 1.0 – 2.0 mm.
- [ ] **Overlap (Lap):** $3t \text{ to } 5t$ — e.g., 12 – 20 mm for 4 mm plate.
- [ ] **Heat input formula (with efficiency):** $H = \dfrac{\eta \times V \times I}{S}$ in J/mm, with $S$ in mm/s.
- [ ] **Heat input formula (KTU compact):** $H = \dfrac{V \times I \times 60}{S \times 1000}$ in kJ/mm, with $S$ in mm/min.
- [ ] **Arc efficiency for SMAW = 0.70 – 0.85** (typically 0.75 in KTU problems).
- [ ] **Fillet weld throat = leg × 0.707** (for equal-leg fillet).
- [ ] **Procedure order:** Clean → Position → Set root gap/overlap → Tack → Set DCEP & current → Strike arc → Weld → Slag removal → Inspect.
- [ ] **Safety:** Helmet, gloves, apron, fume extraction, fire-resistant surroundings.
- [ ] **Inspection:** Visual (cracks, porosity, undercut) + Dimensional (bead width, leg length, throat).
- [ ] **Defects to know:** Porosity, slag inclusion, lack of fusion, undercut, crater cracks, spatter, distortion.
- [ ] **Comparison axis for viva:** Strength (Butt > Lap), Preparation (Lap easier), Inspection (Butt easier), Cost (Lap cheaper for thin sheets).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Exercises on gas welding: Butt welding and lap welding of M.S. plates
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIEL407 - MACHINE TOOLS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Exercises on gas welding: Butt welding and lap welding of M.S. plates

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Gas Welding?

**Gas Welding** is a fusion welding process in which the joining of metals is accomplished by heating them to a suitable temperature above the melting point, using a **flame produced by the combustion of a fuel gas** (typically **acetylene, C₂H₂**) with **oxygen (O₂)**. The molten metal from the parent pieces fuses together, and a filler rod (added metal) may be introduced into the joint to strengthen the weld. No external electrical arc or mechanical pressure is required in the basic gas welding process.

In KTU laboratory practice, the most commonly used combination is **Oxy-Acetylene Welding (OAW)**, also known as **Autogenous Gas Welding** (when no filler is used) or **Braze Welding** (with non-ferrous filler).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Tone):**
> *Gas welding is a fusion welding process wherein the heat required for welding is obtained from the combustion of a suitable fuel gas (e.g., acetylene) with oxygen. The localized flame produced melts the edges of the plates to be joined, which fuse together to form a homogeneous joint upon solidification.*

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you have two pieces of butter on a plate, and you want to join them together. If you bring a candle flame (small, gentle heat) near them, they soften and if pressed together, they fuse into a single piece. Now imagine replacing the butter with two **Mild Steel (M.S.) plates** and the candle with a **super-hot flame (~3200 °C)** produced by burning acetylene in pure oxygen. The steel plates melt at the edges, fuse together, and become one continuous piece — **this is the essence of gas welding**.

The key "ingredients" of the analogy:

| Butter Scenario | Real Gas Welding |
|----------------|------------------|
| Candle flame | Oxy-acetylene flame |
| Softening (melting) of butter | Melting of M.S. edges (≈1500 °C) |
| Pressed together | Held in position with a fixture/jig |
| Cooled & solidified | Weld bead forms upon cooling |

## 1.3 Types of Joints in this Lab Exercise

The experiment focuses on fabricating two specific joint geometries on M.S. plates:

> [!NOTE]
> **Joint A — Butt Joint (Square Butt Weld):** Two M.S. plates are placed edge-to-edge in the same plane, with a small root gap, and welded along the seam. Used to make longer plates from shorter pieces.
>
> **Joint B — Lap Joint:** Two M.S. plates overlap each other by a specified length and are welded along both edges of the overlap. Used to increase the cross-sectional area of a joint or for sheet metal assemblies.

**M.S. (Mild Steel) plates** refer to low-carbon steel plates (Carbon content **0.15% – 0.25%**), which are highly weldable, ductile, and the standard training material in KTU machine tools labs.

## 1.4 Equipment & Materials Used (At a Glance)

- **Oxygen cylinder** (black body, blue shoulder) — working pressure **2.0 – 3.5 bar**
- **Acetylene cylinder** (maroon body) — working pressure **0.7 – 1.0 bar**
- **Regulators** (O₂ and C₂H₂) with twin pressure gauges
- **Welding torch** (Blowpipe) with nozzle (tip size **No. 1 to No. 5**)
- **Filler rod** (M.S. welding rod, **2 mm – 3 mm diameter**)
- **Flint lighter / spark lighter**
- **Welding goggles** (No. 5 / No. 6 shade)
- **Wire brush, chipping hammer**
- **C-clamps / tack-welds** for holding the plates
- **M.S. plates** of specified dimensions (typically **100 × 50 × 6 mm**)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric Reminder:** Mild steel melts at approximately **1500 °C**, and the oxy-acetylene flame can reach up to **3480 °C** (in stoichiometric ratio), comfortably above the melting point of M.S.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Oxy-Acetylene Flame — Heart of the Process

The **oxy-acetylene flame** is produced when **acetylene (C₂H₂)** burns in **oxygen (O₂)**. The complete chemical reaction is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
2\,\text{C}_2\text{H}_2 + 5\,\text{O}_2 &\longrightarrow 4\,\text{CO}_2 + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{Heat} \\
\Delta H &= \text{Approximately } 1300 \text{ kJ/mol of C}_2\text{H}_2
\end{aligned}
$$

The flame is composed of **four distinct cones / zones**, each with a specific role in welding:

1. **Zone 1 — The Mixing Cone (Inner Bright Cone):** Unburnt mixture of O₂ + C₂H₂. **No welding here**; this is the *hottest region* if it touches the metal it causes backfire.
2. **Zone 2 — The Reducing Cone (White/Feather Cone):** Carbon-rich reducing atmosphere, where partial combustion occurs. **This is the welding zone** — held **2 – 4 mm** from the workpiece.
3. **Zone 3 — The Outer Envelope:** Complete combustion with surrounding air; releases final heat.
4. **Zone 4 — The Feather Flame:** Excess acetylene appearing as a luminous "feather" when the flame is **carburizing**.

## 2.2 Types of Oxy-Acetylene Flames

| Flame Type | O₂ : C₂H₂ Ratio | Appearance | Application |
|------------|-----------------|------------|-------------|
| **Neutral Flame** | 1.1 : 1 to 1.2 : 1 | Sharp inner cone, no feather, bluish-white | **M.S. welding (used in this experiment)** |
| **Carburizing Flame** (Reducing) | O₂ less than stoichiometric | Excess acetylene, long feather | Surfacing, hard-facing, low-carbon steel |
| **Oxidizing Flame** | O₂ > 1.2 : 1 | Short, pointed, hissing inner cone | Brass, bronze welding (NOT for M.S.) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Neutral Flame for M.S.?** Mild steel is sensitive to carbon pickup. A carburizing flame adds carbon → brittle weld. An oxidizing flame burns off carbon → porous weld. The neutral flame gives the correct chemistry for M.S. welding.

## 2.3 Why Gas Welding for M.S. Plates?

- **Low heat input** suitable for thin sections (≤ 6 mm).
- **No electricity required** — useful in remote sites and labs.
- **Manual control** over pool and temperature.
- **All-position capability** (flat, horizontal, vertical, overhead).
- **Lower equipment cost** compared to arc welding.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table consolidates the **key numerical relationships** tested in KTU viva and written exams for this experiment.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Typical Value/Unit |
|---|-----------|---------|---------------------|
| 1 | Stoichiometric O₂ : C₂H₂ | Volumetric ratio | **1.0 : 1** (neutral ≈ 1.1 : 1) |
| 2 | Flame temperature (neutral) | Peak inner-cone temperature | **3100 – 3200 °C** |
| 3 | Melting point of M.S. | Depends on C content | **≈ 1500 °C** |
| 4 | O₂ cylinder pressure | For torch operation | **2.5 – 3.5 bar** (≈ 35 – 50 psi) |
| 5 | C₂H₂ cylinder pressure | For torch operation | **0.7 – 1.0 bar** (≈ 10 – 15 psi) |
| 6 | Acetylene flow rate (per mm thickness) | Rule-of-thumb: **75 – 100 L/h per mm** | e.g. 6 mm plate → ≈ 450 L/h |
| 7 | Oxygen flow rate | ≈ (1.1 to 1.5) × C₂H₂ flow | e.g. 6 mm plate → ≈ 550 L/h |
| 8 | Heat input (HI) | $\text{HI} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S}$ (arc) / $\text{HI} = \dfrac{Q}{S}$ (gas) | kJ/mm |
| 9 | Travel speed (S) | $S = \dfrac{\text{Length of weld}}{\text{Time of welding}}$ | mm/min |
| 10 | Specific gravity of M.S. | Density of mild steel | **7.85 g/cm³** |
| 11 | Nozzle size selection | For M.S. up to 6 mm | **Nozzle 1 or 2** |
| 12 | Filler rod diameter | ≈ thickness of plate / 2 | 6 mm plate → **3 mm rod** |
| 13 | Root gap (butt joint) | Gap maintained with shim | **1.5 – 2.5 mm** |
| 14 | Tack weld spacing | 50 – 100 mm apart | Used to hold plates |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Heat Input Note for Gas Welding:**
> Unlike arc welding, gas welding heat input is best expressed as:
> $$\text{HI}_{\text{gas}} = \frac{Q_{\text{flame}}}{S} \;\; \text{(kJ/mm)}$$
> where $Q_{\text{flame}}$ is the total useful heat delivered by the flame and $S$ is travel speed. Excessive HI causes **grain growth, distortion, and burn-through** — a frequent viva question.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Gas welding, despite being one of the oldest welding processes, is still used today in:
- **HVAC & Plumbing** — joining copper/brass tubes
- **Automobile body repair** (sheet metal M.S.)
- **Art & sculpture** (precise flame control)
- **Maintenance welding** in remote, electricity-less sites
- **Jewellery and precision assemblies**
- **Training and skill development** (your KTU lab!)

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Procedure & Analytical Matrix

This section provides the **exhaustive experimental procedure** for both joints, broken into discrete, examiner-friendly steps.

## 3.1 Pre-Experiment Checklist

| Step No. | Action | Purpose |
|----------|--------|---------|
| 1 | Verify oxygen and acetylene cylinder pressures | Safety + correct flame |
| 2 | Open O₂ valve, then C₂H₂ valve at torch | Sequence prevents backfire |
| 3 | Light the torch using a flint lighter (never a match) | Safety — match may ignite gas cloud |
| 4 | Adjust to **neutral flame** — sharp inner cone, no feather | Correct chemistry for M.S. |
| 5 | Check regulators for leaks using soap solution | Safety |
| 6 | Wear **goggles, gloves, apron** | PPE |
| 7 | Clean plates with wire brush, remove rust/scale | Avoid slag inclusions |

## 3.2 Experiment 2A — Butt Welding of M.S. Plates

### 3.2.1 Specimen Preparation
1. Take two M.S. plates of dimension, e.g., **100 mm × 50 mm × 6 mm**.
2. Grind/square the mating edges to **90°** using a file or grinder.
3. Mark a **root gap of 1.5 – 2.0 mm** using a spacer.
4. Place the plates edge-to-edge on a flat **firebrick / welding table**.
5. Secure with **C-clamps** or apply **tack welds** at intervals of **~50 mm** to prevent distortion.

### 3.2.2 Welding Procedure (Leftward Technique — typical for thin plates)
1. Hold the torch in the **right hand**, filler rod in the **left hand**.
2. Angle the torch at **45° to the plate surface** in the direction of travel.
3. Hold the filler rod at **30° to the plate**, opposite the torch angle (forming a **75°–90° angle between torch and rod**).
4. Light the **neutral flame** and bring the inner cone to **2 – 4 mm** from the plate.
5. **Heat the starting point** in a circular motion until a **molten pool** forms.
6. Dip the filler rod into the pool, allowing a drop to melt and fuse with the parent metal.
7. Move the torch forward **slowly and steadily**, maintaining a constant pool size (**3 – 4 mm wide**).
8. Use a **slight weaving motion** for thicker plates; for 6 mm, a **straight or slight oscillation** is sufficient.
9. Continue till the joint is completed. Lift the torch away from the weld.
10. **Shut acetylene first**, then **oxygen** (to prevent soot and backfire).

### 3.2.3 Post-Weld Steps
1. Allow the weld to cool in still air (do not quench).
2. Strike the solidified slag with a **chipping hammer** to remove oxide.
3. **Wire-brush** the weld bead to a clean metallic finish.
4. Inspect the joint for surface defects (cracks, porosity, undercut).

## 3.3 Experiment 2B — Lap Welding of M.S. Plates

### 3.3.1 Specimen Preparation
1. Take two M.S. plates of dimension, e.g., **100 mm × 25 mm × 3 mm**.
2. Clean both surfaces at the overlap region with a wire brush.
3. Overlap the plates by a length equal to **2 × thickness** minimum, typically **25 – 40 mm**.
4. Clamp the plates flat using C-clamps, ensuring **zero gap** between the contact surfaces.

### 3.3.2 Welding Procedure
1. Set up a **neutral flame** (slightly oxidizing acceptable here, but neutral is preferred).
2. Weld **two parallel fillet beads** — one along each edge of the overlap.
3. Maintain a **torch angle of 45°** pointing into the joint root (into the corner formed by the two plates).
4. Use a **smaller filler rod** (e.g., 2 mm) since the plate is thin (3 mm).
5. Form the **fillet** (triangular cross-section) by oscillating the torch and dipping filler alternately.
6. Keep the **fillet leg length ≈ plate thickness**.
7. After completing both fillets, switch off gases (acetylene first).

### 3.3.3 Inspection
1. Check both fillets for **uniformity, leg length, throat thickness**.
2. Look for **undercut, lack of fusion, overlap, and porosity**.

## 3.4 Heat Input & Travel Speed — Worked Example (Typical KTU Numerical)

> **Problem (KTU Style):** A butt weld is made on a **6 mm thick M.S. plate** of length **100 mm**. The torch delivers a useful flame heat of **Q = 2500 J/s**. If the welding time is **90 seconds**, calculate the heat input per unit length.

**Given:**
- Useful flame heat $Q = 2500$ J/s
- Welding time $t = 90$ s
- Weld length $L = 100$ mm

**Step 1 — Total useful heat delivered:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{total}} &= Q \times t \\
&= 2500 \;\text{J/s} \times 90 \;\text{s} \\
&= 225{,}000 \;\text{J} \\
&= 225 \;\text{kJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Travel speed:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= \dfrac{L}{t} = \dfrac{100 \;\text{mm}}{90 \;\text{s}} \\
&\approx 1.111 \;\text{mm/s} \\
&= 66.67 \;\text{mm/min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Heat input per unit length:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{HI} &= \dfrac{Q_{\text{total}}}{L} = \dfrac{225{,}000 \;\text{J}}{100 \;\text{mm}} \\
&= 2250 \;\text{J/mm} = 2.25 \;\text{kJ/mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Equivalent check using speed:**
> $$\text{HI} = \frac{Q}{S} = \frac{2500 \;\text{J/s}}{1.111 \;\text{mm/s}} = 2250 \;\text{J/mm} \;\checkmark$$

## 3.5 Defects in Gas Welding — Examiner's Matrix

| Defect | Cause | Remedy |
|--------|-------|--------|
| **Porosity** | Dirty plates, oxidizing flame, rapid cooling | Clean plates; use neutral flame |
| **Undercut** | Excessive heat, slow travel, wrong torch angle | Reduce HI; maintain 45° angle |
| **Lack of fusion** | Cold weld, insufficient heat | Increase flame; reduce travel speed |
| **Overlap** | Too slow, too much filler | Faster travel; less filler |
| **Cracks** | High carbon, rapid cooling, restraint | Preheating, controlled cooling |
| **Distortion** | Uneven heating | Tack welds; balanced welding sequence |
| **Slag inclusion** | Poor cleaning, incorrect rod angle | Wire brush; correct 30°–45° angle |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Oxy-Acetylene Equipment Layout

The following Mermaid block renders the **block-level functional architecture flow** of the entire gas welding setup, mapping how each component interacts. This is the most reliable Mermaid-renderable representation for a laboratory setup.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    O2CYL[OXYGEN CYLINDER] -->|High pressure gas| O2REG[O2 REGULATOR]
    O2REG -->|Reduced to 2.5 to 3.5 bar| O2HOSE[O2 HOSE RED COLOR]
    O2HOSE --> TORCH[GAS WELDING TORCH]

    C2H2CYL[ACETYLENE CYLINDER] -->|Dissolved gas| C2H2REG[C2H2 REGULATOR]
    C2H2REG -->|Reduced to 0.7 to 1.0 bar| C2H2HOSE[C2H2 HOSE BLACK COLOR]
    C2H2HOSE --> TORCH

    TORCH -->|Mixed gases| NOZZLE[NOZZLE TIP NO 1 OR 2]
    NOZZLE -->|Combustion| FLAME[NEUTRAL FLAME 3200 C]
    FLAME -->|Heat| WELDP[MOLTEN WELD POOL ON M.S. PLATE]

    FILLER[FILLER ROD M.S. 2 to 3 MM] -->|Manual dipping| WELDP
    WELDP -->|Solidification| WELDBEAD[FINAL WELD BEAD]

    LIGHTER[FLINT LIGHTER] -.->|Ignition| FLAME
    GOGGLES[SAFETY GOGGLES SHADE 5] -.->|Operator protection| OPERATOR[STUDENT OPERATOR]
    GLOVES[WELDING GLOVES] -.->|Hand protection| OPERATOR

    style O2CYL fill:#1f3a93,color:#fff
    style C2H2CYL fill:#7b1f1f,color:#fff
    style FLAME fill:#e69100,color:#000
    style WELDBEAD fill:#999,color:#000
    style OPERATOR fill:#0d652d,color:#fff
```

## 4.2 The Four Cones of the Oxy-Acetylene Flame

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[INNERMOST BRIGHT CONE - MIXING ZONE] --> B[WHITE FEATHER CONE - REDUCING ZONE WELDING ZONE]
    B --> C[OUTER ENVELOPE - COMPLETE COMBUSTION]
    C --> D[FEATHER FLAME - EXCESS C2H2 IF CARBURIZING]
    A -.- A1[NO WELDING HERE TOO HOT]
    B -.- B1[2 to 4 MM FROM WORKPIECE]
    C -.- C1[FINAL HEAT RELEASE TO ATMOSPHERE]
    D -.- D1[ABSENT IN NEUTRAL FLAME]
    style A fill:#fbbc04,color:#000
    style B fill:#ffffff,color:#000
    style C fill:#4285f4,color:#fff
    style D fill:#e69100,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Butt Joint Setup (Top View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PL1[M.S. PLATE A] -->|edge| GAP[ROOT GAP 1.5 to 2.0 mm]
    GAP -->|edge| PL2[M.S. PLATE B]
    PL1 --> T1[TACK WELD 1]
    PL2 --> T2[TACK WELD 2]
    T1 -.-> TACKLINE[TACK WELDS HOLD PLATES]
    T2 -.-> TACKLINE
    GAP -.- TORCH1[TORCH AT 45 DEG TRAVELS ALONG GAP]
    GAP -.- ROD1[FILLER ROD AT 30 DEG DIPPED INTO POOL]
    style PL1 fill:#a4c2f4,color:#000
    style PL2 fill:#a4c2f4,color:#000
    style GAP fill:#fff2cc,color:#000
    style TACKLINE fill:#ea9999,color:#000
    style TORCH1 fill:#e69100,color:#000
    style ROD1 fill:#b6d7a8,color:#000
```

## 4.4 Lap Joint Setup (Top View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PLATE1[M.S. PLATE 1 BOTTOM] --> OVERLAP[OVERLAP REGION 25 to 40 mm]
    OVERLAP --> PLATE2[M.S. PLATE 2 TOP]
    PLATE1 --> FILLET1[FILLET WELD 1 ALONG EDGE A]
    PLATE2 --> FILLET2[FILLET WELD 2 ALONG EDGE B]
    FILLET1 -.- TORCH2[TORCH AT 45 DEG INTO CORNER]
    FILLET2 -.- ROD2[FILLER ROD 2 mm FOR THIN PLATE]
    style PLATE1 fill:#a4c2f4,color:#000
    style PLATE2 fill:#a4c2f4,color:#000
    style OVERLAP fill:#fff2cc,color:#000
    style FILLET1 fill:#ea9999,color:#000
    style FILLET2 fill:#ea9999,color:#000
```

## 4.5 Right-Hand vs Left-Hand Welding Technique (Comparison)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph RH[RIGHT HAND WELDING]
        R1[Torch in RIGHT hand] --> R2[Filler in LEFT hand]
        R2 --> R3[Flame points FORWARD in direction of travel]
    end
    subgraph LH[LEFT HAND WELDING]
        L1[Torch in LEFT hand] --> L2[Filler in RIGHT hand]
        L2 --> L3[Flame points BACKWARD opposite to travel]
    end
    RH --> USE1[Used for THIN plates up to 6 mm]
    LH --> USE2[Used for THICK plates above 6 mm]
    style R3 fill:#d9ead3,color:#000
    style L3 fill:#fce5cd,color:#000
    style USE1 fill:#b6d7a8,color:#000
    style USE2 fill:#f4cccc,color:#000
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]**
> **Differentiate between a neutral flame and a carburizing flame in oxy-acetylene gas welding.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Neutral Flame | Carburizing Flame |
|--------|---------------|-------------------|
| O₂ : C₂H₂ ratio | 1.1 : 1 (slight excess O₂) | < 1 : 1 (excess C₂H₂) |
| Inner cone shape | Sharp, well-defined | Blurred, surrounded by luminous feather |
| Atmosphere | Chemically balanced | Reducing (carbon-rich) |
| Application | **Mild Steel, Stainless Steel** | Low-carbon steel, surfacing, hard-facing |
| Effect on M.S. | No chemical change | Adds carbon → harder, more brittle weld |

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
> **List any three safety precautions to be followed during gas welding.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Cylinder handling:** Always secure cylinders upright with a chain; never lift by the valve cap. Keep O₂ and C₂H₂ cylinders **at least 6 m apart** to prevent fire.
2. **Valve sequence:** When opening torch — open O₂ valve, then C₂H₂ valve. When closing — close C₂H₂ first, then O₂. **Never** use oil/grease on oxygen fittings (explosion risk).
3. **Personal protection:** Wear **welding goggles (shade 5 or 6)**, leather gloves, fire-resistant apron, and ensure **good ventilation** to avoid inhaling fumes. Keep a **fire extinguisher (dry powder)** within reach.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> ### Question A — 14 Marks
> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
>
> **(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, describe the **oxy-acetylene gas welding setup**. List the function of each major component.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **step-by-step procedure for butt welding two M.S. plates of size 100 mm × 50 mm × 6 mm** in the lab. State the inspection checks to be performed.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Gas Welding Setup (7 marks):**

The gas welding setup consists of the following components in functional sequence:

1. **Oxygen cylinder** (capacity ~7 m³ at 150 bar): Stores O₂ at high pressure. Color: **black body, blue shoulder**.
2. **Oxygen regulator**: Two-stage regulator with two gauges — cylinder pressure and working pressure. Reduces pressure to **2.5 – 3.5 bar**.
3. **Acetylene cylinder** (capacity ~6 m³): Stores dissolved acetylene in acetone. Color: **maroon body**.
4. **Acetylene regulator**: Reduces pressure to **0.7 – 1.0 bar** (acetylene above 1.5 bar is unsafe — risk of decomposition explosion).
5. **Hoses:** **Red** for O₂, **Black** for C₂H₂. Hose connections use non-interchangeable fittings.
6. **Welding torch (blowpipe):** Mixes O₂ and C₂H₂ in a chamber; delivers mixture to the nozzle.
7. **Nozzle/tip:** Sized according to plate thickness (No. 1 for thin, No. 5 for thick).
8. **Flint lighter:** For safe ignition of the gas mixture.
9. **Filler rod, wire brush, chipping hammer, goggles, C-clamps**: Supporting equipment.

**Sketch description (typical block diagram as shown in Section 4.1):**
O₂ cylinder → regulator → hose → torch → nozzle → flame → weld pool.
Acetylene cylinder → regulator → hose → torch → nozzle (same path).

**Part (b) — Butt Welding Procedure (7 marks):**

1. **Plate preparation:** Square the edges; maintain a **root gap of 1.5 – 2.0 mm** using spacers.
2. **Cleaning:** Wire-brush all edges to remove rust, oil, and scale.
3. **Tacking:** Place plates on a firebrick, fix with C-clamps, and apply 3–4 small **tack welds** spaced 50 mm apart to prevent distortion.
4. **Flame setting:** Light the torch and adjust to a **neutral flame** (sharp inner cone, no feather, light blue outer envelope).
5. **Welding:** Hold torch at 45° to the plate, filler rod at 30° (opposite side). Heat starting point until a molten pool forms. Dip the filler rod; oscillate the torch slightly to maintain a pool of ~3–4 mm wide. Proceed steadily along the joint.
6. **Completion:** At the end, lift the torch, **close acetylene first, then oxygen**.
7. **Post-weld:** Cool in still air. Use chipping hammer to remove any oxide scale, then wire-brush.
8. **Inspection checks:**
   - Visual: cracks, undercuts, porosity, surface uniformity.
   - Dimensional: weld bead straightness, leg length, throat thickness.
   - **Bend test** (in lab): bend the welded specimen over a mandrel — no crack should appear on the weld face for a satisfactory joint.

---

> ### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)
> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
>
> **(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **lap welding procedure for M.S. plates**. What is the significance of fillet leg length?
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** What are the common **defects in gas welding**? Explain the **causes and remedies** for any four defects.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Lap Welding Procedure (7 marks):**

1. **Specimens:** Two M.S. plates (e.g., 100 mm × 25 mm × 3 mm).
2. **Cleaning:** Wire-brush both mating surfaces in the overlap region.
3. **Overlap:** Stack the plates with an overlap of **25 – 40 mm** (≈ 2 × thickness). Clamp flat.
4. **Tack welding:** Place 2–3 small tack welds to hold the plates.
5. **Flame:** Adjust to **neutral flame** (slightly oxidizing acceptable but neutral is preferred).
6. **First fillet:** Weld along one edge of the overlap. Hold the torch at **45° pointing into the root corner** of the joint. Dip filler rod (2 mm) to build a **fillet of triangular cross-section**.
7. **Second fillet:** Flip the assembly and weld the opposite edge in the same manner.
8. **Inspection:** Both fillets should have **equal leg length and throat thickness**, with no undercut or porosity.

**Significance of Fillet Leg Length:**

- The **leg length** is the **largest inscribed triangle side** in the fillet cross-section.
- Ideally, leg length ≈ plate thickness. If undersize → weak joint (low load capacity). If oversized → wasted filler, distortion, excess heat input.
- In design, the **throat thickness** $t = 0.707 \times \text{leg length}$ — used to compute the **strength of the fillet weld** as:
  $$\tau_{\text{allow}} = \frac{P}{0.707 \times s \times L}$$
  where $P$ is load, $s$ is leg size, $L$ is weld length.

**Part (b) — Defects in Gas Welding (7 marks):**

| # | Defect | Cause | Remedy |
|---|--------|-------|--------|
| 1 | **Porosity** (small cavities in weld) | Dirty plates, oxidizing flame, rapid cooling | Clean thoroughly; use **neutral flame**; reduce travel speed |
| 2 | **Undercut** (groove along weld toe) | Excessive heat, wrong torch angle, slow travel | Increase travel speed; correct torch angle to 45° |
| 3 | **Lack of fusion** | Insufficient heat input, cold welding | Increase flame size; preheat plate; reduce travel speed |
| 4 | **Overlap** (excess filler spilling over parent metal) | Too slow travel, excessive filler, wrong rod angle | Faster travel; use smaller filler; correct 30° rod angle |
| 5 | **Cracks** (longitudinal or transverse) | High carbon content, restraint, rapid cooling | Preheat; control cooling; use low-hydrogen filler |
| 6 | **Distortion / warping** | Uneven heating, no clamping, asymmetric welds | Use clamps; balanced welding sequence; tack welds |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:**
> 1. **Do not confuse** *flame cone* with *weld pool*. The flame has 4 cones; the weld pool is molten metal on the plate.
> 2. **Do not state** that gas welding uses an electric arc — it does **not**. Mixing it up with arc welding is a common 1-mark error.
> 3. **Do not forget the gas sequence:** Open O₂ first, light, then C₂H₂. Close C₂H₂ first at the end. Reversing this sequence causes **backfire**.
> 4. **Always mention "neutral flame"** for M.S. — examiners specifically look for this term.
> 5. In a 14-mark question, **sketches fetch 2 marks** — never skip a labelled diagram.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision — Gas Welding Lab (KTU 2024)**

### Key Concepts (Must-Know)
- ☐ Gas welding = **fusion welding** using **oxy-acetylene flame**, no electric arc.
- ☐ Standard fuel mixture = **Acetylene (C₂H₂)** + **Oxygen (O₂)**.
- ☐ Four flame cones: mixing, reducing (welding), outer envelope, feather (carburizing only).
- ☐ **Neutral flame** (1.1 : 1 O₂ : C₂H₂) — used for M.S.
- ☐ Flame temperature ≈ **3200 °C**; M.S. melts at ≈ **1500 °C**.
- ☐ Inner cone held **2 – 4 mm** from the workpiece.

### Procedure Highlights
- ☐ Plate thickness 6 mm → nozzle **No. 1 or 2**; filler rod **3 mm**.
- ☐ Plate thickness 3 mm → nozzle **No. 0 or 1**; filler rod **2 mm**.
- ☐ Butt joint: root gap **1.5 – 2.0 mm**, tack welds every **50 mm**.
- ☐ Lap joint: overlap **25 – 40 mm**; two parallel fillet welds.
- ☐ Torch angle **45°**, filler rod angle **30°** (opposite side).
- ☐ Tack-weld before full welding to prevent distortion.

### Gas Cylinder Safety
- ☐ O₂ cylinder = **black + blue**; C₂H₂ cylinder = **maroon**.
- ☐ O₂ working pressure = **2.5 – 3.5 bar**; C₂H₂ = **0.7 – 1.0 bar**.
- ☐ Open O₂ first, then C₂H₂; close C₂H₂ first, then O₂.
- ☐ Never use oil/grease on oxygen fittings.
- ☐ Cylinders kept **6 m apart**.

### Defects to Memorize (4 minimum)
- ☐ **Porosity** → neutral flame, clean plates.
- ☐ **Undercut** → correct torch angle (45°), right travel speed.
- ☐ **Lack of fusion** → increase heat input.
- ☐ **Overlap** → faster travel, less filler.

### Formulae to Retain
- ☐ Heat input $\text{HI} = \dfrac{Q}{S}$ (kJ/mm) — gas welding.
- ☐ Travel speed $S = L/t$ (mm/min).
- ☐ Fillet throat $t = 0.707 \times \text{leg length}$.

### Examiner Triggers (Use These Words!)
- ☐ **"Neutral flame"** — for M.S.
- ☐ **"Welding zone = reducing cone"** — not mixing cone.
- ☐ **"Right-hand technique for thin plates"** — KTU standard.
- ☐ **"Tack welds prevent distortion"** — examiner's favourite.
- ☐ **"Close acetylene first to prevent soot"** — safety.

---

> *End of Module Experiment 2 — Exercises on Gas Welding (Butt & Lap Joints on M.S. Plates)*
> *Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme, NEP 2020 aligned, Outcome-Based Education framework.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Exercises on TIG welding: Butt welding and lap welding of M.S. plates
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIEL407 - MACHINE TOOLS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Exercises on TIG welding: Butt welding and lap welding of M.S. plates

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **TIG (Tungsten Inert Gas) Welding**, standardized as **GTAW (Gas Tungsten Arc Welding)** per **AWS A3.0**, is a non-consumable electrode arc welding process wherein the arc is established between a **tungsten electrode** and the workpiece, while an **inert shielding gas** (typically **Argon** or **Helium**) protects the weld pool from atmospheric contamination.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme lab curriculum (PCIEL407 – Machine Tools Lab), the experiment requires students to fabricate two fundamental joint configurations on **Mild Steel (M.S.) plates**:

1. **Butt Joint** – Two MS plates are aligned edge-to-edge in the *same plane* and fused together.
2. **Lap Joint** – Two MS plates overlap each other over a defined length and are fused at the interface.

> [!NOTE]
> **M.S. Plate (Mild Steel Plate):** Low-carbon steel (C ≤ 0.25%) with excellent weldability. For lab-scale TIG experiments, plates of thickness **2 mm to 4 mm** are typically used.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Understanding

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy: The "Artist's Brush" of Welding**
>
> Imagine you are a painter working on a delicate canvas. Instead of a stiff house-painting brush, you use a fine, controlled brush. The **tungsten electrode** is your fine brush tip—it never wears out (non-consumable). The **inert gas (Argon)** is an invisible "bell jar" of clean air you create around your work, stopping dust (oxygen, nitrogen) from spoiling your art. You feed a separate thin **filler rod** (the paint) with your other hand, allowing precise control. This is essentially TIG welding: **precision, cleanliness, and operator skill** rather than brute force.

| Feature | Analogy | TIG Welding Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| Fine brush | Never wears out | **Tungsten electrode (non-consumable)** |
| Bell jar of clean air | Stops dust | **Argon shielding gas** |
| Paint | External material added | **Filler rod (consumable separately)** |
| Steady hand | Control | **Foot pedal / fingertip current control** |

> [!NOTE]
> The "I" in TIG originally stood for **"Inert"** (Argon, Helium). In modern terminology, it can also stand for **"Inactive"** because the gas is chemically non-reactive with the molten weld pool.

---

## 1.3 Why TIG for M.S. Plates in a Lab?

| Reason | Explanation |
|---|---|
| **Clean welds** | No slag, minimal spatter — ideal for visual inspection in lab records |
| **Precise control** | Independent control of current, gas, and filler feed — teaches parameter-response |
| **No flux required** | Cleaner working environment, no post-weld slag removal |
| **Versatile joints** | Both butt and lap joints achievable on thin MS sheets |
| **Educational value** | Develops hand-eye coordination for torch and filler manipulation |

---

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Constants Used in TIG Welding of M.S.:**
> - **Density of M.S. (ρ):** **7.85 g/cm³**
> - **Melting Point of M.S.:** **~1500 °C**
> - **Specific Heat of M.S. (c):** **0.486 J/g·°C**
> - **Thermal Conductivity of M.S. (k):** **45 W/m·K**
> - **Typical TIG Process Efficiency (η):** **0.60 – 0.70** (for heat input calculations)
> - **Ionization Potential of Argon:** **15.76 eV**
> - **Argon Purity Required:** **≥ 99.95%** (lab-grade)
> - **DCEN Polarity (Direct Current Electrode Negative):** **Standard for MS welding**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics of the TIG Arc

The TIG process relies on establishing a stable electric arc between a **sharpened tungsten electrode** (cathode) and the workpiece (anode). When using **DCEN (Electrode Negative)** polarity for M.S.:

- **~2/3 of the heat** is generated at the **anode (workpiece)** — this is desirable as it concentrates heat where melting is required.
- **~1/3 of the heat** is generated at the **cathode (tungsten electrode)** — keeps the electrode from melting.

> [!NOTE]
> For M.S. plates, **DCEN** is the universal standard. **DCEP (Electrode Positive)** is *not* used for ferrous metals in TIG because it would overheat and melt the tungsten electrode.

### Step-by-Step Operational Logic

1. **Gas Pre-flow:** Argon is purged through the torch nozzle **5–10 seconds** *before* arc striking to displace atmospheric air.
2. **Arc Initiation:** The arc is struck via **High-Frequency (HF) start** or **Lift-Arc start** — a non-contact initiation to prevent tungsten contamination.
3. **Arc Stabilization:** The arc ionizes the Argon, forming a **plasma column** at temperatures of **~20,000 K**.
4. **Weld Pool Formation:** The arc melts a small region of the M.S. plate, forming a molten pool.
5. **Filler Addition (if used):** The operator manually dips a **filler rod** (e.g., ER70S-6 for M.S.) into the leading edge of the weld pool.
6. **Solidification:** As the torch advances, the molten pool solidifies, forming the weld bead.
7. **Gas Post-flow:** Argon continues to flow for **5–15 seconds after arc extinction** to protect the cooling weld and hot tungsten tip from oxidation.

---

## 2.2 Joint Geometry Theory

### 2.2.1 Butt Joint (Edge-to-Edge)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Gap Clearance (g)} &= 0.5 \text{ to } 1.5 \times \text{plate thickness (t)} \\
\text{Root Face (b)} &= 1 \text{ mm (typical for t ≤ 3 mm)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Edge Preparation Logic:**
- For **t ≤ 2 mm**: **Square butt** (no bevel).
- For **2 < t ≤ 6 mm**: **Single-V groove** (60° included angle, 1 mm root face).
- For **t > 6 mm**: **Double-V or U-groove** (not usually seen in lab-scale MS plates).

> [!TIP]
> **Why a small gap?** A gap ensures **full root penetration** by allowing the arc to melt the base metal at the root. A *zero* gap leads to **lack of penetration (LoP)** defects.

### 2.2.2 Lap Joint (Overlap)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Lap Length (L)} &= 3 \text{ to } 5 \times \text{plate thickness (t)} \\
\text{Optimal lap (lab)} &\approx 4 \text{ mm} \text{ for 2 mm plate}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Critical Positioning Logic:**
- The arc should be aimed at the **leading edge of the top plate**, not directly at the interface. This causes **60–70% of the top plate and 30–40% of the bottom plate to melt**, creating proper fusion.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Parameter | Expression | Typical Value (MS, 2–3 mm) | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Heat Input (HI)** | $HI = \dfrac{\eta \times V \times I \times 60}{S \times 1000}$ | **0.6 – 1.2** | kJ/mm |
| **Welding Current (I)** | Empirical: $I \approx 30 \text{ to } 45 \text{ A/mm thickness}$ | **80 – 150** | A (DCEN) |
| **Arc Voltage (V)** | Read from power source | **10 – 15** | V |
| **Travel Speed (S)** | Operator-controlled | **80 – 150** | mm/min |
| **Shielding Gas Flow** | Empirical: $Q \approx 1.5 \text{ to } 2.5 \times \text{nozzle dia.}$ | **8 – 12** | L/min |
| **Argon Pre-flow Time** | Fixed | **5 – 10** | s |
| **Argon Post-flow Time** | Fixed | **5 – 15** | s |
| **Tungsten Electrode Dia.** | For 80–150 A | **2.4** | mm |
| **Tungsten Type** | Thoriated (Red) preferred | **WT20 (2% ThO₂)** | — |
| **Filler Rod Dia.** | Match to plate thickness | **1.6 – 2.0** | mm |
| **Filler Rod Grade** | For MS | **ER70S-6** | AWS A5.18 |
| **TIG Efficiency (η)** | Fraction of arc heat in workpiece | **0.60 – 0.70** | dimensionless |
| **Lap Length** | $L = 3t \text{ to } 5t$ | **6 – 20** | mm |
| **Butt Joint Gap** | $g = 0.5t \text{ to } 1.5t$ | **1 – 4** | mm |
| **Electrode Stickout** | Distance from collet to tip | **3 – 6** | mm |
| **Arc Length** | Gap between tungsten tip and workpiece | **2 – 4** | mm |
| **Nozzle Size** | Ceramic cup inner diameter | **8 – 12** | mm |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL: No `\|` inside table cells!** All absolute-value and "divided by" notations are rendered using `\vert`, `\mid`, or `\dfrac` to preserve markdown table integrity.

---

## 2.4 Heat Input — The Most Important Calculation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Heat Input (HI)** is the single most important variable in welding metallurgy. It directly controls **grain size**, **HAZ width**, **distortion**, and **mechanical properties** of the weld.

$$
\begin{aligned}
HI &= \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v \times 1000} \quad \text{[kJ/mm]} \\
\text{where:} \quad \eta &= \text{process efficiency} \quad (\text{dimensionless}) \\
V &= \text{arc voltage} \quad \text{[V]} \\
I &= \text{welding current} \quad \text{[A]} \\
v &= \text{welding travel speed} \quad \text{[mm/s]}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is Heat Input important for lab work?**
> If the examiner asks *"Why did your weld crack or have porosity?"*, the answer almost always lies in improper heat input. **Too low HI → lack of fusion.** **Too high HI → burn-through, excessive grain growth, distortion.**

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Application of TIG on MS |
|---|---|
| **Automotive** | Roll cages, chassis components, exhaust headers |
| **Pressure Vessels** | Root pass welding on pipes (M.S. and low-alloy steels) |
| **Aerospace** | Thin-gauge M.S. brackets, repair welds |
| **Food Industry** | Sanitary M.S. tubing (TIG preferred over MIG for cleanliness) |
| **Art & Sculpture** | Decorative MS gates, furniture (clean beads, no spatter) |
| **Tool & Die** | Repair of M.S. dies and machine frames |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation: Heat Input Calculation for a Lab Specimen

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Statement (Typical KTU Numerical):**
> A TIG welding operation is performed on a **3 mm thick M.S. plate** in a butt-joint configuration. The welding parameters are:
> - Welding current (**I**) = **120 A** (DCEN)
> - Arc voltage (**V**) = **14 V**
> - Travel speed (**v**) = **3 mm/s**
> - TIG process efficiency (**η**) = **0.65**
>
> Calculate the **Heat Input per unit length** in **kJ/mm**.

### Step-by-Step Exhaustive Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the governing equation:**

$$
HI = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v \times 1000} \quad \text{[kJ/mm]}
$$

This is the standard **AWS Heat Input equation** for arc welding processes, with the factor 1000 used to convert J/mm to kJ/mm.

**Step 2 — Substitute the known values:**

$$
HI = \frac{0.65 \times 14 \times 120}{3 \times 1000}
$$

**Step 3 — Multiply the numerator terms:**

$$
0.65 \times 14 = 9.1
$$

$$
9.1 \times 120 = 1092
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply the denominator terms:**

$$
3 \times 1000 = 3000
$$

**Step 5 — Divide numerator by denominator:**

$$
HI = \frac{1092}{3000} = 0.364 \quad \text{kJ/mm}
$$

**Step 6 — Final Answer with Units:**

$$
\boxed{HI = 0.364 \text{ kJ/mm} \approx 364 \text{ J/mm}}
$$

**Step 7 — Engineering Interpretation:**

| Heat Input Range | Metallurgical Effect on M.S. |
|---|---|
| < 0.5 kJ/mm | **Cold welding** — risk of lack of fusion, porosity |
| 0.5 – 1.5 kJ/mm | **Optimum** for thin MS plates (1–3 mm) |
| 1.5 – 2.5 kJ/mm | **Risk of grain coarsening** in HAZ |
| > 2.5 kJ/mm | **Excessive** — distortion, burn-through on thin plates |

> [!TIP]
> Our calculated **0.364 kJ/mm** is at the **lower edge** of the acceptable range. For a 3 mm MS plate, this is borderline — a slightly slower travel speed (say **2.5 mm/s**) would yield **HI ≈ 0.437 kJ/mm**, which is more robust for a student lab.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example: Determining Electrode & Gas Flow for a 2 mm MS Lap Joint

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:** You are to perform a **TIG lap weld** on two **2 mm M.S. plates** (overlap = 8 mm). Determine:
> (a) Tungsten electrode diameter
> (b) Recommended current
> (c) Argon flow rate
> (d) Filler rod diameter

### Solution Logic

**(a) Tungsten Electrode Diameter:**

For a current range of **80–150 A on DCEN**, AWS standards recommend a **2.4 mm (3/32")** diameter tungsten. A **2% Thoriated (WT20, Red Tip)** grade is preferred for M.S. due to superior electron emission and arc stability.

**(b) Recommended Current (using rule of thumb):**

$$
I \approx 40 \text{ A per mm of plate thickness}
$$

$$
I \approx 40 \times 2 = 80 \text{ A}
$$

> A safe operating range is **75–95 A** for a 2 mm MS plate to avoid burn-through.

**(c) Argon Flow Rate:**

For a **ceramic nozzle of inner diameter 9.5 mm (No. 8 cup)**:

$$
Q \approx 2 \times \text{nozzle ID (mm)} \text{ L/min}
$$

$$
Q \approx 2 \times 9.5 = 19 \text{ L/min?}
$$

Wait — that exceeds the standard range. The correct empirical rule is:

$$
Q \approx 1.0 \text{ to } 1.5 \times \text{nozzle ID (mm)} \text{ L/min for indoor lab}
$$

$$
Q \approx 1.0 \times 9.5 \approx 9.5 \text{ L/min}
$$

> **Final recommended flow: 9 – 10 L/min**

**(d) Filler Rod Diameter:**

A general rule: **filler rod diameter ≈ plate thickness** for autogenous-feel welds; use **1.6 mm ER70S-6** for 2 mm MS to maintain low heat input.

### Final Recommended Parameter Set

| Parameter | Recommended Value |
|---|---|
| Tungsten electrode | **2.4 mm, WT20 (2% Thoriated)** |
| Polarity | **DCEN (Straight)** |
| Current | **80 A** |
| Voltage | **12 V** |
| Argon flow | **10 L/min** |
| Filler rod | **1.6 mm ER70S-6** |
| Travel speed | **~2 mm/s (120 mm/min)** |
| Estimated HI | $\approx 0.52 \text{ kJ/mm}$ |

---

## 3.3 Decision Matrix: When to Use Filler Rod (Autogenous vs. Filler-Assisted)

| Condition | Decision | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Plate thickness **≤ 1.5 mm** | **Autogenous** (no filler) | Filler addition risks excess heat & burn-through |
| Plate thickness **2 – 3 mm** (lab MS) | **Filler-assisted** (1.6 mm rod) | Provides adequate weld metal volume |
| Butt joint with gap > 1.5 mm | **Filler mandatory** | Gap must be filled with weld metal |
| Lap joint | **Filler recommended** | Ensures tie-in at the interface |
| Cosmetic / structural requirement | **Filler-assisted** | Better bead profile and reinforcement |

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix for Common Defects & Root Causes

| Defect | Visual Appearance | Root Cause | Prevention in Lab |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Porosity (gas pockets)** | Small craters/holes on bead surface | Contaminated plate (oil, rust); insufficient gas flow; wet filler rod | Degrease with acetone; verify gas flow ≥ 8 L/min; dry the filler rod |
| **Lack of Fusion (LoF)** | Bead sits on top, no sidewall fusion | Travel speed too high; arc too long; low current | Reduce travel speed; hold arc length 2–3 mm; increase current by 10% |
| **Lack of Penetration (LoP)** | No root fusion (visible in cross-section) | Insufficient current; zero gap in butt joint | Use proper gap (1–2 mm); ensure adequate current |
| **Tungsten Inclusion** | Hard, brittle spots in weld (visible under macro) | Tungsten dipped into weld pool | Maintain arc length; never touch electrode to pool; re-grind if contaminated |
| **Oxidation (black soot on bead)** | Dark, dull bead surface | Insufficient shielding gas; post-flow too short | Increase gas flow; ensure 5–15 s post-flow |
| **Cracking (longitudinal or crater)** | Visible fissure | High heat input; high carbon equivalent; crater not filled | Reduce HI; pause briefly at crater end to fill |
| **Undercut** | Groove at weld toe | Excessive current; wrong torch angle | Reduce current; maintain **10–20° trailing angle** |
| **Distortion / Warping** | Plate bending after weld | Uneven heat input; improper clamping | Use tack welds; clamp symmetrically; weld alternately |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Complete TIG Welding Setup — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PWR["DC Power Source Constant Current"] -->|DCEN Polarity| TRC["TIG Torch Handle"]
    AR["Argon Cylinder 99.95 percent Purity"] -->|via Hose| REG["Gas Regulator Two Stage"]
    REG -->|via Hose| FM["Flow Meter L per min"]
    FM --> TRC
    TRC -->|Tungsten Electrode 2.4 mm WT20| ARC["Plasma Arc 20000 K"]
    ARC -->|Heat| WP["M.S. Workpiece Cathode"]
    FBR["Filler Rod ER70S-6 1.6 mm"] -->|Manual Dip| WP
    WP -->|via Cable| GND["Work Clamp Grounding"]
    GND --> PWR
    WP -.->|Weld Bead| BD["Solidified Weld"]
    FCTL["Foot Pedal Amperage Control"] -->|Signal Cable| PWR
    style PWR fill:#ffd9a8
    style AR fill:#b8e0ff
    style ARC fill:#ff9999
    style WP fill:#cccccc
    style BD fill:#a8e6a8
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram shows the closed-loop nature of the TIG system. The **power source**, **shielding gas**, and **operator-controlled filler rod** all converge at the **arc-workpiece interface**.

## 4.5 Tungsten Electrode Grinding Orientation (Critical for Arc Stability)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Longitudinal Grind Direction"] --> B["Grind Marks Parallel to Electrode Axis"]
    B --> C["Stable Arc Concentrated Heat"]
    D["Radial Grind Direction WRONG"] --> E["Grind Marks Circumferential"]
    E --> F["Unstable Arc Wandering"]
    style A fill:#cce5ff
    style C fill:#ccffcc
    style D fill:#ffcccc
    style F fill:#ff9999
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For DCEN TIG on M.S. plates:** Grind the tungsten electrode in the **longitudinal direction** (along the length) with a dedicated **diamond-grit grinding wheel**. The tip should be a **truncated cone** with a small flat at the apex (approx **0.5–1 mm flat for 2.4 mm dia. electrode**). Never grind on a bench-grinder meant for steel — cross-contamination ruins the arc.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024]`

**Define TIG welding. Why is Argon used as the shielding gas in TIG welding of M.S. plates?**

**Model Answer:**

> **TIG (Tungsten Inert Gas) welding** is a fusion welding process in which the welding arc is struck between a **non-consumable tungsten electrode** and the workpiece, with the weld pool protected from atmospheric contamination by an **inert shielding gas** (most commonly **Argon**).
>
> **Argon is used because:**
> 1. It is **chemically inert** — does not react with the molten M.S. weld pool (no oxidation, no nitrides).
> 2. It is **heavier than air** — forms a stable blanket over the weld pool, displacing oxygen and nitrogen.
> 3. It has a **low thermal conductivity** — produces a stable, focused arc.
> 4. Its **ionization potential (15.76 eV)** is well-suited to stable arc initiation.
> 5. It is **inexpensive and abundantly available** (≈ 1% of Earth's atmosphere).

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Differentiate between Butt joint and Lap joint with neat sketches. State one industrial application of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Butt Joint | Lap Joint |
|---|---|---|
| **Geometry** | Plates aligned edge-to-edge in the same plane | Plates overlap each other by a length L |
| **Gap** | Small gap (0.5t – 1.5t) required for penetration | No gap; lap length = 3t to 5t |
| **Loading** | Better in tension (efficiency ~100%) | Better in shear/compression |
| **Welding** | Single bead on top (and root for thick plates) | Single bead at leading edge of top plate |
| **Application** | Pressure vessel longitudinal seams | Sheet metal ducting, automotive body panels |
| **Material use** | No overlap → less material | Extra material due to overlap |
| **Distortion** | Symmetric (less) | Asymmetric (more) |

**Industrial Applications:**
- **Butt joint:** Longitudinal seams of **low-pressure M.S. tanks**, pipe welding.
- **Lap joint:** **Automotive chassis panels, sheet metal enclosures, ducts**.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full-Question Choices (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Lab course questions in ESE often ask students to **justify parameter selection** and **link parameters to weld quality**. Marks are *not* awarded for merely listing values — students must **explain the reasoning**. Forgetting units (e.g., kJ/mm vs. J/mm) results in **−0.5 to −1 mark deduction**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU Model Question - PCIEL407 ESE Pattern]`

**(a)** With the help of a **neat block diagram**, describe the **complete setup of a TIG welding station**. List the **essential accessories** required. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** During a TIG butt welding experiment on **3 mm M.S. plates**, the following readings were recorded:
- Current: **110 A (DCEN)**
- Voltage: **13 V**
- Travel speed: **2.5 mm/s**
- Process efficiency: **0.65**

Calculate the **heat input per unit length** and comment on whether it is suitable for the given plate thickness. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to A(a):

A TIG welding station consists of the following **functional units**:

1. **Power Source:** A **constant-current (CC) DC rectifier or inverter** that supplies DCEN polarity. The open-circuit voltage (OCV) is typically **60–80 V** to ensure reliable arc initiation.

2. **TIG Torch:** A handheld assembly comprising a **ceramic nozzle**, **collet**, **tungsten electrode**, and a **gas lens** (optional, for laminar flow).

3. **Shielding Gas Cylinder:** **Argon cylinder** of purity ≥ 99.95%, fitted with a **two-stage regulator** and a **flow meter (rotameter)**.

4. **Work Clamp & Grounding Cable:** Connects the workpiece back to the **positive terminal** of the power source (in DCEN).

5. **Filler Rod:** A consumable **ER70S-6** grade rod (1.6 mm dia.) for M.S.

6. **Foot Pedal (Amperage Control):** Allows real-time **current modulation** during welding — a defining feature of TIG.

7. **HF (High-Frequency) Arc Starter:** Enables **non-contact arc initiation**, preserving the tungsten tip.

8. **Cooling System:** For high-current applications (>200 A), water-cooled torches are used; for lab-scale work, **air-cooled** torches are sufficient.

**Block Diagram (as per Section 4.1).**

**Essential Accessories Checklist:**
- Argon regulator + flow meter
- TIG torch with ceramic nozzle (No. 6, 7, or 8)
- Tungsten electrode (2.4 mm, 2% Thoriated)
- Ground clamp + cable (10–25 mm² copper)
- Filler rod (ER70S-6, 1.6 mm)
- Wire brush (stainless steel — *not* mild steel, to avoid contamination)
- Acetone + clean lint-free cloth (for cleaning)
- Auto-darkening welding helmet (DIN 9–13)
- Welding gloves (leather, gauntlet type)
- Leather apron & closed-toe leather shoes
- Fume extractor / cross-ventilation

---

#### Model Solution to A(b):

**Step 1 — Identify the formula:**

$$
HI = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v \times 1000} \quad \text{[kJ/mm]}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values:**

$$
HI = \frac{0.65 \times 13 \times 110}{2.5 \times 1000}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute numerator:**

$$
0.65 \times 13 = 8.45
$$

$$
8.45 \times 110 = 929.5
$$

**Step 4 — Compute denominator:**

$$
2.5 \times 1000 = 2500
$$

**Step 5 — Final calculation:**

$$
HI = \frac{929.5}{2500} = 0.3718 \text{ kJ/mm} \approx 0.37 \text{ kJ/mm}
$$

**Step 6 — Engineering comment:**

> For a **3 mm M.S. plate**, the **typical acceptable range** of heat input is **0.5 – 1.5 kJ/mm**. Our computed value of **0.37 kJ/mm** is **on the lower side (borderline cold welding)**.
>
> **Consequence:** Risk of **lack of fusion** at the weld toes and **incomplete root penetration**.
>
> **Recommendation:** Reduce travel speed to **~2.0 mm/s** (to raise HI to ~0.46 kJ/mm) or increase current to **~130 A** (yielding HI ≈ 0.44 kJ/mm) to ensure a robust weld on 3 mm MS.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU Model Question - PCIEL407 ESE Pattern - Alternative Choice]`

**(a)** List and explain the **step-by-step procedure** to perform a **TIG lap welding** on two **2 mm M.S. plates** in your college lab. Mention the **safety precautions** to be followed. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** During inspection of the welded joint, the following **defects** were observed:
- Black soot on the bead surface
- A small crater at the end of the weld
- Visible root gap (incomplete penetration)

Identify the **most likely root cause** for each defect and suggest **corrective measures**. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to B(a):

**Step-by-Step Procedure for TIG Lap Welding of 2 mm M.S. Plates:**

1. **Safety Briefing & PPE:** Wear **auto-darkening helmet (DIN 11)**, **leather gloves**, **apron**, and ensure **fume extraction** is on.

2. **Material Preparation:**
   - Cut two M.S. plates of size **100 mm × 30 mm × 2 mm** using a hacksaw/shear.
   - File the edges smooth; remove all burrs.

3. **Cleaning (Critical Step):**
   - **Degrease** both plates with **acetone** using a lint-free cloth.
   - **Wire-brush** the surfaces along the weld zone to remove mill scale, rust, and oil.
   - *Avoid touching cleaned surfaces with bare hands* (oils from skin cause porosity).

4. **Joint Setup:**
   - Place one plate on top of the other with an **overlap of 8 mm** (i.e., 4t).
   - Clamp the assembly on a **welding table** with the lap edge accessible.
   - Apply **two tack welds** (small, 5 mm long) at the ends to hold the plates.

5. **Equipment Setup:**
   - Install a **2.4 mm 2% Thoriated tungsten electrode** in the TIG torch; grind to a **truncated cone** (longitudinal grind).
   - Set electrode stickout to **4 mm**.
   - Fit a **No. 8 ceramic nozzle** (9.5 mm ID).
   - Connect **Argon** cylinder via regulator; set flow to **10 L/min**.
   - Set power source to **DCEN, 80 A, 12 V**.

6. **Pre-flow:** Open Argon valve; wait **5 seconds** for gas to purge air from the torch.

7. **Welding:**
   - Hold the torch at a **75° trailing angle** (15° from vertical, tilted in the direction of travel).
   - Maintain an **arc length of 2–3 mm**.
   - Strike the arc using **HF start**.
   - Dip the **1.6 mm ER70S-6 filler rod** at the **leading edge of the top plate** at a **30° angle**.
   - Move at a **steady travel speed of ~2 mm/s** (120 mm/min).
   - Watch the weld pool — it should be **bright, fluid, and slightly oval** (not balled up).

8. **Crater Fill:** At the end of the bead, **pause briefly** and feed extra filler to fill the crater. Release the foot pedal to ramp down the current.

9. **Post-flow:** Hold the torch in place for **5–10 seconds** to allow Argon to shield the cooling weld.

10. **Inspection:**
    - **Visual:** Look for uniform bead, no porosity, no undercut.
    - **Bend test (180°):** No crack should appear.
    - **Macro-examination (optional):** Cut cross-section, polish, etch with **Nital (5% HNO₃ in alcohol)**, observe under microscope.

**Safety Precautions:**
| Hazard | Precaution |
|---|---|
| UV radiation (arc flash) | Auto-darkening helmet, full-coverage clothing |
| Electric shock | Insulated gloves; ensure grounding is proper; **never weld in rain** |
| Fumes & gases | Fume extractor; ensure ventilation; **Argon is an asphyxiant** — never use in confined spaces without fresh air |
| Fire | Clear the area of combustibles; keep a **fire extinguisher** (Class ABC) nearby |
| Hot metal | Allow the weld to cool before handling; use **welder's pliers** |
| Burns from spatter | Leather apron, gloves, closed-toe boots |

---

#### Model Solution to B(b):

**Defect Diagnosis & Corrective Measures:**

| # | Observed Defect | Most Likely Root Cause | Corrective Measure |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Black soot on bead surface** | **Insufficient shielding gas coverage** — either gas flow too low (< 8 L/min), or post-flow time too short, or nozzle too far from workpiece, or Argon purity compromised | Increase Argon flow to 10–12 L/min; extend post-flow to 10–15 s; reduce nozzle-to-work distance to 8–12 mm; check Argon cylinder pressure and purity |
| 2 | **Small crater at end of weld** | **Current cut-off too abrupt** (no ramp-down or crater fill) — the molten pool solidifies before being filled with weld metal | Use **down-slope (current ramp-down)** at the end; feed extra filler into the crater before breaking the arc; or use the **foot pedal** to taper the current |
| 3 | **Visible root gap (incomplete penetration)** | **Insufficient heat input at the root** — current too low, or travel speed too high, or butt gap too tight (zero gap), or root face too thick | Increase current by ~10%; reduce travel speed; ensure **gap of 1–2 mm** in butt joint; grind a **1 mm root face**; consider a **root pass** with backing |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Common Deductions:**
> - Saying "porosity is due to dirt" without naming the **specific remedy** (acetone cleaning + gas flow increase) costs marks.
> - Stating "increase current" without a **percentage or new value** is considered vague. Always say "**increase current from 80 A to 95 A**" or similar.
> - Forgetting to mention **post-flow** in shielding gas issues loses at least **1 mark**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Last-Minure KTU Prep)**

- [ ] **TIG = GTAW** = Gas Tungsten Arc Welding; **non-consumable** tungsten electrode; **inert** shielding gas (Argon).
- [ ] **DCEN** is the **only** polarity used for M.S. in TIG.
- [ ] **Tungsten electrode:** 2% Thoriated (red tip), 2.4 mm dia., **longitudinally ground** to a **truncated cone** with ~0.5 mm flat.
- [ ] **Argon flow:** 8–12 L/min; **pre-flow 5 s**, **post-flow 5–15 s**.
- [ ] **Filler rod for M.S.:** **ER70S-6**, 1.6 mm dia.
- [ ] **Butt joint gap:** **0.5t to 1.5t**; **Lap joint overlap:** **3t to 5t**.
- [ ] **TIG efficiency (η):** **0.60 – 0.70**.
- [ ] **Heat input formula:** $HI = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v \times 1000}$ **kJ/mm**.
- [ ] **Optimum HI for 2–3 mm M.S. plates:** **0.5 – 1.5 kJ/mm**.
- [ ] **Arc length:** **2–3 mm**; **Torch angle:** **75° trailing** (for filler-fed welds).
- [ ] **Cleaning with acetone** is non-negotiable — skin oils cause porosity.
- [ ] **Tungsten contamination** = arc instability; **re-grind** the electrode.
- [ ] **Black soot = shielding problem**; **crater = no ramp-down**; **LoP = low HI or zero gap**.
- [ ] **Safety:** Helmet (DIN 11), leather gloves, fume extractor, **Argon is an asphyxiant** — ventilate.
- [ ] **Visual + Bend test + (optional) Macro examination** are the standard inspection methods in lab.
- [ ] **Lab report must include:** specimen dimensions, parameters set, parameters observed, defect sketches, heat input calculation, and conclusion.
- [ ] **Examiner loves:** "I observed..." (not "I think..."), proper units, and a **labelled sketch** of the setup.
- [ ] **Examiner hates:** missing units, generic "porosity occurs in welding" answers, no mention of **post-flow** time, and any claim that **DCEP is used for steel in TIG** (it is not — never!).
